questions.rationale: The kidney reabsorbs water and other electrolytes in response to chemical receptors.

questions.stem: What process involves blood being brought by the incoming capillaries into the kidney, which contains nitrogenous substances to be excreted as waste?
questions.rationale: Capillary blood from the renal arteries filters into the kidney for processing as the first step.

questions.stem: What should treatment focus on when a patient is hypovolemic?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Extracellular fluid deficit and limiting drinking water

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertonic intracellular deficit and limiting water intake

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extracellular fluid deficit and encouraging fluid intake

choices.label: D
choices.text: Circulatory system hormone deficit and limiting water intake

questions.rationale: A fluid volume deficit occurs when the fluid volume in the body is inadequate; the nurse may encourage drinking fluids as a nursing action.

questions.stem: A patient is frequently thirsty. To what should the nurse attribute this symptom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Too much sodium and too much water in the body

choices.label: B
choices.text: Too little sodium and too much water in the body

choices.label: C
choices.text: Too much sodium and too little water in the body

choices.label: D
choices.text: Too little sodium and too little water in the body

questions.rationale: Normal thirst is the body’s way of calling for an increase in fluid volume, which could mean that the body is retaining too much sodium and too little water.

questions.stem: What should a nurse instruct a patient with a K+ level of 6.2 to avoid? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Banana, carrots, tomatoes, and celery are all high in K+ and should be avoided. Lima beans are low in K+.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses that a patient with congestive heart failure who is being treated with a diuretic has lost 4.4 lb in 1 day. This weight loss is equivalent to the loss of ____ L of fluid.
questions.rationale: 1 L of fluid is equal to 2.2 lb. A weight loss of 4.4 lb is equal to 2 L.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses deep-rapid respirations in a patient with metabolic acidosis to be an indicator of the homeostatic system at work to reduce the __________ level. (Do not abbreviate your answer.)
questions.rationale: The lungs are primarily responsible for the regulation of CO2 by changing the rate and depth of respirations.

questions.stem: A nurse should anticipate in a patient with respiratory acidosis that the blood pH reading would be lower than ______.
questions.rationale: The lowest normal value for blood pH is 7.35. Any value lower than 7.3 indicates acidosis.

questions.stem: A nurse cautions a group of high school athletes about fluid loss in hot, dry weather, because the normal loss from respiration, which is 300 to _____ mL/day, is doubled.
questions.rationale: The normal fluid loss through evaporation is 300 to 400 mL a day. The fluid loss increases in hot, dry weather.

questions.stem: Which statement best defines concepts to be included in a teaching plan for a patient with cancer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cancer is a group of diseases. The cancer cells are different from the cells in the tissue of its origin in both the growth and spreading of abnormal cells.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cancer is the third leading cause of death in the United States. Many hospitals have the highest number of patients with this diagnosis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Americans who have a diagnosis of cancer die within 1 year or less.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When a person is genetically predisposed to a type of cancer, nothing can be done to prevent its occurrence.

questions.rationale: Information about the disease and disease process is helpful to allay anxiety, as well as to instruct about its pathophysiologic changes.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that the seven warning signs of cancer written by the American Cancer Society (ACS) can be recalled by the acronym CAUTION. What should the nurse change to point out that the A in the acronym represents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A sore that will not heal

questions.rationale: The acronym is a change in bowel or bladder habits, a sore that will not heal, unusual bleeding or discharge, a thickening or lump in the breast or elsewhere, indigestion or difficulty swallowing, and a nagging cough or hoarseness.

questions.stem: A home health nurse is compiling a patient profile on a 23-year-old obese woman who smokes one-half pack of cigarettes a day and drinks 1 beer a week. She works as a cook in a long-term care facility. She has two children and eats a diet high in fats. She exercises 30 minutes a day. Both parents are dead of heart disease. How many risk factors for cancer in this profile should the nurse identify?
questions.rationale: Cancer risks identified are obesity, smoker, and high-fat diet.

questions.stem: What phase is considered the latent period before the period of rapid growth of tumors?
questions.rationale: The stage of promotion is the latest period in which a cell is quiet, just before the rapid growth and tumor production, which begins in the progression phase.

questions.stem: Which is true about both malignant tumors and benign tumors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both contain cells that closely resemble those in the tissue of origin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both travel quickly to invade and destroy other tissues and organs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both always grow and multiply very rapidly, competing for space and nutrients and causing severe pain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both may press on nearby surrounding tissues, such as nerves and blood vessels, causing pain.

questions.rationale: Both benign and malignant tumors may create pressure on or obstruct an organ.

questions.stem: A patient newly diagnosed with cancer says, “I feel like I’ve lost my future. I feel so much harm has been done to me that I’m overwhelmed.” To what type of coping strategies should the nurse recognize this attitude will most likely lead the patient?
questions.rationale: Persons who appraise their cancer diagnosis as harm or loss are more likely to use avoidant coping strategies.

questions.stem: A patient has a cancer that has been TNM staged as T3 (1-4, size of primary tumor), N2 (0-3, degree of spread to regional lymph nodes), and M1 (0-1, presence of metastasis). He has an as needed order of 4 mg of morphine intramuscularly every 4 hours. He requests another pain injection approximately 3.5 hours after the last one. What is the most appropriate nursing action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inform the patient that this narcotic may be given only every 4 hours to prevent addiction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ignore the call bell for 20 minutes and then take at least 10 minutes to prepare and administer the injection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the morphine and evaluate the results of pain relief. Arrange for the physician to evaluate for breakthrough pain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the family to assist in helping the patient accept waiting longer to receive an addicting medication such as morphine.

questions.rationale: Terminal care does not include concerns about morphine addiction. Medication may be given slightly before the allotted time. The occurrence of breakthrough pain is a real concern for this patient.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for a chemotherapy treatment in approximately 30 minutes. Breakfast trays have arrived and are being served on the unit. What is the nurse’s best intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encourage the patient to eat all his breakfast to keep up his strength to fight the cancer. Remind the patient that breakfast is about one third of his daily intake.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Listen attentively to any concerns that the patient expresses regarding the treatment. Offer to hold his tray until after the treatment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offer to call the family to come and be present after the treatment. Encourage the patient to drink at least all of the orange juice and coffee.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suggest that the patient request a dose of strong analgesic instead of eating because this treatment is very painful.

questions.rationale: Chemotherapy causes nausea and vomiting. Holding the tray until later provides for better intake and for holding the food in the stomach for digestion.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that drugs such as cannabinoids or Benadryl are frequently ordered for patients with cancer who are taking chemotherapy. What do these types of drugs accomplish?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Promote amnesia to dampen the fear.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maintain fluid retention to prevent dehydration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Control nausea, vomiting, and taste disorders caused by the therapy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Control bouts of diarrhea or uncomfortable constipation.

questions.rationale: Cannabinoids and antihistimines such as Benadryl are useful to help control nausea.

questions.stem: While bathing a patient with cancer, a nurse assessed several large new bruises on the patient’s upper arms and thighs. What is the best understanding of the possible causes and the correct nurse’s actions for these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient must have fallen last night walking to the bathroom. Teach the patient to use the call bell when assistance is needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient may have disseminated intravascular coagulation. The size, shape, location, color, and tenderness must be reported and recorded fully.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An intravascular fluid overload is occurring because of the chemotherapy. Place the patient on strict input/output (I/O) status and limit fluids.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient must have had a drug-induced seizure, which caused arm and leg thrashing and the bruises. Report and record the findings, and pad the side rails.

questions.rationale: These signs of hemorrhage may indicate the oncologic emergency of disseminated intravascular coagulation. Serious signs need to be reported in a timely manner and fully described. REF: p. 128

questions.stem: What manifestations might be assessed by a nurse when a patient is diagnosed with the oncologic emergency hypercalcemia?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Laboratory test results of potassium 3.5 mEq/L, sodium 143 mEq/L

questions.rationale: The indications for hypercalcemia are fatigue, confusion, weakness, and poor muscle tone. If left untreated, renal failure and death may occur.

questions.stem: A female college student has had a melanoma of the forehead surgically removed and given a course of chemotherapy. Which comment demonstrates an understanding of the treatments and prognosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Why did you bring me this shampoo? You guys took all my hair, so I don’t have anything to wash or fix.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Why don’t my friends from school come to visit? Did you tell them to stay away?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “My spring dance is only 3 weeks away. Do you think I could find a wig to cover my head where the hair fell out from the chemo?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Well, this looks like the end of the problem for me, thank goodness! I won’t have to bother that doctor again until I graduate in a couple of years because all my shots must be up to date now.”

questions.rationale: Acceptance of the diagnosis, treatments, side effects, and prognosis by the patient are reflected in her understanding that she will be bald.

questions.stem: A patient whose cancer has been staged at T4 N2 M1 has been assigned for care. What is the best interpretation of this staging information in planning care for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The primary tumor has shrunk, although some lymph nodes remain involved. Teach the patient that this is good news.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The primary tumor has now responded to a combination of chemotherapy and radiation. The patient should now receive much less analgesic medication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The primary tumor is quite large and has extended to lymph glands and distant areas. Gentle touch and therapeutic listening will be especially helpful.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After the series of radiation treatments, the distant metastases are still present. Prepare the patient to accept only the cure of the primary tumor.

questions.rationale: Correct interpretation and differentiating components of staging data are used to plan effective nursing care.

questions.stem: Which symptom reported by a patient taking doxorubicin (Adriamycin) should be considered a priority for intervention?
questions.rationale: Adriamycin is cardiotoxic medication and can cause heart failure.

questions.stem: A diagnosis of breast cancer is first made at stage T1 N0 M0. Which patient problem would be appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Altered body image, related to threats of anticipated changes.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Potential for infection, related to decreased white blood cell count.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Inadequate coping, related to husband’s expectations regarding anticipated treatments.”

questions.rationale: Early stages of cancer create anxiety about the outcome of treatments for the patient.

questions.stem: What is the most common site of cancer in adult women?
questions.rationale: The gender of the person determines the risk for some cancers and the need for early detection. The incidence of breast cancer is 31% of the reported cases of cancer.

questions.stem: Which complaint should a nurse consider a warning sign for cancer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intense pain in an area such as a hip or groin after carrying several gallons of paint up a ladder and painting the garage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Persistent indigestion associated with difficulty swallowing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diarrhea that lasts 2 days after an all-day picnic at the beach

choices.label: D
choices.text: A painful lump under the umbilicus that recedes when pushed but comes out again with a sneeze or hard cough

questions.rationale: Persistent indigestion associated with dysphagia is an American Cancer Society–published risk for a cancerous sign.

questions.stem: What is the most common site of cancer in adult men?
questions.rationale: Gender defines some of the potential risks for cancer. Prostate cancer accounts for 33% of the reported cases of cancer in men.

questions.stem: Which cytologic test, usually performed in outpatient settings, suggests the probability of a need for further testing for cancer cells?
questions.rationale: Preventive testing (Pap test) and screening reduce the risks for cancer and increase the chances of early treatment and is the most commonly performed screening test.

questions.stem: What is the cause of cell death when a patient receives radiation as a treatment for cancer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Separation of the cell from normal cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alteration of the DNA of the cell

questions.rationale: The immediate effect of radiation is cell death as a result of damage to the cell membrane. The delayed effect is the alteration of the DNS so that it cannot replicate.

questions.stem: What is the best menu choice for a patient with nausea who is undergoing radiation treatments every other day?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bowl of vegetable soup, chopped egg and pickle sandwich on wheat bread, one apple, and 8 oz of orange juice

choices.label: B
choices.text: Broiled chicken with rice, vanilla yogurt, one-quarter cup of spinach, one-half ripe banana, and 8 oz of grape juice

choices.label: C
choices.text: Spanish rice, one-half cup of mixed green salad, one-half cup of canned peaches, and 8 oz of Coke

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spaghetti with tomato sauce, cheddar cheese toast strips, six celery sticks with peanut butter, and 8 oz of whole milk

questions.rationale: Food choices for a nauseated patient should be mild and easily digested, with no spicy sauces or dark colas.

questions.stem: A patient is close to death with terminal liver cancer with widespread metastases and calls the nursing staff to his room every 5 minutes. Which interventions will be most supportive and in the best interest of the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encourage and insist that the family request a transfer to hospice care because the general hospital does not have enough staff members to keep responding to the patient’s end-stage frequent calling and requests for minor help.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use fixed interval and cocktail medication administration. Frequently evaluate for breakthrough pain and anxieties. Answer the call bell quickly on the intercom or in person.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the family that as of this afternoon, all of the patient’s questions, comments, and expressed fears of dying and financial worries will be referred to the social worker, physician, or clergy. Otherwise, one of them can come in and sit beside the bed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plan to limit strictly the time spent with the patient because the nurse cannot do much that could be beneficial at this point.

questions.rationale: Therapeutic touch, the nurse’s presence, and sufficient pain medication to make the patient comfortable are appropriate nursing actions.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient should lead the nurse to conclude that the precolonoscopy instruction provided has been effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I know that the lighted tube he will use will help the doctor look at my tumor, and he might take a small piece of tissue to look at in the lab.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I know that the light on the tube will help cure my cancer.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I know the colonoscopy is very painful and embarrassing, and I hope no one sees me in that position.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “My daughter is coming in to see me today. I am glad to be looking forward to something pleasant.”

questions.rationale: Colonoscopy diagnostic procedures are essentially painless but have no curative benefit.

questions.stem: Which factors in middle-aged persons promote the formation of malignant cells? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Increased age, hormonal changes, heredity, and a poor diet are possible sources of increased incidence of cancer in middle-aged persons.

questions.stem: What recommendations should a nurse make to a visitor of a patient who has an internal radiation implant? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid visitation if you are pregnant.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take off all metals, such as your watch and belt.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stay at least 6 feet away from bedside.

questions.rationale: Visitors are important to reduce the isolation of the patient who is undergoing radiation, but pregnant women should not visit. The visits of all persons should be limited to a few minutes, and they should be at least 6 feet from the bedside. Removing metal objects and wearing protective devices are not necessary.

questions.stem: Why is adjuvant therapy given to patients with cancer who are free of signs of the disease? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduces the extent of the tumor before surgery or radiation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Changes the pH of the system to inhibit cell growth.

questions.rationale: Adjuvant therapy is given to symptom-free patients with cancer to eradicate undetected cells and to diminish the recurrence of breast cancer. Administration before surgery or radiation to reduce tumor bulk is called nonadjuvant therapy. The therapy does not stabilize normal cells or alter the pH.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse implement if an intravenous vesicant cancer drug has extravasated? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place a warm compress on the area.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chill the area with an ice pack.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Raise the patient’s arm above the level of the heart.

questions.rationale: The licensed practical nurse should stop the infusion and notify the charge nurse so that specially trained personnel can intervene.

questions.stem: When is surgical remedy for cancer most successful? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cancer cells have invaded only a nonvital structure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lymph glands have not been involved.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cancer cells are confined to only two areas.

questions.rationale: Surgical remedies have a high success rate if the cancer is detected early, the tumors are not fast growing, the cancer cells have not invaded any vital structure, the lymph glands have not been involved, and the cancer cells are isolated in one area.

questions.stem: A nurse clarifies that cells that change from their tissues of origin and have multiple nuclei are categorized as ______.
questions.rationale: Undifferentiated cells are those that change from normal cells of tissue origin. These cells may have a large nucleus or multiple nuclei.

questions.stem: Radiation is now measured in gray rather than rads. One gray is equal to ______ rads. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: One gray is equal to 100 rads.

questions.stem: List the tumors in order based on the following tissue of origin: 1, fat; 2, fibrous; 3, smooth muscle; 4, glands; 5, bone. ______ (Place the events in the appropriate sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)
questions.rationale: Tumors are associated with the tissue from which they arise: fat tumors are lipomas, fibrous tissue tumors are fibromas, smooth muscle tumors are leiomyomas, and bone tumors are sarcomas. Carcinomas include glands.

questions.stem: The length of time that a nurse should leave heat to an injured hip of a patient is no longer than
questions.rationale: If a heating device is left on more than 30 minutes, the effectiveness of the treatment is diminished, and injury to the tissues may occur.

questions.stem: A patient with an extensive abdominal surgical procedure is assessed by the nurse as having predictable pain. How often should the nurse administer analgesics to this patient to be most effective?
questions.rationale: Using a preventive approach for managing this patient’s pain management is the best plan for the nurse because the pain is predictable and major.

questions.stem: What sympathetic responses to pain might be assessed by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased blood pressure, increased pulse, and increased respiratory rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased blood pressure, decreased pulse, and increased respiratory rate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased blood pressure, decreased pulse, and increased respiratory rate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased blood pressure, decreased pulse, and decreased respiratory rate

questions.rationale: The sympathetic nervous system controls blood pressure, pulse, and respiration; it is stimulated during pain.

questions.stem: A health care provider has prescribed both heat and cold treatments for an older adult patient with a leg injury. The nursing care plan reflects secondary diagnoses of peripheral vascular disease (PVD), diabetes, and an allergy to latex. Which of the prescribed treatments should the nurse administer and why?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse will use cold treatment because patients with diabetes and a latex allergy cannot tolerate heat.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The nurse will use cold treatment for this patient with a fracture because cold will help set the cast.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse will use heat treatment because cold is contraindicated for patients with PVD.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse will use heat treatment because heat will increase circulation and increase the threat of infection in the injured part.

questions.rationale: Patients with PVD have blood flow problems that physiologically slow circulation. This problem would be exacerbated by cold. Heat will increase the circulation, which would be a desired effect.

questions.stem: A nurse notices that a patient seems calm and peaceful despite an assessment that the patient’s injuries might be causing severe pain. The patient tells the nurse that using yoga and meditation lessens the perceptions of pain to tolerable levels. Which other alternative intervention should the nurse suggest to help relax this patient for pain relief?
questions.rationale: Alternate methods of pain relief are effective for many patients. Activities such as yoga, meditation, and listening to music are helpful and relaxing.

questions.stem: What intervention of pain control exemplifies the gate control methods of pain relief?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Instructing the patient in stretching exercises

questions.rationale: Massage, position change, hot or cold applications, and distraction all can close the gate.

questions.stem: To perform a nursing assessment correctly, a nurse must remember that pain perception involves several central nervous system (CNS) processes. Which are examples of CNS processes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Afferent pathways carry messages to the spinal cord.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Efferent pathways stimulate the spinal cord to recognize the location of pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nociceptors in the brain stimulate the spinal cord.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pain receptors in muscle, skin, and subcutaneous tissue stimulate efferent pathways.

questions.rationale: Pain perception ascends to the brain and back down again to imprint the pain. These are functions of the CNS. Efferent pathways carry pain impulses to the body via the spinal cord. Afferent pathways carry messages to the brain for interpretation.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a patient how to use a transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) unit and how it works. What is the most appropriate information for the nurse to relay?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The stimulation of the skin seeks to localize the acute pain and will last for several minutes after the unit is applied.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “This unit stimulates both the skin and the underlying tissues to decrease the intensity of the pain.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The mechanism for use of this unit is well known and can be read.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “During those days when using the TENS unit, no analgesic can be given.”

questions.rationale: The exact mechanism for the pain relief is unknown. The effects last for the time that the unit is applied to the patient and a short time thereafter.

questions.stem: What should greatly reduce postoperative pain for a patient about to undergo a hip replacement?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Practicing leg lifting exercises before surgery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Placing an analgesic patch directly over the incision

questions.rationale: Preoperative femoral nerve blocks will significantly reduce the pain for 24 hours postoperatively.

questions.stem: A patient continues to report pain after the administration of the prescribed analgesic. Why should the nurse change the nursing care plan?
questions.rationale: The sensation of pain is perceived as increased when the pain threshold is lowered. Insufficient data exists in this situation to assume addiction or the need for attention.

questions.stem: A nurse administers nalbuphine (Nubain), an opioid agonist–antagonist, to a 78-year-old patient. The family is worried about the patient and thinks that this drug is too strong and will cause harm. What should the nurse assure the family regarding this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Does not accumulate in the body

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blocks the side effects observed in opioid agonists

questions.rationale: A drug classified as an agonist–antagonist is able to relieve pain at the CNS level and is able to block some side effects of opioid agonists. The drug can be given intramuscularly or orally.

questions.stem: A patient admitted with the diagnosis of possible myocardial infarction complains of pain and tingling in the left arm and says, “How in the world could I be having a heart attack when it’s just my arm that is giving me trouble?” What type of pain should the nurse explain that the patient is experiencing?
questions.rationale: Pain is what the patient says it is and should be communicated as such. However, pain in specific areas may be referred pain, and left arm discomfort is typically referred from the heart. The nurse administers the analgesic as ordered and frequently checks to determine whether the pain is relieved.

questions.stem: Every time the right arm is raised, a patient reports to the nurse that pain is triggered in the right shoulder. How should the nurse document this description?
questions.rationale: The aggravating factor that is causing pain is important information to gather by the nurse and to communicate specifically in the chart, as well as to the registered nurse.

questions.stem: A patient who had a myocardial infarction 2 days earlier has been eating well, is ambulating with assistance, and is receiving antibiotics and morphine by intravenous (IV) drip. The patient complains of constipation this morning. What should the nurse assess as the probable cause of the constipation?
questions.rationale: Opioid administration frequently causes constipation. This patient is eating, taking IV fluids, and walking. Antibiotics rarely cause constipation.

questions.stem: A patient who is obviously in pain refuses the morphine that has been prescribed for pain control because of a fear of addiction. What should the nurse explain is the estimated percentage of patients taking prescribed pain protocols who become addicted?
questions.rationale: When used for severe pain management relief, opioids rarely result in addiction.

questions.stem: Morphine sulfate (30 mg) IM PRN was prescribed for pain for an 80-year-old patient with emphysema who weighs 100 lb. What is the most appropriate action for the licensed practical/vocational nurse (LPN/LVN)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transcribe the order and wait to see if the patient needs it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transcribe the order for an oral dose instead of IM dose.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Call the physician and clarify the order.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell the RN about the order.

questions.rationale: Morphine is usually given in a dose of 10 mg (one-sixth grain) IM. The usual oral dose is 0.5 gr (30 mg). The order should be called to the attention of the RN so that the intent can be clarified before transcribing the order for the older patient, who usually requires a smaller dose.

questions.stem: What assessment should a nurse make to evaluate the presence of pain in a patient who is cognitively impaired?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amount of time spent sleeping during the day

questions.rationale: Patients who are cognitively impaired may show pain by increased confusion, reduction in social contacts, grimacing, or squinting the eyes.

questions.stem: Which steps should the LPN/LVN follow when performing a pain assessment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess vital signs, status of pain, and aggravating factors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess location, quality, and intensity on an identified scale.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the intensity on an identified scale and record findings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess vital signs and location, and report to the RN.

questions.rationale: The assessment of pain requires the nurse assess location, quality, and intensity based on an identified scale. Vital signs are important in addition but are not part of the six steps.

questions.stem: Two patients are hospitalized with the same diagnosis, but one is 23 years old, with acute recent pain from an injury, and the other is 64 years old, with pain of long-standing duration of several years. What is the difference in the anticipated assessments?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute pain for young patients is more intense at the same level, but these patients experience few changes in vital signs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Young patients with acute pain exhibit fewer changes in vital signs but still report true levels of pain at levels 8 to 10.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Older adult patients with chronic pain exhibit increased changes in vital signs and report levels of pain lower than reality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Older adult patients with chronic pain usually report lower levels of pain much less severe than they really are.

questions.rationale: Older adult patients with chronic pain do not report pain as severe at the same level as younger patients do for several reasons. For example, older adult patients believe that pain comes with old age, or they do not want to bother the staff. Frequently, chronic long-standing pain does not change normal values of the vital signs.

questions.stem: How does the International Association for the Study of Pain define the sensation of pain?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whatever the person experiencing it says it is

choices.label: D
choices.text: Physical and psychogenic response to the need for drugs

questions.rationale: The International Association for the Study of Pain defines pain as an unpleasant sensory and emotional experience.

questions.stem: A nurse is notified when a patient, newly admitted with liver and gallbladder disease, complains of pain in the right middle back and asks for some pain medication. What is the best interpretation of this reported assessment by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is just complaining to see whether the staff will give out pain medications.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient has referred pain sensations. The nurse should follow orders for administering pain medication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient has an injury on the back from an unknown cause that needs immediate assessment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient is a chronic complainer with anxieties about his condition.

questions.rationale: Referred pain is a very real physical complaint, and the nurse should give the patient the pain medication as ordered.

questions.stem: What is the most common result of prolonged and unrelieved pain?
questions.rationale: Pain that is unrelieved lowers the pain threshold because the patient becomes anxious that the pain may not ever be relieved. The blood pressure increases, and the effects of endorphins and gate control have been exhausted.

questions.stem: A nurse is administering morphine IM, which was prescribed for a patient reporting severe pain. What should be the nurse’s primary assessment focus on to evaluate the patient’s response to this drug?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased bowel sounds in the gastrointestinal system

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sedative effects in the neurologic system

questions.rationale: Morphine initially produces sedation that allows the patient to sleep and rest from the pain. This result is considered a side effect.

questions.stem: A nurse explains to a patient the gate control theory of pain. Where does the perception of pain originate in the gate control theory?
questions.rationale: The perception of pain is made through small nerve fibers, which transmit impulses up to the brain, where they are interpreted as pain. Large nerve fiber receptors block the gate to decrease transmission to the interpretive brain area.

questions.stem: When a patient with sciatica seats himself in a chair, he gasps and complains of a burning and shooting pain in his hip. What type of pain does this represent?
questions.rationale: Neuropathic pain is characterized by burning and shooting sensations without a stimulus. This situation is not an example of acute pain because neuropathic pain does not follow a nociceptor path as do both visceral and referred pain.

questions.stem: What are the standards for pain management published by The Joint Commission (TJC)? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give adequate discharge instruction about pain relief.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recognize the right of a nurse to manage pain.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Teach patients about pain control methods.

questions.rationale: Performing organized pain assessment, recording results of analgesia, giving adequate discharge information about pain, relief and teaching patients about pain control methods are all standards for pain management published by the Joint Commission. The standards include recognizing the right of the patient to assess and manage pain, not the nurse.

questions.stem: What instruction should the nurse provide to a patient who has had radiofrequency lesioning for leg pain? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: C
choices.text: The foot may discolor and twitch at times.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Snug shoes should be worn at all times.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Caution should be taken to prevent injury to the leg.

questions.rationale: The relief may not be permanent because of the regeneration of nerve tissue. Extra caution should be taken to prevent injury because pain perception is altered. Snug shoes would cause injury. The goal of radiofrequency lesioning is to provide prolonged pain relief and patients may report tingling or buzzing sensations instead of pain.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that afferent pathways are activated by pain receptors called ______.
questions.rationale: Pain receptors that are called nociceptors activate the afferent pathways.

questions.stem: ______ and enkephalins are natural opioid-like substances that block pain perception.
questions.rationale: Endorphins and enkephalins are natural opioid substances that help block the perception of pain.

questions.stem: What are the four types of shock?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multiple organ, cardiogenic, renal, and anaphylactic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal, hypervolemic, obstructive shock, and neurogenic

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypovolemic, cardiogenic, obstructive shock, and vasogenic

questions.rationale: The four large categories of shock are hypovolemic (low-circulating volume), cardiogenic (low-cardiac output), obstructive (occluded vascular pathway), and vasogenic (massive vasodilation).

questions.stem: Although several life-supporting systems of the body are involved in the pathophysiologic characteristics of shock, shock itself results from failure of which system?
questions.rationale: When the heart fails as a pump, the lack of tissue perfusion follows and deprives all the body’s cells of oxygen and the removal of wastes.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient who is in shock. What should the nurse be aware that one common sign will be, regardless of the cause of the shock?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The skin is cool and dry with cyanotic nail beds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The skin is cool and moist with cyanotic nail beds.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nail beds are reddened, and the skin is moist and warm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nail beds are reddened, and the skin is dry and warm.

questions.rationale: Venous blood pools in the extremities of the fingers as a result of the lack of adequate perfusion of tissues, which makes the skin cool and moist from a lack of oxygen and waste exchanges.

questions.stem: What should a nurse assessing a patient in the organ-dysfunction stage of shock expect to find?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bounding pulse, decreased respirations, and decreased blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bounding pulse, shallow respirations, and significantly increased blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thready pulse and deep respirations with decreased blood pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thready pulse and irregular respirations with increased blood pressure

questions.rationale: When the cause of shock is not corrected, irreversible organ damage takes place. The pulse is weak; the respirations increase in an effort to decrease the carbon dioxide level; and, with less volume being pumped, the blood pressure falls.

questions.stem: A licensed practical/vocational nurse (LPN/LVN) is assisting in developing a nursing care plan for a patient in shock. Which patient problem should be included?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excess cardiac output, related to hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excess cardiac output, related to hypotension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inadequate cardiac output, related to hypovolemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inadequate cardiac output, related to hypertension

questions.rationale: Decreased amount of blood is ejected from the heart because of a decreased volume of fluid in the intravascular compartment.

questions.stem: How does the intraaortic balloon pump (IABP) assist a patient who is in cardiogenic shock to increase cardiac output?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adds hypertonic fluid to the circulating volume.

questions.rationale: The IABP inflates during diastole (relaxation) phase and deflates during the systole (constriction) phase, which improves cardiac output.

questions.stem: A nurse is explaining to a family member the pathophysiologic characteristic of distributive shock. What information should the nurse include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The intravascular compartment fills beyond capacity, allowing fluid to leak out, compressing vital organs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The circulating volume causes excessive constriction of the vessels, causing blood pooling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Widely fluctuating blood pressures stimulate vascular collapse, causing severe alterations in peripheral perfusion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Although the circulating volume is intact, excessive vascular dilation causes drastic drops in the blood pressure.

questions.rationale: In distributive shock,    blood pooling from dilated vessels drops the blood pressure without loss of circulating volume.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who has a cervical spine injury and assesses progressive hypotension. What does this signify?
questions.rationale: Gradually decreasing blood pressure in a person with a spinal injury is an indicator of neurogenic shock related to the parasympathetic stimulation, which causes generalized vasodilation.

questions.stem: While shopping in the mall, a nurse sees a lady suddenly fall to the floor. On immediate assessment, the nurse realizes she is not in cardiac arrest and has no need for cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR). What should be the immediate actions by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the pulse and respirations and call for a blood pressure cuff.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the pulse, respirations, skin color, and temperature.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Call for help and check the pulse, respiration, and mental status.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask someone to help place large blankets or coats under her legs and trunk.

questions.rationale: Shock treatment requires expert medical implementation. However, the nurse may provide first-line support until such help arrives. Circulatory collapse has to be monitored first; pulse, respiration, and mental status should be assessed to evaluate whether oxygen is reaching the brain.

questions.stem: A nurse is explaining the rationale behind the use of hypothermic devices to a patient’s family. When relaying information what explanation should the nurse provide when asked why this garment is used?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Warms the patient to create less metabolic demand.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Applies pressure during the systole phase and relax pressure during the diastole phase.

questions.rationale: Hypothermic devices cool the body by circulating ice water while in direct contact with the patient’s skin. These devices may improve neurologic recovery after cardiac arrest of cardiac origin.

questions.stem: In treating a person outside of a medical facility, a nurse knows that immediate circulatory support for the vital organs must begin as quickly as possible because, without oxygen, the brain cells will begin to die in how many minutes?
questions.rationale: Brain cells must have oxygen to live; they are very sensitive to lack of oxygen and begin to die in 4 minutes.

questions.stem: The stages of shock proceed in a definite sequence. What is the correct order?
questions.rationale: The sequence of the stages of shock are pre-shock, shock, and end-organ dysfunction. Understanding the sequence of the progression of shock allows the medical team to plan and implement the correct steps to reverse it. Hypovolemic, obstructive, and cardiogenic are types of shock.

questions.stem: Which position enhances cerebral blood flow to counteract the symptoms of neurogenic shock?
questions.rationale: The Trendelenburg position, with the patient’s head down, allows gravity to pull blood to the cerebrum. All other positions are ineffective for improving cerebral perfusion.

questions.stem: A nurse is administering heparin, subcutaneous twice daily, to a patient in cardiogenic shock. What is the expected action of this drug?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antidysrhythmic to restore normal cardiac contractibility

questions.rationale: Cardiogenic shock may produce clots because of blood stasis, and the heparin will delay clot formation.

questions.stem: One of the most important assessments that a nurse makes is to check urine output. Which value objectively validates minimal acceptable renal perfusion for the average-size person?
questions.rationale: When the kidneys produce at least 0.5 mL/kg/hr of urine, the indication is that the vital organs are also being perfused.

questions.stem: A patient is receiving norepinephrine as a first line treatment for shock in order to increase vascular resistance. Which type of shock is the patient being treated for?
questions.rationale: Norepinephrine is used as a first line treatment for septic shock in order to increase vascular resistance.

questions.stem: A patient’s family voices concern regarding the purpose of some of the interventions for systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS). What explanation by the nurse is most appropriate when explaining the rationale of treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Applying a MAST garment is mandatory to promote and conserve body heat.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Inserting an IABP is required to decrease fluid leaking into the extravascular space.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Maintaining strict isolation is vital to prevent an overlying bacterial infection.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Aggressive treatment is necessary to support the multiple failing organs.”

questions.rationale: SIRS is the final and possibly fatal stage of shock. The body’s defenses are supported aggressively and rapidly. MAST and IABP are measures used to increase circulating volume. Isolation is not indicated.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that pericardial tamponade and tension pneumothorax can place the patient at risk for ______ shock.
questions.rationale: Obstructive shock can result from pericardial tamponade or tension pneumothorax.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that when shock forces the body into anaerobic metabolism, organ damage is caused by a product of that metabolism, which is ______.
questions.rationale: Lactic acid, a by-product of anaerobic metabolism, can cause organ damage in the patient who is in shock.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that the minimal acceptable hourly urine output for a patient in shock who weighs 220 lb is ______ mL. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: 220 lb ÷ 2.2 lb = 100 kg; 0.5 mL/kg/hr x 100 kg = 50 mL.

questions.stem: A nurse is aware that immobility and insertion of urinary catheters, although therapeutic, also places the patient at risk for ______.
questions.rationale: The insertion of a Foley catheter and long-term immobility can cause infections.

questions.stem: How is the term old age or aged best defined?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Person older than 65 years of age

questions.rationale: Aged or old age is defined as advanced in years.

questions.stem: How is aging recognized by gerontologists as a developmental process?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begins at the time of birth

questions.rationale: Geriatrics is the science of old age and the application of knowledge related to the biologic, biomedical, behavioral, and social aspects of aging.

questions.stem: What understanding is a prerequisite for a nurse working with the geriatric patient?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Most geriatric patients will develop dementia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Geriatric patients need to be closely supervised.

questions.rationale: Knowledge, understanding, and caring are prerequisites for working effectively with older adults. Although specialized formal education programs at the graduate level are available for gerontological nurses, many nurses gain specials skills through on-the-job experiences.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old resident of a long-term care facility insists on wearing high heels and miniskirts to the dining room for meals and will not leave her room without first applying glamorous makeup. What should the gerontological nurse assess as the reason for this behavior?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trying to cope with the changes of aging

questions.rationale: Some older people confront aging, but others deny it by acting in a younger manner.

questions.stem: What does Butler, a well-known gerontologist, relay regarding ageism?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is based on the biologic theory of aging.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is based on natural and purposeful occurrences.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It continues to change as the population ages.

questions.rationale: Ageism is the stereotyping of and discrimination against people because of their age.

questions.stem: What are the effects of aging on the nervous system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accelerated loss of neurons in the brain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gradually declining loss of intellectual capability

questions.rationale: Age-related effects on body systems are integral parts of the basis of nursing care for older adults. The aging nervous system is characterized by decreased conduction speed of neurons.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for older adult patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI). What are these patients more likely to develop?
questions.rationale: Approximately 40% of people with MCI develop Alzheimer dementia within 3 years.

questions.stem: What is the most appropriate nursing action when planning activities to improve short-term memory for an older adult patient experiencing memory deficits?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provide a varied and stimulating daily schedule.

questions.rationale: Using mnemonics and memory rehearsal may improve memory performance in some older individuals.

questions.stem: What is the best example of normal memory change or lapse of memory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relying on another person to remember names or important events

choices.label: B
choices.text: Occasional forgetfulness or inability to recall names or facts

questions.rationale: Memory lapses such as forgetting a name or misplacing an item are common, normal memory changes.

questions.stem: Which facts are generally accepted for most older adults?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Creativity and judgment are severely impaired.

questions.rationale: Functional ability may not be significantly affected because reserve cells are able to compensate.

questions.stem: What factor increases the risk of respiratory infection for older adults?
questions.rationale: The ability to perform strenuous work decreases with age. The ciliary action responsible for movement of secretions from the lung is compromised because of epithelial atrophy.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for an older person whose renal changes make it impossible to concentrate or dilute urine. For what is this patient at the greatest risk?
questions.rationale: The kidney’s ability to concentrate urine is a major defense against dehydration.

questions.stem: Which assessment is the greatest priority to report when considering the gastrointestinal (GI) changes that take place in the geriatric patient?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weight loss of 2 lb since admission 2 months ago

questions.rationale: GI changes include bloating, diarrhea, pernicious anemia, and constipation.

questions.stem: Which assessment made by the nurse is a major sign of renal changes related to age?
questions.rationale: Urgency incontinence is related to several age-related changes in the urinary musculature. Renal calculi and hematuria are pathologic symptoms and are not age related. Nocturia is not specifically related to aging.

questions.stem: What should be the most significant assessment when gathering data concerning the musculoskeletal system?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Degree of motion of all joints

questions.rationale: Determine mobility by assessing the range of motion in all joints; in addition, look for signs of inflammation and pain associated with mobility.

questions.stem: What is the most appropriate nursing intervention for a patient with presbycusis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Speak clearly and distinctly while facing the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Announce your presence when entering the patient’s room.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place needed articles within easy reach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Orient the patient to time and place as needed.

questions.rationale: Presbycusis is hearing loss. Get the patient’s attention so that the patient can concentrate on what you are saying or read lips.

questions.stem: What should the nurse suspect a patient is developing when he is observed holding his Bible 6 inches from his face and turns his head to read out of the corner of his eyes?
questions.rationale: The leading cause of new blindness in old age is macular degeneration, which results in the loss of central vision.

questions.stem: What is true regarding the chemosensory changes observed in older adults?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are directly related to the aging process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are most often caused by disease.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They begin in the fifth decade of life.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They affect more women than men.

questions.rationale: Major changes in the ability to taste are often caused by disease or a side effect of certain drugs.

questions.stem: A nurse explains to family members that the final developmental stage is ego integrity. What should occur in the older adult, according to Erikson, if this stage is not mastered?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Needs to repeat a previous stage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inability to advance past the present stage

questions.rationale: The final developmental task is ego versus despair. This negative resolution is often seen as depression and social withdrawal.

questions.stem: What is the best nursing action when assisting an older patient to relieve the discomfort of pruritus related to dry skin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encourage the patient to talk to the primary care physician about the problem.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encourage the patient to take a tepid bath and use moisturizers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach the patient that pruritus is an expected consequence of aging.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Establishing a medication regimen to control the discomfort.

questions.rationale: Because pruritus is caused by loss of oils in the skin, the patient should be encouraged to take tepid baths; use moisturizers; and avoid overuse of antiperspirants, soaps, perfumes, and long hot baths.

questions.stem: A newly admitted 72-year-old resident of a long-term care facility naps frequently during the day, stating that he is tired. What is the best action by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain an order from the primary caregiver for a sedative.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient if he is sleeping well at night.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Plan activities to keep the patient awake during the day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell the patient that he cannot take any more naps.

questions.rationale: Determining if or the reason why the patient is not sleeping at night will help the nurse implement the appropriate nursing actions. Depression may be interfering with adapting to the long-term facility.

questions.stem: What is the best reason that drug toxicity can occur as a result of an age-related change in the liver?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rapid blood flow through the liver

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fluid accumulation in the portal vein

questions.rationale: Decreased liver enzyme activity does not prepare the drug for excretion. The liver size is decreased in older persons; blood flow through the liver is also decreased.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old recently admitted to a long-term care facility refuses to join in activities or go to the dining room for meals. How should the nurse interpret this behavior?
questions.rationale: Some older people respond to loss by losing their sense of personal identity and fulfillment. They have a deterioration in self-esteem and become depressed.

questions.stem: What are included in age-related cardiovascular changes? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Decreased resting cardiac output, increased blood pressure, and increased risk for heart attack are all age-related cardiovascular changes. Coronary blood flow in older adults may be reduced by as much as 35%. The heart becomes increasingly rigid with age.

questions.stem: What recommendation should a nurse make to the family of a patient diagnosed with ataxia when preparing discharge to home?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remove all scatter rugs from the home.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arrange for someone to stay with the patient 24 hours a day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Purchase oversized shoes so that they are easy to get on.

questions.rationale: Scatter rugs can slip and cause a patient to fall.

questions.stem: What should be the first intervention when a nurse finds that a patient has fallen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient to stand up.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the fall according to agency policy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remove or correct the cause of the fall.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the circumstances of the fall and any injuries sustained.

questions.rationale: The first implementation should be to assess what happened, determine whether any injuries have occurred, and then document and correct the cause.

questions.stem: What should discharge planning for a patient who lives alone and is at high risk for falling include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cannot go home unless someone is with him all the time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Must go to a long-term care facility.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Can wear devices around the neck that can signal for help.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Needs to be aware of the dangers of living alone.

questions.rationale: A person who is at risk for falling would be wise to have a call system to obtain help from others. Devices worn around the neck that can send signals to a control center are effective and provide a feeling of well-being for the individual who has the potential for falling.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that older adults account for a large percentage of the total deaths resulting from falls. What is this percentage?
questions.rationale: Older adults constitute only 12% to 13% of the total U.S. population, but they account for 72% of the total deaths resulting from falls.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for an older adult patient who has undergone a total hip replacement. What is the best action to reduce the risk of further injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Leave all the lights on in the room at night.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leave the side rails down at all times to enable the patient to get to the bathroom quickly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep the call bell and other frequently used items in easy reach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep the bed in the high position to discourage the patient from getting out of bed without assistance.

questions.rationale: Keeping the call bell and other frequently used items within easy reach will prevent the patient from having to reach, which increases the risk for falling.

questions.stem: A nurse is talking to the family of a patient who has fallen several times. What is the most important intervention for preventing falls that the nurse to relay to this family?
questions.rationale: The most important implementation for falls is prevention. The best prevention is education that is aimed toward minimizing intrinsic and extrinsic factors.

questions.stem: How often should a nurse remove and release restraints when caring for a patient who requires wrist restraints?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Once every 8 hours for at least 30 minutes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Once every 4 hours for at least 15 minutes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Once every 2 hours for at least 10 minutes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Once every 1 hour for at least 5 minutes

questions.rationale: Physical restraints must be removed and released every 2 hours for 10 minutes. In addition, they should be frequently checked to ensure that the restraint is properly used and is providing adequate protection and comfort without impeding circulation or breathing.

questions.stem: An older adult patient in a long-term care facility is at risk for injury because of confusion. The patient’s gait is stable. What is the best method of restraint to prevent injury to the patient?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wrist or ankle restraint or both

questions.rationale: If a physical restraint is used, the least restrictive device is best. This patient has a stable gait, so the alarm bracelet allows the patient to move about freely while preventing him from leaving the premises.

questions.stem: A nurse is admitting a new patient to the nursing unit. When conducting the admission procedure, what is important for the nurse to ask in order to assess the patient’s risk for falling?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “How many times have you fallen before?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “How many hours do you sleep at night?”

questions.rationale: People who are at the greatest risk for falls and injury are those who have fallen before.

questions.stem: A patient has asked a nurse to assist him to ambulate to the bathroom. The nurse is aware that the patient is currently taking an antidepressant medication. What action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Never leave the patient alone in his room.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient if he could use the bedside commode instead of going to the bathroom.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Make suicidal precautions part of the care plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient to sit on the side of the bed for a minute or two before standing and then stand slowly.

questions.rationale: Psychotropic drugs, such as antidepressants, commonly cause orthostatic hypotension. The patient should sit on the side of the bed and then stand slowly to prevent falling.

questions.stem: In reviewing a patient’s medication administration record, a nurse is aware that some medications are considered to be chemical restraints. Which medication is considered a chemical restraint?
questions.rationale: Alprazolam (Xanax) is a psychotropic drug used as a chemical restraint.

questions.stem: A nurse in a long-term care facility determines the need to place a vest restraint on a patient. The patient does not want the vest restraint applied. What nursing action should be implemented?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Call the physician and obtain an order for the restraint.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Medicate the patient with a sedative and then apply the restraint.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compromise with the patient and use wrist restraints.

questions.rationale: A physician’s order is required for restraint use, and the order must specify the duration and circumstances under which the restraint may be used.

questions.stem: What is the most appropriate nursing intervention after a patient has fallen?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Have the patient begin ambulating as soon as possible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer haloperidol (Haldol) as prescribed or as needed.

questions.rationale: The patient should begin ambulating as soon after a fall as possible to prevent the hazards of bed rest and to restore confidence. Applying restraints after a fall is tempting, but avoiding their use, if possible, is best.

questions.stem: Which unexpected circumstance best defines a fall?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Falls to the ground, floor, or lower level

choices.label: C
choices.text: Loses balance, resulting from a lack of equilibrium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Injures self, resulting from a side effect of a medication

questions.rationale: Definitions of falls vary, but a fall is an unintentional event that is unrelated to medication or loss of consciousness and that results in injury.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing the potential risk factors a patient may have for falling. Which two major factors cause falls?
questions.rationale: Intrinsic factors are related to the functioning of the individual (e.g., aging process, physical illness). Extrinsic factors are related to the environment.

questions.stem: The Omnibus Reconciliation Act (OBRA) was enacted to protect patients from unnecessary restraint in long-term care facilities. According to OBRA regulations, what is a permissible reason to restrain a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Staffing level is inadequate, and nurses are unable to check on the patient at regular intervals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is verbally abusive to the nursing staff.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient is at an extremely high risk for a fall that is life threatening.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Medical procedures cannot be performed because the patient is not being cooperative.

questions.rationale: The only people who are considered restrainable are those who (1) are at high risk for a fall that is life threatening; (2) need postural support for safety, comfort, or both; (3) may be a serious hazard to themselves, objects, or others; and (4) have life-threatening medical symptoms and for whom a restraint may be temporarily used to provide necessary treatment.

questions.stem: Which patient population is at greatest risk for injury from falls?
questions.rationale: Older adults are at particular risk for accidents because of changes brought about by aging, a greater potential for injury, and poorer clinical outcomes.

questions.stem: Where should a patient with a visual impairment of the left eye place items that are frequently used to prevent the risk of injury?
questions.rationale: The unaffected side and within reach. This placement reduces the risk of falling.

questions.stem: An older adult patient with osteoporosis is at risk for falls. What should a nurse advise the patient to do in order to maintain safety in the home?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take the rubber mat out of the shower.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Install a grab rail in the bath and shower and by the toilet.

questions.rationale: The patient who is at risk for falls must have rails to hold to prevent falling. A rubber mat in the shower and rubber-soled shoes are important to prevent slipping. Moderate exercise is beneficial.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses a resident in a long-term care facility with the “get up and go” technique. What should this involve observing the resident do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Walk carefully through a cluttered area without incident.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rise from the bed, and go to the bathroom.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sit and rise from an armless chair.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ambulate in a straight line for 1 foot.

questions.rationale: The “get up and go” technique of evaluation requires that the resident be able to sit and rise from an armless chair.

questions.stem: A nurse is discussing the risk of falling with the family of a 75-year-old patient. The family asks, “Why are you so worried about her falling? She falls all the time and doesn’t get hurt much.” To which fact should the nurse’s response relate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Falls are the most frequent cause of accidental injury and death among older adults.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Worrying is probably unnecessary because she hasn’t been hurt in the past.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Falls usually occur in institutional settings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Falls by older adults are not preventable.

questions.rationale: The risk of injury from falls is highest in people older than 65 years, and falls are the most frequent cause of accidental injury and death among older adults. Older adults account for 72% of total deaths resulting from falls.

questions.stem: A nurse visiting a patient in the patient’s home assesses the environment for extrinsic risk factors for falling. Which factors should the nurse have the patient or family correct?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The kitchen floor is clean, shiny, and slick.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The telephone is placed on the bedside table.

questions.rationale: Slick floors can cause the patient to slip and fall. The other choices are implementations that will help reduce the risk of falls.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a patient methods for getting up after a fall. The nurse instructs the patient to pull up to a sitting position on the floor, shuffle the buttocks to a nearby piece of furniture, and pull up on the knees in front of the furniture. What should the nurse instruct the patient to do next?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place hands on the floor for leverage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pivot so that the furniture is behind the body.

questions.rationale: The last step of the shuffle method is to stand up.

questions.stem: A nurse is aware that many residents in a long-term care facility refuse to wear the hip protector garment. What reason do residents state makes them resistive to wear this protective garment?
questions.rationale: Residents resist wearing the hip protector garment because it is uncomfortable.

questions.stem: What should the home health nurse recommend to a patient if a fall occurs at home?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assume a crawling position and push up from the floor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pull self up using sturdy furniture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Roll to a doorway and pull up using the door knob.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Place the right foot flat on floor and push up on the right knee.

questions.rationale: All techniques for rising after a fall rely on pulling up on sturdy furniture.

questions.stem: A nurse is trying to keep a confused resident from removing a feeding tube by following the “rule of least restriction.” What should replace the wrist restraint?
questions.rationale: Mittens are the lesser of restraints that will hinder the patient from removing the feeding tube.

questions.stem: In what ways might people who have a fear of falling alter their lifestyle? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have increased need for residency in a long-term care facility.

questions.rationale: Restricting physical and social activities, becoming more dependent, and having an increased need for residency in a long-term-care facility all have to do with an altered lifestyle. The development of depression is not a lifestyle alteration but may be a result of the lifestyle change.

questions.stem: A home health nurse cautions the family of a frail 82-year-old woman about the intrinsic factors that may be a potential cause of injury. Which intrinsic factors should be included? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Diminished vision and generalized weakness are the only options related to the individual that cannot be changed (intrinsic). The other options are related to the environment and can be changed (extrinsic).

questions.stem: A nurse is aware that of all the reported falls in the United States, only 1% to 5% result in a ______.
questions.rationale: According to reported falls, only 1% to 5% result in a fracture.

questions.stem: A nurse helps the physical therapist teach residents in a long-term care facility how to diminish the risk of injury from a fall by teaching them rotation maneuvers to help them avoid falling _______.
questions.rationale: Rotation maneuvers can be taught to patients to help them avoid falling sideways.

questions.stem: A nurse suggests that a resident who is at risk for falling come to the _______ class to improve balance.
questions.rationale: The slow rhythmic movements of Tai Chi are helpful in improving balance.

questions.stem: What negative effects does immobilization have on the musculoskeletal system?
questions.rationale: Immobilization has negative effects on the musculoskeletal system such as demineralization of bone, a decrease in aerobic capacity, a decrease in muscle oxidation, and shortening of muscle fibers.

questions.stem: What should the nurse be aware is the best prevention of immobility-related disorders?
questions.rationale: Exercise will help reduce the patient’s risk of immobility-related disorders.

questions.stem: A nurse’s assessment reveals an area of erythema on an immobilized patient’s sacrum. What is the initial nursing action?
questions.rationale: The first intervention is to reposition the patient with follow-up to ensure that the patient is repositioned often.

questions.stem: A nurse is providing discharge instructions to the family of an older adult patient who is unable to get out of bed. What should the nurse instruct the family regarding the most effective way to prevent urinary incontinence associated with immobility?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Restrict fluid intake to 500 mL per 24 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Restrict fluids after dinner and throughout the night.

questions.rationale: Patients should have scheduled toileting times with adjustments in the schedule based on the patient’s voiding patterns. Studies have been inconclusive regarding the effectiveness of limiting fluids.

questions.stem: The care plan of an older adult patient states that the patient should be monitored while in the bathroom because of a history of vasovagal reflex. What should the nurse assess with this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Extremely elevated blood pressure after ambulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nausea and vomiting after a meal

questions.rationale: Constipated individuals may strain to defecate, causing an increase in intra-abdominal pressure. This is called the Valsalva maneuver or vasovagal reflex, and it can lead to cardiovascular alterations.

questions.stem: What is the most effective intervention to prevent constipation in a patient who recently sustained a fractured femur and is currently in traction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Get the patient up and to the bathroom at least twice each day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer enemas each day until the patient has a bowel movement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer pain medication to prevent pain during defecation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage a high-fiber diet and increased amounts of fluids.

questions.rationale: Inactivity, decreased fluid intake, and a lack of adequate fiber in the diet can combine to cause constipation. Activity is not an option for this patient, but encouraging a high-fiber diet and increased fluids can help prevent or relieve constipation.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a patient who has been prescribed bed rest for 1 week notices a reddened area on the patient’s left hip. The skin is intact, but when the nurse presses on the area, the redness does not fade. How should this area of pressure be classified?
questions.rationale: The major characteristic of a stage I pressure ulcer is erythema (redness) that does not blanch when pressed.

questions.stem: What action should the nurse implement when positioning an immobile patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure that the patient’s knees and hips are flexed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Visualize how a person looks while standing and try to have the patient achieve that position while lying down.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reposition the patient no more often than every 4 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Always position the patient on his or her back with the head raised to prevent aspiration.

questions.rationale: Positioning should be done to maintain joints in their functional positions so they are not abnormally flexed or extended.

questions.stem: What intervention is most appropriate to prevent respiratory complications resulting from immobility?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer pain medications as frequently as possible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach the patient the technique of pursed lip breathing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reposition the patient, and encourage him or her to cough and deep breathe at least every 2 hours.

questions.rationale: When a person remains immobile or does not take deep breaths, thick secretions can accumulate and pool in the lower respiratory structures.

questions.stem: A nurse transcribes a discharge order for the patient with left-sided weakness after having a stroke indicating to teach the patient to perform range-of-motion exercises on affected extremities. The patient asks why she needs to do range-of-motion exercises. What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You will regain full use of your arm and leg if you will do the exercises correctly.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “They prevent the muscles and tendons from shortening and becoming unmoveable.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It will give you something to do because you can’t work anymore.”

questions.rationale: Muscular activity maintains range of motion by allowing the joint to remain flexible and functional. When little or no movement of a joint occurs, the muscles shorten and lose their elasticity.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses a patient’s risk for developing a pressure ulcer using the Norton scale. The patient’s score is 18. What nursing action should be implemented?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement a pressure ulcer prevention program.
