questions.rationale: If the total score on the Norton scale is greater than 14, then little risk exists for the development of pressure ulcers. If the total score is less than 14, then significant risk exists.

questions.stem: A patient in traction with a fractured hip is diagnosed with a stage I pressure ulcer. She asks the nurse how a pressure ulcer could occur after only 2 days of immobility. On what knowledge should the nurse base a response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Erythema can occur in 1 to 2 hours even in a person with healthy skin and adequate circulation.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It takes several days for a pressure ulcer to form.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The pressure ulcer probably occurred when you fell.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The cause of pressure ulcers isn’t really known.”

questions.rationale: Because of impaired blood flow, capillaries in the area of pressure can become congested, and erythema can occur in 1 to 2 hours.

questions.stem: A patient is complaining to the nurse that he feels the need to have a bowel movement but has not been able to defecate. He has had cramping and even a small amount of brown watery stool. What should the nurse recognize these symptoms as?
questions.rationale: Symptoms of a fecal impaction include painful defecation, a feeling of fullness in the rectum, abdominal distention, and sometimes cramps and a watery stool.

questions.stem: During the shift report, a nurse is told that a patient she will be caring for has a stage II pressure ulcer. What should the nurse expect to visualize during the dressing change?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ulcer that appears black with possible signs of infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shallow ulcer that appears blistered, cracked, or abraded

choices.label: C
choices.text: Craterlike sore with a distinct outer margin formed as the epidermis thickens and rolls over the edge toward the ulcer base

choices.label: D
choices.text: Redness of skin with no ulceration

questions.rationale: In a stage II pressure ulcer, some skin loss in the epidermis and dermis has occurred.

questions.stem: When preparing a plan care for an older adult patient, a nurse should consider the common problems associated with immobility. What should these problems be classified as?
questions.rationale: Immobility can have a profound impact on both the mind and the body. Psychosocial problems include depression, fear, anxiety, social withdrawal, and apathy. Physically, immobility can have an adverse effect on every body system.

questions.stem: How does the National Pressure Ulcer Advisory Panel prefer to refer to skin breakdown?
questions.rationale: Decubitus means lying down; therefore, decubitus ulcers and bed sores are associated with lying in a bed. Skin breakdown can also develop from sitting.

questions.stem: A patient complains that his “bottom” is sore. The nurse assesses the area and finds an open area on the sacrum that appears blistered. What action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the cause of the burn.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clean with alcohol, apply moisturizer, and cover with a set dressing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Massage the area to promote circulation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clean with mild soap, dry, and apply a light dressing.

questions.rationale: If pressure ulcers develop despite all preventive measures, proper and early treatment improves the chance for reversal. A stage II ulcer should be cleaned with mild soap and water or with sterile normal saline, patted dry, and covered with a dressing that allows airflow.

questions.stem: A nursing assistant is bathing a patient who has a stage I pressure ulcer on the right shoulder. What action by the health care team could cause that the tissue to become more damaged?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Positioning the patient on the left side

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cleaning the area with mild soap and water

choices.label: D
choices.text: Positioning the patient in a prone position

questions.rationale: Any type of massage around or on a reddened area of skin can damage fragile capillaries.

questions.stem: A nurse is planning the care of a patient who is immobile. Why should the nurse consider this patient to be at risk for urinary tract infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Urine will pool in the bladder when the patient remains in a supine position.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is likely to have urinary incontinence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient’s appetite may be decreased.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient may not be able to move quickly enough to get to the bathroom.

questions.rationale: If the body remains in a supine position for even a few days, the flow becomes sluggish, and the urine pools in the bladder, which will increase the risk of a urinary tract infection.

questions.stem: What should the nurse instruct a patient in a wheelchair to do to decrease risk for pressure ulcers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use a ring pillow on the seat of the chair.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lift the weight of the body using the arms of the wheelchair every 15 minutes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Scoot forward and back in the seat to stimulate circulation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wear underwear that holds moisture close to skin.

questions.rationale: Using the arms of the wheelchair to lift the weight off the buttocks and coccyx is beneficial to reduce the risk of pressure ulcers in patients using wheelchairs.

questions.stem: A nurse is instructing a patient on performing isometric exercises. What instruction should the nurse include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contract the muscle for several seconds, then relax the muscle for a few seconds, and contract it again.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform full range-of-motion exercises of each joint.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have a family member perform full range-of-motion exercises on each of the patient’s joints.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stand in front of a wall and push with the arms without bending the elbow.

questions.rationale: Isometric exercises maintain muscle tone without moving the joint. This type of exercise is helpful in maintaining muscle strength after a fracture.

questions.stem: A nurse is talking with a patient who recently became paraplegic as a result of a cervical spinal cord injury. When some home equipment is discussed, the patient becomes angry and says, “I don’t need to worry about any kind of home equipment.” What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I know you will be walking soon, but you may need some equipment until then.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “There is very little chance that you will ever walk.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Tell me what it is about this equipment that bothers you.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Let me call the physician to come explain your injuries to you.”

questions.rationale: The nurse should use therapeutic communication techniques to explore the patient’s feelings.

questions.stem: During a skin integrity assessment, a nurse notices an area on the right heel that is black and draining purulent, foul-smelling exudate. How should the nurse document this as a pressure ulcer?
questions.rationale: In a stage IV pressure ulcer, full-thickness skin loss has occurred with extensive destruction of the deeper underlying muscle and, possibly, the bone tissue.

questions.stem: What is the classification of incontinence in older adults related to the inability to get to the bathroom in time?
questions.rationale: Functional incontinence occurs when the older adult patient cannot move quickly enough to reach the toilet in time.

questions.stem: Which are characteristics of a stage I pressure ulcer? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The area is regular and well defined.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Swelling has occurred at the site.

questions.rationale: A stage I ulcer has irregular and poorly defined margins, with swelling and hardening at the site of the nonblanching erythema. At this stage, the ulcer is reversible.

questions.stem: What should a nurse document when assessing a new pressure ulcer? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Location of the wound and its description

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amount and characteristics of the drainage

questions.rationale: Documentation should include the precise location, color, size, shape, and drainage, as well as treatment applications.

questions.stem: What therapeutic reasons exist that explain why a patient might become immobile? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduction of the workload of the heart

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reversal of the effects of gravity

questions.rationale: A reduction of the heart’s workload, a reversal of the effects of gravity (as in the treatment of a hernia or prolapse), and the healing of a fracture are all therapeutic reasons for immobilization. The fear of falling and bereavement are not therapeutic reasons.

questions.stem: A home health nurse instructs a family about boosting the patient in bed so that a(n) ______ type of skin injury will not occur.
questions.rationale: Shearing force injuries occur when a patient is dragged up in bed, causing the skin to be abraded against the bed linens.

questions.stem: A nurse evaluates the effectiveness of the treatment for a stage III pressure ulcer as satisfactory when the bed of the ulcer is pink, indicating the presence of ______, which is an indicator of tissue perfusion.
questions.rationale: The appearance of healthy pink granulation tissue in the bed of a pressure ulcer is a positive sign for improved perfusion and the beginning of closure.

questions.stem: A nurse takes into consideration that such emotions as worry, anxiety, and depression can contribute to the common nutritional problem of ______.
questions.rationale: Anorexia can be caused by emotional factors such as worry, anxiety, and depression.

questions.stem: When bacteria are localized at the site of a stage III pressure ulcer, it is said to be ______.
questions.rationale: Colonized bacteria are those who are in one location, such as an ulcer, and not systemic.

questions.stem: The negative impact of immobilization on a patient depends on the duration, degree, and type of ______.
questions.rationale: Duration, degree, and type of mobility limitation have the greatest impact. The other choices may affect the impact of immobilization when the mobility limitation becomes an issue.

questions.stem: The family of a patient with Alzheimer disease asks the nurse, “When will my mother quit being so confused?” On what information regarding dementia should the nurse base a response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a short-term confusional state that is typically reversible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a state of confusion caused primarily by medications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a state of confusion that usually begins abruptly and lasts a short period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a syndrome that is chronic and irreversible.

questions.rationale: Alzheimer disease is a type of dementia that is chronic and irreversible. Delirium is a short-term confusional state that has a sudden onset and is typically reversible.

questions.stem: A nurse is admitting a patient who has been diagnosed as having confusion. What is the most important observation that the nurse should make regarding this patient?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Behavior, orientation, memory, and sleeping habits

questions.rationale: The first step in assessing a confusional state is to observe the patient’s behavior, orientation, memory, and sleeping habits.

questions.stem: While a nurse is dressing a patient who has dementia as a result of Huntington disease, the patient states, “I don’t want to wear clothes today” and begins to resist help putting on her clothes. What is the nurse’s most appropriate action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the patient that she must wear clothes or she cannot see her family later.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Get another nurse to help her force the patient to get dressed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Talk to the patient about her family coming this afternoon and continue to assist the patient gently with dressing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Let the patient go without clothes but make her stay in her room.

questions.rationale: When patients with dementia resist activities such as bathing or dressing, avoiding confrontations and diverting their attention elsewhere are best.

questions.stem: What are the adaptations to interventions that the Cognitive Developmental Approach (CDA) to caring for patients with dementia designed to achieve?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offer a wide variety of choices.

questions.rationale: The CDA adapts implementations based on the patient’s cognitive abilities as they are, modifies the environment, and offers limited choices.

questions.stem: A nurse is gathering information from the family of a patient who is experiencing confusion. What important question should the nurse ask the family?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Are you sure she is confused? Maybe she just didn’t hear what you were saying.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “When did you first think she might be confused? Tell me exactly what happened.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Did something bad happen to her during her childhood?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “How can you say she is confused? She knows who she is.”

questions.rationale: Family members may be able to provide helpful information when the patient cannot. The nurse should ask when the symptoms of confusion started and whether the confusion is constant or intermittent.

questions.stem: The family of a patient with dementia expresses concern to the nurse about the patient wandering at night. They are afraid that the patient might get up while they are sleeping and go outside. What is the best advice for the nurse to provide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply a vest restraint at night.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Put new locks on the outside doors in new places.

questions.rationale: Take advantage of the fact that patients with dementia are usually unable to learn new things. They will probably not be able to figure out how to work a new lock.

questions.stem: A nurse is planning for the nutritional needs of a patient with Alzheimer disease. What is the best plan to have the dietary department provide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pureed diet to be fed with a syringe

choices.label: B
choices.text: Foods that the patient can cut up to keep busy and not lose interest in eating

choices.label: C
choices.text: Finger foods several times a day

questions.rationale: Small, frequent meals are less confusing to patients. Finger foods high in protein and carbohydrates allow patients to feed themselves more easily.

questions.stem: What initial nursing action should be implemented when assisting a patient with dementia to dress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hand the patient her clothes and ask her to put them on.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hand the patient each article of clothing separately and ask her to put it on.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assist her with each article, giving specific instructions such as, “Put your arm in this hole.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: Put the patient’s clothes on without assistance from the patient.

questions.rationale: The goal should be to maintain the highest level of functioning possible, but tasks must be broken down into individual steps to be performed one at a time.

questions.stem: Reality orientation is helpful for some patients with confusion. What patient diagnosis is most appropriate for the nurse to implement this technique?
questions.rationale: Acute confusional state is another name for delirium. The other choices are other names for dementia. Reality orientation may be helpful for patients with delirium but tends to agitate patients with dementia.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient for the possibility of confusion. What two major types of confusion should the nurse be aware of to appropriately assess this patient?
questions.rationale: The two major types of confusion are acute confusional states, or delirium, and chronic confusion dementia.

questions.stem: A patient with delirium repeatedly cries out for her husband. What is the most appropriate initial nursing intervention?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply restraints so that the patient will not harm herself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calmly tell the patient that she is in the hospital and that her husband is not there.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Call the husband and tell him that he needs to come and stay with his wife.

questions.rationale: Anyone dealing with a delirious patient should be calm, warm, and reassuring. Frequent orientation to the surroundings and situation is important as well.

questions.stem: When admitting a patient who has recently become confused, the nurse asks the family for a list of all the medications that the patient is currently taking. Which medication identified by the family should the nurse be aware could be causing confusion?
questions.rationale: Drugs that most commonly cause confusion include anticholinergics, digoxin, histamine-2 receptor blockers, benzodiazepines, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and many antiarrhythmic and antihypertensive medications.

questions.stem: A patient has been admitted with a diagnosis of confusion. The physician’s admission note states that he wants to assess for delirium versus dementia. What should the nurse be aware that the main differences include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whereas delirium usually lasts several years, dementia lasts only a few days.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whereas delirium usually has sudden onset and is reversible, dementia is chronic and irreversible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whereas dementia is usually caused by medications, delirium is not.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whereas dementia is easily treated with reality orientation, delirium is not.

questions.rationale: Delirium is a short-term, confusional state that has a sudden onset and is typically reversible. Dementia is a syndrome that is often chronic and irreversible.

questions.stem: A nurse has found a patient with delirium in other patients’ rooms several times. What is the best action by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Firmly tell the patient that he must stay out of other patients’ rooms and tell him to return to his room.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take him back to his room and put him in bed with the side rails up.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take him to the nurses’ station and let him visit for a while.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer a dose of lorazepam (Ativan) as ordered.

questions.rationale: Avoid using physical restraints, which tend to increase anxiety and agitation. Sitting at the nurses’ station will allow the nurses to monitor his activity and frequently orient him to his surroundings.

questions.stem: A nurse in a long-term care facility is taking patients to the dining room for lunch. She asks the patient who has been diagnosed with delirium if she is ready to go eat lunch. The patient does not respond. What should be the nurse’s next action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take the patient by the arm and lead her to the dining room.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assist the patient to bed and bring her lunch to her.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the patient that she can go to the dining room whenever she gets hungry.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient again if she is ready to go eat lunch.

questions.rationale: A patient with delirium may have difficulty focusing or paying attention, and questions must often be repeated several times.

questions.stem: A patient asks a nurse what causes dementia. What two most prevalent types of dementia should the nurse consider before responding?
questions.rationale: Alzheimer disease and vascular dementia are the two most prevalent types of dementia.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient for delirium versus dementia. What should the nurse expect the patient with dementia to display?
questions.rationale: The patient with dementia will have a flat or indifferent affect. The other three choices would be presented by a patient with delirium.

questions.stem: A nurse is taking a patient who has Alzheimer disease to the bathing room for a tub bath. The patient states, “Please don’t make me take a bath today. I am so afraid that I will be washed down the drain.” What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Don’t be silly; there’s no way you would fit in the drain.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I am your nurse, and I will stay with you, so you shouldn’t be afraid of your bath.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Let’s go back to your room, and I will bathe you there.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Today is your day for a bath.”

questions.rationale: The nurse should recognize irrational fears and arrange alternative ways to give personal care.

questions.stem: Which characteristics are most likely to be present in the patient with dementia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Forgets things relatively quickly and is usually unable to learn new things

choices.label: B
choices.text: Can remember new tasks but will forget any previously taught tasks

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cannot learn new information but will probably remember anything you ask about the past

choices.label: D
choices.text: Responds well to reality orientation and needs to have a flexible schedule

questions.rationale: Keeping in mind the following two important concepts when taking care of patients with dementia is helpful: (1) they usually forget things relatively quickly, and (2) they are usually unable to learn new things.

questions.stem: A patient is displaying confusion, which began very suddenly and lasted less than 1 week. What should the nurse suspect is present?
questions.rationale: Delirium begins abruptly and generally lasts a short period. It usually lasts 1 week and rarely lasts longer than 1 month.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing a room for a patient being transferred from the emergency department with a diagnosis of delirium. What should the nurse ensure in regard to the room?
questions.rationale: The patient should be in a private room with continual supervision. The room should be quiet and uncluttered, and lighting should be soft and diffuse to avoid shadows.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a patient with dementia notices that the patient stays awake most of the night. What is the nurse’s most appropriate action?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tell the patient that it is nighttime and that she must go to sleep.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s record to see whether she is sleeping during the day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Put the patient to bed and put the side rails up.

questions.rationale: Sleep and awakening are often reversed in patients with dementia. Trying to keep the patient awake during the day is helpful.

questions.stem: A nurse is discussing home care of a patient with dementia with the patient’s family. What should the nurse advise the family do to prevent the patient from wandering?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply a vest restraint to keep the patient in bed or in a chair.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Put locks on any doors that it would be dangerous for the patient to open (e.g., outside doors, medicine cabinet).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have someone remind the patient at least every 2 hours that he or she must not go outside by him or herself.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Set up a reward system for the times the patient stays where the family has requested.

questions.rationale: Patients with dementia must have the environment adapted to them rather than trying to adapt the patient to the environment. They usually forget things relatively quickly and will probably not remember what you have told them.

questions.stem: A patient asks a nurse, “My doctor says I get confused sometimes because I have vascular dementia. What caused me to have that?” What is the most appropriate response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It is usually caused from damage to brain cells because of inadequate blood supply, like a small stroke.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It is probably just some abnormal electrical activity in your brain.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I’m sure he will explain it to you later.”

questions.rationale: Patients with vascular dementia often have had a series of small strokes that cause progressive damage.

questions.stem: What should nursing care focus on to best support a patient with mild cognitive impairment (MCI)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reorienting the patient to the physical environment

questions.rationale: Persons with MCI need strategies for improving their memory. These persons have memory impairment but have otherwise normal cognition.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old patient with delirium related to high fever is hallucinating about large animals being in the room. What is the most reassuring nursing response to this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes, the animals are in here, but they are sound asleep.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I’m going to turn out the lights so you won’t have to look at the animals.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You are in the hospital. There are no animals in this room.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The hospital does not allow animals in the room.”

questions.rationale: Reorientation and presentation of reality are helpful with patients who have delirium.

questions.stem: What are believed to be causes of Alzheimer disease? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The cause of Alzheimer disease is still unclear, but protein deposits of amyloid have been found during autopsies of the brains of patients with Alzheimer disease, as well as tangled neurofibers. In addition, a deficiency of acetylcholine exists. Lewy bodies are associated with another type of dementia, and small strokes are thought to be the cause of vascular dementia.

questions.stem: When a normally oriented 87-year-old resident in a long-term care facility exhibits acute confusion, the nurse should first assess for a(n) ______.
questions.rationale: Infections, especially those that cause fever, can result in an older patient becoming confused or delirious.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman who has Alzheimer disease is restless, wanders during mealtimes, and will not sit down to eat. The nurse assisting with writing the care plan prioritizes the following interventions for the goal: The patient will eat at least 25% of each meal. ______ (Prioritize the options in sequence, from the most therapeutic to the least therapeutic. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place her in a chair with a vest restraint.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assign a nursing assistant (NA) to feed her.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give her a high-protein drink in a small cup to carry with her.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Offer peanut butter crackers as she passes by.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Leave her alone. She will eat when she is hungry.

questions.rationale: Offering a high-energy drink in a small cup partially meets the goal without further agitating the patient. Offering a cracker accomplishes the same thing, but accurate evaluation of whether the crackers are eaten or just dropped might be difficult. Assigning an NA to feed her may agitate her further and reduce her intake even more; in addition, it may not be the best use of available personnel. Placing her in a chair with a vest restraint is not a very desirable intervention and may not encourage her to eat. Leaving her alone does not meet the goal nor does it reduce the nutrition deficit.

questions.stem: What instruction should a nurse provide to a patient scheduled for a postvoid residual (PVR) test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Call the nurse immediately after voiding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After voiding, wait 10 minutes and void again.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid fluid intake for 8 hours before the test.

questions.rationale: The nurse must catheterize the patient immediately after voiding and measure the amount of urine.

questions.stem: Bladder training instructions are being given to a patient who has a history of urinary incontinence. What initial instructions should the nurse give to the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Wait until you feel the urge to void.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Don’t void any more often than every 4 to 6 hours.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Void any time you feel the urge.”

questions.rationale: Bladder training uses scheduled voiding; the patient is encouraged to delay voiding and void only every 2 to 3 hours while awake.

questions.stem: A male patient with urinary incontinence has been using an external (condom) catheter. A nurse is assessing the patient’s technique of applying the device. What techniques demonstrated by the patient would indicate the need for further instruction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Washes the penis with warm soapy water and dries the area well before applying the device.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encircles the penis with tape to secure the device.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uses elastic tape and wraps in a spiral pattern to secure the device.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carefully assesses the penis for any signs of irritation before applying the device.

questions.rationale: Encircling the penis with tape can restrict circulation and cause damage to the tissue.

questions.stem: A patient being assessed by the physician states, “I wet my pants every time I cough.” The nurse recognizes this as which type of incontinence?
questions.rationale: Stress incontinence is the involuntary loss of small amounts of urine during physical activity that increases abdominal pressure, such as coughing, laughing, sneezing, and lifting.

questions.stem: What instruction should a nurse provide to a patient who has been diagnosed with stress incontinence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Restrict fluid intake to less than 1000 mL/day.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Avoid fluids such as tea, coffee, and cola.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Delay voiding until you feel the urge to void.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Void no more often than every 4 hours.”

questions.rationale: Fluids such as tea, coffee, and cola have a diuretic effect and should be avoided.

questions.stem: A home health nurse is performing an evaluation of the home of an older adult patient to assess for any safety issues. What should the nurse recognize as an environmental factor that could lead to functional incontinence?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient’s room located on the opposite end of the house from the bathroom

choices.label: C
choices.text: Handrails located around the toilet and bathtub

choices.label: D
choices.text: Caregiver’s room located close to the patient’s room

questions.rationale: Functional incontinence is the term used when a person voids inappropriately because of an inability to get to the toilet or manage the mechanics of toileting. The patient’s room should be located close to the bathroom.

questions.stem: What condition should a nurse specifically ask a patient about when taking the medical history to reveal clues to the potential cause of urinary incontinence?
questions.rationale: Patients who have diabetes may develop neurologic problems that affect the bladder and are uncontrolled; they may produce large volumes of urine.

questions.stem: A patient, talking to a home health nurse about urinary incontinence, gives the nurse a list of the current medications she is taking. What medication should the nurse recognize as possibly contributing to the patient’s urinary incontinence?
questions.rationale: Valium is a sedative that can increase the incidence of incontinency of urine.

questions.stem: A nurse is instructing a patient on the procedure for a clean-catch urine specimen. The patient has tried several times but is having difficulty understanding the instructions. What is the best action for the nurse to implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take whatever specimen the patient can obtain.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide the patient with a clean bedpan to obtain the specimen.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the laboratory personnel to come and obtain a urine specimen.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Call the physician for a catheterization order.

questions.rationale: If the patient cannot cooperate with the clean-catch procedure, catheterization may be necessary.

questions.stem: A patient who is scheduled for an urodynamic test asks the nurse why he is having this test. What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “To test the capacity of the bladder.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “To see how much urine is left in the bladder after you have voided.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “To test the function of the nerves and muscles of the bladder.”

questions.rationale: Urodynamic procedures assess the neuromuscular function of the lower urinary tract.

questions.stem: A nurse is asked to instruct a patient on performing Kegel exercises to improve muscle endurance. The patient should be instructed to contract the muscles normally used to stop the flow of urine. Which proper technique should the nurse explain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contract for 3 to 4 seconds and relax for 10 seconds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contract for 10 seconds and relax for 10 seconds.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contract for 10 seconds and relax for 3 to 4 seconds.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Contract for 3 to 4 seconds and relax for 3 to 4 seconds.

questions.rationale: Long (6-12 seconds) contractions, followed by relaxation for 6-12 seconds, improves endurance. This patient should hold the contraction for 10 seconds and then relax for 10 seconds. The goal is to work up to 10 repetitions three or four times each day.

questions.stem: A patient who uses a pessary to help control incontinence is given instruction for its care. What should these instructions include?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Douche daily with a cleansing solution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check for proper placement once a month.

questions.rationale: A pessary is a device that is inserted into the vagina to hold the pelvic organs in place. The device must be removed periodically for cleaning and replaced as needed.

questions.stem: A patient who has urinary incontinence is at risk for urinary tract infection and urinary calculi. What should the nurse teach the patient and family regarding the best way to prevent these complications?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Restrict the patient’s fluid intake and frequency of incontinence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Be sure the patient’s voiding schedule is no more often than every 4 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage the patient to void at least every 2 hours and to take at least 2000 mL of fluid daily.

questions.rationale: The risk of urinary tract infection and calculi can be reduced by having the patient empty the bladder as scheduled and providing adequate fluids.

questions.stem: A patient is having problems with fecal incontinence. What should the nurse encourage the patient to include in the diet to help with this problem?
questions.rationale: Foods that thicken the stool, such as potatoes, bread, bananas, rice, cheese, yogurt, oatmeal, oat bran, boiled milk, and pasta, should be encouraged.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include as an essential factor when providing patient education about managing fecal overflow incontinence?
questions.rationale: Initially, the colon needs to be cleansed, and then regular evacuation is essential.

questions.stem: A patient tells a nurse that his bowel movements normally occur every morning after breakfast. What should the nurse understand as the rationale for this occurrence?
questions.rationale: When food enters the stomach, it stimulates activity throughout the digestive tract and causes the movement of fecal mass into the rectum.

questions.stem: A physician’s admission report states that a patient has a history of tarry stools. What should the nurse anticipate when assessing characteristics of this patient’s stool?
questions.rationale: Tarry is used to describe stools that are shiny, sticky, and black, which is usually an indication of blood in the stool.

questions.stem: What education should a nurse provide to a patient diagnosed with anorectal incontinence?
questions.rationale: Anorectal incontinence is associated with nerve damage that causes the muscles of the pelvic floor to be weak. Pelvic muscle exercises can help strengthen these muscles. The other choices would cause the incontinence to worsen.

questions.stem: Which result of postvoid catheterization would indicate adequate bladder emptying?
questions.rationale: If the catheterization immediately after voiding is less than 50 mL, the voiding can be viewed as adequate or normal.

questions.stem: A nurse is cleaning a patient with fecal incontinence when the patient says, “This is so embarrassing, and it makes me really angry.” What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Don’t worry about it; it’s my job to clean you up.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If you would have called me sooner, this wouldn’t have happened.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Would you rather let your family clean you up?”

questions.rationale: The nurse should use therapeutic communications of reflection to validate the patient’s feelings.

questions.stem: What foods should a nurse explain to a patient can cause diarrhea?
questions.rationale: Foods such as cabbage, raw vegetables, and spicy foods can cause diarrhea. Cheese, rice, and yogurt thicken stool.

questions.stem: A patient with fecal incontinence should be taught the importance of maintaining good skin integrity. What should be the focus of a nurse’s teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cleanse the perianal area thoroughly after each stool.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Change incontinence undergarments once a day.

questions.rationale: Skin integrity can be best maintained by keeping the perianal area clean and dry. The other choices may cause an impairment of skin integrity.

questions.stem: What is the cause of symptomatic incontinence?
questions.rationale: Symptomatic incontinence is the result of colorectal disease. Medical care should be sought to identify and treat the cause.

questions.stem: A patient asks a home health nurse if the periurethral bulking procedure will be a permanent remedy to urinary incontinence. On what knowledge regarding the effects of this procedure should the nurse base a response?
questions.rationale: The periurethral bulking procedure that injects a bulking product around the urinary meatus usually has to be repeated every 2 to 3 years.

questions.stem: What does the uroflowmetry diagnostic tool measure?
questions.rationale: The uroflowmetry is a diagnostic tool designed to measure voiding duration, rate, and amount.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include when providing instructions to a patient as to what to do when feeling the urge to void? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Breathe deeply and try to relax.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform several Kegel maneuvers without resting in between.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Walk to the bathroom at a normal pace while performing Kegel maneuvers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Distract herself with a book or a television program.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stop what she is doing and sit down or stand quietly.

questions.rationale: Breathing deeply, trying to relax, and performing Kegel maneuvers are all helpful in urge suppression. Distraction is seldom effective.

questions.stem: What should the nurse include in the plan of care to protect the skin integrity of an incontinent patient? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediately remove wet garments and linens.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wash skin with an antiseptic and towel dry.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inspect for areas of redness and breakdown every morning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply cornstarch to the perineum to absorb moisture.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Apply protective creams per agency policy.

questions.rationale: Any wet clothing or linens should be removed, and protective creams should be applied according to agency policy. Applying an antiseptic or cornstarch is not recommended because antiseptics are drying and cornstarch gives rise to yeast infections. The skin should be inspected every time the brief is changed.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding the habit training technique prompted voiding? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Is useful with cognitively impaired persons.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Helps the patient to recognize incontinence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Is based on giving praise for staying dry.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Uses the Valsalva maneuver to force urine from bladder

questions.rationale: Prompted voiding technique is used with cognitively impaired persons. This technique helps the patient recognize incontinence and the praise for staying dry.

questions.stem: A patient complains, “My allergies make me sneeze and urinate in my pants. I take my allergy drug and I urinate in my pants even more.” The nurse assesses that the drug the patient is referring to is an ______.
questions.rationale: Many antihistamine preparations increase the incidence of incontinence.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that the normal bladder will empty when it reaches the capacity of 200 to ______ mL. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: The urge to void will occur when the bladder is holding 200 to 250 mL of urine.

questions.stem: The method by which a nurse manually expresses urine from the bladder by pressing gently on the lower abdomen is the ______ method.
questions.rationale: The Credé method calls for the manual expressing of urine from the bladder be gently pressing down on the lower abdomen and pressing the bladder.

questions.stem: Which carrier protein is responsible for absorption of vitamin B12?
questions.rationale: Active transport requires the input of energy for the movement of particles across a membrane against an energy gradient. This movement requires a carrier protein. The best-known carrier protein is the intrinsic factor, which is responsible for the absorption of vitamin B12.

questions.stem: An adolescent patient reports she is following a severe fad diet that only allows 80 g of carbohydrate a day. What should the nurse warn that continuing this diet can result in when providing education?
questions.rationale: Diets with only 50 to 100 g of carbohydrates per day are likely to lead to ketosis.

questions.stem: Which foods have the least amount of fat?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vegetables, cottonseed oil, and safflower oil

questions.rationale: Fruits, vegetables, and cereal grains are relatively low in fat.

questions.stem: Energy balance is measured by weight gain or weight loss. How is protein balance measured?
questions.rationale: The body cannot store protein, so it needs to be eaten in the diet each day. Nitrogen in the urine is a good indicator of protein levels in the body. If protein intake is inadequate, nitrogen will be conserved by the kidneys, causing the urine nitrogen level to be low.

questions.stem: What is the function of protein in the body?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provides the main source of energy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serves as a catalyst for fat catabolism.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Slows the rate of digestion and absorption of nutrients.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Furnishes amino acids to build and repair tissue.

questions.rationale: Functions of proteins include serving as building blocks (amino acids) to build and repair body tissue. Carbohydrates and fats are the major sources of energy.

questions.stem: What is the minimum amount of protein needed for a well-balanced diet?
questions.rationale: Protein should make up a minimum of 10% in the adult diet.

questions.stem: Which cereal would be most effective to increase the fiber in a patient’s diet?
questions.rationale: Bran products offer more fiber content than rice, corn, or wheat.

questions.stem: An older man weighs 125 lb. How much should his daily fluid intake be for adequate hydration?
questions.rationale: Water is the largest component of the body and body tissues and is essential to all life processes. To prevent dehydration in an older adult, 30 to 35 mL/kg are recommended daily. 125 lb = 56.8 kg 56.8 kg  30 mL = 1704 mL 56.8 kg  35 mL = 1988 mL

questions.stem: A 13-year-old patient is 5 feet, 6 inches tall, weighs 102 lb, and is constantly worried about getting “too fat” and is refusing to eat. What condition should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Anorexia nervosa is an eating disorder characterized by self-imposed starvation. Persons with this disorder believe that they are fat even though they appear quite thin.

questions.stem: A nurse observes a young patient who accepts her food tray and appears to eat it enthusiastically, but the patient goes into the bathroom immediately after eating. The nurse suspects that the patient is causing herself to vomit. What do these assessments suggest?
questions.rationale: Bulimia is an eating disorder characterized by periods of binge eating followed by purging.

questions.stem: For what complication should a nurse monitor in a patient on total parenteral nutrition (TPN)?
questions.rationale: Central parenteral nutrition can cause pulmonary congestion if the fluid is not absorbed quickly enough. TPN may also cause hyperglycemia and hypertension but not vomiting.

questions.stem: What is usually infused with standard peripheral parenteral nutrition intravenous (IV) therapy?
questions.rationale: Peripheral parenteral nutrition, the standard IV therapy, may be made up of dextrose (5% to 10%).

questions.stem: A patient on an enteral feeding suddenly complains of feeling faint and is sweating. The diastolic blood pressure has dropped 20 points. The nurse recognizes this as signs of the dumping syndrome. What is the cause of dumping syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertonic fluid entering the jejunum and pulling large amounts of water from the circulating volume

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rich enteral feeding causing bowel irritation with severe cramping

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertonic solution rapidly entering the stomach causing pyloric spasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid drop in blood glucose as a result of the hypertonic solution pooling in the jejunum

questions.rationale: Enteral tube feedings can cause dumping syndrome by pooling feeding in the jejunum, which pulls fluid from the circulating volume and causes hypotension.

questions.stem: Cysteine, proline, isoleucine, valine, tryptophan, phenylalanine, methionine, and histidine are all classified as _____.
questions.rationale: The essential amino acids are cysteine, proline, isoleucine, valine, tryptophan, phenylalanine, methionine, and histidine.

questions.stem: A nurse calculates the needed kilocalories (kcal) for a 150-lb moderately active person to be _____ kcal. (Use only numeric characters.)
questions.rationale: 150 pounds ÷ 2.2 pounds = 68.1 kg 30 kcal = 2043 kcal.

questions.stem: In an assessment of a patient who has been receiving intravenous (IV) fluids for the past 6 hours, a nurse finds that the pulse is now bounding, the blood pressure is more than 15 mm Hg higher than the last reading, and pedal edema has developed. What should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Fluid volume excess accounts for the changes in the vital signs.

questions.stem: As part of a written standard protocol for the unit, a nurse adds that irrigation of an occluded cannula is not recommended. What is the rationale against performing this procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It may damage a venous valve.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It may introduce an air embolus into the line.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It may cause the patient pain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It may force blood clots into the main bloodstream.

questions.rationale: A cannula may be occluded because a clot has formed against the end of the shaft. By irrigating it, the clot is forced into the bloodstream.

questions.stem: What is a major advantage when medication is administered intravenously?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Better maintained at a therapeutic blood level

choices.label: C
choices.text: Safer than administering by oral or intramuscular route

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lower incidence of allergy than other routes

questions.rationale: Patients who receive intravenous medications can be better ensured of a more constant therapeutic blood level.

questions.stem: How often should intravenous (IV) rounds be performed during a nursing shift?
questions.rationale: IV checks every hour, made by a nurse, ensure maintenance of a proper rate, infusion condition, and complication detection.

questions.stem: Using an intravenous (IV) infusion system that delivers 60 drops/L, a nurse hangs a 1000-mL bag of 5% dextrose in water (D5W), which the physician has ordered to infuse at 80 mL/hr. It is now 1000. What time should the nurse anticipate the IV will need to be changed?
questions.rationale: 1000 mL (whole volume) ÷ 80 mL (volume infused per hour) = 12.5 hours. 1000 + 12.5 hours = 2230. Military times should be used.

questions.stem: Using an IV infusion system that delivers 60 drops/mL, a nurse hangs a 500-mL bag of normal saline (NS) at 0800. The physician has ordered a rate of 20 mL/hr. What should the nurse set the roller clamp to deliver?
questions.rationale: 20 mL (amount to be infused in 1 hr)  60 gtt = 1200 gtt/hr. 1200 gtt ÷ 60 min in 1 hr = 20 gtt/min. This roller clamp is an old method to determine rates, but in the case of a nonavailability of electronic delivery devices, it is a good thing to know.

questions.stem: A physician prescribes a hypertonic intravenous line for an extremely edematous patient. What solution should the nurse anticipate to be prescribed?
questions.rationale: D5W in 0.25 NS is hypotonic. D5W in NS and lactated Ringer solution are isotonic. 10% glucose is hypertonic.

questions.stem: What is the source of calories in IV solutions?
questions.rationale: Dextrose is sugar and the source of calories.

questions.stem: What signs of infiltration should be assessed by a nurse?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Burning sensation and no feeling at the site

questions.rationale: Intravenous fluid in the immediate tissues causes pain and swelling of the adjacent tissues.

questions.stem: A physician orders an infusion of 1000 mL of 5% dextrose in 0.45% NS to be completed in 8 hours. The IV delivery system’s drop factor is 20 gtt. How many mL/hr should the nurse set the electronic infusion pump to deliver the infusion?
questions.rationale: Whole volume (1000 mL) divided by number of hours (8) = 125 mL/hr. Volume per hour (125 mL)  8 hr = 1000 mL.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses an area where an intravenous (IV) line had been recently removed. The area has redness, swelling, and warmth. What should the nurse suspect as the cause?
questions.rationale: IV sites may show signs of inflammation or infection or both after an IV line has been removed.

questions.stem: A nurse has a patient with a central line. The insertion site is covered by an occlusive dressing with yesterday’s date. The nurse is to give an intravenous drug through the central line. What should be the initial action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use any of the three ports for delivery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Affirm catheter placement by withdrawing 10 mL of blood.

questions.rationale: Checking the drug for the proper dilution is essential. The dressing is not due to be changed. Drawing 10 mL of blood for site placement is excessive. Only two of the ports, which are color coded, are to be used for drug, fluid, or blood administration.

questions.stem: A nurse is choosing an intravenous cannula for an older adult patient and will choose the smallest size that will deliver the appropriate fluid. What size cannula is the most appropriate choice?
questions.rationale: The inside diameter, called the gauge, is expressed in reverse numerical order.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses for signs of infected phlebitis. How should the nurse most accurately describe this complication when documenting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rupture of the cannula with a lump under the skin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pale, cool skin with swelling at the puncture site

choices.label: C
choices.text: Firm, cool, raised, painful area at the puncture site; oozing and purulent drainage

choices.label: D
choices.text: Puncture site red, warm, with an oozing drainage

questions.rationale: Infection causes redness, warmth, and drainage from the intravenous site. Red streaks following the path of the vein may be visible.

questions.stem: What instruction should a nurse provide to a patient when removing a central catheter?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take a deep breath and bear down.

questions.rationale: The patient is instructed to take a breath and bear down to prevent air from entering the bloodstream as the catheter is removed.

questions.stem: Where is the best place to begin to select a vein for an initial intravenous (IV) site in a left-handed patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antecubital vein of the right arm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antecubital vein of the left arm

questions.rationale: Unless other reasons are identified, IV sites should be started in the most distal portion of the nondominant arm or hand.

questions.stem: Where should a nurse inject medication when administering an intravenous (IV) push medication to a patient who is receiving a continuous infusion?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Directly into the insertion cannula after temporarily disconnecting the IV bag

choices.label: C
choices.text: Into the port nearest to the insertion site to ensure quick delivery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Into the port nearest to the IV bag for less painful administration

questions.rationale: An IV push is to be administered through the port closest to the patient.

questions.stem: An intravenous (IV) administration of doxycycline (Vibramycin) has extravasated. What nursing action should be implemented after stopping the IV line?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the physician, and restart the IV line in another site.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Restart the IV line at another site and document the extravasation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Flush NS through cannula at the insertion site.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discard the IV tubing and the IV bag.

questions.rationale: Because doxycycline is a vesicant, the physician should be notified. The IV line should be restarted to maintain the drug at a therapeutic level.

questions.stem: A patient is to receive ampicillin (Unasyn) IV piggyback in 100 mL of fluid every 8 hours. The main intravenous (IV) line of D5W is running at 80 mL/hr and is on time. A nurse’s responsibility is to calculate the total 24-hour intake. At the end of the 24-hour shift, how much IV intake should the nurse document that the patient has received?
questions.rationale: 80 mL/hr  24 hr = 1920 mL; 100 mL  3 = 300 mL. Therefore, 1920 mL + 300 mL = 2220 mL in 24 hours.

questions.stem: A patient with a subclavian line complains of shortness of breath after an infusion. The patient is diaphoretic, and the blood pressure is 168/100 mm Hg, higher than a previous reading of 140/86 mm Hg. What should the nurse assess these symptoms as indicating?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fluid overload from too rapid an infusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Incorrect dilution of the infused drug

choices.label: D
choices.text: Embolus from introduced air or blood clot

questions.rationale: Air can be introduced into the subclavian line from any of the ports that are left unclamped. The symptoms have occurred too quickly for an overload or infection.

questions.stem: A patient has had an air embolus. What should be the immediate action of the quick-thinking nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Turns the patient to the left side and lowers the head of the bed

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gives oxygen at 100% in a non-rebreathing mask

questions.rationale: Lowering the head of the bed and turning the patient to the left side traps the air in the left atrium, where it can be more readily reabsorbed.

questions.stem: An older adult patient is quite ill and confused and begins to cry pitifully when a nurse approaches the bed to start an intravenous (IV) line. What is the best action for the nurse at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep the infusion equipment out of sight as much as possible, talk slowly, and divert the attention of the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inform the patient that the physician has ordered the IV and calmly continue to prepare the site and start the IV.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give an analgesic as ordered, wait a few minutes, and then proceed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Restrain the patient’s arm to a padded arm board and proceed as directed.

questions.rationale: Confusion during a bout of illness in older adults is common. Distraction and reassurance usually gain compliance. Medication and restraints are not indicated.

questions.stem: What action should the nurse implement when discontinuing an intravenous (IV) line?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remove the dressing, remove the catheter, dispose of the used equipment in the sharps container, and chart observations and actions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Observe the site for redness, swelling, and pain, and put on sterile gloves. Remove the dressing catheter and chart the findings and action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Observe the site for redness, swelling, and pain, and put on clean gloves. Remove the dressing and catheter, place a 2  2 dressing over the site, and chart the findings and action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Observe the site for redness, swelling, and pain and put on clean gloves. Remove the dressing and catheter; chart the findings and action.

questions.rationale: This procedure is not sterile. Clean gloves protect the nurse from the body fluids. Placement of a small 2  2 dressing keeps the area clean until the insertion site closes.

questions.stem: Which order should be clarified by a nurse when transcribing orders?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Potassium chloride, 80 mEq in 1000 mL D5W in 24 hours

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potassium chloride, 40 mEq IV in 10 mL D5W IV push

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potassium chloride, 50 mEq in 500 mL D5W in 4 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Potassium chloride, 80 mEq in 1000 mL D5W in 12 hours

questions.rationale: Potassium chloride is never given by intravenous push in such a small amount of diluent. Potassium chloride is always dissolved in D5W and should be infused at no more than 10 mEq/hr.

questions.stem: A nurse explains to a patient that, in the event of an accidental needlestick, the nurse should adhere to hospital policy. What directives should the nurse follow? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antibiotics are taken if infection is present.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood is drawn from both the nurse and the patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Repeat blood draws are performed 4 weeks after the stick.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain the physician’s permission to return to work.

questions.rationale: Most policies follow the general guidelines of making an incident report in case of time lost from the injury, drawing blood from both the nurse and the patient to determine whether an infection might be present and what type it is, and giving the antibiotic protocol to the nurse in the event of an infection in the patient. As a rule, permission from a physician to return to duty or a blood draw in 4 weeks is not necessary.

questions.stem: A nurse explains to a patient that the peripheral intravenous (IV) tubing administration set and dressing should be changed per ______.
questions.rationale: To prevent infection, the IV tubing administration set and dressing should be changed according to individual facility protocol.

questions.stem: What steps should a nurse take when administering an intravenous (IV) push drug through a peripheral intermittent device? ______ (Place the events in the appropriate sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flush the device with NS only or a combination of NS and heparin.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Slowly administer the drug through the device.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Check the concentration of the drug.

questions.rationale: IV push medication requires careful checking of the appropriate dilution, checking the placement of the device, flushing the device with NS to clear it, slowly injecting the drug, and flushing the device with NS or a combination of NS and heparin.

questions.stem: What actions should a nurse implement when assessing a peripheral intravenous (IV) line for an infiltration? ______ (Place the events in the appropriate sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply warm compresses to the area.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Restart the infusion at a different site.

questions.rationale: The infusion must be stopped to reduce the risk of further infiltration and then restarted to ensure that the adequate dose is received. The affected arm is elevated, warm compresses are applied, and the charge nurse is notified.

questions.stem: A postoperative patient is complaining of incisional pain. An order has been given for morphine every 4 to 6 hours as needed (PRN). What should the nurse assess first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess for the presence of bowel sounds.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient’s family if she is having pain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determine when the patient last received pain medication.

questions.rationale: Verifying the time of the last dose decreases the risk of a dose of medication being given too soon.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a postoperative patient. What should the nurse ask when assessing for the complication of malignant hyperthermia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you think you might have a fever?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Has anyone in your family ever had problems with general anesthesia?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Have you ever had any type of malignancy?”

questions.rationale: Malignant hyperthermia is a life-threatening complication that occurs in response to certain drugs. Susceptibility to this response is inherited.

questions.stem: A patient who had a hysterectomy yesterday has not been allowed food or drink by mouth (NPO). The physician has now ordered the patient’s diet to be clear liquids. What should the nurse assess prior to providing this patient with clear liquids?
questions.rationale: The absence of bowel sounds would contraindicate a diet of clear liquids.

questions.stem: Which technique should a nurse implement when changing a postoperative dressing?
questions.rationale: The aseptic technique is important to reduce the risk of infection.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a postoperative patient who has had spinal anesthesia. Which assessment is a priority for this patient?
questions.rationale: One complication of spinal anesthesia is postspinal headache, which is caused by the leaking of cerebrospinal fluid at the puncture site.

questions.stem: During a nurse’s preoperative assessment, the nurse notices that a patient is extremely anxious. The patient’s blood pressure is 142/92 mm Hg, the heart rate is 104 beats/min, and respirations are 32 breaths/min. What nursing action should be implemented?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the preoperative medicine early to help calm the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Call the surgical department and cancel the surgery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Instruct the patient on possible postoperative complications.

questions.rationale: When significant fear is associated with surgical complications, sometimes surgery is postponed until the anxiety level is reduced.

questions.stem: A nurse is performing a postoperative assessment on a patient who has just returned from a hernia repair. The patient’s blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg, and the apical pulse is 108 beats/min. What should be the nurse’s first action?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increase the rate of infusion of intravenous fluids.

questions.rationale: A decrease in blood pressure and tachycardia could indicate postoperative bleeding. The first action of the nurse should be to check the dressing and then report to the RN.

questions.stem: A postoperative patient who has no previous medical conditions is difficult to arouse when transferred to the surgical unit from the postanesthesia care unit. A nurse monitors the pulse oximeter and gets a reading of 85%. What should be the nurse’s next action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the pulse oximeter reading again in 1 hour.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Arouse the patient, have him cough, and encourage deep breathing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer a dose of pain medication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suction fluid from the oral cavity.

questions.rationale: If the pulse oximeter reading is less than 90%, the patient should be aroused and encouraged to take deep breaths. The patient’s respirations may not be adequate as a result of the effects of anesthesia.

questions.stem: A nurse has completed giving discharge instructions to a patient after a hernia repair. What verbalization by the patient should lead the nurse to determine that the patient understands the instructions?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Do not change the dressing until he sees his physician in 2 weeks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ignore changes in the size of his abdomen.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Report fever, redness, swelling, or increased pain at the incision site.

questions.rationale: The patient should report any signs and symptoms of infection (e.g., fever, redness, swelling, pain).

questions.stem: A nurse should include the proper use of an incentive spirometer in teaching a preoperative patient. What postoperative assessment of this patient would reveal that the incentive spirometry teaching has been effective?
questions.rationale: An incentive spirometer is used to promote lung expansion, which opens airways, reduces atelectasis, and stimulates coughing to clear secretions.

questions.stem: The suprapubic area of a postoperative patient is distended. The patient states that he has not voided since surgery approximately 9 hours ago. What should be the nurse’s first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the head nurse or physician.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insert a catheter and document insertion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Seat the patient on the side of the bed to try to void.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prepare the patient to return to surgery.

questions.rationale: The patient should be encouraged to try to void in a natural position before other measures are taken. Seated on the bedside or on a bedside commode may make urination easier.

questions.stem: Which modification should the nurse implement when caring for a postoperative patient after cataract surgery?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Omit use of an incentive spirometer for deep breathing.

questions.rationale: There are only a few instances in which coughing is contraindicated. They include surgeries for hernias, cataracts, and brain surgery.

questions.stem: When obtaining a patient’s signature on the surgical consent form, the patient seems confused about the procedure to be performed. What is the most appropriate response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the patient to talk to the physician after he or she gets to the surgical department.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient to go ahead and sign the consent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient what the physician told him and then call the physician if necessary.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage the patient to ask his family what the physician told them.

questions.rationale: The patient may not understand some of the medical terms used by the physician, and the nurse may be able to explain them. If the patient needs further information, notify the physician. The physician is responsible for explaining the procedure and the risks to the patient.

questions.stem: A nurse is doing an assessment of a patient who has returned from a cardiac catheterization and had conscious sedation. Which finding should the nurse report?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient complaints of the need to void

questions.rationale: Conscious sedation uses intravenous drugs to reduce pain intensity or awareness without a loss of reflexes. A complication may be excessive sedation approaching that of general anesthesia. The patient should be easily aroused.

questions.stem: What is the goal of palliative surgery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remove and study tissue to make a diagnosis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relieve symptoms or improve function without correcting the basic problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remove diseased tissue or correct defects.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Correct serious defects that only affect appearance.

questions.rationale: Palliative surgery is performed only to relieve symptoms or to improve function. It is not curative.

questions.stem: What information should a nurse ask a patient during the preoperative assessment?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allergies, medications, and past medical conditions

questions.rationale: If an emergency should arise, any allergies can be promptly managed. Knowledge of the patient’s medications can enable the nurse to anticipate possible drug interactions. Past medical conditions may increase surgical risks or require special attention during the perioperative period.

questions.stem: Which member of the surgical team administers anesthetics and monitors the patient’s status throughout the procedure?
questions.rationale: The anesthesiologist and nurse anesthetist have special training and are the members of the surgical team that administer anesthesia and are responsible for closely monitoring the patient during surgery.

questions.stem: A nurse is assisting in the transfer of a postoperative patient from the postanesthesia care unit to the surgical nursing unit. What action should the nurse implement to ensure the safety of the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Put the side rails up after moving the patient from the stretcher to the bed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient to move from the stretcher to the bed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Move the patient rapidly from the stretcher to the bed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Uncover the patient before transferring from the stretcher to the bed.

questions.rationale: The patient will probably still be experiencing residual effects of anesthesia; the side rails should be up to prevent the patient from falling out of bed.

questions.stem: A patient who has just undergone a colon resection complains to a nurse that he felt something pop under his dressing while trying to get out of bed. The nurse removes the dressing and finds that dehiscence of the wound has occurred. What nursing action should be implemented first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replace the dressing; dehiscence is normal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pull the wound edges together and replace the dressing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cover the wound with sterile dressings saturated with normal saline.

questions.rationale: The first action of the nurse should be to cover the wound with saline-saturated dressings to prevent damage of the exposed organs from drying and then to call the physician.

questions.stem: A patient has just returned to the surgical unit after varicose vein stripping and ligation. What is the best technique for a nurse to evaluate pain relief?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the patient’s record for the last dose of pain medication administered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient to rate the severity of the pain on a scale of 1 to 10.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the family if they think that the patient is having pain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell the patient to ask for pain medicine when it is needed.

questions.rationale: Having the patient rate the pain provides a system for evaluating response to the pain medication. Pain is controlled better if treated before it becomes severe, and the patient may not ask for pain medicine soon enough.

questions.stem: A patient scheduled for a liver biopsy has given a nurse a list of medications routinely taken at home. Which medication should the nurse question?
questions.rationale: Aspirin is an anticoagulant, which can increase the risk of postoperative bleeding. Drugs that have been taken for a long time may require dose adjustments because of the effects of surgery or the effect of additional drugs, which may be held or modified.

questions.stem: What should a nurse suggest to a patient to prevent the effects of postoperative immobility on the gastrointestinal system?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Limit her activity for the first 3 to 4 days.

questions.rationale: The intake of oral fluids and ingestion of a normal diet help stimulate peristalsis.

questions.stem: A postanesthesia care nurse is evaluating a patient for possible transfer to the surgical unit. Which assessment should prevent the patient’s transfer?
questions.rationale: The pulse oximeter reading should be 95% to 100%. The patient should not be transferred from the recovery room until the vital signs are stable, respiratory and circulatory functions are adequate, pain is minimal, the patient is easily awakened, no complications have been experienced, and the gag reflex is present.

questions.stem: Why should a nurse assess a patient’s limbs and position the limbs frequently after a regional anesthesia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pain is not perceived, although motion is possible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rashes and skin eruptions would indicate an allergy.

questions.rationale: After a regional anesthesia, movement is possible, but pain is not perceived immediately after surgery, which leaves the patient susceptible to injury.

questions.stem: A patient who received Penthrane as an inhaled anesthesia complains of a sore throat and a raspy voice. What should the nurse explain as the probable cause of these discomforts?
questions.rationale: Inhalant anesthesia is administered via an endotracheal tube that is inserted after the patient is unconscious.

questions.stem: Patients with preoperative disorders put them at risk during recovery. What disorders should a nurse be aware may pose this hazard? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Diabetes, hepatitis C, and COPD all complicate recovery related to blood-clotting deficiencies, respiratory problems, or disturbance in the healing process. Warfarin therapy is not a disorder and should have been discontinued well before surgery, and fungal skin infections do not pose a threat.

questions.stem: A patient has an extensive bowel preparation of oral laxatives and enemas for a colon resection. What rationales should the nurse list when asked about the rigorous preparation? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduces possibility of fecal contamination of the operative site.

questions.rationale: Preoperative bowel preparation reduces the risk for infection from bowel contents and decreases postoperative distention, constipation, and straining at stool.

questions.stem: A nurse carefully monitors an obese patient after a hysterectomy for the peculiar postoperative complications. Which postoperative complications are associated with obesity? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Obese patients are especially prone to postoperative respiratory complications of pneumonia and atelectasis. Obese patients are at increased risk for infection because of the amount of adipose tissue.

questions.stem: What are the responsibilities of a circulating nurse? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assisting the surgeon with the procedure

questions.rationale: The circulating nurse is in charge of the operating room, monitors asepsis, provides supplies, and documents patient care. The first assistant helps the surgeon with the procedure and the scrub nurse handles the instruments.

questions.stem: A nurse discovers on the preoperative assessment that a patient has a condition that would require increased amounts of general anesthesia. The condition is ______.
questions.rationale: Individuals who use alcohol excessively usually require greater amounts of anesthesia.

questions.stem: Which term appropriately defines an artificial opening into a body cavity?
questions.rationale: An ostomy is an artificial opening into a body cavity.

questions.stem: Why is a patient who has undergone a colostomy instructed to measure the width of the stomas for the first 6 weeks postoperatively before applying each new pouch?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The stoma will shrink during this time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A poor-fitting pouch will cause infection of the stoma.

questions.rationale: During the first 6 weeks, the stoma normally shrinks. The pouch needs to fit as closely to the stoma as is comfortable and safe to prevent skin irritation.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old patient with a permanent colostomy reports some abdominal discomfort and rigidity 3 days after surgery. Which assessment should the nurse report and record?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vital signs are temperature, 100 F; pulse, 92 beats/min; and blood pressure, 160/98 mm Hg.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stoma is swollen and red; small amount of blood is observed at the base.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pouch has drained 110 mL of green-brown liquid, oozing from the pouch edges.

questions.rationale: Vital signs, in conjunction with the complaint of abdominal discomfort, should be reported and recorded as possible signs of impending peritonitis.

questions.stem: A nurse is aware that many patients with ostomies have an altered self-image. What might this cause?
