questions.rationale: A damaged self-image or body image may cause patients with ostomies to feel unattractive and embarrassed about possible sexual activity. Open-ended questions assist the patient to talk about their feelings.

questions.stem: What should a nurse instruct the patient to do to ensure a good fit of the appliance to avoid leakage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place the pouch only when lying down.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check pouch placement to ensure a firm seal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Confirm that the pouch fits tightly to the edges of the stoma.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Confirm that the pouch covers the entire abdomen.

questions.rationale: Placement of the pouch should provide a good fit and be comfortable in all positions but not too snug on the stoma to risk laceration. The pouch needs to only cover enough of the abdomen to allow for a firm fit.

questions.stem: What should a nurse suggest to a patient with a colostomy choose when selecting an appropriate diet to reduce excess gas or diarrhea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Roast beef, mashed potatoes, and peeled stewed tomatoes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Broiled pork chop, boiled potato, and corn on the cob

choices.label: C
choices.text: Broiled trout, mashed potatoes, and spinach

choices.label: D
choices.text: Barbeque pork on a white bun, coleslaw, and French fries

questions.rationale: Gas-forming or spicy foods and roughage, such as corn, fish, and cabbage, usually cause gas and diarrhea.

questions.stem: A patient who has had a temporary colostomy to rest his ulcerated bowel says, “I don’t know how I will continue to work at my job with this thing stuck to my stomach.” Which response by the nurse is most likely to stimulate communication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This is only a temporary adjustment for you, and the colostomy will be reanastomosed in less than 6 months.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “A nurse with special training will be in to help you.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “What is there about your job that you feel you cannot do?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Many people feel as you do, but they learn to dress and act and work just like they did before the surgery.”

questions.rationale: Open-ended questions without prejudgment or belittling encourage the patient to identify sources of anxiety and help the patient cope with, adapt to, or problem solve stressful events.

questions.stem: What is the primary advantage of a J-pouch anal anastomosis procedure?
questions.rationale: Preoperative teaching includes the expectation of near-normal bowel elimination. As with any bowel elimination, odor and possibly occasional diarrhea will occur. An irrigation is not necessary.

questions.stem: A patient with an ostomy asks the nurse what limitations should be observed in the immediate postoperative period when at home. What is the most informative information that the nurse should share?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid heavy lifting for at least 3 months.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Limit fluid intake to no more than 1000 mL/day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wear loose clothing without belts or elastic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cover your appliance with plastic sheeting while showering.

questions.rationale: Avoiding heavy lifting for 3 months is advised. People with ostomies should take in at least 2000 mL of fluid every day. They may wear ordinary clothes that do not bind the stoma. Showering is allowed because the appliance is waterproof.

questions.stem: A patient with a colostomy continues to worry about odor. What instruction should a nurse provide to allay this concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pierce the top of the appliance bag with a pin to allow gas to escape.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rinse the pouch in a vinegar solution.

questions.rationale: The problem of odor is a frequent cause of anxiety to patients with colostomies. Rinsing the bag with a vinegar solution or putting a small amount of vinegar in the bag is helpful in odor control. Piercing the bag allows gas to escape more easily, and wearing tight-fitting clothing does nothing to alleviate odor. Personal hygiene is not a consideration in controlling the odor of feces.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old patient with a permanent colostomy asks whether she will be able to become pregnant. What is the most informative response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “No. The colostomy weakened the pelvic floor to the point that it will not support a pregnancy.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Yes. Pregnancy may be accomplished with artificial insemination because the fallopian tubes are usually damaged by a colostomy.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “No. The abdominal pressure exerted by a pregnancy will cause the prolapse of the stoma.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Yes. The colostomy will not interfere with pregnancy or delivery.”

questions.rationale: Colostomies do not interfere with pregnancy or delivery.

questions.stem: A baby born without a urinary bladder has a surgically created cutaneous ureterostomy. One stoma exists. What should be discussed with the child’s family regarding the care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This urinary diversion is permanent, and urine will drain from it continually.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “In the future, a second surgery will offer an exit for the urine from the other kidney.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This pouch needs to be changed only once a week.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You should notify the surgeon if the stoma becomes paler in color.”

questions.rationale: The baby’s ureterostomy and drainage of urine are constant. This is a permanent solution because of the lack of a bladder. Both ureters are joined for the release of urine through the stoma. The pouch will be on continually and needs to be changed as needed several times a day.

questions.stem: What should the initial assessment of a patient that just returned from surgery for the creation of an Indiana pouch include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drainage of urine from the Penrose drain at the operative site

choices.label: B
choices.text: Condition and color of the stoma

choices.label: C
choices.text: Appearance of mucus in the urine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Copious and odorous urine drainage from the incision

questions.rationale: Indiana pouches initially have a Penrose drain to drain the small amount of urine; it will have mucus in it but no odor. No stoma exists to observe. Irrigations may be necessary to remove clots and mucus.

questions.stem: A patient says, “I hate this yucky paste under my appliance. I think I will just tape it on.” What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Taping will not seal the wafer tight enough to prevent leakage or fill increases.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Taping with waterproof tape is just as effective as the paste.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Taping is far more irritating to the skin than the paste would be.”

questions.rationale: Reminding the patient that the paste both bonds and waterproofs is the best information.

questions.stem: A patient comes to the industrial nurse and is frantic because the stoma to the colostomy has prolapsed 1 year after surgery. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “If there are still feces coming from the stoma, then it is not blocked. Contact your surgeon for an evaluation.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You must come in immediately because the stoma may completely retract into your abdomen.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This is an emergency situation because it has stenosed.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Don’t worry about that. Coughing or sneezing might have caused the prolapse. It will come back out in a few hours.”

questions.rationale: The prolapse of a stoma is very disturbing to a patient. The condition should be evaluated by the surgeon. However, if the stoma is still patent, emergency implementation is not necessary. Prolapse can be caused by coughing or sneezing, but the stoma will still need evaluation.

questions.stem: A patient is receiving discharge instructions and shares with the nurse that he intends to do a lot of traveling. What instruction should the nurse include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Pack plenty of extra colostomy supplies in your checked airline luggage. Some places you might visit do not always carry the supplies you will need.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Exercise caution with new foods, especially local fruits and vegetables because they may cause diarrhea or gas.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “If visiting somewhere where drinking local water is not advised, irrigating the colostomy with the local water is still okay.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Repeat back to me what we just talked about so that you will remember everything you have been taught.”

questions.rationale: Warning about foods in a different country is appropriate. Supplies should be placed in a carry-on bag for quick access or in the case of lost luggage. Water that is not safe to drink is not appropriate as irrigation fluid.

questions.stem: Which assessment by a nurse caring for an immediate postoperative patient with an ileal conduit should be reported or receive attention immediately?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Small amount of blood in the drainage

questions.rationale: The distended abdomen suggests that the gastrointestinal suction is not effective to prevent bowel distention. The nurse must check the efficiency of the suction. Lack of bowel sounds, mucus in the urine, and a small amount of blood in the drainage are to be expected as normal postoperative assessments.

questions.stem: A patient asks if rectal suppositories can be used to assist with constipation problems with his colostomy. What should the nurse clarify regarding suppositories?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They can be used in double-barreled colostomies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They can be used in a stoma.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They should not ever be used in a colostomy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They will not penetrate well enough to relieve constipation.

questions.rationale: Suppositories can be used effectively in double-barreled colostomies and in stomas of a single colostomy.

questions.stem: What might the continual loss of liquid stool result in with an ileostomy patient?
questions.rationale: Metabolic acidosis can result from a loss of bicarbonates in the stool.

questions.stem: Which is the best nursing strategy for encouraging an ostomy patient to perform self-care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Plan to change the pouch when family members will be present, and have the patient watch and listen to the procedure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Frequently tell the patient that if he or she does not learn stoma self-care, no one is going to do it for him or her.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encourage the patient to watch the stoma care procedure, gradually encouraging participation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Shield the patient from sight of the stoma until the patient actually asks to see it.

questions.rationale: The goal for teaching patients with ostomies is to assist them to care for themselves without pressure or forcing.

questions.stem: A patient asks a nurse if karaya products can be used to seal the urostomy appliance. On what knowledge should the nurse base a response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Any adhesive is effective on a urostomy appliance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Karaya products can cause urinary infections.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Formation of urine crystals is increased with the use of karaya products.

questions.rationale: Urine breaks down karaya products and should not be used as a paste with urinary diversion appliances.

questions.stem: What is caused by some adhesive pouch material used to hold the appliance in place?
questions.rationale: Pouch adhesives can cause allergic reactions, but they do not melt the pouch or cause odor. Because the paste is not in contact with the stoma, it does not affect it.

questions.stem: What is the most effective way for a nurse to help provide support to the patient with an ostomy who has ineffective regimen management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask a volunteer from the American Cancer Society or United Ostomy Association to visit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask a volunteer from the Reach for Recovery Society to visit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Send a close family member for psychiatric counseling.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain humor books pertaining to illness, such as Anatomy of an Illness, or watch several episodes of The Three Stooges on television.

questions.rationale: Contact with persons who have coped with all the aspects of ostomies are excellent resources for individuals with new ostomies. Every effort is made to send a volunteer of the same age and gender.

questions.stem: A postoperative patient with an ostomy has the potential for loss of fluid volume and electrolyte imbalance. Which assessments indicate such loss? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The loss of base products from the bowel that allow for metabolic acidosis can be a very serious postoperative complication. Signs and symptoms include changing mental status, muscular twitching, poor skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, and weakness.

questions.stem: What can be caused by lack of thorough cleansing of fecal matter from around a stoma? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Fecal matter left on the skin and trapped under the pouch can cause fungal, bacterial, and yeast infections, as well as odor.

questions.stem: What are complications that may occur with continent pouches (Kock and Indiana)? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The most frequent complications are incontinence, difficult catheterization, and reflux pyelonephritis. Rupture and peritonitis are not threats to the patient from this surgery.

questions.stem: What should be considered when deciding placement of a stoma? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The two major considerations for the placement of the stoma are that the placement allows for a good seal and ease in self-care. Stabilization is nice but not necessary. The placement should not be near the umbilicus.

questions.stem: When a patient complains of urine crystals forming on the urostomy stoma, the home health nurse recommends dissolving them with a pad saturated with ______.
questions.rationale: A pad soaked in vinegar with dissolve urine crystals that may form on the stoma of a urostomy.

questions.stem: How should a nurse position the body after the death of a patient?
questions.rationale: The body should be placed in the supine position, with the arms at the sides or with the hands across the abdomen.

questions.stem: According to Strauss and Glaser, three states of awareness of terminal illness have been identified. What do these states include?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Closed awareness, mutual pretense, and open awareness

questions.rationale: Strauss and Glaser have identified three states of awareness: closed awareness, mutual pretense, and open awareness.

questions.stem: When planning the care of a patient who is terminally ill, a nurse should include the prevention of pain and relief from discomfort. What should the nurse implement when administering pain medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Narcotics have the potential for addiction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pain medication must be given before the pain becomes unbearable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pain medication should be given no more often than every 6 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Narcotics must be given as needed only.

questions.rationale: Pain control must be consistent to provide constant relief rather than waiting until the pain is unbearable and then trying to relieve it. Addiction to narcotics is of little concern.

questions.stem: What is one of the most important interventions for a nurse to implement when caring for a patient who is terminally ill?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Touching and listening to the patient

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encouraging the patient to express any regrets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessing for signs and symptoms of impending death

choices.label: D
choices.text: Talking to the patient about how other patients have handled the dying process

questions.rationale: The simple presence of someone provides support and comfort. Neither words nor actions are necessary unless the patient requires something. Holding hands, touching, and listening are quality nursing responses.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient who is terminally ill for clinical signs of impending death. What should the nurse expect when assessing the patient’s respirations?
questions.rationale: Breathing may sound wet and noisy. Noisy, wet-sounding respirations, termed the death rattle, are a response based on mouth breathing and accumulation of mucus in the upper airways.

questions.stem: What is believed to be the last sense to remain intact during the death process?
questions.rationale: Hearing is commonly believed to be the last sense to remain intact during the death process.

questions.stem: A patient’s family asks a nurse what to expect when the patient dies. What is the most accurate response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: His heart will stop, and then later he will quit breathing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: His respirations will cease first, and then the heart stops beating within a few minutes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: His heartbeat and breathing will just stop suddenly without warning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He will quit breathing first, and then it could be several hours before his heart stops.

questions.rationale: The body gradually relaxes until all functions end. The respirations cease first, and then the heart stops beating within a few minutes.

questions.stem: While a nurse bathes a patient, he says, “Why are you giving me a bath? I’m going to die no matter what.” What should be the nurse’s most appropriate response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “A bath will make you feel better.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Would you like to talk about how you are feeling?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I can give you some medicine to make you feel better.”

questions.rationale: Anger is a common and normal response to grief. Therapeutic communication should focus on the patient’s feelings.

questions.stem: A patient recently diagnosed with inoperable lung cancer tells a nurse, “I am looking forward to seeing my daughter graduate from college in 2 years.” What stage of grief should the nurse recognize this as according to Elizabeth Kübler-Ross?
questions.rationale: Kübler-Ross identifies denial as the person refusing to acknowledge the impending loss.

questions.stem: The family of a patient who is showing signs of impending death tells a nurse that they would like for any organs that can be used to be donated. What action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wait until the patient has died before discussing organ donation with the family.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tell the family that the patient has not signed a consent, so the organs cannot be donated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient’s record to see whether the physician has written an order for the patient to have organs donated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the physician of the family’s wishes.

questions.rationale: The physician should be notified immediately because some tissues must be harvested within hours after death. The decision to donate organs may be made by the patient or immediate family.

questions.stem: A patient who is terminally ill has asked a nurse if he can request that cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) not be used in the event he has respiratory or cardiac arrest. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The physician is the only one that can make that decision.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will be glad to get you some information on advance directives, which are written statements of a person’s wishes regarding medical care.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Yes, you can make that decision; I will tell all the nurses.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Your family will have to make the decision.”

questions.rationale: Written information concerning a patient’s rights to accept or refuse treatment must be provided to the patient. Advance directives are written statements of a person’s wishes regarding medical care.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who is terminally ill whose religion is Orthodox Jew. This patient’s physician’s orders specify “do not resuscitate” (DNR). The patient is alone and is having signs of impending death. What action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wait until the patient dies and then notify the family.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transfer the body to the mortuary or the morgue immediately after death.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stay with the patient until death occurs, prepare the body, and then call the family.

questions.rationale: Only designated Orthodox Jewish persons or a Jewish burial service may care for the body. The dying person is not left alone. The body is not left alone between death and burial, which must occur within 24 hours. The body is not to be touched for up to 30 minutes after death.

questions.stem: In caring for a dying patient who is grieving, a nurse bases patient care on the theory that grief is helpful and assists the person in accepting the reality of death. What is this type of grief is called?
questions.rationale: Uncomplicated grief assists the person in accepting the reality of death.

questions.stem: A nurse is on duty in the emergency department when a patient is brought in who has died almost immediately after admission. The family states that the patient has had no history of medical problems and that no apparent reason for death is known. What should occur in this situation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The family may choose to have an autopsy performed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An autopsy is required by law, and the coroner or medical examiner must be notified.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The body must be held at the hospital for 24 hours.

questions.rationale: Under law, in most states, an autopsy is required if a person expires by suicide, homicide, within 24 hours of admission to a health care facility, or from unknown causes.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing a patient’s body after death and discovers that the patient’s dentures were not in his mouth before death. What action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insert them gently into the mouth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Send them to waste management to be disposed of properly.

questions.rationale: Dentures may be inserted gently to maintain the normal facial appearance. If the dentures cannot be inserted easily, do not force them.

questions.stem: A patient who is terminally ill asks a nurse what is meant by a medical power of attorney. What is the nurse’s best explanation of this written document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It allows another person to manage your financial affairs.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It allows the physician to make any medical decisions that need to be made for you.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It says you have given up all rights to make medical decisions for yourself.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It allows you to select someone to make health care decisions for you only if you are unable to do so for yourself.”

questions.rationale: A medical power of attorney allows someone other than the patient to make medical decisions for the patient only if the person is incapable of making decisions as certified by the physician.

questions.stem: What right does the Omnibus Reconciliation Act of 1990 require institutions to provide written information to patients concerning?
questions.rationale: The Omnibus Reconciliation Act requires that all institutions that participate with Medicare must provide written information to patients concerning their rights to accept or refuse treatment.

questions.stem: What important need should a nurse caring for a dying patient understand?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Not imposing repeated and unnecessary assessments

choices.label: C
choices.text: Current, updated health history from the patient

choices.label: D
choices.text: Limiting the amount of visitors allowed

questions.rationale: Being sensitive and not imposing repeated and unnecessary assessments on the dying patient are important.

questions.stem: Nurses who have been caring for patients who are terminally ill frequently begin to feel helpless and frustrated. What is best for the nurse to implement when caring for the terminally ill patient?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Express feelings to a friend or co-worker.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ignore these feelings because they will soon be gone.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask to care for different patients.

questions.rationale: The basic recognition of the nurse’s feelings allows openness with the patient and the family.

questions.stem: What should be the primary nursing goal when caring for a patient who is experiencing dysfunctional grieving?
questions.rationale: Dysfunctional grief is disruptive to a person’s typical lifestyle and must be resolved.

questions.stem: A patient who has been diagnosed with a terminal illness is crying when a nurse enters the room. The patient states, “I promised God that I would be a better person if He will just let me get over this disease.” What stage of grieving according to Kübler-Ross should the nurse recognize this as?
questions.rationale: In the bargaining stage, the person wishes for more time to avoid the loss. The patient may express feelings that the loss is occurring as a punishment for past actions and may try to bargain with a higher power to gain time.

questions.stem: A dying 10-year-old boy tells a nurse, “I am going to leave and live with my grandpa in heaven.” How should the nurse interpret this child is feeling?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accepting his own death based on adult attitudes

questions.rationale: A 10- to 12-year-old child accepts death based on adult attitudes.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy who is positive for the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) confesses to the school nurse that he sneaks out of his home at night and rides his motorcycle at high rates of speed. How should the nurse interpret this behavior?
questions.rationale: Teenagers between the ages of 13 and 18 years may act out with dangerous or risky behavior in defiance of death.

questions.stem: What is the focus of the provision of palliative care? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The concept of palliative care is to provide a painless death and psychologic support to the patient and family. Care outside of the hospital is provided by nurses from a hospice service that come into the home.

questions.stem: A nurse counsels the family of a 6-year-old child experiencing the death of his grandfather not to shield the child from the grandfather’s death. What feelings might this child develop if protected from the pain of bereavement? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Small children who are protected from the grief of the death of a loved one frequently develop feelings of fear and abandonment. They frequently regress to a younger level of developmental behavior and feel an inability to express their feelings.

questions.stem: How is death frequently viewed in American society? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Americans essentially view death and impending death as an event that is negative and unacceptable to their values of preservation of health and prolonging life.

questions.stem: What preferences does the document “Five Wishes” help a dying person communicate to the family? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: What medical treatment will be acceptable

choices.label: C
choices.text: How to distribute assets such as real estate

choices.label: E
choices.text: How they wish to be treated

questions.rationale: The document “Five Wishes” does not act in the place of a will, but it is recognized as a patient’s advance directives.

questions.stem: When the family of a dying person does not discuss the issue of death openly and avoids the subject of dying altogether, the nurse recognizes this behavior representative of the ______ stage described by Straus and Glaser.
questions.rationale: The process of mutual pretense occurs with the family and the dying patient denying the approaching death and avoids any discussion about it.

questions.stem: What concept is believed to be the cause of illness with nontraditional therapies?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lack of harmony with the environment

questions.rationale: Alternate therapies are designed to recreate harmony and balance with the environment.

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing a patient for surgery. Which statement by the patient would cause the greatest concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I take black cohosh to ease menopausal symptoms.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I take garlic supplements daily to lower my cholesterol.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I find St. John’s Wort helps my depression.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “When I feel nauseous I take ginger to settle my stomach.”

questions.rationale: Garlic supplements taken daily can cause bleeding and must be discontinued before any surgical procedure.

questions.stem: A patient reports occasional constipation. Which herbal dietary supplement would the nurse recommend?
questions.rationale: Flaxseed can be used to treat occasional constipation due to its laxative effect.

questions.stem: Which complementary/alternative is built on complete systems of theory and practice that have evolved in different cultures apart from conventional medicine?
questions.rationale: Whole medicine systems are built on complete systems of theory and practice that have evolved in different cultures apart from conventional medicine.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman reports to the nurse she has been experiencing hot flashes due to menopause. Which herbal supplement is most likely to help with this symptom?
questions.rationale: Black cohosh can be used to treat menopausal symptoms.

questions.stem: Why are nontraditional remedies becoming popular? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Many remedies are available without a prescription.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remedies do not interfere with traditional medications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Practitioners charge lower fees than medical physicians.

questions.rationale: Alternative remedies are frequently available without a prescription and can be self-taught. The fees of the practitioners are much less than physicians, and the remedies are not invasive. The remedies may conflict with regular medications, however.

questions.stem: Which of the following herbal supplements have anticoagulant effects? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Cranberry juice, feverfew, garlic, and ginger root can all have anticoagulant effects.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that a patient who is taking Valium for a nervous disorder but is also taking passion flower tablets to combat symptoms is using a combination of traditional and alternative therapy that is called ______.
questions.rationale: Complementary therapy is the use of traditional medication with the addition of nonprescription herbs.

questions.stem: A nurse describes yoga as an alternative therapy that creates a mind–_____ intervention.
questions.rationale: Yoga is an alternative therapy that creates a mind–body intervention. Some individuals can attain a trancelike status.

questions.stem: The most commonly used natural product in America is ______.
questions.rationale: A large NIH survey assessed the use of nonmainstream products and practices among Americans. The most commonly used natural product was fish oil.

questions.stem: To what does the neural synapse refer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Length of time it takes for afferent neurons to carry impulses to the central nervous system (CNS)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Length of time it takes for efferent neurons to carry impulses to the motor neurons

choices.label: C
choices.text: Space between the axons and the dendrites of a neuron

choices.label: D
choices.text: Space between the axons of one neuron and the dendrites of the next

questions.rationale: Smooth, coordinated transmission must travel from one neuron to another across the neural synapse.

questions.stem: An older adult patient is experiencing extreme stress related to an admission to the hospital. What should the nurse expect the patient to demonstrate?
questions.rationale: Stress stimulates the fight-or-flight reaction with the release of epinephrine and norepinephrine, which causes increased heart rate and BP, reduced peristalsis, and pupil dilation.

questions.stem: Which neurologic finding would be considered abnormal in an 88-year-old patient?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Absence of the Achilles tendon jerk

questions.rationale: Dizziness and vertigo, although common, are considered abnormal.

questions.stem: What is the most reliable indicator of neurologic status?
questions.rationale: The ability to respond readily and correctly to person, place, and time is good evidence of intact sensorium.

questions.stem: A patient is stuporous but reacts by withdrawing from painful stimuli. What term is most appropriate for this patient?
questions.rationale: A stuporous patient who reacts to pain is semicomatose. The patient with no reaction to pain is comatose.

questions.stem: Which describes the Babinski reflex?
questions.rationale: Normal cortical function causes the toes to curl downward. Abnormal findings would be the toes turning up and spreading.

questions.stem: What should the nurse assess when a patient is scheduled for an angiogram?
questions.rationale: Allergy to shrimp and other shellfish also indicates a probable allergy to contrast medium.

questions.stem: What diagnostic test might be contraindicated for a patient who has a pacemaker?
questions.rationale: Metal appliances may be affected by the magnetic field during MRI.

questions.stem: A patient with a severe head injury begins to assume a posture of flexed upper extremities, with plantarflexed lower extremities. What do these assessments indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing intracranial pressure (ICP) with decorticate posturing

questions.rationale: Increasing pressure on the tissue above the midbrain results in abnormal flexion (decorticate posturing).

questions.stem: What should be immediately reported by the nurse caring for a 90-year-old patient with a closed head injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure change from 147/72 to 176/70-mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: Respiration rate increase from 14 to 18 breaths/min

choices.label: D
choices.text: Temperature decrease from 100.2 F to 97.6 F

questions.rationale: The widening pulse pressure is an indicator of increased ICP. Respirations and temperature are returning to more normal levels. Older adults have a slowed pupillary response as they age.

questions.stem: Which assessment on a patient on mannitol therapy for cerebral edema indicates the medication is effective in decreasing ICP?
questions.rationale: Mannitol is a hyperosmolar diuretic that draws fluid from brain tissue into the bloodstream, which is then excreted by the kidneys. Decreasing pulse and widening pulse pressure indicate increased ICP.

questions.stem: What should a nurse instruct a patient after a lumbar puncture to prevent a headache?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ambulate in the room with assistance.

questions.rationale: Lying flat for a prescribed period will allow the loss of cerebrospinal fluid during the procedure to replenish.

questions.stem: Which three symptoms are characteristic of Cushing triad associated with increased ICP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypotension, tachycardia, and narrowing pulse pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Widening pulse pressure, headache, and seizure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bradycardia, hypertension, and widening pulse pressure

questions.rationale: Bradycardia, increasing BP, and widening pulse pressure are all signs of increased ICP.

questions.stem: How can the nurse help reduce ICP in caring for the patient after a craniotomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keeping the patient flat in bed

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevating the head of the bed 30 degrees

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turning the patient to the right side

questions.rationale: Elevating the head of the bed at least 30 degrees helps reduce ICP.

questions.stem: Which neurotransmitters support smooth neural transmission? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Acetylcholine, dopamine, and epinephrine are neurotransmitters. CSF bathes the brain and spinal cord but has no transmission activity; the dendrite is the locus of the synapse.

questions.stem: Which are normal brain alterations associated with age? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: All brain alterations listed are expected changes that affect the older adult’s neurologic function except for tiny clot formations, which are a pathologic change.

questions.stem: The nurse assessing the level of consciousness in a patient will perform the following: ______ (Arrange in order from the simplest to the most complex. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply pressure to the nail bed.

questions.rationale: The assessment begins with simply approaching the patient and progresses to imposing painful stimuli.

questions.stem: The nurse conducting a Romberg test will ask the patient to do what? ______ (Arrange in the correct sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Touch his or her nose with the index finger with the eyes open.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Touch his or her nose with the index finger with the eyes closed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Touch his or her fingertip to nurse’s fingertip.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pat the knees with the palms and then the back of the hands rapidly.

questions.rationale: These simple exercises used to assess balance and perception should be performed in order from least to most difficult.

questions.stem: A patient with generalized convulsive disorder is unaware about the side effects of phenytoin (Dilantin). Which instruction would be most appropriate?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Brush teeth vigorously to encourage gingival growth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Limit fluids and eats foods that reduce diarrhea.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduce stimuli and take warm baths to induce drowsiness.

questions.rationale: Dilantin is irritating to GI tissues. Dilantin causes gingival hyperplasia, constipation, and drowsiness.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with meningitis who has a positive Brudzinski sign. Which assessment led to this conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Flexed hips when the neck is flexed by the nurse

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inability to extend the flexed leg fully because of hamstring pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resisting efforts of the nurse to flex his or her neck

choices.label: D
choices.text: Flexing the big toe upward and fan out the other toes

questions.rationale: Inflamed meninges will stimulate hip flexion to reduce meningeal discomfort.

questions.stem: A nurse is evaluating the effectiveness of teaching for a patient with multiple sclerosis (MS). Which statement by the patient indicates that accurate patient learning has taken place?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Now that I am taking steroids, I will be able to work like I used to.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I’m making a list of things that are important and things I will simply have to let go.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will make a plan to allow for long rest periods at least four times a day.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I am working on balancing time among rest, work, and family time.”

questions.rationale: Balancing time between various activities indicates that the patient with MS understands the need to conserve energy, not just to give up things or attempt to perform at a preillness level.

questions.stem: A patient in the emergency department states that she fell and hit her head and blacked out for a while but became alert again. The nurse suspects an epidural hematoma. For what should the nurse be diligent to assess?
questions.rationale: Increasing BP, drowsiness, and a widening pulse pressure are indicators of increased ICP.

questions.stem: Which intervention supports nutritional intake in a patient with Parkinson disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offer large meals with a variety of finger foods.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thicken liquids to make them easier to swallow.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Puree all foods and drink through a straw.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Offer a diet high in carbohydrates and fat and low in protein.

questions.rationale: Thickened feedings are easier to swallow. Several small, protein-rich meals are preferable to large ones. A pureed diet is unappealing.

questions.stem: A patient with Parkinson disease is depressed because his drug protocol of L-dopa and Sinemet is no longer controlling his symptoms. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Other drugs can be combined with L-dopa to increase its effectiveness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The effect of these drugs has an uneven course; symptoms will begin to subside again soon.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The two drugs can be given in higher doses to control the symptoms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical interventions have been very effective in the control of parkinsonian symptoms.

questions.rationale: The addition of other drugs to L-dopa may improve the conversion of L-dopa to dopamine. Palliative surgical implementations all have had little effect on controlling the symptoms.

questions.stem: A patient with Parkinson disease is considering taking St. John’s wort, an herbal remedy for depression, in addition to Sinemet and L-dopa. What is the most appropriate nursing response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depression is reduced by the use of herbal remedies such as St. John’s wort.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Doses of St. John’s wort and parkinsonian drugs should be taken on alternate days.

choices.label: C
choices.text: St. John’s wort must be taken in large doses to reduce depression.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Herbal remedies can interfere with the effectiveness of the parkinsonian drugs.

questions.rationale: Herbal remedies interfere with effectiveness of prescribed parkinsonian drugs.

questions.stem: Which nursing assessment would indicate a need for suctioning a patient with Guillain-Barré who is experiencing impaired breathing patterns because of neuromuscular failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased pulse rate and respiration of 20 breaths/min

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased pulse rate and adventitious breath sounds

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased pulse rate and respiration of 16 breaths/min

questions.rationale: Increased pulse rate, adventitious breath sounds, and abdominal breathing indicate an impaired breathing pattern.

questions.stem: A family member asks the nurse what would be an appropriate gift for a patient with Parkinson disease. What is the most useful suggestion?
questions.rationale: Satin sheets make moving in bed easier. Card games and jigsaw puzzles are frustrating because of the palsy. Hard-soled shoes provide better support than soft-soled shoes.

questions.stem: What action should the nurse implement when a patient falls to the floor in a generalized seizure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cradle the head to prevent injury.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insert an object between the teeth to prevent the patient from biting the tongue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep the patient on his or her back to prevent aspiration.

questions.rationale: Cradling the head and turning it to the side prevents injury and aspiration; restraint of limbs and insertion of an object into a patient’s mouth often result in injury.

questions.stem: Why is a patient with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) uniquely prone to depression?
questions.rationale: Because of their unimpaired intellect, patients with ALS are able to assess their deterioration, which increases their risk for depression. Altered mobility, nutrition, and communication are common to many disorders.

questions.stem: A nurse is careful about limb position in caring for an unconscious patient who sustained a head injury 10 days ago. What is the nurse trying to prevent?
questions.rationale: An unconscious patient should be positioned in anatomic alignment to prevent flexion deformities. Passive range of motion and frequent position changes are essential to maintain the limbs in a functional position.

questions.stem: What should the nurse implement before giving an enteral feeding to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Palpate the abdomen to check for residual feeding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevate the head of the bed 30 degrees.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient to tip his head forward.

questions.rationale: The head of the bed should be elevated 30 degrees to prevent aspiration.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for an immobilized patient with a brain tumor stimulates the patient several times a day with range-of-motion exercises and changes his position every 2 hours to try to prevent a disuse syndrome. What signs and symptoms does disuse syndrome include? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: A disuse syndrome includes pooling of pulmonary secretions, pressure ulcers, weakness, and stiff joints.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with symptoms of a migraine headache. What assessment information supports this diagnosis? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Migraine pain is usually unilateral, often begins in the temple or eye area, and is often very intense. Tearing and nausea and vomiting may occur. The patient is hypersensitive to light and sound and prefers a dark, quiet environment. WBC count is not expected to be elevated.

questions.stem: The ______ of the seizure is characterized by stiffening of the muscles or extremities with loss of consciousness.
questions.rationale: The tonic phase of the seizure is characterized by stiffening of the muscles or extremities with loss of consciousness.

questions.stem: A(n) ______ hematoma forms in the space between the inner surface of the skull and the outermost meningeal covering of the brain.
questions.rationale: An epidural hematoma forms in the space between the inner surface of the skull and the outermost meningeal covering of the brain.

questions.stem: A patient has weakness on the right side and impaired reasoning after having a cerebrovascular accident (CVA). What part of the brain is affected?
questions.rationale: Impaired motor strength on the right side in conjunction with impaired reasoning indicates a lesion in the left hemisphere of the cerebrum. The cerebellum controls balance and is not contralateral.

questions.stem: Which patient is at the greatest risk for a CVA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 20-year-old obese Latin woman who is taking birth control pills

choices.label: B
choices.text: A 40-year-old athletic white man with a family history of CVA

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 60-year-old Asian woman who smokes occasionally

questions.rationale: Older African Americans have a higher incidence of CVA than occasional smokers, young persons, or athletes. Hypertension increases the risk.

questions.stem: A patient experienced a period of momentary confusion, dizziness, and slurred speech but recovered in 2 hours. Which assessment in the diagnosis of this episode would be most helpful?
questions.rationale: TIA is generally suspected based on the health history and physical examination findings. Confirmation requires brain imaging studies, preferably with magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). BP of 140/90 mm Hg, although at the high end, is considered within normal limits. Headache and nausea alone are too common to be definitive.

questions.stem: A nurse is updating a teaching plan for a patient who sustained a TIA. What should the nurse be sure to include?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduction of fluid intake to 800 mL/day

questions.rationale: Daily aspirin reduces platelet aggregation and may prevent another attack. Reductions of fluid and long rest periods encourage clot formation.

questions.stem: A patient recovering from a CVA asks the purpose of the warfarin (Coumadin). What is the best response by the nurse regarding the purpose of Coumadin?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prevents the formation of new clots

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dilates the vessels to improve blood flow

questions.rationale: Coumadin and heparin prevent more clots rather than dissolving them. Coumadin has no effect on vasodilation or blood cell production.

questions.stem: A patient has had a complete stroke as a result of a ruptured vessel in the left hemisphere. How should this patient’s CVA be classified?
questions.rationale: A ruptured vessel in a hemisphere is an intracerebral hemorrhagic CVA. It did not occur in the subarachnoid space. Ischemic CVAs are the result of occluded vessels.

questions.stem: What should a nurse ensure as a priority for a patient immediately after a CVA?
questions.rationale: Adequate oxygenation prevents hypoxemia, which can extend and worsen effects of the CVA.

questions.stem: When should a nurse recognize that the acute phase of a CVA has ended?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Forty-eight hours has passed from its onset.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient begins to respond verbally.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vital signs and neurologic signs stabilize.

questions.rationale: When the vital and neurologic signs stabilize, the acute phase has ended. Verbal response, lower BP, and the passage of time without other signs are not adequate evidence that the acute phase has ended.

questions.stem: A patient in the acute phase of a CVA who has been speaking distinctly begins to speak indistinctly and only with great effort but still coherent. What should this nurse determine when assessing this patient?
questions.rationale: As symptoms worsen, the CVA is still evolving. Speech that is coherent but difficult is dysarthria rather than any type of aphasia. Dyspraxia is a motor impairment, not a speech impairment.

questions.stem: Several days after a CVA, a patient’s family asks a nurse if tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) is a drug therapy option now. The nurse’s response is based on the knowledge that this drug must be used within how many hours after the onset of symptoms?
questions.rationale: tPA is to be given within 3 hours of the onset of symptoms per the U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s guidelines. In some special treatment centers, this drug is given intravenously up to 6 hours after the stroke.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that a lumbar puncture is most helpful as a diagnostic tool for a new patient who has had a CVA. What would this diagnostic test help determine regarding the stroke?

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is complete or in evolution.

questions.rationale: Blood in the spinal fluid indicates a hemorrhagic stroke and will help direct medical protocol in the subsequent treatment.

questions.stem: A patient who has sustained a hemorrhagic stroke is placed on a protocol of 60 mg of calcium channel blocker (nimodipine) every 4 hours. The patient’s pulse is 82 beats/min before the administration of the prescribed dose. Which action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the full dose as prescribed without further assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Omit the dose, recording the pulse rate as the rationale.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delay the dose until the pulse is below 60 beats/min.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give half of the prescribed dose (30 mg).

questions.rationale: The dose should be given; it would be held only if the pulse is below 60 beats/min. Assessments should be made regarding BP, urine output, and edema.

questions.stem: During the acute CVA phase, a risk for falls related to paralysis is present. Which intervention best protects the patient from injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep the bed in a high position for ease of nursing care.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keep the side rails up, according to agency policy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess vision deficit related to ptosis.

questions.rationale: Rails keep patients in bed. The bed should be low, monitoring the patient should be more frequent than every 2 hours, and visual assessment is not directly related to fall prevention.

questions.stem: Pneumonia is the most frequent cause of death after a stroke. Which intervention would be contraindicated in the acute care of a patient with a hemorrhagic CVA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thicken liquids to ease swallowing and prevent aspiration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maintain adequate fluid intake, orally or IV.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage forceful coughing to stimulate deep breathing.

questions.rationale: Forceful coughing is contraindicated for the patient with a hemorrhagic CVA because it may cause increased intracranial pressure.

questions.stem: Which assessment indicates a fluid volume excess in a patient in the acute phase of a CVA?
questions.rationale: Crackles in the lung fields are a major indicator of fluid excess. The pulse and BP are elevated in fluid excess. Urine-specific gravity is low in fluid excess.

questions.stem: Which intervention should the nurse include in a patient’s plan of care to help preserve joint mobility in the acute phase of a CVA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pull the limbs on the affected side into a functional position.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform aggressive full range-of-motion exercises for all extremities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Support affected points in good functional alignment.

questions.rationale: Limbs maintained in a functional anatomic position and gently exercised (never pulled) into an acceptable range of motion several times during a shift will maintain optimal mobility.

questions.stem: A patient in the acute phase of an embolic CVA has an order for 400 units of heparin per hour IV. The heparin is in a solution of 5000 units/100 mL normal saline (NS). The nurse should set the electronic IV monitor at how many milliliters per hour?
questions.rationale: Regardless of the method of calculation, 50 units of heparin are in each milliliter of the solution; 8 mL/hr delivers 400 units (5000 units ÷ 100 mL NS = 50 units/mL. 400 units ÷ 50 units/mL = 8 mL).

questions.stem: Which assessment indicates that a patient with a CVA is in transition to the rehabilitation phase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: BP has been within normal limits for 24 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient makes positive statements about his condition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No further neurologic deficits are observed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Successful attempts are made at independent function.

questions.rationale: When no further deficits are noted and all vital signs have stabilized, the patient is considered to be in the rehabilitation phase. Positive statements and attempts at independence are not sufficient.

questions.stem: A patient with homonymous hemianopsia is in the rehabilitation phase of a CVA. When arranging this patient’s environment where should the nurse assure persons approaching and important items are visible and available?
questions.rationale: Making the patient scan the affected side helps stimulate the return of normal function in the rehabilitation phase.

questions.stem: Which outcome criterion is the most appropriate for a patient with “Inadequate nutrition, related to dysphagia, with the goal of adequate intake of nutrients”?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offers a variety of food groups

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eats half of all meals offered

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maintains body weight of 150 to 155 lb

questions.rationale: The maintenance of a desired weight is indicative of adequate nutrition. Eating a portion of a meal or eating independently does not adequately measure the extent to which the goal was met. Offering a variety of foods is a nursing or dietary function, not an outcome.

questions.stem: Which is the most effective intervention for best support of regular bowel elimination and the prevention of constipation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Limit fluid intake from 32 to 50 oz daily to compact the stool.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer small soapsuds enema every other day to cleanse the bowel.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give stool softeners daily, establishing a consistent time to attempt elimination.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer a strong laxative on a daily basis to encourage evacuation.

questions.rationale: Daily stool softeners, rather than daily laxatives or frequent enemas, help restore regularity and bowel tone.

questions.stem: A patient in the rehabilitation phase after a CVA accidentally knocks the adapted plate from the table and bursts into tears after failing to feed himself. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Don’t cry. You’ll be mastering eating in no time.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I don’t believe crying will help. Let’s try drinking from a special cup.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Bless your heart! Let me get a new meal and feed you.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Learning new skills is hard. Let’s see what may have caused the trouble.”

questions.rationale: Recognizing effort and showing support are the best approaches to depression and frustration. Babying the patient and admonitions against crying add to the problem. Redirection to the task at hand is therapeutic.

questions.stem: Which instruction is most helpful in teaching the family and patient who is in the rehabilitation phase after a CVA about altered sensation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Make frequent assessments for signs of pressure or injury.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use the affected side in supporting the patient in ambulation and transfer to stimulate better sensation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply ice packs to the affected limbs to encourage a return of sensation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply a heating pad to the affected limbs to increase circulation.

questions.rationale: Frequent assessment using the National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale will allow early detection. The use of hot or cold applications and using the affected limbs in transfer or ambulation may cause injury.

questions.stem: Which posthospital option should the nurse encourage a patient to do when recovering from a CVA to provide the most comprehensive assistance?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discharge to home with scheduled visits from home health care nurses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discharge to home with scheduled visits from a physical therapist.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discharge to home with scheduled visits from an occupational therapist.

questions.rationale: A rehabilitation center with all modalities of support (e.g., physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, simulated home environments) is obviously the best option.

questions.stem: The wife of a husband who has had a CVA asks why he is being treated with insulin since he has no history of diabetes. What is the best response by the nurse as to why hyperglycemia occurs after a stroke?
questions.rationale: Hyperglycemia occurs after a CVA as the body’s response to stress. If left untreated, the hyperglycemia will cause increased brain damage and worsen the outcome of the stroke.

questions.stem: What purposes exist for a stent in the carotid artery of a person with a TIA?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Measure the pressure in the artery.

questions.rationale: The only purpose of a stent is to keep an artery open.

questions.stem: What signs and symptoms characterize expressive aphasia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Speech that sounds normal but makes no sense

choices.label: C
choices.text: Difficulty understanding the written and spoken word

questions.rationale: Expressive aphasia makes it difficult for the patient to initiate speech.

questions.stem: Which transitory symptoms might occur when a patient is diagnosed with a TIA? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: All, except transitory incontinence, are classic symptoms of a TIA. These deficits usually disappear without permanent disability in approximately 24 hours.

questions.stem: How does a lacunar stroke differ from an ischemic CVA? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Causes a great deal of pain

choices.label: E
choices.text: Produces a small amount of neurologic damage

questions.rationale: The lacunar CVA only affects small arteries and produces a small amount of neurologic damage.

questions.stem: Which patients with CVAs are considered candidates for treatment with tPA? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 62-year-old construction worker who had a subdural hematoma 6 months earlier

choices.label: B
choices.text: A 58-year-old executive with a bleeding ulcer

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 44-year-old individual who had a seizure at the onset of a stroke

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 40-year-old individual who is taking warfarin (Coumadin) and has an INR of 2.5

choices.label: E
choices.text: A 19-year-old young adult with leukemia with a platelet count of 200,000

questions.rationale: The criteria for exclusion are a head injury within the last 3 months, a platelet count less than 100,000, active gastrointestinal bleeding, current treatment with an anticoagulant, and a seizure noted at the time of the CVA.

questions.stem: Which home modifications will support rehabilitation for a patient who had a stroke? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provision of a seat in the shower

questions.rationale: A raised commode seat, a seat in the shower, and bathtub rails assist the patient who is recovering from a stroke with self-care. Low chairs are difficult to manage, and scatter rugs pose a hazard for falls.

questions.stem: What causes the 5% of strokes known to occur in persons younger than 45 years of age? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Strokes in younger people are caused by drug abuse, birth control pills, sickle cell anemia, leukemia, atrial fibrillation, and rheumatic fever. Alcohol abuse and hemophilia do not have a causative role in stroke.

questions.stem: Approximately 30% of individuals who experience a TIA have a stroke within ______ years. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: Approximately 30% of individuals who experience a TIA have a stroke within 5 years.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that the spinal cord extends from the brainstem to the level of which vertebra?
questions.rationale: The cord starts at the brainstem and extends to the second lumbar vertebra.

questions.stem: On admission to the emergency department, a patient with a C5 compression fracture can move only his head and has flaccid paralysis of all extremities. The distraught family asks if the paralysis is permanent. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes. In all likelihood, the paralysis is probably permanent.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “No. Significant recovery of function should occur in a few days.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It is too early to tell. When the spinal shock subsides, we will know more.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You should talk to your physician about things of that nature.”

questions.rationale: Spinal shock caused by swelling may last from a few days to months, clouding the issue of the true extent of the injury.

questions.stem: Which assessment would indicate the resolution of spinal shock?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Extension and rigidity in affected limbs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spastic involuntary movements in affected limbs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tingling and burning in affected limbs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Voluntary purposeful movements of affected limbs

questions.rationale: Spastic involuntary movements after a period of flaccid paralysis announce the end of spinal shock.

questions.stem: Which assessment leads the emergency department nurse to suspect that a patient’s spinal cord injury (SCI) is below C4?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ability to make a facial grimace

questions.rationale: The phrenic nerve, which is at C1 to C4, controls the diaphragm and intercostal function for ventilation.

questions.stem: During a neurologic assessment, a nurse asks a patient to dorsiflex the foot against the resistance of the nurse’s hand. The patient is unable to perform this action. Where does this assessment confirm that cord damage has occurred?
questions.rationale: The muscle group that controls the feet is at L5.

questions.stem: What technique should the nurse implement to move the impaired legs of a patient with an SCI to avoid stimulation muscle spasm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Firmly grasping the calf muscle and the thigh muscle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Manipulating the limb by supporting the knee and ankle joints

choices.label: C
choices.text: Holding the foot upright and slowly dragging the limb into position

choices.label: D
choices.text: Requesting assistance to support the calf and thigh

questions.rationale: Undue muscle stimulation can cause spasticity. Using the joint locations to support limbs when repositioning them reduces likelihood of spasticity.

questions.stem: Which technique of opening the airway in the newly admitted patient with an SCI is the most appropriate?
questions.rationale: The jaw thrust does not require spinal movement.

questions.stem: Brown-Séquard syndrome results in which neurologic deficit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bilateral loss of pain sensation below the level of injury

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bilateral loss of temperature and motor function below the level of injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Motor and sensory loss in the upper extremities only

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ipsilateral loss of motor function and contralateral loss of pain sensation and temperature

questions.rationale: Brown-Séquard syndrome is a hemisection of the cord resulting in ipsilateral motor loss and contralateral loss of pain and temperature.

questions.stem: Which level of independence is an appropriate nursing care plan goal for a patient with a C8 transection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Manage a mechanical wheelchair with a joystick.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Manage a mechanical wheelchair with hand control.

questions.rationale: Upper extremity mobility and enhanced hand grip allow the use of an ordinary wheelchair by an individual with a C8 level SCI.

questions.stem: A paraplegic patient excitedly reports seeing his foot move when he was being turned. How is this phenomenon best explained?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Result of hypertonicity of the muscle

questions.rationale: Reflexive action is a movement that does not require communication to the brain via the spinal cord.

questions.stem: After spinal shock has been resolved, an indwelling catheter is removed. What way should the nurse expect this patient to empty the bladder?
questions.rationale: After spinal shock resolves, spasticity of the bladder causes spontaneous emptying.

questions.stem: A distressed family member asks about the purpose of the Gardner-Wells tongs. Which is the most helpful explanation by the nurse regarding the action of Gardner-Wells tongs?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allow the patient to be moved out of bed.

questions.rationale: The Gardner-Wells tongs are secured to the skull to separate and align the cervical vertebrae, but they do not immobilize the head. When the tongs are in place, the patient is bedridden.

questions.stem: What is the major advantage of the halo device over the Gardner-Wells tongs?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allows the patient out of bed

questions.rationale: The halo device and the Gardner-Wells tongs do exactly the same thing in terms of separation and alignment. The only advantage of the halo device is the mobility it allows. Neither traction modality specifically relieves pain.

questions.stem: A patient is receiving methylprednisolone. What purpose should the nurse explain this drug has in treating a patient with an SCI?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increases blood supply to the injured cord

questions.rationale: Methylprednisolone, if given within the first 8 hours of the injury, can significantly reduce cellular damage to the cord.

questions.stem: A patient with an SCI begins to have seizures, and the blood pressure (BP) rises rapidly to 210/160 mm Hg. Which is the third indicator of the syndrome of autonomic dysreflexia?
questions.rationale: Bradycardia, hypertension, and seizure are the three signs of autonomic dysreflexia.

questions.stem: What should be the immediate intervention when a nurse recognizes autonomic dysreflexia in the patient with an SCI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Flex the patient’s legs using the knee gatch of the bed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cool the patient with alcohol solution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Raise the head of the bed to at least 45 degrees.

questions.rationale: Raising the head of the bed reduces the BP. Flexed legs, cooling, and oxygen will not alleviate the syndrome.

questions.stem: Which intervention by a nurse is effective in the prevention of autonomic dysreflexia in the patient with an SCI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure patency of the urinary catheter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give warm baths to the patient to stimulate vasodilation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep lighting at a minimum to reduce stimulation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Offer the patient four or five small meals daily.

questions.rationale: A distended bladder, constipation, and sudden jarring can all set off autonomic dysreflexia. Vagal stimulation retards vasodilation. The number and size of meals have no effect on preventing this syndrome.

questions.stem: The family members of a patient with an SCI, who is in the rehabilitation phase, want to take the patient outdoors for a visit. It is 90 F outside and very humid. What should the nurse suggest?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Do not go outside at all but remain in the hospital.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take a spray bottle to spray water to cool the patient by evaporation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take a light sweater to insulate the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have the patient drink at least 32 oz of water during the outing.

questions.rationale: Water will evaporate and cool the patient, similar to perspiration.

questions.stem: A nurse notes that no urinary output has occurred in a patient who underwent a laminectomy 2 hours earlier. What action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turn the patient to the right side.

questions.rationale: The nurse should continue to monitor the patient for urine output. Two hours is too soon to expect a continent patient to void. Informing the charge nurse and catheterization are not necessary. Turning this patient to the side is contraindicated.

questions.stem: Which statement made by a male patient with an SCI could be assessed as a positive adaptation to sexual dysfunction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I know I will never have a sexual relationship again.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I need some suggestions as to how to direct my sexual energy into gardening or painting. or just anything.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Can you arrange an appointment with a sex counselor so I can begin to examine alternative methods of sexual activity?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I think that after a while I will be able to have sexual relationships just like I had before my accident.”

questions.rationale: Seeking help from a counselor indicates an acceptance of learning alternative techniques. Remarks eliminating all possibilities of a sexual relationship are defeatist remarks and are not positive. However, a patient should realize that his or her sexual relationships will alter as a result of the SCI.

questions.stem: What should a nurse emphasize regarding the rehabilitation of the patient with an SCI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rehabilitation is usually achieved within a few months after stabilization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rehabilitation will return the patient with an SCI to the preaccident functional level.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rehabilitation focuses on adjustments necessary to reenter society and the workplace.

questions.rationale: The goals of rehabilitation are modification of lifestyle, as well as expectations and adjustments, necessary to attain the highest level of independence possible.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include in a patient’s plan of care when considering interventions for the outcome of prevention of contractures in a patient with an SCI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply cold wraps to the limbs twice a day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform full ROM exercises every 2 hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use significant tactile stimuli each shift.

questions.rationale: Applying splints will reduce contractures. Cold application, agitation of the limb with ROM exercises too frequently, and tactile stimuli increase spasticity.

questions.stem: The family of a patient with an SCI is concerned with the lack of bowel function 2 days after the injury. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Because of his injury, he will always need to have enemas for bowel evacuation.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Medical management is delaying bowel action because it places pressure on the injury.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Bowel function should return in approximately 3 days after the accident.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “We’ll just have to wait and see if bowel action returns this week.”

questions.rationale: Bowel action usually returns with peristalsis on the third day after the accident. The bowel responds to dilation from the content in the bowel and moves without voluntary action from the patient.

questions.stem: A home health nurse encourages the family of a patient with an SCI to use the assisted cough technique. What does this technique require the caregiver to do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assist the patient to inhale a bronchodilator spray and then cough.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Forcefully press on patient’s back below the rib cage while the patient is in the prone position.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assist the patient to lean forward, breathe deep, and then cough.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply pressure to diaphragm as the patient coughs.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Slap the patient on upper back while the patient is in the prone position.

questions.rationale: To assist the patient with an SCI to cough, the caregiver applies pressure on the diaphragm as the patient attempts to cough after having taken a deep breath.

questions.stem: What has occurred in the past 10 years to enhance rehabilitation of individuals with SCIs? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: New assistive aids, the development of decentralized trauma centers, and the rapid transport of victims have all increased the potential for rehabilitation. Rehabilitation personnel and health insurance are not new.

questions.stem: What changes occur with the intervertebral disks in older adults that increase the risk of injury? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Age affects the water content in intervertebral disks, which makes them less able to absorb shock. Herniation and swelling can occur at any age. Disks do not fill with calcium.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a despondent young female patient with an SCI at C5. The patient verbalizes concern regarding sexual dysfunction. What should the nurse assure this patient she can still experience? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Intercourse, normal menses, and childbirth are all possible for a woman with a C5 lesion, but no vaginal sensation occurs. Orgasm is possible but not vaginally stimulated.

questions.stem: What portion of the internal nose traps particles and kills bacteria?
questions.rationale: The mucous membrane traps particles and bacteria that are inhaled; then an enzyme in the mucus destroys them. The cilia then sweep the particles into the throat to be swallowed.

questions.stem: A patient comes into the clinic complaining of a runny nose and facial pain. What should the nurse’s initial assessment include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assessment for nasal drainage and sinus tenderness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Palpation of the frontal and maxillary sinuses and tonsillar inspection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turbinate assessment and assessment for patency of the nares

questions.rationale: The assessment of the characteristics of the nasal drainage and location of the facial pain would be the first evaluation for sinusitis.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old patient tells the nurse that her sense of smell is not as acute as before, her nose is drier, and she occasionally gets a nosebleed. What should the nurse suspect?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allergies that are causing her symptoms

questions.rationale: These options describe normal age-related changes. A suggestion that would make the patient more comfortable would be to use a humidifier to keep the mucous membranes moist.

questions.stem: What does age-related relaxation of the esophageal sphincter in a 70-year-old patient cause?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Burning in the throat when lying down

questions.rationale: A common age-related change in the throat is a weakened esophageal sphincter. This allows gastric contents to flow back up into the throat and irritate the larynx. Elevating the head of the bed is a common treatment.

questions.stem: When a patient with sleep apnea says, “I’m not wearing that silly mask. I look like something out of Star Wars,” what should the nurse remind the patient about the function of the mask?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sounds an alarm when the oxygen concentration drops

choices.label: D
choices.text: Uses positive pressure to keep the airway open
