questions.rationale: The sleep apnea mask, through positive pressure, keeps the airway open during sleep.

questions.stem: What should patient education for a patient being given nose drops for the first time include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asking the patient to sit down and tip her head to the side to allow for a better angle for the instillation of the drops

choices.label: B
choices.text: Holding the dropper against the side of the nose so that all the medication flows into the nares

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asking the patient to return any unused medication to the bottle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tipping the head back and holding the dropper over the nostril and then telling the patient to keep her head back for a few minutes

questions.rationale: Appropriate instillation of nose drops requires that the head be tipped back and the bottle not touch the nose.

questions.stem: A patient comes into the clinic complaining of waking up with a dry mouth and nose and asks if the dryness has caused the colds she has had in the past few months. What is the most appropriate suggestion for the nurse to suggest?
questions.rationale: A humidifier would be helpful in keeping the nasal mucous membranes moist, which can decrease nasal infections.

questions.stem: Which assessment should indicate the necessity for a nurse to suction a patient with a tracheostomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Becomes restless and has increases in vital signs

questions.rationale: The patient signals the need for suctioning by increased restlessness and an increase in vital signs. Peak airway pressures increase when suctioning is necessary. Frothy mucus is an expectation.

questions.stem: Which position is the most appropriate for a patient returning from surgery with a nasal pack and mustache dressing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Side-lying position to prevent aspiration of drainage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Semi-Fowler position and apply a warm compress to reduce pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: High Fowler position and apply a cold dressing to reduce swelling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sims position and apply a cold dressing to facilitate drainage and reduce swelling

questions.rationale: Patients who have a nasal pack should be placed in semi- or high Fowler position with a cold dressing. The position and cold dressing will reduce swelling. Any side-lying position makes it more difficult for the patient to breathe with a nasal pack in place.

questions.stem: What intervention should a nurse implement when providing tracheostomy care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wash and rinse the inner cannula in tap water and then dry it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use a sterile solution of normal saline or other solution to wash the inner cannula and then rinse with sterile water.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Clean the area around the stoma with tap water and a gentle soap.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remove the inner cannula, wash both hands with a bactericidal soap, and then don sterile gloves to clean the inner cannula.

questions.rationale: The recommendation is to use a sterile technique for tracheotomy care.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses wheezes in a patient with asthma. What should the nurse know is the cause of wheezes?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use of accessory muscles of respiration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Movement of air through narrowed airways

questions.rationale: Wheezes are adventitious sounds made by air passing through narrowed passages.

questions.stem: The patient has had anterior nasal packing placed for severe epistaxis. The nurse notes that he is swallowing frequently. What should a nurse suspect?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient’s saliva production is excessive.

questions.rationale: Frequent swallowing after nasal surgery is a sign of bleeding.

questions.stem: A patient asks the nurse how air goes from the nose to the lung. The nurse draws the route according to which sequence?
questions.rationale: The route of inspired air is pharynx, trachea, bronchi, and alveoli.

questions.stem: A nurse charts that a patient has had periods of tachypnea during the night. What does this means in regard to the respiration rate?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gradually deepening, then shallow, and then periods of apnea

questions.rationale: Tachypnea is a respiration rate above 20 breaths/min. Option a describes bradypnea, option b describes Biot respirations, and option c describes Cheyne-Stokes respirations.

questions.stem: A 90-year-old patient complains to the nurse of shortness of breath after walking up a flight of stairs. What age-related change should the nurse explain results in this problem?
questions.rationale: Enlarged bronchioles require the inspiration of greater amounts of air. Other age-related changes make increased inspiration difficult.

questions.stem: What should the nurse exclude when documenting the findings in the functional assessment portion of the nursing assessment for a patient with a respiratory disorder?
questions.rationale: Previous respiratory disorders are assessed in the medical history portion of the assessment.

questions.stem: To auscultate breath sounds in the right middle lobe from the anterior aspect, the nurse should place the diaphragm of the stethoscope at which intercostal space?
questions.rationale: The fifth intercostal space is the optimal position for auscultating the right middle lobe.

questions.stem: What should the nurse suspect regarding the bronchus when auscultating coarse crackles in the lower right lobe?
questions.rationale: Coarse crackles are indicative of fluid in the bronchi. Many times these sounds can be cleared by coughing.

questions.stem: A worried patient asks the nurse to explain the advantage of a fluoroscopy. What is the nurse’s best response regarding fluoroscopy?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Helps the physician evaluate ventilation-perfusion ratio

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allows the physician to take tissue samples

choices.label: D
choices.text: Facilitates the removal of fluid from the bronchi

questions.rationale: A fluoroscopy allows the visualization of both lungs while the patient is in the process of ventilation.

questions.stem: Which nursing intervention is inappropriate in the immediate post-procedure care of a patient who has had a fiberoptic bronchoscopy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place the patient in a semi-Fowler position.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Offer fluids to assess swallowing ability.

questions.rationale: Patients are placed on nothing by mouth diet until the gag reflex returns.

questions.stem: What is the importance of the nurse closely monitoring bilateral breath sounds and chest movement after a thoracentesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fluid may quickly accumulate as a result of inflammation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The lung may have been punctured during the procedure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Asthma may result after the procedure.

questions.rationale: A possibility exists that the lung could have been punctured during the procedure. Bronchospasm, fluid collection, and asthma are not concerns related to a thoracentesis.

questions.stem: Which nursing assessment indicates a positive reading of a tuberculin (TB) skin test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1 day after injection with a 10-mm area of redness and swelling

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2 days after injection with a 5-mm area of redness and swelling

choices.label: C
choices.text: 4 days after injection with a 3-mm area of redness and swelling

choices.label: D
choices.text: 5 days after injection with a 2-mm area of redness and swelling

questions.rationale: A positive reading of a TB skin test is an area of redness and swelling of 5 mm or larger 24 to 48 hours after injection.

questions.stem: A nurse performs an Allen test before performing the arterial stick for an arterial blood gas. What does this test assess?
questions.rationale: The perfusion of the hand by the radial and ulnar arteries is assessed because the puncture of the radial artery might cause it to occlude.

questions.stem: A patient who is severely dyspneic and cyanotic enters the emergency department. What rate should a nurse administer oxygen to the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2 L to preserve the hypoxic drive

choices.label: D
choices.text: 10 L, humidified aerosol, to dilate the bronchi

questions.rationale: Low-dose oxygen is a safe initial dose to ensure that the hypoxic drive be preserved, especially for a patient whose history is unknown.

questions.stem: Which assessment indicates to the nurse that the chest tube in a water seal drainage device is working correctly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Constant bubbling in the suction control chamber

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decrease of accumulation in the drainage chamber

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fluctuation of the column of water in the water seal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Constant bubbling in the water seal chamber

questions.rationale: The fluctuation of the level in the water seal indicates patency of the tubes with the reinflating lung. Constant bubbling in the wet suction control is normal. Constant bubbling in the water seal indicates an air leak. Decreasing drainage is normal.

questions.stem: Which assessment by the nurse at the bedside of a patient with a chest tube attached to a water seal drainage device should require intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dependent loops in the chest tube

choices.label: C
choices.text: Changing level of water in the water seal chamber

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased level of drainage to 20 mL in 8 hours

questions.rationale: Dependent loops in the chest tube can collect drainage and occlude the system.

questions.stem: A home health nurse who is caring for an 88-year-old patient with severe hypertension in addition to a respiratory problem notices several drugs on the bedside table. Which medication should the nurse suggest the patient avoid?
questions.rationale: Decongestants increase the blood pressure.

questions.stem: How should a nurse position a patient who had a left pneumonectomy in the morning in an effort to enhance gas exchange?

choices.label: D
choices.text: In a flat position with a small pillow

questions.rationale: Elevation of the head helps gas exchange in the patient with a new pneumonectomy. A complete side-lying position on the unaffected side may cause mediastinal shift.

questions.stem: How should a nurse position a patient during a thoracentesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Side-lying with bed in a Trendelenburg position

choices.label: B
choices.text: High Fowler position with feet elevated

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sitting on the side of the bed bent over bedside table

questions.rationale: The patient sits on the side of the bed and leans the upper torso over the bedside table with the head resting on folded arms or pillows. If the patient is unable to sit up, then a side-lying position with the head of the bed elevated 30 degrees may be used.

questions.stem: How does the ventilator function of positive end-expiratory pressure assist the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keeps pressure in the lungs after expiration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allows the patient to control expiratory pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Delivers an inhalant medication under positive pressure

questions.rationale: The positive end-expiratory pressure setting keeps the pressure in the lungs above the atmospheric pressure, which prevents atelectasis.

questions.stem: The patient has had anterior nasal packing placed for severe epistaxis. The nurse notes that he is swallowing frequently. What should a nurse suspect?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient’s saliva production is excessive.

questions.rationale: Frequent swallowing after nasal surgery is a sign of bleeding.

questions.stem: What should the postoperative care of a patient who has had nasal surgery include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Changing the nasal packing when saturated

choices.label: B
choices.text: Placing the patient in a semi-Fowler position without a pillow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Providing humidification for dry mucous membranes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Assessing the back of the throat for bleeding

questions.rationale: Only the physician removes the nasal packing. The nurse may change the mustache dressing, however.

questions.stem: How should a nurse explain that the breathing pattern has been altered when a patient complains of tachypnea? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased pH levels stimulate chemoreceptors in the aorta and carotid arteries, which stimulates the phrenic nerve.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased oxygen level signals the phrenic nerve to alter the respiration rate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Muscles of respiration respond to the stimulus.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The brain has become hypoxic and causes an alteration in the respiration rate.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Deflated lung tissue results in an altered respiration rate.

questions.rationale: A decreased oxygen level stimulates the phrenic nerve to signal the muscles of respiration to do the work of breathing. A decreasing pH level is the stimulus to the chemoreceptors. Neither the brain nor the lungs signal for tachypnea.

questions.stem: What assessment findings would indicate respiratory dysfunction when examining a patient with respiratory difficulty? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Clues to respiratory dysfunction are a distended abdomen, cyanotic nail beds, and clubbed fingers from inadequate oxygenation.

questions.stem: Which instructions should a nurse provide to a patient just before a scheduled spirometry test? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid smoking 4 to 6 hours before test.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Do not use bronchodilator medications for at least 4 hours.

questions.rationale: Patients should not smoke, use bronchodilators, or exercise just before the test. Normal-sized meals and drinking fluids do not adversely affect the test.

questions.stem: A nurse computes the number of “pack-years” of a 24-year-old man who has smoked packs of cigarettes every day since he was 15 years old. This patient has _____ pack-years. (Use only numeric characters. If the answer is not a whole number, use decimals instead of fractions.)
questions.rationale: Pack-years are calculated by multiplying the number of years of smoking by the number of packs smoked each day. A 24-year-old patient who has smoked since he was 15 years of age = 9 years multiplied by 1.5 = 13.5.

questions.stem: What should a nurse do when taking a specimen for a throat culture? _____ (Place the appropriate actions in the correct sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depress the tongue with a tongue blade.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the applicator in a culture tube.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient to tilt the head back.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Swab the back of the throat and tonsils.

questions.rationale: When collecting a specimen for throat culture the patient is asked to tilt the head back, the tongue is depressed with tongue blade, the back of the throat and tonsils are swabbed, and an applicator is placed in a culture tube.

questions.stem: What instructions should a nurse give to a patient when teaching deep breathing and coughing techniques? _____ (Place the options in the appropriate sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place the hand on the abdomen to check the rise and fall.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhale through the nose, pause 1 to 3 seconds, and then exhale through the mouth.

questions.rationale: The exercise is performed in a sequence to ensure open bronchioles and a good deep cough.

questions.stem: A patient complains of morning headaches, a feeling of fullness in her head, and a pain similar to that of a toothache under her eye. What should the nurse recognize that these symptoms indicate?
questions.rationale: Sinusitis has the classic signs of headache, sense of fullness in the head, and a sensitive area over the sinuses.

questions.stem: A patient complains that he wants an antibiotic medication for his cold. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Antibiotics are not effective with viral infections.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You will get better faster without the antibiotics.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You might try echinacea or vitamin C.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “A cold is not that serious. Try forcing fluids.”

questions.rationale: Antibiotics are not appropriate with colds because colds are caused by viruses. Overuse of antibiotics can promote resistant strains of bacteria to develop.

questions.stem: What should the initial action of a nurse be when providing first aid to a person with spontaneous epistaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply direct pressure for 3 to 5 minutes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Have the person sit down and lean forward.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have the person lie down and apply an ice pack.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have the person clear the nasal passages by blowing the nose.

questions.rationale: The first action is to sit down and lean forward. Applying pressure just below the nose will also help.

questions.stem: What is true regarding bacterial pharyngitis that is untrue for viral pharyngitis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presents a normal complete blood count (CBC)

questions.rationale: Bacterial pharyngitis has an abrupt onset, an elevated white count on the CBC, and a positive culture and can lead to glomerulonephritis, rheumatic fever, and mastoiditis.

questions.stem: Which is the best candidate for a tonsillectomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 52-year-old patient with a hearing deficit related to otitis media from tonsillitis

choices.label: B
choices.text: A 23-year-old patient with a peritonsillar abscess

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 34-year-old patient with enlarged tonsils and adenoids

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 15-year-old patient with one bout of tonsillitis in the previous 12 months

questions.rationale: The patient with the peritonsillar abscess is the most likely candidate. The hearing deficit in a middle-aged person would need more investigation before surgery. Enlarged tonsils and adenoids without a respiratory obstruction does not qualify as a need for surgery; only one episode of tonsillitis in a year does not qualify.

questions.stem: What is best for a nurse to offer when encouraging a new patient after a tonsillectomy to increase fluids?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tap water sipped through a straw

questions.rationale: Flavored popsicles provide fluid and cold applications to the surgical area. Citrus juices, the use of a straw, and ice cubes have the potential to injure the operative site.

questions.stem: What are the most common causes of laryngitis?
questions.rationale: Upper respiratory infections and voice strain are the most common causes of laryngitis.

questions.stem: What is the most significant topic for a nurse to include in a teaching plan for a patient with frequent episodes of laryngitis?
questions.rationale: Patients with laryngitis are advised to rest their voices.

questions.stem: A patient who has cancer of the larynx has been told that he needs a total laryngectomy. What action should this nurse consider to help the patient cope with the loss of his voice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offer to have a volunteer from a local laryngectomy organization visit the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Explain in detail the available vocalization aids and techniques.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Explain to the patient what will happen directly after the surgery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the hospital chaplain of the patient’s needs.

questions.rationale: Offering to request a volunteer from the laryngectomy organization is a recommended implementation to reduce the stress of losing the ability to speak. You should consult the patient before making the referral.

questions.stem: Which nursing concern takes priority in the care of a patient after a laryngectomy?
questions.rationale: Establishing a communication system with the patient who has undergone a laryngectomy is a primary concern.

questions.stem: A patient who had a laryngectomy 3 months earlier returns to the physician’s office with the complaint of increasing dyspnea. Which common postlaryngectomy complication should the nurse recognize this complaint as indicating?
questions.rationale: Tracheal stenosis causes the otherwise healthy recovering patient who has undergone a laryngectomy to experience increased dyspnea.

questions.stem: What is one major postoperative difficulty for a patient having a supraglottic laryngectomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Teaching the patient to use an assistive device to speak

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coughing without letting food escape through the tracheostomy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Taking care of the tracheostomy, because the patient will always have to have one

choices.label: D
choices.text: Teaching the patient to swallow without aspiration

questions.rationale: The patient who has had a supraglottic laryngectomy may never be able to swallow correctly, which could easily lead to aspiration pneumonia.

questions.stem: Which actions should the nurse include in a care plan to effectively assist the patient with a total laryngectomy to maintain airway clearance? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Placing the patient in a semi-Fowler position

questions.rationale: Providing a communication method has high priority, but it is not related to airway clearance.

questions.stem: A nurse reminds a patient, who is to have a partial laryngectomy, that the temporary tracheostomy that he will have after the original surgery will be closed within ______ days. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: The temporary tracheostomy, which is done as part of the partial laryngectomy surgery, is usually closed 5 days after the original surgery.

questions.stem: A patient with acute bronchitis is being discharged with a prescription for an antimicrobial medication to be taken for the next 14 days. What should the nurse stress when providing discharge teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take the drug on an empty stomach before meals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete the entire course as prescribed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maintain a thorough oral hygiene regimen.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Maintain a daily fluid intake of 500 mL.

questions.rationale: The entire course of the prescription should be taken to destroy the pathogen completely; otherwise, the pathogen may become resistant to the drug.

questions.stem: Which group of patients should a nurse advise to have a vaccination with conjugated pneumococcal?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Parents of children younger than 24 months

questions.rationale: The conjugated product is especially designed for young children. Unconjugated vaccine is recommended for older adults and those with cardiovascular disorders.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse implement to reduce the risk of aspiration in a patient receiving continuous enteral feedings at a rate of 70 mL/hr?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the position of the tube during every shift.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Notify the charge nurse or physician about a residual volume of 20 mL.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevate the patient’s head during and for 10 minutes after feeding.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Position the patient on the left side after the feeding.

questions.rationale: A residual of more than 20% of the hourly rate should be reported so that the rate can be reduced (70 mL multiplied by 0.20 = 14).

questions.stem: What symptoms should a nurse expect to see in a patient with hypoxemia?
questions.rationale: The universal symptoms of hypoxemia, regardless of cause, are restlessness, tachycardia, and tachypnea.

questions.stem: A patient comes to the emergency department with a sucking chest wound. Which type of dressing should the nurse apply to begin the process of lung reinflation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Petroleum dressing covered with an airtight bandage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pillow weighted down with a sandbag

choices.label: D
choices.text: Air-occlusive dressing taped on three sides (vented dressing)

questions.rationale: The vented dressing occludes air from entering but allows air to escape, avoiding a tension pneumothorax and mediastinal shift.

questions.stem: What should a nurse prepare when assessing paradoxical movement in a patient with a flail chest who has significant dyspnea?
questions.rationale: A patient with an unstable chest usually requires intubation and mechanical ventilation.

questions.stem: Which intervention would be inappropriate for decreasing the risk of further emboli in a patient with a pulmonary embolism?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Performing passive range-of-motion exercises, especially of the lower limbs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Placing pillows under the knees to elevate the legs

questions.rationale: Nothing should be placed under the knees; doing so might impair circulation.

questions.stem: Assessment of 24-year-old driver after an automobile accident, who is complaining of right-sided chest pain and is dyspneic, reveals the following: •        Respirations: 26 breaths/min •        Significant pain on inspiration •        Hand is pressed to the rib area; large bruise is forming on the right chest •        Blood pressure: 182/98 mm Hg Based on these assessments, the nurse suspects _____ ribs.
questions.rationale: The placement of the bruise and the pain on inspiration are the main clues to the rib fracture.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with asthma and assesses signs and symptoms of inadequate oxygenation. Which intervention is the most appropriate?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Position the patient flat in bed with small pillow.

questions.rationale: Increasing fluid intake thins the mucus in the lungs, making it easier to cough up, which helps clear the bronchioles and decrease ventilation-perfusion mismatch. Increasing O2 is not helpful if no air pathway exists to the alveoli. Increasing O2 to 8 L is excessive.

questions.stem: What is a characteristic of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease that places a patient at risk for poor nutrition?
questions.rationale: Respiratory effort interferes with swallowing, depletes energy, and increases caloric needs.

questions.stem: Which nursing intervention enhances the nutritional status of a patient with COPD?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assist the patient to exercise before meals.

questions.rationale: Small meals are not as tiring for the patient and are more appealing.

questions.stem: Which walking program would be the most effective for the nurse to recommend as part of a progressive walking program for an obese patient with COPD?
questions.rationale: Walking for as little as 10 to 15 minutes a day and progressing up to 45 minutes a day has proven beneficial for persons with COPD because it improves oxygenation and helps with weight loss.

questions.stem: What is the result of status asthmaticus that is not corrected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pneumothorax, severe hypoxemia, and respiratory arrest

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertension, cerebrovascular accident (CVA), and cardiac arrest

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lung abscess, cor pulmonale, and respiratory failure

questions.rationale: Status asthmaticus, because of severe bronchospasms, can result in hypoxemia, which could lead to pneumothorax and arrest.

questions.stem: What should a nurse focus on when assessing for major sources of infection in a patient with COPD?
questions.rationale: Retained static secretions in the lungs are major sources of bacterial infiltration and infection.

questions.stem: A young patient with acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) reports debilitating night sweats. Why should the home health nurse suggest that the patient visit the clinic?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To get a prescription for antibiotics

questions.rationale: The symptoms of TB are low-grade fever, night sweats, and cough. Patients with AIDS and anyone who is immunosuppressed are extremely prone to TB and should be carefully monitored for the development of the disease.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for an 80-year-old patient with COPD and suspects right-sided heart failure after assessing and recording the data. What should decrease with right-sided heart failure?
questions.rationale: The decreasing urine output is one of the signs. The fluid, instead of being excreted as urine, is trapped in the tissues as edema. Blood pressure, respirations, and heart rate will increase with right-sided heart failure.

questions.stem: A patient with TB asks the nurse how long he will have to take his TB medications. What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Depending on the drug, it may be as long as 2 years.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “TB drugs are usually taken throughout the life span.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “People frequently ask that question. It depends on many things.”

questions.rationale: Some TB drugs are continued over the course of several years.

questions.stem: A patient with TB asks how to protect family members from the disease. Which discharge instruction given by the nurse is most informative?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Your family will need to take treatments to prevent infection.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You will need to wear a mask at home to protect your family members.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You should always cover your mouth and nose if coughing or sneezing.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You should avoid intimate contact with everyone.”

questions.rationale: Covering the mouth and nose to prevent droplet spread and carefully disposing of tissues are two significant ways to control the spread of infection. Masks or isolation is not necessary because before discharge, the patient will have been stabilized on an anti-TB medication.

questions.stem: A nurse is providing education to a patient taking rifampin as a result of an exposure to TB. What side effect of this drug should the nurse include?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Illness if aged cheese or smoked meats are consumed

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral contraceptive pills to become ineffective

questions.rationale: Rifampin will color body fluids red-orange and will result in stained clothing and soft contact lenses.

questions.stem: A patient with asthma asks the purpose of learning how to use a peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) device. What is the nurse’s best response regarding PEFR?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dilates the bronchi to relieve dyspnea

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measures expired air to evaluate ventilation

questions.rationale: The PEFR measures expired air. When the PEFR rate decreases 20% below the baseline, adjustments are usually made in the medications.

questions.stem: A nurse is assigned to care for a patient with the diagnosis of centriacinar (centrilobar) emphysema. What is a characteristic of this type of emphysema?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No significant smoking history in the patient

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enlarged and broken down bronchioles with intact alveoli

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deficiency of the enzyme inhibitor alpha1-antitrypsin.

questions.rationale: This type of emphysema is characterized by a long smoking history, enlarged and broken down bronchioles, and hypoelastic bronchi.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old patient with cystic fibrosis (CF) tells the home health nurse that he wants to take a nice vacation. What is the best suggestion for the nurse to make?
questions.rationale: New York is the best choice because individuals with CF sweat profusely and lose many salts, leading to significant electrolyte imbalance. Those with CF also have impaired respiration and should avoid heat (Greece in July, Mexico in August) and higher altitudes (Colorado at any time).

questions.stem: Which assessment made by a nurse indicates that respiratory arrest is imminent in a patient with asthma?
questions.rationale: An absence of wheezing indicates a diminished ventilation effort.

questions.stem: A patient with COPD is observed to have extreme shortness of breath when ambulating. Which nursing intervention is most inappropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bunch all nursing activities and treatments close together.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schedule rest periods during the day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assist the patient only when needed to encourage independence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide daily ambulation to build tolerance.

questions.rationale: Bunching nursing activities is tiring to the patient with COPD. Assisting only when needed saves patient energy, as well as enhancing independence. Activities should be spread out to allow for uninterrupted rest periods. Progressive ambulation is an acceptable way to build tolerance.

questions.stem: A nurse recognizes that a patient diagnosed with COPD has a rising level of partial pressure of carbon dioxide (CO2) in arterial blood (PaCO2). How should the nurse interpret this assessment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: More arterial O2 is available than is needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The ventilation-perfusion ratio is becoming balanced.

questions.rationale: A rising PaCO2 level is acidic in nature and causes respiratory acidosis.

questions.stem: A patient with COPD asks a nurse if nicotine patches are very effective for smoking cessation. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Yes. The success rate is between 50% and 60%.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “No. Prescriptions such as Wellbutrin are 90% effective.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Yes. Individual success has been obtained with combination of patches and gum.”

questions.rationale: The patches have a lower than 25% success rate. Smoking addiction is too strong to be overcome by medication or gum without a very unusual commitment from the patient. Successful smoking cessation is measured by 1 year of no smoking.

questions.stem: A patient with cystic fibrosis (CF) furiously refuses any more manual chest physiotherapeutic treatment. Which alternative is appropriate for the nurse to suggest?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase ambulation to 1 to 2 hours a day

choices.label: C
choices.text: Steam inhalator several times a day

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drink 3 quarts of fluid per day

questions.rationale: A flutter mucus clearance device is a handheld vibrating tool that helps loosen and evacuate secretions in the lung.

questions.stem: What should a nurse expect when assessing the CBC results of a patient with chronic bronchitis?
questions.rationale: Patients with chronic bronchitis show a large increase of RBCs with an attendant higher hemoglobin level because they must produce more RBCs for the transport of O2. Frequently, the WBCs are elevated because of the chronic inflammation. Decreased levels of platelets and increased eosinophils are indicative of pathologic characteristics other than bronchitis.

questions.stem: A patient with COPD delightedly tells the nurse that he has quit smoking and is using chewing tobacco. What is the most appropriate nursing intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Congratulate him on his quitting smoking.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Warn him of the dangers of oral cancer.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Suggest that he add nicotine patches in addition to the chewing tobacco.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Point out that he is still addicted and is using tobacco.

questions.rationale: Smokeless tobacco has adverse effects, including oral cancer.

questions.stem: A newly diagnosed patient with non–small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC) is anxious about upcoming surgery. Which intervention by the nurse would be most helpful?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Support the patient in preparation for surgery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Educate the patient regarding the high survival rate with this type of carcinoma.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assure the patient that chemotherapy and radiation can be used in this sort of cancer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Refer the patient to the American Cancer Society for postdischarge follow-up.

questions.rationale: Surgery is the treatment of choice of NSCLC carcinomas. The survival rate is only approximately 14%. Although referral may be in the long-range plan, the patient’s need is immediate for information that is within the scope of nursing.

questions.stem: A nurse documents and reports the presence of foul, bulky stool in a patient with cystic fibrosis (CF). What does this finding indicate about the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Is being adequately maintained on the present dose of pancreatic enzyme

choices.label: C
choices.text: Has diarrhea related to excess mucus in the bowel

questions.rationale: Foul, bulky stools are the result of inadequately digested food if oral pancreatic enzymes are inadequate.

questions.stem: What should a patient who had the BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin) vaccine 2 years ago anticipate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: False-positive result from TB skin tests

choices.label: B
choices.text: Being at risk for contracting TB

choices.label: C
choices.text: 3-week prophylactic protocol of rifampin or isoniazid (isonicotinic acid hydrazide [INH])

questions.rationale: Inoculation with BCG causes a false-positive result on TB skin tests that may be administered afterward. BCG is not used very much in the United States, but it is administered in most other countries.

questions.stem: What nursing action should be implemented to help combat anorexia in a patient with COPD?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recommend a large meal in the middle of the day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Suggest taking only cold liquid nutritional drinks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gently exercise for 10 minutes before a meal.

questions.rationale: Oral hygiene freshens the mouth and removes unpleasant tastes from medications or coughed-up secretions.

questions.stem: A nurse uses a picture to demonstrate the bullae and blebs associated with emphysema. How do blebs differ from bullae? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are between the alveolar spaces in the lungs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are in the lung parenchyma.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They can rupture, causing the lungs to collapse.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are responsible for diaphragm flattening.

questions.rationale: Blebs are growths inside the organ of the lung that enlarge and rupture, causing lung collapse. Bullae are the lesions between the alveolar spaces. Neither are the cause of diaphragm flattening nor are they precancerous.

questions.stem: A nurse cautions a group of individuals with COPD that using O2 at levels greater than 1 to 3 L/min can cause the loss of their _____.
questions.rationale: The hypoxic drive is the stimulus of CO2 in the system that drives respiration. If the CO2 level is reduced by excessive administration of O2, then the patient will cease to breathe.

questions.stem: A nurse explains to a family how the asthma attack progresses by using a progressive list of pathologic events. _____ (Place the options in the correct sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)
questions.rationale: After the allergen has triggered the inflammatory response, bronchoconstriction occurs, which leads to the formation of mucous plugs in the bronchioles that block O2 from entering the alveoli, causing a ventilation-perfusion mismatch and resulting in hypoxemia and hyperventilation.

questions.stem: Which is considered an approximate normal hematocrit value?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The same as the hemoglobin value

choices.label: C
choices.text: Four times lower than the red blood cell count

choices.label: D
choices.text: Same as the red blood cell count

questions.rationale: Hematocrit is approximately three times the hemoglobin value.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient receiving a transfusion and assesses that the patient is wheezing and is complaining of back pain. What nursing action should take place after stopping the transfusion?
questions.rationale: The charge nurse should be notified immediately after the transfusion is stopped. The charge nurse will notify the physician and the laboratory or blood bank. The head of the bed should be raised to aid in respiration, and oxygen should be administered in high doses. The blood tubing and bag should not be discarded because the blood bank will want it to check the accuracy of the typing.

questions.stem: A nurse is taking the history of a patient who has come in for evaluation of large areas of purpura on her limbs. The patient reports using alternative therapy for her menopausal symptoms. What alternative therapy is most likely responsible for the patient’s symptoms?
questions.rationale: Black cohosh interferes with blood clotting.

questions.stem: To what level should the platelet count rise when the patient with a platelet count of 20,000/mm3 receives 1 unit of platelets?
questions.rationale: Platelet transfusions are given when the platelet count falls below 20,000/mm3. One unit is expected to raise the count by 5000 to 10,000/mm3.

questions.stem: When a nurse is preparing to give ferrous sulfate (Feosol) to a home health care patient, what is the most appropriate nursing action to implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mix the drug with a high-protein milkshake.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give it undiluted with a small snack.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mix it with coffee or cola to disguise the bitter taste.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dilute it and offer through a straw and a few crackers.

questions.rationale: Patients should avoid taking iron with milk or caffeine because both inhibit drug absorption. The liquid form of the drug is offered with food in a diluted form through a straw to prevent staining the teeth.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an initial assessment on a new patient being seen in the hospital clinic. The presentation of this female patient includes vague symptoms of tiredness and large areas of ecchymosis. Which question is most important for the nurse to ask?
questions.rationale: Bleeding gums are indicative of general bleeding tendencies. Sleep and frequency of menstrual periods are not significant, but the heaviness of the period is significant. History can reveal information pertinent to assisting the physician in making a diagnosis.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient 20 minutes after a bone marrow biopsy. Which statement by the patient is cause for the most concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “There is fresh blood on my dressing.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “My hip feels bruised where they stuck the needle.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I had a sharp pain in my leg when they pulled the needle out.”

questions.rationale: Fresh blood on the pressure dressing 20 minutes after the aspiration needs to be addressed. Usually, redressing with a pressure dressing and an ice pack is sufficient. Feelings of bruising and pain on extraction are to be expected. Thirst is of no clinical significance.

questions.stem: At 1000 a nurse receives 2 units of blood for a patient to be transfused. What is the most appropriate nursing action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Set up 1 unit for the infusion to start by 1030 and send the other unit back until the first one has infused.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Set up both units to infuse at the same time and to start at 1100.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Set up one unit for infusion and place the other in the refrigerator for the later infusion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Send both units back and ask for a reissue of 1 unit only.

questions.rationale: Blood must be started within 30 minutes of its receipt after it has been checked by two licensed staff members. In many settings, licensed practical nurses do not start the blood but can set up the infusion. The best option is to send the second unit back immediately, with an explanation that it will be called for later. One unit of blood usually takes about 2 to 4 hours to infuse.

questions.stem: A nurse is giving iron dextran intramuscularly (IM). Why should the nurse implement the Z-track method?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allows another injection to be given at the same location

questions.rationale: The Z-track method only ensures that no iron will be staining the skin after injection. The amount of pain is the same and, after all IM injections, the needle is cleaned on withdrawal. Injections are never given at recent injection sites.

questions.stem: What is the major difference between fresh-frozen plasma (FFP) and cryoprecipitate (CPP)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: FFP has a longer infusion time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: FFP has a very high probability of causing an allergic reaction.

questions.rationale: FFP contains no platelets.

questions.stem: What medical history information is significant to potential bleeding problems? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drinks two glasses of wine a day

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eats red meat three times a week

choices.label: C
choices.text: Takes nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for the relief of arthritic pain four times a day

choices.label: E
choices.text: Had a cardiac valve replaced 6 months earlier

questions.rationale: NSAIDs and liver disorders enhance the probability of bleeding. The valve replacement of a few months earlier suggests that the patient is using anticoagulant drugs.

questions.stem: A nurse plans the interventions to prepare a patient for a bone marrow aspiration. _____ (Place the options in the correct sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assist the patient to lie on his or her abdomen and drape the hip and lower limbs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Confirm the presence of laboratory personnel to stain the specimen.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply a pressure dressing and help the patient lie on his or her back.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that a signed permission form is obtained.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Explain that the procedure will take about 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: The appropriate sequence is the following: (1) explain the procedure; (2) when the patient indicates an understanding, obtain a signed permission form; (3) assist the patient to lie on his or her abdomen and drape the hip and lower extremities; (4) confirm the presence of laboratory personnel to stain the specimen; and (5) apply a pressure dressing and help the patient lie on his or her back.

questions.stem: What should be a major focus in a teaching plan for a teenager with sickle cell anemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Limit tobacco use to no more than two cigarettes a day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eat foods high in iron and vitamin B.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maintain environmental temperature at 65 F to 68 F.

questions.rationale: The maintenance of adequate fluid intake (4 to 6 L/day) prevents hemoconcentration. The use of alcohol and tobacco is contraindicated for the patient with sickle cell anemia as the cause vasoconstriction. Warm environments are more therapeutic as warm environments do not cause vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who is having radiation treatment for cancer. How many days after the start of radiation should the nurse know that the threat of thrombocytopenia exists?
questions.rationale: Thrombocytopenia becomes a nursing concern 10 to 14 days after therapy has begun. This concern is true for radiation and chemotherapy.

questions.stem: What happens to the sickle-shaped red blood cells during a sickle cell crisis?
questions.rationale: Circulatory obstruction causes severe pain in patients with sickle cell anemia, which is the major symptom in sickle cell crisis. The hemoglobin S does not, in itself, cause the crises until the cells obstruct a vessel.

questions.stem: What information should a nurse be sure to include when preparing discharge plans for a patient recently diagnosed with pernicious anemia?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Avoiding prolonged exposure to direct sunlight

choices.label: D
choices.text: Providing sufficient rest periods throughout the day

questions.rationale: Fatigue and weakness are seen in all anemias.

questions.stem: What is the rationale for administering injections of vitamin B12 to patients with pernicious anemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient’s body does not normally manufacture enough vitamin B12.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient may lack the intrinsic factor necessary for vitamin B12 absorption.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vitamin B12 is found in very small quantities in the patient’s body.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vitamin B12 is a mineral necessary to aid in the formation of strong bones.

questions.rationale: The patient with pernicious anemia lacks the intrinsic factor, found in the stomach, which is essential for vitamin B12 absorption.

questions.stem: Which foods should a nurse include in a nutrition teaching plan for a patient with iron deficiency anemia?
questions.rationale: Iron-rich foods include beans, dried fruit, liver, red meat, fish, and whole-grain breads.

questions.stem: A child with sickle cell anemia is prescribed the drug hydroxyurea. What effect from the drug should the patient expect to have?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Produce a hemoglobin that resists sickling

questions.rationale: Hydroxyurea produces a hemoglobin that resists sickling.

questions.stem: A newborn infant has developed significant jaundice and has a positive Coombs test result resulting from high levels of bilirubin. What should a nurse be aware that these symptoms may indicate?
questions.rationale: Newborns can develop hemolytic anemias resulting from blood incompatibility to their mother. These are typical signs of hemolytic anemia in the newborn.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old African-American child is diagnosed with sickle cell anemia. The parents know that sickle cell anemia is hereditary but do not understand why their child has the disease because neither of them has it. What is the most accurate information to provide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At least one of the parents has to have the disease.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only one parent has to have the disease or the trait.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Someone in previous generations had the disease.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both parents were carriers of the sickle cell trait.

questions.rationale: Sickle cell anemia is a genetic disease carried by the recessive genes of both parents, who will not exhibit any symptoms of the disease.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man is examined in an urgent care clinic. His presenting symptoms suggest polycythemia vera. Which extreme laboratory value would confirm this possible diagnosis?
questions.rationale: The symptoms of polycythemia vera are extremely high hemoglobin and hematocrit values because of the excessive production of red blood cells. Patients with polycythemia vera have 1 pint of blood taken from them until the blood values become more normal. The blood is collected as it would be for a blood donation, but it cannot be used for transfusion purposes.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man has a diagnosis of aplastic anemia. What information in the patient history is pertinent to this diagnosis?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gain of 5 lb in the last 2 years

questions.rationale: Exposure to toxic chemicals can cause aplastic anemia.

questions.stem: At the end of a shift, a nurse documents the effectiveness of parent teaching concerning the transmission of hemophilia. Which statement by the mother would best indicate an accurate parental perception?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Hemophilia is a genetic disorder, and I am a carrier, although I do not have the disease.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “My son developed hemophilia because I had measles while I was pregnant.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Because my husband isn’t affected by the disease, our daughter will not be a carrier.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I know it is not necessary to have my two daughters tested for the disease.”

questions.rationale: Women carry the trait and pass it on to their sons.

questions.stem: What should a nurse be careful to observe for when assessing a patient with thrombocytopenia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Distended neck veins and skin discoloration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discoloration of the nails and sclera

choices.label: C
choices.text: Petechiae on the skin and bleeding gums

questions.rationale: Symptoms of thrombocytopenia include petechiae, purpura, bleeding gums, and epistaxis.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a patient with crushing injuries from an automobile accident notes that the patient is bleeding profusely from the nose, mouth, and rectum, as well as from the injuries. What should the nurse suspect as the cause of this patient’s bleeding?
questions.rationale: DIC occurs in massive crushing injuries, burns, and allergic responses. The body’s clotting ability is exhausted because of its attempt to repair so many areas with coagulation. When the platelet supply is gone, the clotting ability is lost, and massive hemorrhaging occurs.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with hemophilia who is experiencing a bleeding episode. Which intervention(s) should the nurse anticipate implementing? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Providing intravenous morphine for pain relief

questions.rationale: Medical treatment for patients experiencing a bleeding episode is symptomatic. The physician usually prescribes transfusions of FFP or cryoprecipitate, or both. The pain can be very severe. The patient needs adequate pain relief during these episodes, even if they last several days. Intravenous morphine is commonly prescribed for pain relief. Rectal suppositories would be avoided due to high risk for mucous membrane bleeding if inserted. Bed rest would be anticipated during a bleeding episode. Aspirin would enhance bleeding due to anticoagulant effects.

questions.stem: ______ is a condition in which too many RBCs are produced.
questions.rationale: Polycythemia vera is a condition in which too many red blood cells are produced.

questions.stem: What is responsible for initiating the inflammatory response in addition to immunoglobulin E (IgE)?
questions.rationale: Basophils initiate a massive inflammatory response with histamine that quickly brings other white blood cells (WBCs) to the site of an infection.

questions.stem: Where are histamine-releasing mast cells located?
questions.rationale: Mast cells are located in organ tissue when they release their histamine. The organ to which they are attached is the host of the inflammatory response. If the organ is the lung, the response may be asthma; if the organ is the colon, the response may be diarrhea.

questions.stem: What is true concerning passive-acquired immunity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antibodies are acquired from outside the host and instilled in the host.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antibodies are manufactured in response to a disease in the host.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibodies are innately acquired because of being born a human being.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antibodies are cell mediated inside the host.

questions.rationale: Gamma globulin injections provide passive-acquired immunity. The antibodies that are injected have been produced by another host, collected, fused in the mixture, and injected into a separate host. This gives the host a passive-acquired immunity that lasts for only 2 to 3 months.

questions.stem: A patient taking dexamethasone (Decadron) reports insomnia. What is the best information to provide this patient regarding administration of this medication?
questions.rationale: Patients taking steroids should take them early in the day to avoid sleep disturbances.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing a patient for a liver and spleen scan. Which intervention is most important to implement before the procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prepare the biopsy site with a clean field.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check for any allergies to contrast media.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Explain the procedure to the patient’s family.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have the patient eat a complete regular diet.

questions.rationale: Allergies should always be checked before any diagnostic test.

questions.stem: After a bone marrow transplant, a patient is placed on a protocol of chemotherapy and radiation and has the potential for injury. Which nursing assessment should cause the nurse concern?
questions.rationale: High doses of chemotherapy and radiation can damage the liver, which would lead to increasing abdominal girth with ascites and increasing bilirubin levels.

questions.stem: A patient diagnosed with leukemia has had a bone marrow transplant and has completed chemotherapy. What is the greatest risk for this patient while healthy bone marrow is growing back?
questions.rationale: Patients are at greater risk for infection and bleeding while their healthy bone marrow is growing back.

questions.stem: What is the primary function in the immune process of the spleen?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Store lymphocytes used to fight infections.

questions.rationale: The spleen filters microorganisms from the blood.

questions.stem: Which laboratory result for a patient with acute leukemia should alert the nurse to the fact that the drug protocols are not effective?
questions.rationale: A low platelet count predisposes a patient to bleeding. A count less than 50,000/mm3 is a cause for concern.

questions.stem: What type of bone marrow transplant uses the patient’s own bone marrow?
questions.rationale: An autologous bone marrow transplant uses the patient’s own bone marrow.

questions.stem: Which human immunity is an example of innate immunity?
questions.rationale: Humans, by nature of their innate properties at birth, have an innate immunity to hoof-and-mouth disease. Cows also have an innate immunity to measles.

questions.stem: Which nursing action should be implemented when performing skin testing?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the site in 2 to 3 days for swelling.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wrap the site with a pressure dressing.

questions.rationale: A cell-mediated response will show swelling in 2 to 3 days, indicating antibodies working at the site of the exposure to an antigen.

questions.stem: What are the four distinct stages of the inflammatory process? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The four processes are rubor (red), tumor (swelling), calor (heat), and dolor (pain).

questions.stem: A nurse explains that early in life, lymphocytes migrate from the marrow of the bones to the ______, in which they mature into T cells.
questions.rationale: The lymphocytes migrate and mature to T cells in the thymus.

questions.stem: Cells in the bone marrow that are capable of developing into RBCs, WBCs, or platelets are the ______ cells.
questions.rationale: Adult stem (progenitor) cells can evolve into WBCs, RBCs, or platelets. Stem cells from an embryo can mature into any specialized cell. Adult stem cells are limited to cells of their origin.

questions.stem: Which population, according to statistics from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), has the greatest incidence of human immunodeficiency viral (HIV) infection in the United States?
questions.rationale: Of those with HIV infection in the United States, African Americans make up 49%, whites 27%, and Latinos 12%. Asian Americans were not reported.

questions.stem: What does enzyme reverse transcriptase transcribe?
questions.rationale: Reverse transcriptase reverses the normal process and allows the RNA to be transcribed to the DNA rather than the DNA to be transcribed to the RNA.

questions.stem: What is the most common form of transmission of the HIV virus?
questions.rationale: Male-to-male transmission is still the most common mode.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient with AIDS for risk factors. What is recognized as the most risky behavior in the patient history?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral sex without contact with the glans penis

questions.rationale: Anal sex, even with a condom, is a higher risk behavior than the other three options.

questions.stem: When is a patient with HIV considered to have progressed to AIDS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Two or more opportunistic infections are diagnosed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient tested positive for enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA).

questions.rationale: A person with an HIV infection is not diagnosed with AIDS until the CD4 count falls to 200. Other AIDS markers exist as well.

questions.stem: A very anxious young man comes to the clinic believing that he may have HIV infection because of his persistent influenza-like symptoms and his risky sexual behavior. What should the nurse anticipate that a positive blood analysis would show?
questions.rationale: In the initial phase of HIV infection, high levels of HIV-infected cells, high levels of T cells, and high levels of antibodies are present as the body attempts to rid the body of the virus through the immune response.

questions.stem: How long does the initial stage of an HIV infection usually last?
questions.rationale: The initial phase of an HIV infection lasts from 4 to 8 weeks.

questions.stem: A young man at the HIV clinic tells the nurse how relieved he is that he does not have HIV because he now has no symptoms at all when just a few weeks ago he felt awful. What is the most appropriate nursing response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Flulike symptoms frequently are misdiagnosed as HIV.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “In the latent stage, the physical symptoms are reduced, but the HIV is still present in the lymph nodes.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “A high antibody count can overwhelm HIV infection in the early stage.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Antiretroviral drugs are very effective in the first stage in reducing symptoms.”

questions.rationale: In the latent stage, the symptoms are reduced as the virus enters the lymph nodes.

questions.stem: A nurse is educating patients about the progression of HIV infections. Which statement by the patient in the latent stage indicates that teaching has been effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I had better get my affairs in order. I don’t have a lot of time left.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Whew! I thought when I got AIDS that I was a ‘goner.’”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Now I won’t have to take all those expensive drugs that I have been using.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I can still enjoy life and live pretty much as I want for the next several years.”

questions.rationale: The latent stage may last as long as 12 years without developing into AIDS. Medications will be continued.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with HIV infection taking Retrovir, a nucleoside antiviral that is a reverse transcriptase inhibitor. For what should the nurse be especially observant?
questions.rationale: Retrovir has the potential of causing a fatal hepatotoxic reaction. Jaundice is a possible sign of hepatic impairment.

questions.stem: What indicates that a patient has entered the third stage of HIV infection?
questions.rationale: In the third stage of HIV infection, T-helper CD4 cells drop to approximately 500. Antibodies are always high throughout the infection but are ineffective. The viral count is high.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing a teaching plan for a patient with HIV who has been diagnosed with microsporidiosis. Which implementation should be included?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drink 3 quarts of fluid a day to combat dehydration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Include milk products with every meal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Consume liberal amounts of fat for increased energy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Limit protein intake to reduce serum ammonia levels.

questions.rationale: The patients need plenty of fluids to combat the diarrhea and proteins for calories. They should avoid milk products and fat.

questions.stem: What should a patient be encouraged to do before the initiation of any anti-HIV drug protocol?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give up sexual activity for several months.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Involve the partner in a support program.

questions.rationale: Patients with HIV are assessed for their willingness to comply with the drug protocol because nonadherence causes the HIV organisms to become resistant to the drug.

questions.stem: A patient with HIV complains to the home health nurse that he has been having watery diarrhea for the past 10 days. The nurse suspects toxoplasmosis. What is the most significant question for the nurse to ask?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Have you stopped taking your antiviral medication?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Have you been eating aged cheese or organ meats?”

questions.rationale: Cat litter boxes and undercooked meats are the major sources of toxoplasmosis, which causes a persistent watery diarrhea.

questions.stem: A patient with HIV is diagnosed with progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML). What should a nurse encourage the patient to do?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid excessive fats in the diet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remove all potted plants from inside the home.

questions.rationale: The completion of advanced directives is essential as this disease is rapidly progressing, and death usually occurs 4 to 6 weeks after diagnosis.

questions.stem: Which should take priority in the care of an outpatient with AIDS?
questions.rationale: Failure to take anti-HIV drugs as scheduled can encourage resistant strains of HIV.

questions.stem: A nurse removes a potted plant from the room of a patient with HIV. What is the nurse trying to prevent?
questions.rationale: Aspergillosis can be contracted from the potting soil in and around the plant in the pot.

questions.stem: What childhood exposure causes painful shingles experienced by the patient with HIV?
questions.rationale: Chickenpox can be reactivated as shingles.

questions.stem: Which nursing action should be implemented to increase the comfort of a patient with oral hair leukoplakia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Allow aspirin to melt in the mouth and then wash out with warm water.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encourage mouth rinses with warm salt water several times a day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Limit intake of ice cream and other cold foods.

questions.rationale: Using a straw keeps fluids from flooding the entire oral cavity. Warm or acidic items are to be discouraged because they add to the discomfort. Ice cream and popsicles can numb the area.

questions.stem: What should a patient with HIV avoid to prevent bacillary angiomatosis (BA)?
questions.rationale: Cats and their fleas are thought to transmit BA.

questions.stem: What sign should a nurse report when caring for the patient with AIDS who has cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma?
questions.rationale: Abdominal pain may be an indication of organ involvement from Kaposi sarcoma.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient diagnosed with AIDS should lead a nurse to suspect an infection by CMV?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I need to get glasses; I can’t see as well as I did a few months ago.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I need to drink more water. This diarrhea has really dehydrated me.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I need to get smaller clothes. I have lost 10 lb in the past 6 weeks.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I need to take some pep pills. I don’t have any energy.”

questions.rationale: Visual changes indicate the presence of CMV retinitis, which will eventually lead to blindness. Diarrhea is indicative of a fungal infection, and decreases in weight and energy are expected manifestations of AIDS.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with HIV infection who has been prescribed highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART). What should the nurse warn the patient that inconsistent administration of the drug can result in?

choices.label: A
choices.text: HIV strain becoming resistant to the drug

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decrease in antibodies in the circulating volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: Addition of another antiretroviral agent to the protocol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid increase in the symptoms of AIDS

questions.rationale: Inconsistent administration of HAART drugs can cause the HIV strain to become resistant to the drug.

questions.stem: Which symptom should a nurse recognize as being pertinent to a possible diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)?
questions.rationale: The classic butterfly rash of the face is one of the most recognizable signs. Because the symptoms come and go, SLE is extremely hard to diagnose quickly.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include when developing a plan of care for a patient with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Careful aseptic technique to prevent infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruction to limit fluids to prevent congestive heart failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral alcohol rinses to control mouth infections

choices.label: D
choices.text: Selections of high-fat foods in the daily diet

questions.rationale: A major complication of HIV is opportunistic infections.

questions.stem: An 11-year-old girl is diagnosed with idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP). Which parental statement helps the nurse evaluate that teaching is successful?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Our daughter can still be involved in gymnastics.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “When our daughter’s hemoglobin falls below 3.5, she’ll need blood.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Our daughter will need genetic counseling before she marries.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Our daughter should avoid drugs containing sulfonamides.”

questions.rationale: Drugs known to induce ITP include sulfonamides.

questions.stem: What is most appropriate for a nurse to include when preparing discharge plans for a patient with SLE?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Need to consume 2 L of fluid daily

choices.label: B
choices.text: Close monitoring of daily blood glucose level

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use of daily sunscreens with a sun protection factor (SPF) higher than 15

choices.label: D
choices.text: Careful concern for certain food allergies

questions.rationale: Patients with SLE are photosensitive to sunlight.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura who is having plasmapheresis every day. Which assessment alerts the nurse of a complication?
questions.rationale: During the period of treatment by plasmapheresis, the patient can become hemodynamically unstable and have a reduced cardiac output with the attendant hypotension. This is a serious complication and can lead to renal failure.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses a neutrophil count of 900/mm3 in a patient with acute leukemia. What should the nurse anticipate initiating?
questions.rationale: Patients with neutrophil counts of approximately 1000 cells/mm3 are placed on compromised host precautions.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient in the last stages of leukemia and is aware that the patient is at risk from the bacteria of his own body. Which is an example of internal bacteria?
questions.rationale: Internal bacteria such as P. aeruginosa and Escherichia coli are capable of attacking the compromised immune system from inside the body.

questions.stem: The nursing staff of an oncology unit cautions visitors to be free of infections before visiting patients. What can chemotherapy and decreased bone marrow production cause in these patients?
questions.rationale: Neutropenia occurs when the total number of neutrophils is abnormally low, placing the patient at increased risk for infection.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital for a complete medical examination. Her current complaints are indicative of SLE. Which symptom would indicate this diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Musculoskeletal symptoms are experienced by 95% of patients with SLE at some time during the course of their disease.

questions.stem: Which populations are at the highest risks of contracting HIV? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Health care workers who mishandle infected sharps

choices.label: C
choices.text: Persons sharing living quarters with an HIV-infected person

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heterosexual partners of an HIV-infected person

questions.rationale: Sharing living quarters without intimate contact does not expose a person to HIV infection.

questions.stem: A nurse is designing a teaching plan for a patient with AIDS. What should be included relative to food preparation precautions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check expiration dates on frozen foods.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leave produce unwashed to preserve protective spray.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drink a small glass of red wine before each meal to stimulate the appetite.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Eat three large, well-balanced meals daily.

questions.rationale: Using food before the expiration date and avoiding leftovers reduce the risk of food contamination. Individuals with AIDS should wash all fresh produce to get rid of contaminants, eat several small meals daily, and avoid alcohol and caffeine.

questions.stem: Which factors explain the increase in HIV infections in persons over the age of 50 years? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Older persons are usually not questioned by health professionals about sex or drug abuse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Older persons are more promiscuous in earlier years.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Older persons are less likely to seek HIV screening.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Older persons mistake HIV symptoms as part of the discomforts of increased age.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Older persons tend to use hormonal forms of contraception.

questions.rationale: Individuals older than 50 years of age are less likely to be questioned by health care professionals relative to sex activities or illicit drug use. Older adults are less likely to seek HIV screening and frequently accept the symptoms of HIV as part of increasing age.

questions.stem: Which opportunistic fungal diseases threaten patients with HIV? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Aspergillosis and P. jiroveci are caused by fungi. Herpes simplex and leukoplakia are caused by viruses. Tuberculosis is caused by bacteria.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that in autoimmune diseases, the body identifies its own proteins as foreign matter and sets out to destroy itself. Which are examples of autoimmune diseases? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The autoimmune diseases are SLE, type 1 DM, and RA.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that HIV is introduced to the systemic circulation by the _____, which is found in the mucous membranes.
questions.rationale: The macrophage introduces HIV into the system.

questions.stem: A nurse explains to a pregnant patient with AIDS that her baby will be treated with antiretroviral drugs for _____ weeks after birth. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: The usual antiretroviral protocol for an infant born to a mother with AIDS is for 6 weeks.

questions.stem: A nurse performs an apical-radial pulse evaluation, with the result of 100/88. What is the pulse deficit?
questions.rationale: To detect an apical-radial pulse deficit, the rates should be counted simultaneously and compared for differences. If a difference exists between the apical rate and the radial rate, then a pulse deficit is present. For example, in atrial fibrillation, a pulse deficit exists.

questions.stem: What is increased in hypertension that in turn causes an increase in the work of the heart?
questions.rationale: An increase blood pressure creates an increase in afterload because the heart must work harder to push the blood out of the left ventricle into the circulating volume.

questions.stem: Which heart sound should the nurse record as normal?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Medium diastolic murmur in a 50-year-old patient

questions.rationale: Ventricular gallops are considered normal in individuals younger than 30 years of age. All other options are pathologic abnormalities.

questions.stem: A patient asks what a transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) is and what it is expected to do? What is the best explanation by the nurse?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evaluates the efficiency of the valves

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checks the volume of the preload

questions.rationale: TEE evaluates the efficiency of the valves.

questions.stem: A nurse records the finding of a normal sinus rhythm (NSR) when the P, Q, R, S, and T are all present in the electrocardiographic complex. What additional information should the nurse document?
questions.rationale: NSR requires the presence of P, Q, R, S, and T waves, in that order, and all pointing in the same direction, with a rate of 60 to 100 seconds. Normal intervals are a PR interval of 0.12 to 0.20 seconds and a QRS complex less than 0.10 second.

questions.stem: A nurse should anticipate that a patient taking Vasotec, an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, should have which positive outcome to this drug?
questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors suppress the excretion of angiotensin, which lowers the blood pressure, reduces fluid retention, and leads to increased urine output.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old patient is to receive cardioversion for a dysrhythmia. What should the nurse instruct the patient to expect?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Digoxin dose to be taken as scheduled

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pacemaker spikes to be carefully monitored

questions.rationale: A cardioversion has risks, such as ventricular fibrillation. Emergency equipment should be available. The digoxin dose is held before a cardioversion, and the patient is given a short-acting sedative such as Versed or Valium, which will require recovery. The electrocardiogram R wave is synchronized via the computer, and no pacemaker is involved.
