questions.stem: A 68-year-old patient is scheduled for open heart surgery in the morning and is crying. What is the most appropriate response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Everything will go great! Dr. C. is the best!”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I know how you feel, so do not cry.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Tell me what concerns you the most.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will call the physician for a sedative. You are too upset.”

questions.rationale: Therapeutic implementations identify and acknowledge feelings. Do not assume that you know how the patient feels and do not give false assurances.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that cardiac rehabilitation lasts from the end of acute care to the return to home and beyond. What does this service include?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Focus on the patient rather than the family

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rejection from the program for noncompliance

questions.rationale: Cardiac rehabilitation programs are supervised by a team of experts who arrange for telemetry-supervised exercise and other modalities, such as diet and medical protocol management. The focus is on the family, as well as the patient. Although some patients reject the program, they are rarely rejected by the rehabilitation center.

questions.stem: On auscultation, a nurse detects a heart murmur. What should the nurse know that a heart murmur indicates?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Valves that do not close correctly

questions.rationale: Heart murmurs indicate turbulent blood flow and can be caused by valves that are stiff and do not shut correctly; consequently, blood flows back into the chamber.

questions.stem: A physician has ordered continuous pulse oximetry. What should the nurse explain to the patient about this procedure?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measures the amount of oxygen in the blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identifies damaged cells in the myocardium

questions.rationale: Pulse oximetry measures arterial oxygen saturation noninvasively by attaching a clip to a digit, an ear, or a nose.

questions.stem: A stress test is scheduled for a 41-year-old patient. What action should the nurse implement to prepare the patient for the examination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Have the patient sign a consent form.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the patient a special heart diet.

questions.rationale: A stress test is a noninvasive test that consists of a patient walking on a treadmill while an electrocardiogram records the activity. A consent form is required.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse expect to implement when a patient returns from a cardiac catheterization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ambulate the patient in the hall.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remove the gel from all sites on the skin.

questions.rationale: Cardiac catheterizations are invasive procedures during which a catheter is threaded through an artery. Postprocedure care requires bed rest and monitoring the puncture site.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses an inverted T wave on the ECG of a patient who had an acute MI two days earlier. How should the nurse interpret this finding?
questions.rationale: The abnormal waveform of the inverted T wave is an indicator that tissue death has occurred in part of the cardiac wall. The cardiac wall now has no ability to conduct or to contract and sends that message to the ECG via the inverted T. The tissue will take 6 weeks to regenerate.

questions.stem: Laboratory tests are performed to identify damage to the heart muscle. Which test is elevated the earliest with heart damage?
questions.rationale: Troponin is elevated within 3 to 6 hours and is often measured in the emergency department. CPK-MB is elevated in 12 to 24 hours. Three serial samples are drawn. The LDH increases with heart damage within 3 to 6 days. The lipid profile is not elevated with heart damage.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for a heart catheterization. What action should the nurse implement in preparation for this examination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient about allergies to seafood or iodine.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the patient a special heart diet.

questions.rationale: The dye injected during the cardiac catheterization is iodine based. An allergy to seafood is correlated with a reaction to this dye as well.

questions.stem: A patient has had atropine sulfate that has been administered intravenously to treat a dysrhythmia. What should the nurse assess this patient for after administration?
questions.rationale: Atropine increases the heart rate. The nurse should watch for tachycardia, which increases the workload of the heart. This medication causes urinary retention.

questions.stem: A dopamine infusion is being administered to a patient with shock. For what should the nurse be alert?
questions.rationale: Dopamine has a direct effect by elevating the blood pressure. The criterion is to titrate to the target blood pressure. Urinary output should also be monitored for a decreased amount because a heightened blood pressure may slow urine filtration and reduce urine output.

questions.stem: A patient with atrial fibrillation is prescribed amiodarone for the dysrhythmia. Which potential adverse reaction should the nurse report?
questions.rationale: The drug amiodarone is meant to quiet atrial activity and modify rapid pulse rate, high blood pressure, and decreased cardiac output caused by the dysrhythmia. The drug interferes with the thyroid and causes an ataxic gait and trembling of hands as adverse effects.

questions.stem: A medication, simvastatin (Zocor), is administered to lower a patient’s cholesterol level. Follow-up lipid levels are reviewed by the nurse. Which level indicates the desired therapeutic range?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-density lipoprotein (HDL), 29 mg/dL; low-density lipoprotein (LDL), 160 mg/dL

questions.rationale: The reading that has both an HDL level above 40 mg/dL and an LDL level below 100 mg/dL is in the therapeutic target range.

questions.stem: A diuretic medication, furosemide (Lasix), is being administered for congestive heart failure. Which assessment is not an anticipated consequence of the therapy?
questions.rationale: Increased urinary output, weight loss, and thirst are all anticipated consequences of the therapy. Muscle weakness is a sign of hypokalemia.

questions.stem: A patient is receiving digoxin 0.25 mg/day. What should the nurse do prior to administering this medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Count an apical pulse for 15 seconds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the dose if the apical rate is 57 beats/min.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the dose if the apical rate is 59 beats/min.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Double the dose if the rate is 62 beats/min.

questions.rationale: The dose should be held if the apical rate is less than 60 beats/min for 1 minute.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old patient is receiving propranolol (Inderal), a nonselective beta-adrenergic blocker, for a heart condition. What patient teaching is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sit or lie down when taking the drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Double the dose if symptoms occur.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Never stop taking the drug abruptly.

questions.rationale: Beta-blockers should never be stopped abruptly because they can cause angina or MI. Patients are gradually weaned off these medications.

questions.stem: Which assessment should be immediately addressed in a patient on lidocaine?
questions.rationale: A temperature that goes up drastically indicates an adverse reaction to lidocaine, malignant hyperthermia. The slowed ventricular rate, even with occasional PVCs, is an expected outcome of lidocaine infusion. Nausea and vomiting are adverse effects.

questions.stem: A nurse records a “1” for the pulse quality of the pedal pulse. What interpretation is correct regarding the pulse?
questions.rationale: A “1” in a pulse evaluation indicates a thready pulse that is easily obliterated by pressure.

questions.stem: A patient with an irregular sinoatrial (SA) node conduction has a permanent pacemaker with the code AAIOO and is now going home. The patient asks, “What happens when my real SA node fires on its own?” How should the nurse respond regarding what the pacemaker should do?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fire both the atria and the ventricles.

questions.rationale: The code is A (chamber-paced) atria, A (sense impulse) atria only, I (inhibit) inhibit firing from the pacemaker, O (rate modification) no rate modification, and O (multichamber) no other chambers to be stimulated by the pacemaker. If the SA fires on its own, the pacemaker does nothing until it fails to sense an impulse.

questions.stem: What is a normal age-related change in older adults that makes them susceptible to cardiovascular disease?
questions.rationale: As adults age, their peripheral vessels become stiff, their oxygen capacity and stroke volume are reduced, and their aorta thickens and calcifies.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses a patient’s capillary refill time as less than 3 seconds. What does this assessment indicate?
questions.rationale: Capillary refill is determined by compressing the nail bed until it blanches. With a normal capillary refill, color returns to the blanched skin within 3 seconds.

questions.stem: A nurse performs Homans maneuver by flexing the knee and sharply dorsiflexing the foot. What response indicates a positive Homans sign?
questions.rationale: A positive Homans sign indicates the possible presence of a DTV because of the pain produced in the calf of the leg when the foot is dorsiflexed.

questions.stem: Which technique should the nurse implement when performing the Allen test on a patient to evaluate the adequacy of circulation in the radial artery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asks the patient to relax the hand by the side

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compresses only the ulnar artery to blanch the hand

choices.label: C
choices.text: Releases pressure on both arteries at the same time

choices.label: D
choices.text: Observes whether the color is returning to the hand, which indicates perfusion

questions.rationale: The Allen test is performed to evaluate circulation in the hand, both in the radial and the ulnar arteries. The patient is asked to make a fist. The nurse compresses both the ulnar and the radial artery to blanch the hand. The patient is asked to open the hand as the nurse releases pressure on one or the other of the arteries. Color returning to the hand confirms perfusion.

questions.stem: A nurse records that a patient has a 3+ edema to the right foot. How deep did the nurse’s thumb depress the edematous area?
questions.rationale: Edema is measured by the depth of the depression of the thumb: 1 = less than inch, 2 = to inch, 3 = to 1 inch, and 4 = more than 1 inch.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who has had an angiogram. What should the nurse make a point of care to assess and document on this patient?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Peripheral pulses in the affected leg

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inquiring about an allergy to iodine

questions.rationale: Checking and recording the presence and strength of the pulses in the affected leg ensure that the injection site has not occluded the vessel and that vascular spasm has not impaired circulation. An inquiry about an iodine allergy is made before the procedure.

questions.stem: A nurse is educating a patient regarding a stress test on a treadmill. Teaching includes that this test is a noninvasive procedure. What additional information is appropriate for the nurse to include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Is monitored continuously by blood pressure and an electrocardiogram

choices.label: C
choices.text: Is meant to stimulate claudication and dyspnea

choices.label: D
choices.text: Will require a period of bed rest afterward

questions.rationale: The examination requires the patient to walk at a rate of approximately 1.5 miles per hour. The exercise is continually monitored and is terminated if the patient experiences pain or dyspnea.

questions.stem: A patient inquires how something as simple as walking could help his venous vascular disorder. What is the best response by the nurse when explaining the benefits of walking?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Improves the strength of the vascular walls

choices.label: B
choices.text: Boosts venous circulation through leg muscle activity

questions.rationale: Walking is helpful because the muscle action of the legs that massage the valves of the veins boosts circulation.

questions.stem: What is contraindicated for a patient performing Buerger-Allen exercises?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Raising legs for 2 minutes until they blanch

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lowering the legs until the color returns

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keeping legs flat for 5 minutes and then repeat the exercise

questions.rationale: Buerger-Allen exercises promote emptying of the blood vessels by gravity. Initially, lying on the back and elevating the legs will result in pallor, and then lowering the legs will allow color to return.

questions.stem: A nurse cautions a patient with peripheral vascular disease (PVD) that continued smoking causes detrimental vasoconstriction for up to ____ after only one cigarette.
questions.rationale: Smoking restricts circulation by vasoconstriction and lasts up to 1 hour after a cigarette; it also causes vasospasm.

questions.stem: A nurse is performing an intake examination on a patient with peripheral vascular disease (PVD). Which lifestyle information identified by the patient aggravates vascular disease?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Being employed as an air traffic controller

questions.rationale: Employment as an air controller is a stressful occupation. Stress increases vasoconstriction and increases vascular resistance. Wine and chocolate actually have beneficial effects on circulation, as does bicycle riding.

questions.stem: Vascular disease disorders often require the use of elastic stockings. Which action should the nurse implement when caring for a patient with elastic stockings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply the stockings and roll down the cuff.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remove the stockings for skin inspection two times a day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remove the stockings when the patient is ambulating.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inspect the skin for pressure or irritation daily.

questions.rationale: Elastic stockings improve blood flow. They should be applied early in the morning. They should be removed twice daily for 20 to 30 minutes, and the skin integrity of the feet should be examined.

questions.stem: Which instruction is most appropriate for a patient with arterial insufficiency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Frequently allow the legs to dangle dependently.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stand often to keep blood flow in the legs.

questions.rationale: Dangling legs can use gravity to help with arterial circulation. Vigorous rubbing of the legs is contraindicated, and prolonged standing strains the vascular system. The patient should never walk barefoot.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH). What is a major advantage related to the administration of LMWH?

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is given only after partial thromboplastin time (PTT) laboratory work.

questions.rationale: LMWH can be given as a fixed dose without waiting for the results of the PTT. It is only given subcutaneously and does not have an immediate effect. PTT is not done to monitor LMWH.

questions.stem: Which statement made by a patient indicates to the nurse that a teaching plan for the use of warfarin was not effective?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I read that grapefruit interferes with warfarin.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I’m drinking too much tea. My urine looks like tea.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I wear my medical alert bracelet all the time.”

questions.rationale: Anticoagulants, such as warfarin (Coumadin), can cause bleeding. A sign of bleeding may be bruising, tea- or cola-colored urine, or dark-colored stool.

questions.stem: What medication obtained in a patient’s history will lessen the effects of warfarin (Coumadin)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Simvastatin (Zocor) for the control of cholesterol

questions.rationale: Oral contraceptives lessen the effects of warfarin (Coumadin).

questions.stem: Which factors affect stroke volume? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Stroke volume is dependent on contractility, preload, and afterload. Age may affect all three, but the stroke volume, regardless of age, is dependent on these three factors.

questions.stem: Which age-related changes in the heart should a nurse take into consideration? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Aging thickens and stiffens the valves and reduces the cells in the SA node. Age decreases the nerve fibers in the ventricles.

questions.stem: Why do older persons adapt more slowly to changes in the peripheral vascular system? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Age-related changes include a slowing of the heart rate, a decrease in both cardiac output and stroke volume, and a stiffening and thickening of blood vessels.

questions.stem: A nurse suspects a circulatory disorder in one leg. Which assessments should the nurse include when comparing both legs? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Muscle strength is not a circulatory assessment. Color, warmth, pulse quality, and loss of superficial hair are indicators of decreased arterial perfusion.

questions.stem: At rest, the cardiac cells in the myocardium are electrically polarized, with the inside of the cell being more ______ than the outside of the cell.
questions.rationale: When the heart is at rest, the inside of the cell is negatively charged.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that the lining of a vessel that allows for smooth blood flow and also reduced resistance in the vessel is the ______ of the vessel.
questions.rationale: The interior lining of a blood vessel is referred to as the intima.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that when a patient history reveals a recent episode of vomiting and diarrhea, the nurse anticipates that this fluid loss will cause ______ and increased blood viscosity.
questions.rationale: Hemoconcentration occurs when fluid is lost through dehydration, which makes the blood more viscous and shows an inaccurately high value of hemoglobin.

questions.stem: A nurse uses a picture to demonstrate the conduction pathway through the chambers of the heart. ______ (Arrange the following options in the correct sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Conduction occurs through the bundle branches.

questions.rationale: The conduction pathway begins in the SA node, travels down the atrial wall, depolarizing the atria, to the AV node, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers, contracting the ventricles.

questions.stem: A 49-year-old patient has multiple risk factors for coronary artery disease. Which risk factor is considered modifiable?
questions.rationale: Smoking, a high-fat diet, hypertension, sedentary lifestyle, and stress are considered modifiable risk factors.

questions.stem: What do fibrous plaques of atherosclerosis serve as when they are laid down in the vessels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stent to keep the vessel open

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trap to which other substances adhere

choices.label: C
choices.text: Threat to the integrity of the vessel wall

questions.rationale: The plaque surface acts as a trap to which fibrous plaques can adhere, causing more narrowing of the vessel. The enlarging plaque can become a thrombus but not an embolus because emboli are usually considered to be traveling aggregations that lodge in a small arteriole.

questions.stem: A patient with angina pectoris complains of chest pain at rest and needs to take three nitroglycerin (NTG) pills to relieve the pain. Of what should the nurse assess this as a major symptom?
questions.rationale: A patient with angina who has pain at rest that is not relieved with one NTG pill is considered to have unstable angina, a precursor to an acute MI.

questions.stem: What is an important teaching point for a patient with mitral stenosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain a place on the heart transplant list.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase daily fluid intake to over 2000 mL.

questions.rationale: Patients with mitral stenosis need to balance their activity with their oxygen supply and avoid overhydration.

questions.stem: What information should a nurse include in a patient’s discharge instruction after an acute myocardial infarction (MI)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cautions about the use of morphine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Detailed symptoms that indicate impending MI

choices.label: C
choices.text: Written instructions on diet and follow-up appointments

questions.rationale: The patient needs written instructions for diet, follow-up appointments, and exercise protocols. Giving detailed information about symptoms is not necessary other than to remind the patient about reporting chest pain and shortness of breath. A high-energy exercise program is not appropriate. Morphine is not part of the home care after an MI.

questions.stem: A patient with acute congestive heart failure has jugular vein distention, crackles bilaterally, and dyspnea. Which nursing diagnosis should have the highest priority?
questions.rationale: Fluid volume excess increases the workload of the heart and interferes with breathing.

questions.stem: What actions should a nurse implement to decrease the workload of the heart in a patient with acute congestive failure? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct the patient in active range-of-motion exercises.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Help the patient change positions every 2 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assist the patient to ambulate to the bathroom.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Give a partial bed bath rather than full bed bath.

questions.rationale: To minimize the workload of the heart, the nurse would adjust nursing care to eliminate all unnecessary activities, assist in position changes, and give a minimal bath. Ambulation and active range-of-motion exercises are unnecessary activities at this time.

questions.stem: A nurse urges a 50-year-old overweight executive who had a myocardial infarction (MI) 3 months earlier to take up some conditioning exercises for 30 minutes a day. What rationale supports this suggestion? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Conditioning exercises performed daily for 30 minutes can reduce weight, decrease the workload of the heart, and decrease LDLs. Exercise does not affect dysrhythmias.

questions.stem: ______ is an abnormal thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls.
questions.rationale: Arteriosclerosis is an abnormal thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls.

questions.stem: Stable angina (also called chronic stable angina) occurs most often with exercise or activity and usually subsides with ______.
questions.rationale: Stable angina (also called chronic stable angina) occurs most often with exercise or activity and usually subsides with rest.

questions.stem: What should a nurse ask a patient related to past history of deep-vein thrombosis (DVT) and other vascular problems?
questions.rationale: Pain in the chest or dyspnea suggests that a pulmonary embolism may have occurred from the presence of a DVT. Approximately 10% of individuals with DVT develop pulmonary emboli.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old patient reports a burning, aching pain in the legs when walking to the mailbox. These symptoms are relieved with rest. What should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Arterial vascular disorders that produce pain with activity are defined as claudication, which is the result of ischemia of the tissues caused by a lack of adequate perfusion.

questions.stem: A nurse records the assessment of stasis dermatitis on an intake assessment for a patient with peripheral vascular disease (PVD). What is the best way to describe this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Brownish skin discoloration on the lower legs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ulceration on medial surface of the lower legs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Purple rash on medial surface of the lower legs

questions.rationale: Stasis dermatitis is a brownish skin discoloration on the lower legs, which is indicative of venous stasis.

questions.stem: A nurse notes ulcerations on the surfaces of a patient’s toes. What should this assessment most likely indicate?
questions.rationale: Arterial stasis ulcers on the tips of the patient’s toes are indicators of arterial insufficiency. This is a serious and probably progressive disorder that leads to further potential skin breakdown.

questions.stem: What is a characteristic of a venous stasis ulcer?
questions.rationale: Venous ulcers are painless ulcers near the ankle that are warm and have a ruddy color.

questions.stem: A patient has returned from a vein ligation and stripping. What are the appropriate instructions for a nurse to provide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dangle the legs to prevent edema.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cross the legs to apply pressure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wear compression stockings to promote circulation.

questions.rationale: Postoperative care of a patient with a vein ligation and stripping includes elevating the extremity, wearing compression stockings, taking anticoagulant therapy, and assessing the circulation of the affected extremity.

questions.stem: An obese postsurgical patient complains of sudden discomfort in her leg. The nurse assesses the leg and finds it cold and pale with no pedal or popliteal pulse. What should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Signs of an acute arterial occlusion can include severe pain, absent pulses, or very pale or mottled skin.

questions.stem: Which postoperative sign should a nurse report immediately when caring for a patient with an endarterectomy with a synthetic graft?
questions.rationale: A fever in a patient with a synthetic graft is a serious postoperative event. The infection may lead to an amputation.

questions.stem: A patient with Raynaud disease has poor tissue perfusion and is being given discharge instructions. What should the nurse include when providing this information?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wear gloves and warm socks when outdoors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chafe hands frequently to warm them.

questions.rationale: Chafing hands to warm them does not provide vasodilation and may cause tissue damage. Avoiding exposure to cold is paramount to prevent pain and tissue damage. Raynaud disease involves the constriction of the arterioles of the hands, toes, and nose. Pain is a cardinal symptom and can be relieved with methods to promote vasodilation.

questions.stem: What assessment should a nurse perform on a patient after the repair of an abdominal aortic aneurysm?
questions.rationale: Repair of aortic abdominal aneurysms causes a temporary cessation of peristalsis. Although this condition is expected, the beginning of bowel sounds indicates important progress in the recovery. Rising blood pressure is an expected recovery indication from surgery.

questions.stem: What patient teaching should be included for a patient with varicose veins?
questions.rationale: Varicose veins are caused by a dilation of incompetent valves. Obesity, pregnancy, restrictive clothing, and prolonged standing aggravate the condition.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient diagnosed with arterial embolism. What patient teaching information will the nurse anticipate providing to the patient? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply ice packs to affected extremity as needed.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Report any pain or numbness to health care provider.

questions.rationale: Patient Teaching for Arterial Embolism should include to protect affected limbs from pressure, trauma, and temperature extremes; exercise to improve blood flow; gradually increase activity as you are able to tolerate it; report pain, numbness, coolness, and pale or bluish skin color to your health care provider.

questions.stem: ______ is intermittent constriction of arterioles that affects the hands primarily, although it can affect the toes and tip of the nose.
questions.rationale: Primary and secondary Raynaud phenomena are forms of an intermittent constriction of arterioles that affects the hands primarily, although it can affect the toes and tip of the nose.

questions.stem: Hyperbaric O2 therapy may be prescribed in an attempt to promote healing of the ulcer by reducing capillary pressure and ______ the blood.
questions.rationale: Hyperbaric O2 therapy may be prescribed in an attempt to promote healing of the ulcer by reducing capillary pressure and hyperoxygenating the blood.

questions.stem: A patient inquires if his blood pressure is normal. What is the nurse’s most accurate response regarding the definition of normal blood pressure?
questions.rationale: Normal blood pressure is defined as a systolic pressure of less than 120 mm Hg and a diastolic blood pressure of less than 80 mm Hg.

questions.stem: An obese African-American man reports that he smokes. How should the nurse stage this patient’s hypertension?
questions.rationale: Criteria for grade B are that the patient has more than one risk factor (African American, obese, smoker), with no target organ damage and no clinical cardiovascular problems. Criteria for grade A indicate that no risks have been assessed, and the criteria for grade C include target organ damage and clinical cardiovascular disease or diabetes mellitus.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding hypertension in older adults?
questions.rationale: Older adults with hypertension have good results with aggressive therapy, although the age-related changes cannot be avoided.

questions.stem: A patient is being evaluated every week for possible hypertension and is classified as prehypertensive. Which assessment would support this conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure reading over 120/80 mm Hg for two consecutive visits

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood pressure reading over 130/85 mm Hg for over 2 months

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood pressure reading over 140/95 mm Hg for 2 months

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood pressure reading over 144/100 mm Hg at one visit

questions.rationale: Prehypertension is diagnosed after two readings of over 120/80 mm Hg. The reading of 135/85 mm Hg for 2 months is longer than the diagnostic criteria require. Blood pressures consistently over 140/90 mm Hg are classified as hypertension.

questions.stem: A patient with hypertension reports the symptom of headache. How do headaches related to hypertension characteristically occur?
questions.rationale: Hypertension occipital headaches occur on awakening.

questions.stem: What does treatment for essential hypertension focus on?
questions.rationale: The therapeutic approach to essential hypertension is a nonpharmacologic treatment. The treatment plan is to identify and reduce the modifiable risks, which include weight loss, dietary changes, and increased exercise.

questions.stem: What is the focus of treatment for secondary hypertension?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Weight loss program designed to reduce weight rapidly

questions.rationale: Secondary hypertension has an identifiable cause. Treating the cause is the focus of care for secondary hypertension.

questions.stem: What do blood pressure readings need to exceed for a patient to be diagnosed with hypertension?
questions.rationale: The official diagnosis of hypertension is dependent on a period of observation during which the BP is consistently higher than 140/90 mm Hg.

questions.stem: A patient is taking hydrochlorothiazide (HydroDIURIL) for hypertension. When providing dietary teaching what should the nurse encourage the patient to increase the intake of?
questions.rationale: Diuretics reduce fluid volume and can cause hypokalemia. Encourage foods high in potassium, such as bananas and orange juice.

questions.stem: An obese 38-year-old African-American patient with diabetes is being evaluated for the use of propranolol (Inderal) in controlling hypertension. What is a contraindication for the use of propranolol (Inderal) in this patient?
questions.rationale: Hypoglycemia can occur with this medication and places those with diabetes at high risk for adverse effects.

questions.stem: An 89-year-old patient is taking an antihypertensive medication. What should the nurse include when providing home care teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Get up out of bed slowly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stop taking the drug if side effects occur.

questions.rationale: Patients taking antihypertensive medications should get up slowly to prevent falling. They should avoid hot baths because they promote vasodilatation, and they should never stop the drug abruptly. Sexual dysfunction is a possible side effect, but it does not have to be reported immediately.

questions.stem: What should a nurse instruct a patient who has been started on verapamil (Calan) for elevated blood pressure to monitor?
questions.rationale: Verapamil (Calan) is a calcium channel blocker and most often causes bradycardia.

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, enalapril (Vasotec). What is an appropriate instruction for the nurse to provide?
questions.rationale: ACE inhibitors can cause neutropenia or other blood dyscrasias, and follow-up with blood assessments should be periodically performed.

questions.stem: An 87-year-old patient is taking furosemide (Lasix) for elevated blood pressure. What should the nurse know that older patients are at risk for?
questions.rationale: Older adults are at greater risk for side effects, especially hypokalemia.

questions.stem: A nurse is providing patient education regarding a low-sodium diet. In addition to the use of spices, what should the nurse recommend to help decrease sodium in the diet?
questions.rationale: Spices, garlic, and onions are good substitutes for sodium as flavorings. Catsup, soy sauce, and cheese all have large amounts of sodium.

questions.stem: Which lifestyle change has the greatest effect on reducing hypertension?
questions.rationale: Weight reduction has the greatest effect on reducing hypertension.

questions.stem: A nurse is educating a group of patients regarding Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH) diet. What recommendation should the nurse include as a source of protein and fat?
questions.rationale: The DASH diet uses whole grains as a source of protein and fat.

questions.stem: Which antihypertensive medication is contraindicated in patients who have chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and asthma?
questions.rationale: Beta-blocking adrenergic agents block stimulation of catecholamine receptors and cause bronchoconstriction.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who is taking antihypertensive therapy. The patient reports fatigue and has a pulse of 54 beats/min. Which medication administered is most likely the cause of these symptoms?
questions.rationale: Calcium channel blockers such as diltiazem and verapamil can cause bradycardia because conduction is slowed in the heart. These drugs also reduce stroke volume and cardiac output, which may result in fatigue.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include when developing a teaching plan for a patient with hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stop medications if side effects occur.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maintain a diet high in unsaturated fat.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Advise no further visits if no other symptoms occur.

questions.rationale: Patients with hypertension should never stop medications without discussing it with their physicians. A diet low in saturated and unsaturated fat should be followed. Visits to monitor blood pressure should continue because hypertension is often asymptomatic. Stress can aggravate hypertension; stress management or relaxation strategies should be implemented.

questions.stem: A patient reports drowsiness after initiating atenolol (Tenormin) for the treatment of hypertension. What is the nurse’s best response when educating this patient about atenolol (Tenormin)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduce the dose of the medication until the desired effect occurs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Talk to the physician because drowsiness is not an anticipated side effect.

questions.rationale: Sedation is an expected side effect of atenolol. This medication can be taken daily at bedtime. Doses should not be changed without talking to the physician. Activities requiring that the patient be alert should be postponed until later in the day.

questions.stem: A patient taking methyldopa (Aldomet) for hypertension reports to the emergency department with a severe headache, blurred vision, and a blood pressure reading of 200/94 mm Hg. What should the nurse suspect is happening to this patient?
questions.rationale: Abruptly stopping antihypertensive medications can result in a hypertensive crisis. The patient’s presenting symptoms may include rebound hypertension and reports of confusion, nausea, restlessness, headache, and vision abnormalities.

questions.stem: Which patient is at the highest risk for malignant hypertension?
questions.rationale: Malignant hypertension occurs suddenly and usually exhibits a diastolic blood pressure higher than 140 mm Hg. It most commonly occurs in young African-American adults.

questions.stem: A patient with hypertension comes to the emergency department and is experiencing nausea and vomiting and is restless and confused. Vital signs are assessed at blood pressure, 220/130 mm Hg; pulse, 120 beats/min; and respirations, 32 breaths/min. What should the nurse conclude the patient is experiencing based on these assessments?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension crisis from the cessation of a drug

questions.rationale: Patients with a hypertensive crisis usually experience nausea and vomiting, headache, and elevated vital signs because of an abrupt cessation of the hypertensive drug.

questions.stem: What should be the nursing priority when caring for a patient with a hypertensive crisis?
questions.rationale: The care of a patient with hypertensive crisis focuses on monitoring for seizure activity and level of consciousness, which would suggest the occurrence of a stroke.

questions.stem: What are the advantages of hydrochlorothiazide (HydroDIURIL)? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Hydrochlorothiazide is a potassium-sparing diuretic that provides consistent blood pressure control and is effective for African Americans. It does have side effects, however, and does nothing to decrease heart rate directly.

questions.stem: A nurse is providing care to a patient diagnosed with essential hypertension. What should the nurse know is included in a first-line therapeutic approach to lowering this patient’s blood pressure? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The first methodology is a nonpharmacologic approach, using lifestyle changes rather than drugs.

questions.stem: Which conditions are considered potential causes of secondary hypertension? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Renal disorders, constriction or stiffening of the aorta, and conditions that result in ICP are common causes of secondary hypertension.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that the force against which the left ventricle must work to force the blood into the circulation is called ______.
questions.rationale: Peripheral vascular resistance is the pressure against which the left ventricle must work to put blood in the circulating volume.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that hypertension occurs when a chain reaction of events takes place. ______ (Place the following options in the appropriate sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ephedrine constricts vessels and increases the heart rate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Renin constricts vessels and decreases the blood flow to the kidneys.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Angiotensin constricts vessels in response to renin.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Norepinephrine constricts vessels, increasing peripheral vascular resistance.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The adrenal cortex produces aldosterone, causing fluids and sodium to be retained.

questions.rationale: The sequence is that norepinephrine, in response to the sympathetic nervous system, constricts vessels, causing an increase in peripheral vascular resistance; epinephrine constricts vessels and increases the heart rate; and renin is released because of the reduced blood flow to the kidneys, which stimulates the production of an angiotensin. Angiotensin stimulates the adrenal cortex to excrete aldosterone, causing retention of fluid and sodium.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to give a tube feeding using a large syringe. What action should the nurse implement before starting the infusion?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check for a residual formula and return the residual to his or her stomach.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place the end of the tube in water and check for bubbles.

questions.rationale: Verifying tube placement by pulling up the residual formula is a standard of care for a tube feeding.

questions.stem: After receiving a tube feeding, a nurse assesses the patient to be sweaty with abdominal distention and diarrhea. What is the most likely cause of this response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expected reaction to the tube feeding

questions.rationale: Dumping syndrome is caused by infusing a tube feeding too fast or infusing a tube feeding that is too rich a formula.

questions.stem: A nurse administers promethazine (Phenergan) for nausea. Which extra precautionary action should the nurse implement because of the common side effect of antiemetic medications?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check vital signs for erratic blood pressure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Add a blanket to prevent chilling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provide extra water to combat thirst.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Put up side rails to prevent falls.

questions.rationale: Most antiemetic medications cause drowsiness because of their effects on the central nervous system, resulting in dizziness and confusion.

questions.stem: A patient complains about the placement of the total parenteral nutrition (TPN) line and asks why it cannot be inserted in the arm. What fact regarding the placement of this line should the nurse base a response on?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Subclavian artery allows for ease in dressing the puncture site.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arm prevents the use of large-bore cannulas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Subclavian artery allows for rapid dilution.

questions.rationale: The rich TPN solution is rapidly diluted in the larger vessel, preventing phlebitis.

questions.stem: A patient inquires if this newer type of gastric analysis is going to require passage of a nasogastric tube. What is the nurse’s most accurate reply?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes, but just for the instillation of the dye.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “No. You take a dye orally, which will be excreted in the urine in approximately 2 hours.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Yes. You will take the dye orally, and then several gastric withdrawals through the tube will show the dye.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Yes. Only one withdrawal will be made through the tube, which will be treated with dye and read in approximately 2 hours.”

questions.rationale: Dye is given orally, and if hydrochloric acid is present, the dye will be excreted in the urine in approximately 2 hours. The older method of taking serial gastric samples every 15 minutes through a nasogastric tube may still be used.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient receiving total parenteral nutrition (TPN). Which nursing action is most appropriate to implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use a clean technique for site care.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer medications through the TPN line.

questions.rationale: Temperature should be monitored for signs of potential infection. When caring for a patient receiving TPN, sterile technique is used for site care. If solution is given too rapidly, the patient may have circulatory overload. The TPN catheter should never be used for medication administration.

questions.stem: Which patient assessment indicates hyperglycemia with TPN feeding?
questions.rationale: Increased urine output would indicate a probable increase in blood glucose.

questions.stem: The TPN feeding is running at 20 mL and is 1 hour behind schedule. What is the most appropriate initial nursing intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase the flow rate to 22 mL/hr (10%) and inform the charge nurse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reposition the patient to the right side and lower the head of the bed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dilute the thick feeding formula with 10 mL of sterile water and inform the charge nurse.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the event and inform the charge nurse.

questions.rationale: Increasing the speed of giving TPN feedings is never a consideration because doing so will cause hyperglycemia. The event should be documented and the charge nurse informed.

questions.stem: What is the most current endoscopic procedure for examining the small intestine?
questions.rationale: The capsule camera is swallowed and transmits information about the small bowel to a receiver on a belt around the patient’s waist.

questions.stem: A nurse has collected several stool specimens for ova and parasites that are to be sent to the laboratory. What action is most appropriate for the nurse to implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediately take the specimens to the laboratory to be tested for parasites and ova.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take the specimens to the laboratory to be tested for culture and sensitivity and leave them for later pickup.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take the specimens to the refrigerator to be tested later for parasites and ova.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Leave the specimens in a warm place until convenient time to deliver to the laboratory.

questions.rationale: Parasite and ova specimens should be immediately taken to the laboratory while the parasites are still alive. Specimens for evaluating pathogenic organisms should be kept cool.

questions.stem: Stool softeners are prescribed to promote normal elimination of feces. What is the most appropriate way to ensure effectiveness of this type of drug?
questions.rationale: Adequate fluids must be maintained to ensure the liquid is available; otherwise, the fecal mass will remain hard.

questions.stem: Which set of findings best indicates that a patient with intestinal obstruction has achieved normal hydration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulse and blood pressure are within the patient’s norms, mucous membranes are moist, and fluid intake and output are equal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulse rate is strong (at least 60 beats/min), bowel sounds are normal, and a respiratory rate of 22 breaths/min is recorded.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood pressure is within the patient’s norm, the temperature is below normal, and adequate tissue turgor is observed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mucous membranes are moist, the 24-hour fluid intake is higher than the 24-hour output, and the pulse rate is elevated.

questions.rationale: Vital sign within normal limits, moist mucous membranes, and equal fluid intake and output are indicative of normal hydration.

questions.stem: After abdominal surgery, a patient must cough and take deep breaths. How can the nurse best achieve this with this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Withhold analgesics until the patient performs this task.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Help the patient splint the incision with a pillow.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Explain that pneumonia occurs if deep breathing is not carried out every 4 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ambulate the patient 40 feet to increase his need for oxygen.

questions.rationale: Splinting decreases pain by supporting the muscles, thereby allowing for better lung expansion.

questions.stem: A patient is being seen for the first time at a physician’s office. When assisting with the assessment, a nurse notices abdominal striae. What alternative term should the nurse use when the patient asks what it is all over her abdomen?
questions.rationale: Striae is the medical term for stretch marks.

questions.stem: What information about when and where specific digestion of food takes place should be included in a patient teaching plan? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Renin breaks down milk protein in the stomach.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lipase breaks down fats in the stomach.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pepsin begins to break down proteins in the stomach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Liver and pancreatic secretions break down fats in the small bowel.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ptyalin (amylase) breaks down carbohydrates in the colon.

questions.rationale: Ptyalin (amylase) breaks down carbohydrates in the mouth.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient for risk factors that increase the chances of developing oral cancer. Which information from this patient’s history indicates a risk factor?
questions.rationale: Alcohol is statistically proven to be a factor because of irritation of the oral mucosa.

questions.stem: A home health nurse observes a patient with esophageal cancer tilt his head back while eating. What might this cause?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Limiting the types of food that can be consumed

questions.rationale: Tilting the head back not only makes it more difficult to eat, but it also increases the risk of aspiration.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with esophageal surgery who has had stents placed in the esophagus and instructs the patient how best to avoid regurgitation. What should the nurse include in this instruction?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lie on the right side after meals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drink three glasses of fluid with each meal.

questions.rationale: Eating small meals will help with reflux. Keeping the head of the bed raised and not taking in excessive fluid with meals should be practiced.

questions.stem: A nurse is constructing a teaching plan for a patient with a hiatal hernia. What should be included in this plan to help reduce the complaints of heartburn, regurgitation, and eructation?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Eating nothing for several hours before bedtime

questions.rationale: Eating just before bedtime encourages reflux into the hernia and possible aspiration.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old patient who has just been diagnosed with cancer of the stomach says, “I feel blank and numb.” What is the best nursing response?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I’m sure you are considering what you should do now that you have cancer.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Would you like me to bring you a sedative?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “What do you mean when you say ‘blank and numb’?”

questions.rationale: Patients who seem overwhelmed often need to talk and express their feelings even if they are not sure of what their feelings are.

questions.stem: A goal for a patient with gastritis who has experienced nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea is to have a return of normal elimination patterns. Which statement best reflects this goal in a measurable manner?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will have fewer stools.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diarrhea will be controlled and not return.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will have no more than one stool per day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient’s bowel pattern will return to normal.

questions.rationale: Goals are to be specific and measurable. The patient knows his or her normal pattern.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient hemorrhaging from a peptic ulcer when the patient complains of a sharp sudden pain and has a rapidly deteriorating condition. What is the best first action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Roll the patient flat and assess the vital signs.

questions.rationale: With a rapidly deteriorating patient, the nurse should collect all the information that will need to be reported, such as vital signs, patient condition, and subjective complaints.

questions.stem: A long-term care nurse is assisting a well-nourished, 80-year-old resident with the diagnosis of esophageal cancer on methods to deal with dysphagia. What nursing intervention will best help to improve the resident’s condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient to tilt his or her head slightly forward.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assist patient to a semi-Fowler position.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encourage the resident to eat meals in the main dining area.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insert a nasogastric tube for feedings.

questions.rationale: General interventions helpful in managing dysphagia include a quiet, relaxed environment and an erect position with the head slightly tilted forward. If dysphagia prevents adequate nutritional intake, then alternative feeding method must be used and ordered by the health care provider.

questions.stem: A home health nurse is assigned to follow-up on a patient recently diagnosed with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Which primary symptom should the nurse take into consideration when updating the nursing interventions on this patient’s care plan?
questions.rationale: The onset of GERD symptoms may be sudden or gradual. Patients typically report a painful burning sensation that moves up and down, commonly occurs after meals, and is relieved by antacids. Acid regurgitation, intermittent dysphagia, and belching are also common.

questions.stem: A patient experiencing nausea reports to the nurse that she adds ginger root to her morning tea to calm her stomach. Which classification of medication in the patient history alerts the nurse to provide further education?
questions.rationale: Ginger root is effective in calming upset stomach, reducing flatulence, and preventing motion sickness. It enhances the action of anticoagulant and antiplatelet agents.

questions.stem: When assessing the tongue of patient in the outpatient clinic, a nurse observes bluish-white lesions on the mucous membranes. When reviewing the patient history, the nurse notes the patient has been on long-term antibiotic therapy for chronic prostatitis. What should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Candida albicans, a yeast-like fungus, causes the oral condition known as thrush or candidiasis. Bluish-white lesions can be seen on the mucous membranes of the oral cavity. Patients at high risk for candidiasis include those on steroids or long-term antibiotic therapy.

questions.stem: When assisting with the admission of a new resident to a long-term care facility, a nurse notes a current history of peptic ulcer disease. What type of pain should the nurse expect the resident to describe?
questions.rationale: Some patients with gastric ulcers have no pain, but others experience a burning or cramping pain 2 to 4 hours after meals.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with achalasia. What nursing actions should be implemented to help the patient reduce swallowing difficulty? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Identify foods that cause the problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevate the head of the bed at night.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Offer small bites of fresh vegetables.

questions.rationale: Eating rapidly and eating small bites increase swallowing difficulties.

questions.stem: Which instruction given to a patient with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) should lessen discomfort?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take small bites and chew well.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Include dietary fiber in at least two meals per day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drink herbal teas and low-calorie cola drinks.

questions.rationale: Taking small bites, chewing food well, and eating slowly will reduce some of the discomfort associated with IBS. Caffeine and high-fiber foods should be avoided.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a 34-year-old patient admitted with severe diarrhea that has been going on for 2 weeks. What assessment should the nurse anticipate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Edema of lower legs and feet

questions.rationale: Diarrhea of long-standing duration will cause dehydration and fatigue with accompanying hypotension. The patient will most likely be in metabolic acidosis as a result of the loss of the essentially basic bowel contents.

questions.stem: A nurse describes a patient as morbidly obese because the patient has a weight of 387 lb and a height of 2 meters. What is the patient’s body mass index (BMI)?
questions.rationale: Body mass index is calculated by dividing the weight in kilograms by the height in meters squared. Anyone weighing more than 30 kg is considered obese; 387 lb ÷ 2.2 lb/kg = 176 kg; 176 kg ÷ 4 m = BMI of 43.9.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient with an ileostomy as a remedy for ulcerative colitis indicates the need for further teaching?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I should select food with less dietary fiber.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will be glad when the surgeon closes this ileostomy.”

questions.rationale: The ileostomy is permanent. The diet of a person prone to ulcerative colitis is low roughage, no milk products, and no alcohol.

questions.stem: A nurse identifies a risk factor in an older man that places him at risk for developing diverticulosis. What patient information indicates such a risk factor?

choices.label: C
choices.text: History of using nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)

questions.rationale: A low-fiber diet increases the risk for diverticulitis.

questions.stem: Which foods should an individual with diverticulosis avoid?
questions.rationale: Foods containing seeds or small hard particles could become lodged in small pouches.

questions.stem: Colonoscopy results indicate the diagnosis of irritable bowel disease (IBD) in a patient admitted to the hospital with diarrhea. What information should the nurse include when preparing patient education regarding diet?
questions.rationale: A low-roughage diet without milk products is prescribed for mild to moderate IBD.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient diagnosed with diverticulosis and assesses a temperature of 102.4 F and abdominal rigidity. What should the nurse be aware is the most likely cause of these signs and symptoms?
questions.rationale: The nurse caring for a patient diagnosed with diverticulosis should be alert for signs of perforation including fever, abdominal distention, and rigidity.

questions.stem: A patient is diagnosed with cancer of the large intestine. What is the most likely initial recommended medical intervention?
questions.rationale: Colorectal cancers are usually initially treated surgically.

questions.stem: A nurse is performing an assessment of a patient after an abdominoperineal resection. How many incision sites will be present?
questions.rationale: After an abdominoperineal resection, the patient will have three incisions: one on the abdomen, a second for the colostomy, and a third on the perineum.

questions.stem: A patient reports severe pain after an abdominoperineal resection. What position should the nurse assist this patient into in order to promote comfort?
questions.rationale: Pain is severe for several days after an abdominoperineal resection. At first, the patient will be most comfortable in a side-lying position.

questions.stem: A nurse provides education to a patient after a hemorrhoidectomy. Which statement by the patient demonstrates the need for further instruction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Sitz baths are ordered to soothe the area.”

questions.rationale: After hemorrhoidectomy, the patient should be encouraged to ingest a high-fiber diet and drink plenty of fluids to promote regular, soft stools.

questions.stem: A nurse notes a diagnosis of pilonidal cyst on a patient’s admission assessment. What anatomical location should the nurse expect to assess this cyst?
questions.rationale: A pilonidal cyst is located in the sacrococcygeal area.

questions.stem: A home health nurse is instructing an older adult patient regarding dietary changes to help prevent constipation. What changes should the nurse indicate when providing this education? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: A decrease in sugar intake will help stem diarrhea.

questions.stem: For which complication should a nurse be careful to monitor a patient after a liver biopsy?
questions.rationale: Liver biopsy places the patient at risk for hemorrhage. Liver disorders make patients especially vulnerable to hemorrhage.

questions.stem: What is pruritus related to in the patient diagnosed with hepatitis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Poor appetite and therefore poor protein intake

choices.label: C
choices.text: Accumulation of bile salts under the skin

questions.rationale: Bile salts accumulate under the skin, causing irritation.

questions.stem: A young woman with severe jaundice has an altered body image. The patient says, “Will I always be this horrible color?” What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes, but your sclera will return to their previous white color.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “No. The color will fade gradually as liver inflammation decreases.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Yes, but cosmetics can disguise the color.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “No. The color will change to freckles.”

questions.rationale: Jaundice causes patients to be self-conscious and reclusive because of the change in physical appearance. Patients can be reassured that the color improves as liver function improves, usually in 2 to 4 weeks.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse implement to prevent complications in a patient with hepatitis who has been prescribed bed rest?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Raise the knee gatch to prevent the patient from sliding down in bed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide undisturbed periods of 6 hours to encourage rest.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage turning, coughing, and deep breathing every 2 hours.

questions.rationale: The nurse must encourage measures that will prevent pneumonia and improve impaired skin integrity because of the increased risk factors associated with bed rest.

questions.stem: Which sign indicates that the need for increased fluid intake would be contraindicated in a patient diagnosed with a hepatic disorder?
questions.rationale: Edema may indicate fluid overload; therefore, question the intake, as well as electrolyte and cardiac status.

questions.stem: What intervention should a nurse implement when assessing a patient with jaundice who has been given the nursing diagnosis of disrupted skin integrity?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply mittens or socks to the hands.

questions.rationale: Jaundice causes itching, which can cause the patient to scratch and create a break in the skin. Mittens provide some comfort without causing further skin impairment.

questions.stem: Which vaccination does the Occupational Health and Safety Administration (OSHA) require all health care providers to receive?
questions.rationale: OSHA requires that all health care providers be vaccinated against hepatitis B.

questions.stem: What is the meaning of a dropping bilirubin level in a patient diagnosed with hepatitis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Red blood cell destruction is decreasing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Kidneys are compensating for liver dysfunction.

questions.rationale: As liver function improves, the bilirubin level will decrease because of the liver’s ability to conjugate and excrete the bilirubin. The flow of bile out of the liver increases.

questions.stem: A goal of medical treatment for patients with cirrhosis is to prevent complications and limit cell damage. A major approach is to promote rest. What rationale supports this approach?
questions.rationale: With rest, the liver will regenerate healthy tissue and return to normal functioning. Rest must include other measures to promote healing, such as dietary measures and no alcohol.

questions.stem: What is necessary to restrict when the ammonia level of a patient diagnosed with cirrhosis continues to rise?
questions.rationale: Ammonia is the waste product of protein breakdown. Decreasing protein intake will decrease the end product.

questions.stem: What actions should a nurse implement to correctly assess the progress of ascites on a daily basis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Daily weights and abdominal girth measurements

questions.rationale: Daily weights and abdominal girth measurements will accurately measure the fluid accumulating in the peritoneal cavity.

questions.stem: A patient with ascites is scheduled for a LeVeen peritoneal-venous shunt. The patient asks why this needs to be done instead of a paracentesis. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It helps the kidneys retain needed sodium.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It will decrease the need for analgesics.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “This procedure will prevent the loss of protein.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The risk of infection is lessened with this procedure.”

questions.rationale: Fluids containing protein are returned to the vascular compartment to retain important elements such as albumin. The retention of albumin reduces fluid accumulation.

questions.stem: A high ammonia level contributes to hepatic encephalopathy. Which nursing implementation needs to be added to the nursing care plan as this level continues to increase?
questions.rationale: As the ammonia level rises, the patient becomes at greater risk for confusion and hepatic coma related to encephalopathy.

questions.stem: A nurse is educating a patient diagnosed with hepatitis A. What should the nurse instruct this patient to avoid sharing?
questions.rationale: Hepatitis A is spread from contact with saliva, which can be transmitted by shared food or drinks.

questions.stem: Which dietary selection should lead the nurse to conclude that the dietary teaching is successful for a patient on a low-sodium diet?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hotdog on a bun with pickle relish and skim milk

choices.label: C
choices.text: Baked chicken, white rice, and apple juice

choices.label: D
choices.text: Peanut butter and jelly sandwich with tomato soup

questions.rationale: A meal of baked chicken, white rice, and apple juice has the lowest sodium levels.

questions.stem: Which nursing measure takes priority in relation to the care of a patient with a gastroesophageal balloon tube?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Withhold medications that could decrease restlessness.

questions.rationale: Because of the close proximity of the esophagus and trachea, any upward movement of the tube could cause airway obstruction.

questions.stem: Which instruction should be given to a patient with portal hypertension to reduce the threat of hemorrhage?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid straining to have a bowel movement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use an electric razor to shave.

questions.rationale: Straining can increase pressure and may cause the dilated vessels in the gastrointestinal tract to bleed. Shaving with an electric razor does not prevent serious bleeding.

questions.stem: What precaution should a nurse initiate when caring for a patient with hepatitis B?
questions.rationale: Standard Precautions protect the nurse from organisms that may be in all body fluids.

questions.stem: A patient was positive for hepatitis B virus, although she had the disease 4 years ago and now is symptom free. What is the nurse aware is true regarding this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Is likely to have hepatitis B again

choices.label: C
choices.text: Is an infectious carrier and always will be

choices.label: D
choices.text: Is at risk for hepatitis E

questions.rationale: A certain percentage of persons who have had hepatitis B convert to carriers. They have the live virus, which causes no symptoms in them, but they are able to transmit the disease and always will be infectious.

questions.stem: A patient in acute pain is admitted with pancreatitis. A nurse reviews a laboratory report showing an elevation that is diagnostic for acute pancreatitis. Which laboratory report did the nurse most likely review?
questions.rationale: Serum amylase is the most significant of the diagnostic findings.

questions.stem: What is the highest nursing priority outcome when planning the care for the patient with pancreatitis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient claims satisfaction with pain control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient states an understanding of medications needed on discharge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient’s activity level tolerance shows an increase.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient can maintain a normal bowel pattern.

questions.rationale: Pain control is the most important priority.

questions.stem: What should a nurse often find in the medical history of a patient diagnosed with pancreatic disease?
questions.rationale: Pancreatic disease is often related to alcohol abuse.

questions.stem: Which observation by a nurse would indicate blocked flow of bile from the liver to the intestine?
questions.rationale: Bile is unable to get to feces to give it the normal brown color.

questions.stem: Which chronic condition is related to the presence of chronic pancreatitis?
questions.rationale: Patients with chronic pancreatitis are at risk for developing DM because of the destruction of the insulin-secreting cells in the pancreas.

questions.stem: Which risk is significantly increased in patients diagnosed with liver disease?
questions.rationale: Because many drugs are metabolized in the liver and a diseased liver does not adequately clear the system of drugs, drug toxicity is an ongoing problem.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include in the discharge teaching for a patient after a laparoscopic procedure for cholelithiasis?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expect light-colored stools for several days.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep dressing over the T-tube dry.

questions.rationale: After the laparoscopic procedure, the patient is to follow a low-fat diet and take fat-soluble vitamins. Placement of the T-tube is not done with the laparoscopic procedure.

questions.stem: A nurse reminds a patient with liver disease that the level of ______ in the blood is an indicator of the how well the liver is functioning.
questions.rationale: The level of indirect bilirubin indicates the effectiveness of the metabolism of proteins by the liver.

questions.stem: In assessing a dark-skinned patient for jaundice, the nurse would assess the ______ for a yellow color.
questions.rationale: Jaundice can be assessed by the yellow pigment on the sclera of a dark-skinned person.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient with renal impairment. Which facial characteristic is a sign of fluid retention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Broken blood vessels around the nose

questions.rationale: Periorbital edema is a sign of fluid retention. Because the patient with renal impairment has generalized edema, this facial feature is extremely significant in assessing edema.

questions.stem: What laboratory value change should indicate to a nurse that a patient with renal failure has entered the oliguric stage?
questions.rationale: In the oliguric stage of renal failure, the urine output decreases to less than 400 mL/day; the BUN, creatinine, and potassium increase; and the serum calcium decreases. The patient becomes hypervolemic as the urine osmolality increases.

questions.stem: What should nursing care focus on when caring for a patient with a ureteral catheter in place after the removal of a kidney stone?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Including ureteral output with the bladder output

questions.rationale: Patency of the ureteral catheter is essential to prevent injury to the kidney. The patient is on bed rest until the ureteral catheter is removed. The output from the ureteral catheter is measured and recorded separately, and irrigation, if performed, is not done on a regular schedule and is not more than 5 mL.

questions.stem: What is true about the urine osmolality when the kidney is adequately functioning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Equal to the osmolality of the serum

choices.label: C
choices.text: In a ratio of 10:1 with the serum

choices.label: D
choices.text: Equal to the excretion of urea

questions.rationale: If the blood osmolality is high, the kidneys need to dilute the blood and excrete more concentrated urine, and the reverse is true. The osmolality of the serum and the urine should be equal.

questions.stem: Which urine test provides the most accurate measurement of renal function?
questions.rationale: Creatinine is not affected by diet, hydration, or liver function and is a better measurement of liver function than the BUN.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient after urinary diversion surgery. What postoperative nursing assessment is the priority?
questions.rationale: The bowel is manipulated during urinary diversion surgeries and frequently leads to the patient with a paralytic ileus.

questions.stem: A nurse is performing frequent catheterizations for residual urine. What causes the greatest concern for the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Introduction of pathogens into the bladder

choices.label: B
choices.text: Frequent genital exposure of the patient

questions.rationale: The frequency of introducing a catheter into the bladder offers a very real risk of infection.

questions.stem: A patient has a nephrostomy tube that has been inserted because of an obstruction in the ureter. What special precautions in the care of the nephrostomy tube should the nurse implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clamping every 2 hours to allow expansion of the kidney pelvis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instilling no more than 50 mL of sterile water if sterile irrigations are ordered

choices.label: C
choices.text: Being certain the tube is connected, not kinked, or not clamped to ensure that it continually drains

choices.label: D
choices.text: Leaving the nephrostomy site open to air

questions.rationale: Because of the small capacity of the renal pelvis, drainage must be continuous; otherwise, the urine may back up and destroy the kidney.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient on dialysis, taking gentamicin (Garamycin), should cause the nurse the most concern?

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I’ve had diarrhea once or twice today.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I’m thirsty. I can’t get enough water.”

questions.rationale: Garamycin is ototoxic. Indication of hearing impairment suggests drug toxicity.

questions.stem: Erythropoietin is a hormone produced by the kidney. What will a deficiency of erythropoietin in a patient in chronic renal failure result in?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diminished immunologic function with fewer white blood cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated lipid levels in the bloodstream, contributing to accelerated atherosclerosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anemia as a result of the diminished number of red blood cells being produced

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypertension as a result of the increased, concentrated blood volume

questions.rationale: Erythropoietin is excreted by the kidneys and stimulates bone marrow to produce red blood cells.

questions.stem: A nurse is planning the care for an older adult patient. Which age-related changes in kidney function should the nurse consider when providing care to this patient? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreasing ability to concentrate or dilute urine

questions.rationale: Sclerosis of renal blood vessels, decreasing glomerular filtration, decreasing ability to concentrate urine, and decreasing erythropoietin are associated with aging.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with a Foley catheter. What actions should the nurse implement to decrease this patient’s risk for infection? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep the bag below the level of the bed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide perineal care twice a day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use Standard Precautions when handling urine and tubing.

questions.rationale: Keeping the bag below the level of the bed, providing perineal care twice daily, and using Standard Precautions will assist in decreasing infection risk. Tubing is only flushed with a physician’s order if required. The drainage system should be closed.

questions.stem: A nurse reads the serum calcium laboratory report of a patient as 4.2 mEq/L. Which symptoms should the nurse anticipate that the patient might exhibit? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Symptoms of hypocalcemia include irritability, tingling sensations, tetany, muscle twitching, and muscle contractions.

questions.stem: A patient comes to the medical clinic with complaints of urgency, frequency, pain in the area of the symphysis pubis, and dark cloudy urine. What should the nurse suspect that this patient has?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Urinary calculi, probably located in the ureter

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interstitial cystitis (although rare in a male patient)

questions.rationale: Cystitis causes urgency, dysuria, and pain behind the symphysis pubis. Cystitis is usually caused by bacterial infection.

questions.stem: A patient who has cystitis has been told to drink at least 30 mL for each kilogram of body weight. Her weight is 154 lb. How many mL/day should the nurse instruct the patient to drink?
A home health patient diagnosed with cystitis has been prescribed the medication phenazopyridine (Pyridium). When providing patient teaching, what should the nurse caution the patient about?
questions.rationale: Pyridium causes the urine to be a bright orange color, which can stain clothing.

questions.stem: A nurse is collecting data from a hospital patient who has been admitted with pyelonephritis. He is acutely ill with a high fever, chills, nausea, and vomiting. He also has severe pain in the flank area. What is the primary goal of treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide adequate nutrition with a stable body weight.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide adequate hydration with pulse and blood pressure within patient norms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give pain relief with analgesics and antispasmodics.

choices.label: D
