choices.text: Prevent further damage to his kidneys that could lead to renal failure.

questions.rationale: Pyelonephritis can cause scarring of the renal parenchyma and result in atrophy of the affected kidney, which means the kidney is failing.

questions.stem: What is the usual cause of the autoimmune disease of acute glomerulonephritis?
questions.rationale: The cause is an upper respiratory infection caused by a beta-hemolytic Streptococcus.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with acute glomerulonephritis. What should the nurse be aware that the inflammation of the capillary loops in the glomeruli will lead to?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Low levels of BUN and creatinine

questions.rationale: The inflammatory process in the glomeruli decreases the filtration rate, and the blood volume increases, raising the patient’s blood pressure.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old patient with acute glomerulonephritis complains of boredom with bed rest and asks when he can become more active. He asks, “What has to happen for me to get off of bed rest?” What is the most accurate statement by the nurse?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood pressure drops to normal levels.

questions.rationale: Bed rest, when ordered, is for the protection of the patient because of high blood pressure. Bed rest will continue until the treatment causes diuresis and a drop in the blood pressure.

questions.stem: What discharge teaching is appropriate for the nurse to provide to a patient who has had a lithotripsy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check for edema of the legs and ankles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Watch for stone debris in the urine in 1 to 4 weeks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remain on restricted activity for a week.

questions.rationale: The stones that have shattered with the sound waves will show up as debris in 1 to 4 weeks. Fluid intake is encouraged, and activity is resumed the next day. Edema is not a concern.

questions.stem: Which outcome is most necessary for a patient diagnosed with renal calculi?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient states an awareness of signs and symptoms of kidney stones and knows where to find pain relief.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will measure intake and output so that they will be approximately equal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will avoid infections and situations that would increase stress.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient is able to describe measures to prevent recurrence of calculi.

questions.rationale: Recurrence of renal calculi is common. The patient needs to possess the information necessary to understand the formation of stones to reduce the risk of their recurrence.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with an atrioventricular (AV) fistula in the forearm and assesses that a trill is absent when palpating the venous side of the fistula. What action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inject the ordered amount of heparin into the fistula.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply warm compresses and lower the arm below the heart level.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Send the patient to dialysis for remedy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Report to the charge nurse that the fistula is occluded.

questions.rationale: If the trill is absent, the fistula is occluded and should be reported. Dialysis is not possible with the occlusion. Injecting the shunt is not in the scope of practice of the licensed practical nurse (LPN). Warm compresses are not helpful.

questions.stem: A patient on dialysis asks why he is receiving aluminum hydroxide gel (Amphojel), a phosphate binder, for his renal disorder. What should the nurse explain regarding the action of that Amphojel?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Calms the frequent upset stomach experienced by patients on dialysis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Binds with phosphorus to increase the serum calcium level

choices.label: D
choices.text: Corrects the pH of the bowel

questions.rationale: Amphojel binds phosphorus, which increases the serum calcium level and decreases hypocalcemia.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient on dialysis, taking gentamicin (Garamycin), should cause the nurse the most concern?

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I’ve had diarrhea once or twice today.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I’m thirsty. I can’t get enough water.”

questions.rationale: Garamycin is ototoxic. Indication of hearing impairment suggests drug toxicity.

questions.stem: A family member of a patient who has returned to the special unit after renal transplantation is alarmed by blood in the urine of the patient. What is the nurse’s best explanation when explaining the reason for hematuria in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It is related to the immunosuppressant drugs taken before transplantation.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It is caused by dye injected during surgery.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It is caused by a small vessel that may be bleeding but will coagulate as urine flow increases.”

questions.rationale: Blood in the urine is an expected postoperative expectation and will gradually clear up.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy tells a nurse that he wants to give his kidney to his grandfather. How many years of age should the nurse explain that kidney donors must be?
questions.rationale: The donor must be at least 18 years old, have no systemic disease, and have normal renal function.

questions.stem: A patient with chronic renal failure is to begin renal dialysis treatment and asks for advice about which type of dialysis would be best. The patient is considering peritoneal dialysis because it is less expensive and has fewer dietary and fluid restrictions. What is the most accurate information for the nurse to provide about peritoneal dialysis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It gives more independence and more closely resembles normal kidney function.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a lot more work than hemodialysis, in which the health care staff takes care of everything.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It usually does not work very well and has many complications, such as a high blood sugar level.

questions.rationale: Peritoneal dialysis increases independence and resembles normal kidney function. It can be performed in any hospital or at home.

questions.stem: Why are patients diagnosed with chronic renal failure and on dialysis prone to injury?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Multiple life changes and hormone deficiencies

questions.rationale: Loss of calcium from the bones leaves them weak, and the lack of sensation in the hands and feet leaves patients with a lack of proprioception. Realizing these factors, the nurse can draw up implementations to help prevent injuries.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses a Grey Turner sign in a patient who was admitted 2 days earlier after an automobile accident. What does this finding indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retroperitoneal bleeding and bruising over the flank

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bladder spasms on palpation of abdomen

questions.rationale: The Grey Turner sign is bruising over the flank and retroperitoneal bleeding. This is observed in blunt trauma to the kidney.

questions.stem: A female patient reports very painful urethritis. What should the home health care nurse question the patient about the use of? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Bath additives and vaginal sprays are causative for urethritis. Vitamins, herbal preparations, and exercise machinery are noncontributory.

questions.stem: The major risk of peritoneal dialysis is ______.
questions.rationale: Peritonitis is the major risk of peritoneal dialysis.

questions.stem: A nurse is aware that if a ureter is blocked by a kidney stone, the urine backs up into the kidney, causing ______.
questions.rationale: Hydronephrosis results when a ureter is obstructed and urine backs up into the pelvis of the kidney. If unrelieved, this condition will require the removal of the kidney.

questions.stem: A nurse is collecting a health history from a patient with a connective tissue disease. What is the most important inquiry by the nurse?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Last time the patient had his or her blood tested

choices.label: C
choices.text: History of a prior injury to a specific body part

questions.rationale: Previous injuries may be relevant to a patient’s current problem.

questions.stem: A nurse is educating an osteoporotic patient taking alendronate (Fosamax). Which instruction should the nurse stress?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid the use of supplemental vitamin D.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sit or stand for 30 minutes after administration.

questions.rationale: After taking the drug Fosamax with 8 oz of fluid, the patient should sit or stand for 30 minutes so the drug will be evenly distributed. The drug is taken on an empty stomach.

questions.stem: Which diagnostic test result should the nurse expect for a patient with polymyositis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Muscle biopsy positive for muscle degeneration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) blood test result

choices.label: C
choices.text: Positive 24-hour urine test result for urate crystals

choices.label: D
choices.text: Urate crystals in the synovial fluid

questions.rationale: A biopsy, positive for muscle degeneration, is the only result that pertains to polymyositis.

questions.stem: A nurse is educating a patient with gout about the medication probenecid (Benemid). What active effect should the nurse relay when explaining why this medication is prescribed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduces inflammation in the affected joint

questions.rationale: Probenecid (Benemid) increases the excretion of uric acid to reduce the symptoms of gout.

questions.stem: The physician orders antinuclear antibodies for a patient with suspected rheumatoid arthritis. What information will the nurse provide to the patient in preparation for this study?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Refrain from exercise the day before test.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Do not take medications 24 hours before test.

questions.rationale: Patients should be instructed to fast for 8 hours before antinuclear antibodies.

questions.stem: A patient is preparing for tomography as ordered to enhance visualization of tissue hidden by bone. What assessment by the nurse should be immediately addressed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: History of recent urinary tract infection

questions.rationale: Tomography requires lying in a cylindric scanner. The nurse should assess for claustrophobia and inform the radiologist.

questions.stem: A nurse questions an older patient about the age-related changes she has experienced in her connective tissue, which have lessened her mobility. What do these changes most commonly include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Loss of bone mass, which may cause fragile bones

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decline in muscle mass, causing loss of strength

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bony deposits in the joints, causing pain and altered movement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of cartilage, causing more friction in joints

questions.rationale: Reduced energy, although observed in older adults, is not caused by a change in connective tissue.

questions.stem: Immobility reduces muscle contractions and can produce muscle ______.
questions.rationale: Immobility reduces muscle contractions and can produce muscle atrophy.

questions.stem: A(n) ______ measures the range of movement of the joints.
questions.rationale: A goniometer measures the range of movement of the joints.

questions.stem: ______ uses sound waves to determine the presence of pulses in the extremities.
questions.rationale: Doppler ultrasound uses sound waves to determine the presence of pulses in the extremities.

questions.stem: A 51-year-old professional tennis instructor is newly diagnosed with osteoarthritis. What is the nurse’s best explanation to the patient when asked what this diagnosis means?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of antibodies in the synovial fluid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dislocation of the patella over the tibia

questions.rationale: Degeneration of articular cartilage is one of the pathophysiologic changes of arthritis.

questions.stem: A nurse explains to a patient with rheumatoid arthritis that the drug leflunomide (Arava) is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD). What is the action of this medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retards the progress of the disease

questions.rationale: Arava is a DMARD and slows the progression of the disease.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with osteoarthritis. What is the best recommendation by the nurse to this patient to control chronic pain?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer analgesics as prescribed on a routine basis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Plan activities with no rest periods to complete the activities quickly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wear high-heeled shoes to keep the body in alignment.

questions.rationale: The routine administration of prescribed analgesic medications is the most appropriate treatment for chronic pain.

questions.stem: An older patient with osteoarthritis complains of stomach discomfort and shortness of breath after years of taking aspirin for pain relief. What change in pain control medication would be most appropriate for the home health care nurse to suggest?
questions.rationale: The use of NSAIDs is less irritating than aspirin or glucocorticoids. Mild exercise is good but not for pain relief.

questions.stem: A home health care nurse is visiting a patient after a total hip replacement. What should the nurse include when teaching the patient how to protect the new joint?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Put an extension on the toilet seat.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keep the legs crossed when at rest.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Frequently change positions from side to side.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Slowly pull the knee to the chest twice a day to stretch the hip abductors.

questions.rationale: Placing an extender on the toilet seat will assist in the objective of not flexing the hip more than 90 degrees. Crossing the legs adducts the hip, which is contraindicated.

questions.stem: After a knee replacement, an 87-year-old patient rejects the use of the continuous passive motion (CPM) machine, saying, “I did not march when I was a child, and I am not marching now.” What benefits of CPM should the nurse point out to encourage patient use?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase in circulation in the new joint

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increase in flexibility for the new joint

questions.rationale: The CPM machine’s major benefit is to increase flexibility, although it does cause discomfort. No strength-building potential is present with passive motion.

questions.stem: What do connective tissue diseases affect?
questions.rationale: Connective tissue diseases affect bones, ligaments, cartilage, and tendons.

questions.stem: Which patient is most likely to develop a connective tissue disease?
questions.rationale: Women have a greater chance than men of developing connective tissue disease.

questions.stem: Which is true regarding connective tissue function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Helps provide a source of storage for calcium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stores hormones in the pores of bone tissue

questions.rationale: Providing protection is a function of connective tissue.

questions.stem: Which characteristic should a nurse recognize as diagnostic of rheumatoid arthritis?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evening stiffness that improves with activity

questions.rationale: Symmetric bilateral joint swelling is a classic symptom of rheumatoid arthritis.

questions.stem: A patient asks why systemic glucocorticoid medications are used as the last choice for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. What is the nurse’s most informative reply?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The other drugs are just as effective and work in similar ways.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “They are used as a last choice or for short periods because they have many side effects.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Those drugs are given three or four times daily, which is more difficult for patients to remember.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “A higher incidence of vomiting occurs with prolonged use.”

questions.rationale: Glucocorticoids are used as a last choice because they have many side effects.

questions.stem: A nurse, in conjunction with a patient, establishes a plan to treat the pain associated with arthritis. What is the most effective strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid exercise to spare painful joints.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply warm, moist compresses before doing activity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid assistive devices that encourage dependence.

questions.rationale: Applying heat before exercise loosens the joints and decreases pain.

questions.stem: What is the best physiologic reason for a patient with osteoporosis to maintain a regular exercise regimen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Involves the patient in her or his own care

choices.label: D
choices.text: Promotes bone formation and improves strength

questions.rationale: Regular exercise promotes bone formation, which is important for patients with osteoporosis for physiologic reasons.

questions.stem: A nurse is organizing a teaching plan for a patient with gout. What should the nurse caution this patient he is at an increased risk for?
questions.rationale: The threat of kidney stones is a lifelong problem for patients with gout. Tophi are symptomatic of the disease but are not a complication. Facial lesions and visual disturbances are noncontributory.

questions.stem: A nurse is educating a patient with gout about a low-purine diet. Which food choice by the patient would indicate the need for further teaching?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Seafood platter with scallops and mussels

questions.rationale: Seafood, such as scallops and mussels, are high in purine.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient immediately after total knee replacement surgery. What assessment requires priority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Quality of pulses in the affected limb

questions.rationale: Assessments related to postoperative circulatory efficiency are priority assessments.

questions.stem: Inadequate nutrition is the patient problem applicable to a patient with progressive systemic sclerosis. What is the most important point for the nurse to teach this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eat three large meals spaced throughout the day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schedule rest periods to prevent overtiring.

questions.rationale: Smaller, more frequent meals may be better tolerated by a patient who has esophageal involvement.

questions.stem: What instruction should a nurse include in a teaching plan for a patient with carpal tunnel syndrome?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Splinting to prevent flexion and hyperextension

questions.rationale: Resting and supporting the joint are first-line treatments.

questions.stem: Two days after a total hip replacement, a patient is being discharged. Which statement indicates that the patient understands the discharge teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I can sit comfortably with my legs crossed.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will ask my husband to tie my shoes for me.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I am glad I won’t have to use that bulky pillow between my legs at night.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “My straight dining room chair will be helpful when I do the hip flexion exercises.”

questions.rationale: If the patient bends over to tie her own shoes, her hips would have more than 90 degrees of flexion.

questions.stem: What action would best benefit the patient diagnosed with bursitis of the shoulder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lifting a 5-lb weight as a daily exercise

choices.label: B
choices.text: Walking the fingers of the affected arm up the wall

choices.label: C
choices.text: Splinting the affected arm to keep the shoulder immobile

choices.label: D
choices.text: Performing gentle push-ups on the floor

questions.rationale: Walking the fingers up the wall is a gentle exercise to increase range of motion.

questions.stem: What are the goals of therapy for patients with rheumatic arthritis? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plan frequent periods of bed rest.

questions.rationale: Bed rest of any long period increases the problems of immobility.

questions.stem: What actions would be best for patients with osteoarthritis to seek the assistance of physical therapy? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Instruction with a transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) unit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Measures to increase range of motion

questions.rationale: Isotonic exercises place increased stress on the joints.

questions.stem: What signs of progressive systemic sclerosis does the anonym CREST represent? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: CREST stands for calcinosis, Raynaud phenomenon, esophageal dysfunction, sclerodactyly, and telangiectasis.

questions.stem: To decrease osteoporosis, a nurse explains that women can benefit from ______ for 15 years after the onset of menopause.
questions.rationale: A program of oral estrogen replacement therapy can decrease the occurrence of osteoporosis.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses ischemic spots around the nail beds of a patient with rheumatoid arthritis and recognizes that these are a complication of medical diagnosis, rheumatoid arthritis, related to ______.
questions.rationale: Vasculitis occurs when the vessels become inflamed and cause ischemia and necrosis.

questions.stem: A nurse clarifies that a postmenopausal woman who is not taking hormone replacement therapy should take ______ mg elemental calcium on a daily basis. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: Women who are not taking hormone replacements need calcium, 1500 mg/day.

questions.stem: Two days after surgery for a crushed pelvis, a certified nursing assistant (CNA) reports that the patient is complaining of a shortness of breath and is demonstrating signs of confusion and restlessness. What should a nurse suspect, from these signs alone, that the patient has developed?
questions.rationale: These are the classic symptoms of a fat embolus that has escaped from the crushed marrow.

questions.stem: What should a nurse teach an older patient with a newly casted Colles fracture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply cool compresses to the cast.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Let the hand and arm dangle to increase the drainage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep the hand immobile to reduce swelling.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Move the shoulders to reduce contractures.

questions.rationale: Movement of the shoulders will help decrease the threat of contracture from immobility.

questions.stem: A patient who has osteomyelitis after multiple fractures inquires what the physician meant when he said that surgery would follow the antibiotic therapy. What is the nurse’s most helpful reply to explain why this surgery will be performed?

choices.label: B
choices.text: To close the open draining wound

choices.label: C
choices.text: To close the area with casting material

questions.rationale: After the antibiotic has controlled the infection in the bone, surgery will be performed to remove the dead bone.

questions.stem: A patient with a crushed forearm cannot get pain relief with opioid medications. The injury is swollen, cool, and cyanotic, with weak distal pulses. What should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Compartment syndrome may occur after a massive injury or an inappropriately tight cast. The tissues become swollen to the point that they cut off their own circulation.

questions.stem: A patient who sustained a simple fracture of the left fibula 7 days earlier asks in what stage of bone healing he might be. What stage of healing should the nurse relay to the patient?
questions.rationale: Callus formation occurs at the end of the first week after injury.

questions.stem: Which assessment is of the greatest concern to a nurse when caring for a patient just admitted with a pelvic fracture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pain level rating of 8 on a scale of 1 to 10

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence of bruising along the patient’s hips and buttocks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complaints of the need for back care from resting in bed

questions.rationale: The absence of urinary output could indicate a perforated bladder.

questions.stem: An older woman falls down at church and immediately complains of severe pain in her left hip. Which observation is recognized as the cardinal sign of a fractured hip?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shortened left leg compared with the right

choices.label: C
choices.text: Internal rotation of the left leg

questions.rationale: The classic sign of a fractured hip is a shortened limb on the affected side, with an externally rotated limb.

questions.stem: A patient has just had a plaster of Paris upper extremity cast placed because of a fractured radius. Which statement indicates that the patient understands the discharge teaching related to cast care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “When I get home, I will remove some of the padding if it feels tight so my fingers don’t swell.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “When I get home, I will wrap the cast in plastic so it will conserve the heat.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “When I get home, I will use a spoon handle to scratch inside if my arm itches.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “When I get home, I am going to rest in bed with my arm elevated above my heart.”

questions.rationale: Resting with the limb elevated above the heart helps prevent swelling.

questions.stem: What special precaution should a nurse implement when assisting with the application of a short arm plaster cast?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dampen the skin to make the stockinette adhere.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tape the arm before applying the stockinette.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Smooth the stockinette to prevent a pressure ulcer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Roll the stockinette tightly above and below the margins of the cast.

questions.rationale: The stockinette is smoothed on the limb before applying the casting material to help reduce the threat of a pressure ulcer.

questions.stem: An older patient who sustained a fractured hip and femur in a motor vehicle accident is to be in Russell traction for several weeks. What should be the focus of care for the nurse?
questions.rationale: DVT is a threat for the person who is going to experience lengthy periods of inactivity. Although nutrition, pain relief, and boredom will be nursing concerns as well, the prevention of DVT is the priority.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old retired teacher with a history of osteoporosis has fallen in her bathroom and sustained a subcapital femoral fracture. She is scheduled for an open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) procedure in the morning. Which type of traction will most likely be implemented?
questions.rationale: Buck traction is used to stabilize the fracture. The other options are not applicable.

questions.stem: A nurse is told that a patient has a compound comminuted fracture. What characteristic of the bone in this type of fracture causes the nurse to be concerned?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is bent but not completely broken, and the bent piece protrudes through the skin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is compressed, and bone pieces protrude through the skin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is twisted, and the fragments are separated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is broken into two or more pieces, and bone fragments protrude through the skin.

questions.rationale: A compound comminuted fracture is a severe fracture with the bone broken in two or more pieces, with the pieces broken into small fragments and a portion of the bone protruding through the skin.

questions.stem: A patient with bilateral avascular necrosis of the hips is to walk with crutches using a four-point gait for 6 weeks after her bone decompression surgeries. Which statement would indicate that the patient understands this technique?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The axillary bars on the crutches should support my weight when I walk.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will move both crutches and then swing my legs to the crutches—2 and 2 equals 4!”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I will move my right crutch and then my left leg and then the left crutch and my right leg.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I will move both crutches and then swing my legs through the crutches together.”

questions.rationale: This option describes the correct sequence for a four-point gait, which allows bearing of weight and one foot to be placed in front of the other.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse implement when caring for a patient diagnosed with a compound fracture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Limit narcotics for 8 hours after surgery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Monitor the patient’s respirations every hour.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess for pulses distal to the injury.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Verify that the patient is not allergic to sulfa.

questions.rationale: Assessing for pulses distal to the injury is performed to monitor for inadequate circulation.

questions.stem: Assistive devices such as canes, crutches, and walkers are used for people who need to limit weight-bearing activities on joints. Which statement by a nurse best illustrates an understanding of the appropriate use of these devices?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Canes provide minimal support and balance and are carried on the unaffected side.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “When using a cane, slide it as you go to decrease the arm strain.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “A three-point gait is used when walking with a walker.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “When using crutches, the unaffected leg goes down the steps first.”

questions.rationale: “Canes provide minimal support and balance and are carried on the unaffected side” is the only true statement.

questions.stem: Which patient is most appropriate for a nurse to refer to home health care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A married man with a laundry room on the first floor

choices.label: B
choices.text: A single woman with a bedroom in a rooming house

choices.label: C
choices.text: A student living in a college dormitory but going home to stay with parents

choices.label: D
choices.text: A woman staying with her daughter and son-in-law at their one-story home

questions.rationale: The patient will need help with laundry and other activities of daily living.

questions.stem: What should a nurse who is documenting and reporting the signs and symptoms of an infection underneath a cast include in the medical record?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Full pulses and absence of pain

questions.rationale: Elevated temperature on the affected extremity may be a symptom of an infection under the cast.

questions.stem: Which patient problem has the highest priority after surgery for the open reduction and external fixation of an ankle?
questions.rationale: Potential for infection would have the highest priority because bone infections are serious complications.

questions.stem: A patient in a full body cast (spica) complains of nausea and abdominal distention. What potential complication should a licensed vocational nurse (LVN) suspect?
questions.rationale: Cast syndrome is an uncommon complication for a person in a spica cast, in which compression of a portion of the duodenum occurs between the mesenteric artery and the spinal column.

questions.stem: An older adult patient is at risk for constipation after sustaining a pelvic fracture. Which nutritional suggestion by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Select food with high sodium content.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid foods high in dietary fiber.

choices.label: C
choices.text: While immobilized, drink at least 2 to 3 L of fluids daily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Include milk products at every meal.

questions.rationale: During periods of immobilization, a daily fluid intake of 2 to 3 L is recommended to promote bowel and bladder function. Food with sodium causes fluid retention. Dietary fiber helps diminish constipation.

questions.stem: Which finding should produce the most concern when performing pin care for a patient with an external fixator?
questions.rationale: Purulent drainage is the only abnormal finding.

questions.stem: Which assessment is considered abnormal when a nurse performs a neurovascular assessment on a patient in skeletal traction?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Limb is the same color as the unaffected side

questions.rationale: Delayed capillary refill reflects possible inadequate circulation.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse implement when dealing with the weights that are applying traction to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remove them to pull the patient up in bed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold them while the patient is changing positions in bed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold them for a few minutes if the patient complains of pain.

questions.rationale: Weights must always hang freely to prevent complications.

questions.stem: Which is true about a greenstick fracture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Line of the fracture goes across the bone in right angles to the longitudinal axis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Periosteum is not torn away from the bone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fracture is incomplete, and one side is bent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fracture occurred in one of the long bones of the body.

questions.rationale: Greenstick fractures are most commonly seen in children, with the bone broken on one side but only bent on the other.

questions.stem: A patient with a fractured pelvis says that she will not ambulate because of pain. What should a nurse inform the patient can be prevented with early ambulation?
questions.rationale: Early ambulation, although painful, avoids many of the complications of immobility such as DVT, constipation, and atrophy.

questions.stem: To what can delayed union of a fracture be attributed? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Delayed union can be caused by inadequate immobilization, infection, poor nutrition, and poor alignment of the bone fragments.

questions.stem: Which characteristics are present when crutches are properly fitted? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The axilla piece is 3 to 4 fingerbreadths below the axilla.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They fit close to the axilla for secure support.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are measured and adjusted when the patient is in the tripod position.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adjusted hand grips allow for a 45-degree flexion of the elbow.

choices.label: E
choices.text: They are padded so patient can bear weight on the axilla piece when ambulating.

questions.rationale: Crutches should allow for 3 to 4 fingerbreadths between the axilla and the axilla piece, the crutches should be adjusted when the patient is in the tripod position, the elbow flexion should be adjusted for a 30-degree flexion, and the weight should not be borne on the axilla because of the possibility of nerve damage.

questions.stem: A fracture that occurs because of osteoporosis is classified as a(n) ______ fracture.
questions.rationale: A fracture that occurs as a result of a tumor of another pathologic condition is classified as a pathologic fracture.

questions.stem: A nurse uses a diagram to show the process of a fractured bone healing. ______ (Arrange the options in the appropriate sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)
questions.rationale: The sequence of healing is hematoma, fibrocartilage, callus, ossification, and consolidation.

questions.stem: Arrange the process of stair climbing with crutches in the correct sequence: ______ (Arrange the options in the appropriate sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Body weight is supported with crutches.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Crutches are moved to the next step.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The affected leg moves to the next step.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The unaffected leg is moved to the next step.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Body weight is transferred to the unaffected leg.

questions.rationale: When climbing steps on crutches, the body weight is supported with the crutches. While the unaffected leg is moved to the next step, the body weight is transferred to the unaffected leg; while the crutches are moved up, the affected leg moves up.

questions.stem: A nurse is aware that a patient who is having his leg amputated is also having a prosthesis fitted during at the same time as the surgery. Which fact should the preoperative teaching plan include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Extra preoperative medications will be needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A rigid dressing will be applied to accommodate the prosthesis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A series of temporary prostheses will be put in place before the permanent one.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wiring the residual limb will be needed to ensure acceptance of the prosthesis.

questions.rationale: A rigid dressing will be applied to the residual limb to accommodate the prosthesis immediately after surgery.

questions.stem: A patient asks when he should expect to be up and walking after his below-the-knee amputation. When should the nurse assure him that most people with amputations can fully bear weight?
questions.rationale: Most people with amputations can fully bear weight 3 months after surgery.

questions.stem: A nurse is conducting a safety seminar and reminds the audience that upper extremity amputations are most frequently caused by trauma. Which population has the highest incidence of this type of amputation?
questions.rationale: Young men are at greater risk from work trauma because they are traditionally the ones working with farm and heavy machinery.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with diabetes has had vascular problems with his feet and lower legs for 10 years and is scheduled for a left below-the-knee amputation. Which remark by the patient indicates an understanding of the procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I am glad this amputation will end my diabetic problems.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “After they have hacked my leg, I won’t be able to drive.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “If this heals well, how long until I get a prosthesis?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I hate that my left knee is going to be useless without a foot.”

questions.rationale: Only this option indicates that the patient realizes the extent of the surgery.

questions.stem: What routine preoperative diagnostic tests are appropriate for a patient anticipating a below-the-knee amputation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulse volume recording and white blood cell (WBC) count

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulse volume recording and radiographic images

questions.rationale: Pulse volume recording and WBC count are two diagnostic tests for patients anticipating a below-the-knee amputation.

questions.stem: Where is the limb severed with an elbow disarticulation?
questions.rationale: Disarticulations sever the limbs through the joints.

questions.stem: A patient with peripheral vascular disease (PVD) and diabetes asks what he can do to help prevent an amputation. What is the best response of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “There is not really anything you can do to help.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You will not need to check your blood glucose levels.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It is important to eat big meals so your body can heal.”

questions.rationale: Smoking causes vasoconstriction, which aggravates PVD.

questions.stem: Why is a closed amputation usually performed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To create a weight-bearing residual limb

choices.label: B
choices.text: To alleviate the effects of trauma

choices.label: C
choices.text: To allow infection to heal and drain

choices.label: D
choices.text: To treat a limb with gangrene

questions.rationale: A closed amputation is performed to create a weight-bearing limb.

questions.stem: What controls the movement with a myoelectrically controlled prosthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient’s muscle movement and the prosthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Battery-operated muscles implanted in the prosthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Motion-sensing mechanism that swings the prosthesis forward

choices.label: D
choices.text: Internal computer chip in the prosthesis

questions.rationale: A patient’s muscle movement and the prosthesis control movement with a myoelectrically controlled prosthesis.

questions.stem: A patient who had a below-the-knee amputation 24 hours earlier is complaining of burning pain in his left foot. Which intervention is most appropriate for the nurse to implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remind the patient that it is only phantom pain.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Medicate the patient with the prescribed pain remedy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remind him that such sensations will go away in a few weeks.

questions.rationale: The nurse should medicate a patient for pain. Phantom pain is real. Although distraction is a possible intervention, it is not the most effective.

questions.stem: During the admission of a patient scheduled for an amputation, a patient relates that she is a practicing Orthodox Jew. What arrangements are appropriate for the nurse to make for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A veil should cover the amputated part.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A rabbi must be present for the surgery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The amputated part should be buried.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A family member should be present to read the Torah.

questions.rationale: Orthodox Jews bury all body parts.

questions.stem: What is an appropriate outcome for a patient with feelings of anxiety related to a perceived threat of disability?”

choices.label: A
choices.text: Comfort is increased; verbalized pain is less.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anxiety is relieved; the patient verbalizes concern related to disability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Grief is resolved; the patient expresses an acceptance of loss.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Residual limb is cleaned; no exudate, redness, or edema is observed.

questions.rationale: The relief of anxiety speaks directly to the patient’s feelings of anxiety. Other options are possible outcome goals but do not pertain to anxiety.

questions.stem: What is the greatest danger in the early postoperative period after an amputation?
questions.rationale: Hemorrhage is the greatest danger in the early postoperative period after an amputation.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who has undergone replantation of a body part. What might the saliva of leeches be used to treat in this patient?
questions.rationale: Leeches are used to treat venous congestion.

questions.stem: A patient who amputated his thumb in a lawnmower accident hands the emergency department nurse his thumb in a glass jar. What is the best action for the nurse to implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place the thumb in a baggie with iced lactated Ringer solution.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wrap the thumb in plastic wrap and place it on ice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leave the thumb in the jar and place it in the refrigerator.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wrap the thumb in a cloth saturated with normal saline and place it in a baggie.

questions.rationale: The amputated part should be wrapped in a towel soaked with normal saline solution, placed in a baggie, and put in a cool bath.

questions.stem: A patient who has a below-the-elbow prosthesis shows the home health care nurse the residual limb, which is red, edematous, and warm to touch. What should the nurse instruct this patient to do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply soothing lotion to the residual limb before replacing the prosthesis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dampen the prosthetic limb sock to hydrate and cool the residual limb.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pad the socket with lamb’s wool and replace the prosthesis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Leave the prosthesis off and notify physician.

questions.rationale: An inflamed residual limb suggests an infection. The prosthesis should be removed, the limb should be gently cleansed with soap and water, and the physician should be notified.

questions.stem: A child comes to the school nurse with his index finger partially amputated and hanging by a shred of skin and muscle. What is the best action by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Flush the hand with warm water and wrap it in a towel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carefully cut the skin holding the finger and wrap the finger and hand in a clean towel.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pinch the finger to stop the bleeding and take the child to the hospital.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wrap the hand and finger securely and place it on an ice water–filled plastic bag.

questions.rationale: The nurse should leave the shred of skin and muscle intact, wrap the hand and finger in a towel soaked in normal saline, and place it on a cool surface.

questions.stem: What nursing action should be implemented in the postoperative care for a patient with replantation of the right thumb?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreasing the temperature of the room to 70 F

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevating the hand but keeping it below the level of the heart

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offering coffee, tea, or cola to help increase fluid intake

choices.label: D
choices.text: Placing an antiembolus sleeve on the right arm

questions.rationale: Slight elevation of the hand will encourage drainage but not affect arterial perfusion. The room temperature should be 80 F. The patient should avoid all caffeine drinks and tight clothing or dressings.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with a recent below-the-knee amputation. What should the nurse recommend to this patient to prevent the loss of calcium and protein?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drink 1 to 2 L of fluid daily.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ingest at least four milk products each day.

questions.rationale: Even a small amount of ambulation will decrease the loss of calcium and protein.

questions.stem: What are late signs of hemorrhage in the postoperative period after an amputation?
questions.rationale: Cyanosis and hypotension are late signs of hemorrhage in the postoperative period after an amputation.

questions.stem: The verbalization of microvascular precautions is a criterion for measuring the achievement of which outcome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adequate circulation in the replanted limb

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adjustment to change in appearance and function

questions.rationale: Verbalizing the information related to microvascular precautions is evidence of the patient’s achievement of having acquired the necessary knowledge.

questions.stem: Which postoperative observation must be reported to the physician immediately?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Brownish-red drainage on the dressing, which is damp

questions.rationale: Bright-red bleeding is not expected and indicates hemorrhage. Direct pressure should be applied over the dressing and the physician or charge nurse should be notified.

questions.stem: A home health care nurse suspects a neuroma in a patient who had an above-the-knee amputation 1 month earlier. Which complaint by the patient led the nurse to suspect a neuroma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Area of swelling and bruising on distal portion of residual limb

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sharp severe pain in the residual limb

choices.label: D
choices.text: Area of numbness on distal portion of residual limb

questions.rationale: A neuroma causes sharp severe pain in the residual limb. A neuroma occurs when severed nerve endings attempt to regenerate. The neuroma requires excision by the surgeon.

questions.stem: Which action should a nurse implement to diminish swelling of the residual limb in the postoperative period after an above-the-knee amputation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevate the foot of the bed on blocks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevate the residual limb on pillows.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevate the head of the bed 15 degrees.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turn the patient on the affected side.

questions.rationale: Elevating the foot of the bed on blocks will help diminish edema and will not cause hip contracture deformity.

questions.stem: Which major situational occurrences might lead to amputations? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The categories of occurrences that lead to amputation are trauma, disease, tumors, and congenital defects. Carelessness frequently leads to trauma.

questions.stem: What is a closed amputation designed to do? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cover the stump with tissue and muscle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place sutures immediately over the bone.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Be immediately ready for a prosthesis.

questions.rationale: Closed amputations are meant to prepare the limb for weight-bearing activities with tissue and muscle applied to the residual limb. Sutures are not placed over the bone for future comfort and better healing. Closed amputations are not staged because they are open amputations, and they may or may not be prepared for an immediate prosthesis.

questions.stem: How should a nurse modify a teaching plan for an older adult who has had an above-the-knee amputation? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offer smaller units of information at a time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place less emphasis on chronic health problems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clarify the reality of phantom pain.

questions.rationale: Older adults are quite capable of learning, but the amount of information offered at one time should be lessened, the time for learning should be increased, the probability of phantom pain should be clarified, and repetition should be frequently used.

questions.stem: A nurse clarifies that the precise term for the patient’s amputation, which will be through the knee joint, is called ______.
questions.rationale: Disarticulation is the appropriate term for an amputation through the knee joint.

questions.stem: An amputation of a gangrenous limb that is left open for 10 days before closure is classified as a staged or _____ amputation.
questions.rationale: A staged or guillotine amputation is one in which the wound is left open until the area is free of infection or evidence of gangrene.

questions.stem: To reduce the possibility of hip contractures in a patient with an above-the-knee amputation, a nurse periodically places the patient in a(n) ______ position.
questions.rationale: The prone position will cause the muscles of the thigh to stretch and prevent contracture.

questions.stem: Preoperative exercises for a patient undergoing a lower-extremity amputation include ______ training.
questions.rationale: Upper body training will strengthen the arms to aid in movement after the loss of a lower extremity.

questions.stem: A patient is receiving the medication octreotide (Sandostatin) as a treatment for acromegaly. What should the nurse explain regarding this medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It reverses the effects of acromegaly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It should be given on a daily basis by injection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It increases insulin secretion causing hypoglycemia.

questions.rationale: Sandostatin will suppress growth hormone, but it will not reverse the effects of acromegaly. It is administered three times a week and suppresses insulin secretion causing hyperglycemia.

questions.stem: Which instruction should a nurse provide when a patient starts taking a saturated solution of potassium iodide (SSKI)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sip medication through a straw to prevent tooth staining.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Double the dose if a dose is missed.

questions.rationale: SSKI can discolor teeth if not sipped through a straw; no iodide drug should be doubled; excessive salivation is a sign of toxicity; and the medication should be taken after meals.

questions.stem: Which significant need should be included in instructions to a patient scheduled for a thyroid scan (123I)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide a special container to collect urine for the next 24 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wear a protective apron to shield him or her from radiation for the next 24 hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Request that visitors keep a distance of at least 6 feet away for the next 24 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Do not consume iodine for one week before the test..

questions.rationale: The patient should not consume iodine for one week before the test. Iodine is in radiographic dyes, some oral contraceptives, weight control drugs, multivitamins, all thyroid drugs, and some food, especially seafood.

questions.stem: A patient asks about his laboratory test, which showed a high level of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and a low level of T4. What is the most accurate explanation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It means that you have an inconsistency in your thyroid tests, and you will need more testing.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I am sorry. You will have to ask your physician about your laboratory results. We are not allowed to discuss them.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “The TSH is sending a message to your thyroid gland to increase production, but your thyroid isn’t producing enough hormone.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “That means that you will have to go on hormone therapy for the rest of your life.”

questions.rationale: The test determines whether the problem is in the pituitary gland or in the thyroid gland. In this patient, the high level of TSH is coming from the pituitary gland as it should, but the thyroid gland is not responding with adequate hormone production.

questions.stem: A patient has been given an antithyroid drug called methimazole. What appropriate nursing implementations should be included?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using special radioactive precautions for her urine for the first 24 hours

choices.label: B
choices.text: Monitoring her vital signs and withholding the medications if her pulse is greater than 100 beats/min

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teaching her to watch for and report any signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism or infections

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keeping her on a low-calorie, low-protein diet

questions.rationale: The drug targets the thyroid gland to slow its function, so signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism or infections need to be reported. Thionamides may cause suppression of neutrophils leading to a lowered resistance.

questions.stem: An older patient with hypothyroidism asks why her daily dose of thyroid hormone, which she has taken for 15 years, has been reduced. What is nurse’s best rationale when explaining what the decreased dose is related to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Improved efficacy of the thyroid preparation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Changes in your diet and activity level

questions.rationale: Older patients have slower drug metabolism; consequently, the drug stays in their systems. All patients receiving hormone replacement need to be periodically evaluated.

questions.stem: A nurse makes a list of symptoms that a patient who is taking methimazole (Tapazole), a thionamide drug, should report. What should this list include? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Urine retention is not a side effect of methimazole (Tapazole).

questions.stem: What are the common age-related changes in the endocrine system? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Endocrine function usually remains adequate in healthy older adults, but small changes are important when an illness alters homeostasis. Age-related endocrine changes include dimished response to ADH; decline in GH production, which leads to a reduction in protein synthesis; and a decline in cortisol secretion. Although not normal, hypothyroidism is more common in older adults, especially in women.

questions.stem: What should preoperative teaching for a patient scheduled for a transsphenoidal hypophysectomy include that the patient should do postoperatively?
questions.rationale: The patient should be taught to avoid sneezing, coughing, drinking through a straw, and using a stringent mouthwash that might dislodge the graft.

questions.stem: What hormone causes the features of a patient with acromegaly?
questions.rationale: Excess growth hormone in an adult will cause the flat bones to grow because the adult has little capacity for heightened growth. In a child, this same excess would cause giantism.

questions.stem: What are the classic symptoms of diabetes insipidus (DI)?
questions.rationale: Common signs and symptoms of DI are massive diuresis, dehydration, and thirst. Dehydration is characterized by hypotension, tachycardia, dizziness, decreased skin turgor, weakness, and possible fainting episodes.

questions.stem: A patient states that he is confused because the physician told him that his diabetes insipidus (DI) is nephrogenic. What should the nurse state when describing the difference between nephrogenic DI and neurogenic DI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nephrogenic DI will eventually resolve without medication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nephrogenic DI requires the nasal spray lypressin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nephrogenic DI does not respond to ADH.

questions.rationale: Nephrogenic DI does not respond to ADH.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old adolescent male patient has been diagnosed with Addison disease. Which effect of Addison disease should this patient be aware of?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He will not develop pubic hair.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He will grow a heavy beard.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He will become bald at an early age.

questions.rationale: The boy with Addison disease will not grow facial, axillary, or pubic hair. Balding and enlarged joints are not associated with Addison disease.

questions.stem: What is the cardinal indication of a pheochromocytoma?
questions.rationale: The patient with a pheochromocytoma exhibits dangerously high hypertension. Hypertension and its attendant symptoms are what bring the patient to the physician. The tumor is found incidentally.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient diagnosed with Addison disease. Which signs and symptoms should lead the nurse to suspect an adrenal crisis?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Widening pulse pressure and shortness of breath

questions.rationale: Confusion and tachycardia are signs that the patient may be in adrenal crisis, which is a medical emergency and should be brought to the attention of the charge nurse.

questions.stem: A nurse includes in the discharge plan for a patient with Addison disease, “Potential for injury.” What should measures to deal with this include?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rising slowly from a lying position

choices.label: D
choices.text: Providing instructions in the use of a walker

questions.rationale: Hypovolemia lowers the blood pressure and may cause orthostatic (postural) hypotension, which may cause sudden profound weakness.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include when planning education to a patient with Addison disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discontinue hormonal replacement therapy if the patient becomes nauseated or has diarrhea.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decrease medication if the patient is under stress or is being treated for an infection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wear a medical alert tag and carry emergency dexamethasone.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begin a vigorous exercise program to overcome weakness and muscle wasting.

questions.rationale: The medical alert bracelet will reduce the patient’s risk of not receiving appropriate and timely care in an emergency situation.

questions.stem: A patient with long-term asthma develops Cushing syndrome. What is the cause of this condition?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lacking ACTH, related to the pituitary gland

questions.rationale: Long-term corticosteroid use is a prime cause of Cushing syndrome.

questions.stem: Which findings are expected when assessing a patient with Cushing syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Edema of the trunk, extremities, and face

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wasting of the abdomen with thick, calloused skin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excess adipose tissue in the trunk, slender extremities, and moon face

choices.label: D
choices.text: High levels of potassium and low levels of sodium, weakness, and wasting

questions.rationale: Truncal obesity, thin extremities, and moon face are the classical signs of Cushing syndrome caused by long-term corticosteroid therapy.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient diagnosed with Cushing syndrome leads a nurse to conclude that teaching has been effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “I know I should add salt to everything I eat.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I make a point to avoid excessive exposure to sun.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I avoid being exposed to anyone with an infection.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I am careful to wear well-fitting shoes.”

questions.rationale: Patients with Cushing syndrome are especially prone to infection. Adding salt would increase fluid retention. Sun exposure and well-fitting shoes are not significant for Cushing syndrome.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient with Simmonds cachexia. What symptom should the nurse anticipate the patient will exhibit?
questions.rationale: Simmonds cachexia is a panhypopituitarism condition in which muscle wasting, small organs, very pale complexion, virtually no body hair, and subnormal body temperature are symptoms.

questions.stem: A nurse making a care plan for a 10-year-old boy with hyperpituitarism identifies an altered body image. What should the nurse relate this nursing diagnosis to?
questions.rationale: A 10-year-old boy will be excessively tall for his age. Hair is not lacking, and skin eruptions associated with giantism are observed. Most 10-year-old boys have small genitalia.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include when caring for a patient after a hypophysectomy, during which the entire pituitary was removed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maintaining strict intake and output fluids

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keeping the patient flat in bed for the first 24 hours

choices.label: C
choices.text: Withholding analgesics to assess the level of consciousness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Providing mouth care with thorough cleansing of the oral cavity

questions.rationale: With the removal of the entire pituitary gland, the patient will have no effective ADH and will excrete large amounts of urine. The patient is usually kept in a semi-Fowler position and is medicated as needed for pain. Because of the graft, mouth care is minimal, if provided at all.

questions.stem: What can bring on an addisonian crisis?
questions.rationale: Infection is one of the many stresses that can bring on an addisonian crisis.

questions.stem: A 6-foot, 2-inch, 16-year-old girl who is being treated for hyperpituitarism says, “I can’t stand it that I look like a freak.” What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Your height could help you be a basketball star or a model.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “What is it about your height that makes you a freak?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Your height is something you will have to get used to.”

questions.rationale: Using a question that encourages further discussion will help the nurse understand the distress that the patient is trying to convey. Listening to patients’ concerns helps them get in touch with their own feelings.

questions.stem: A patient with hypopituitarism must take medications for the rest of his or her life. What should the patient teaching plan include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Constipation must be prevented because straining increases intracranial pressure.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You must become familiar with the signs and symptoms of inadequate or excessive hormone replacement.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It is not necessary to wear a medical alert bracelet or necklace.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Your self-image is important. Take positive steps to improve your appearance.”

questions.rationale: To prevent complications, recognizing the importance of continuing to replace the missing hormones is essential for the patient.

questions.stem: Two days after a hypophysectomy a patient complains of a headache and nuchal rigidity. What action should the nurse take based on these assessments?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Report suspected meningitis to the head nurse.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Closely monitor the patient’s blood pressure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevate the head of the bed to 45 degrees.

questions.rationale: The headache and the nuchal rigidity are signs of meningitis.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with diabetes insipidus (DI). Which signs should the nurse report that indicate a change in condition?
questions.rationale: A dropping blood pressure is an indication that the hypovolemia with DI has reached a significant point and will require medical implementation. All other options are the expected signs of this disorder or an indication that therapy is effective.

questions.stem: What symptoms should a nurse expect a patient with the diagnosis of SIADH to report during an intake interview? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Retained fluid and hyponatremia cause weight gain and elevated blood pressure with an attendant headache. The hyperkalemia causes the patient to feel weak and have muscle cramps.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old girl is diagnosed with adenoma of the pituitary gland. What signs of this diagnosis should the nurse assess? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Pituitary adenomas that secrete hormones may cause amenorrhea (cessation of menses), galactorrhea (abnormal milk production), hyperthyroidism, and Cushing syndrome.

questions.stem: A nurse noting a peaked T wave on the electrocardiogram (ECG) of a patient with Addison disease recognizes this complex as suggestive of ______.
questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia will cause an elevated, peaked T wave.

questions.stem: A nurse prepares a family for the altered appearance of the patient returning from stereotactic radiosurgery to see a(n) ______ in place.
questions.rationale: The stereotactic frame, which helps direct the radiation, is attached to the patient’s head with pins.

questions.stem: A physician ordered T3 and T4 tests for a young woman complaining of fatigue, weight gain, muscle aches and pains, and constipation. Which laboratory test results will help confirm the diagnosis of hypothyroidism?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The T3 test elevates, and the T4 test decreases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The level of thyroxin rises and then falls back to subnormal levels.

questions.rationale: These complaints are strongly suggestive of thyroid disorder; T3 and T4 laboratory diagnostic tests are the most useful.

questions.stem: A patient with a hyperthyroid complains of fatigue but still cannot get to sleep. What is the best suggestion by the nurse?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drinking a cup of cocoa before bedtime

questions.rationale: Bedtime rituals such as a warm bath, reading, and listening to music cue the body for sleep. Naps during the day may make nighttime sleep difficult; exercising and drinking caffeine-filled drinks are stimulating and should be avoided by the person with insomnia.

questions.stem: A patient with exophthalmos is distressed about her appearance and asks when it will go away. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It can be disguised with sunglasses and makeup.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It usually subsides after medication for hyperthyroidism is started.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It can be minimized with plastic surgery to the eyelids.

questions.rationale: The “startled” appearance of the patient with exophthalmos usually subsides several weeks after therapy for hyperthyroidism becomes effective.

questions.stem: A nurse is explaining Graves disease to a newly diagnosed patient. Which statement by the nurse best clarifies the pathophysiologic changes of Graves disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Your thyroid gland is not producing enough hormones; consequently, you will need replacement therapy.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Your thyroid gland is overactive, but there are ways to treat it through medicine or surgery.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It’s an autoimmune disorder that has no satisfactory treatment.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Graves disease is a temporary disorder that will gradually subside.”

questions.rationale: The patient needs to recognize the nurse’s role in giving accurate, timely information.

questions.stem: A nurse assessing a patient 1 day after a subtotal thyroidectomy notes that the patient’s color is poor, the pulse and respirations are rapid, and the patient feels warm to the touch. The patient says that she feels frightened. What is the best initial implementation by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell her that there is nothing to be afraid of and stay to calm her.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask her if she would like pain medication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Call the charge nurse; these are signs of a thyroid storm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Get a tracheostomy set at the bedside.

questions.rationale: Call the charge nurse; these signs and symptoms suggest excessive stimulation caused by an elevated level of thyroid hormones, and the patient needs immediate care.

questions.stem: What is the most appropriate nursing concern for the patient recently diagnosed with hyperthyroidism?
questions.rationale: The patient with hyperthyroidism has trouble staying asleep because of the metabolic disorder. Persons with hyperthyroidism feel uncomfortably warm, which also contributes to their sleeping difficulty.

questions.stem: A patient, newly diagnosed with hypothyroidism, is anxious to begin her drug regimen. What should the nurse’s instructions relative to hormone replacement include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Be certain that no dose is skipped.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Be sure and take these drugs just before bedtime.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Know the signs and symptoms of hyperthyroidism.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You will be able to notice the benefits of thyroid replacement therapy right away.”

questions.rationale: Overdosing on the thyroid replacement medication will lead to signs and symptoms of hyperthyroidism. The medication is best taken every morning so as not to unduly interrupt sleep patterns.

questions.stem: What patient recommendation should a nurse include when preparing to present presurgical teaching of a patient scheduled for a subtotal thyroidectomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lie flat on her back for 24 hours to prevent undue strain on the suture line.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Be able to verbalize the signs and symptoms of thyroid crisis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Demonstrate how to deep breathe and support her head during position changes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have a tube in her trachea to assist in breathing.

questions.rationale: Teaching the patient to hold and support the head after a thyroidectomy will ease the postoperative period. Consistently supporting the head will prevent stress on the suture line.

questions.stem: On returning from surgery after undergoing a thyroidectomy, a patient is alarmed about the large tracheostomy tray on the bedside table. What is the nurse’s most reassuring response when the patient asks why it is there?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “We have it there as a precautionary measure in the unlikely event that you have difficulty breathing.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If you start bleeding, we’ll be able to take care of it right here at the bedside.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “We have to keep it there in case of an emergency and the physician needs it.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “It’s hospital policy to have it available for persons who are likely to have respiratory arrest.”

questions.rationale: The honest answers without any embellishments are best. Suggesting that any emergency is imminent will alarm the patient further. The presence of the tray is an item that should be covered in preoperative teaching.

questions.stem: What is the appropriate action of the nurse when assessing for hemorrhage in a post-thyroidectomy patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess upper chest for the patient positioned in high Fowler position.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turn the patient to the side to check; the patient must be kept flat in the bed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lift up the neck dressing to assess for excessive bleeding.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Examine behind patient’s neck and upper back to assess for hemorrhage.

questions.rationale: Because the dressing is on the front of the neck, blood might flow under the dressing to the back of the neck, since it flows to the most-dependent position. Patients are positioned in a high Fowler position after a thyroidectomy to diminish swelling.

questions.stem: How do foods such as soybeans, turnips, and rutabagas affect people with thyroid disorders?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decrease the hypothermia of the person with hypothyroidism.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Supplement the diet of a person with hypothyroidism.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Counteract the effect of iodide therapy.

questions.rationale: Turnips, rutabagas, and soybeans are goitrogen substances that suppress the thyroid hormone. Such foods would synergize iodides and increase the symptoms of the patient with hypothyroidism.

questions.stem: A nurse taking the blood pressure of a patient who had a total thyroidectomy 2 days earlier notes that the patient’s hand goes into a carpopedal spasm. What should the nurse recognize this movement as an indication of?
questions.rationale: The carpopedal spasm is the Trousseau sign, which indicates hypercalcemia. Chvostek sign also signals hypocalcemia.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse implement to address dry skin in the patient with hypothyroidism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase the frequency of bathing to get rid of dry skin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply lotions and creams to help maintain moisture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase activities to stimulate circulation in the skin.

questions.rationale: The skin requires moisturizing lotion to decrease the risk breakdown. Frequent bathing and antihistamines will dry the skin. Exercise does little for skin perfusion.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse implement to initiate the Chvostek sign?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient to grimace and note if the facial response is symmetrical.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inflate a blood pressure cuff to the systolic level and watch for a carpopedal spasm.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tap the face over the facial nerve and watch for a spasm of the facial muscle.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check the pupillary response to light and determine whether the pupil accommodates and reacts.

questions.rationale: Spasm of the facial muscles is an indicator of low serum calcium levels.

questions.stem: What symptoms should a nurse anticipate in the history of a patient with hyperparathyroidism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fatigue, hyperactive reflexes, muscle cramps, and twitching

choices.label: B
choices.text: Poor muscle tone, bone pain, urinary calculi, and fractures

questions.rationale: The calcium has been leeched from the bones, leading to hypercalcemia and leaving the patient with multiple problems such as a risk for fractures, urinary calculi, and bone pain.

questions.stem: What is the priority nursing concern for a patient with hyperparathyroidism?
questions.rationale: Excessive calcium in the bloodstream leads to the formation of calcium stones in the urinary system.

questions.stem: What is the nurse aware is happening when the patient with hypoparathyroidism complains of fatigue and a lack of energy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension is the cause of the fatigue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypocalcemia has caused decreased cardiac output.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dyspnea has sapped the patient’s energy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Poor muscle tone makes any activity tiring.

questions.rationale: A decreased amount of calcium in the bloodstream decreases the contractility of the heart and, consequently, reduces cardiac output.

questions.stem: Which intervention is necessary to assist a patient with hypothyroidism to understand how he can live a full and normal life?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Teach the importance of taking antithyroid medication until it is no longer needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encourage exercise to burn extra calories and maintain a normal weight.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach him to take care of energy needs through adequate nutrition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage treatment with thyroid replacement therapy.

questions.rationale: Hormones can adequately and effectively replace the missing thyroid hormone.

questions.stem: Why are antithyroid medications provided presurgically to a patient with hyperthyroidism? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: To decrease the level of hormone in the blood before surgery

choices.label: B
choices.text: To help reduce the risk of hemorrhage during surgery

choices.label: C
choices.text: To decrease the threat of a thyroid storm

questions.rationale: The antithyroid medication will do all of the above except increasing weight in a patient with hyperthyroidism.

questions.stem: What should a nurse caring for a patient with hyperthyroidism include when developing a plan of care? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: A patient with hyperthyroidism does not need to lose weight, but he or she needs to gain it. All other options are appropriate concerns for such a patient.
