questions.stem: To meet the nutritional needs of a patient with Graves disease, the nurse recommends a diet of 4,000 to ______ calories. (Use numeric characters only, no punctuation.)
questions.rationale: The patient with Graves disease has a high metabolism, which requires a large caloric intake. These patients need 4000 to 5000 calories a day.

questions.stem: Congenital hypothyroidism, if left untreated, will result in ______.
questions.rationale: Cretinism is the result of untreated congenital hypothyroidism.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that type 1 diabetes mellitus is a disease in which the body does not produce enough insulin. What is the reason that the blood glucose is elevated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prolonged elevation of stress hormone (cortisol, epinephrine, glucagon, growth hormone) levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Malfunction of the glycogen-storing capabilities of the liver

choices.label: C
choices.text: Destruction of the beta cells in the pancreas

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insulin resistance of the receptor cells in the muscle tissue

questions.rationale: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is a disease in which the pancreas does not produce adequate insulin because of the destruction of beta cells.

questions.stem: A patient newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus asks the nurse why she has to take a pill instead of insulin. The nurse explains that in type 2 diabetes mellitus, the body still makes insulin. What other information is pertinent for the nurse to relay?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overweight and underactive people cannot simply use the insulin produced.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Metabolism is slowed in some people, so they have to take a pill to speed up their metabolism.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sometimes the autoimmune system works against the action of the insulin.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The cells become resistant to the action of insulin. Pills are given to increase the sensitivity.

questions.rationale: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a disease in which the cells become resistant to the action of insulin and the blood glucose level rises. Oral hyperglycemic agents make the cells more sensitive.

questions.stem: A patient tells a nurse that she eats “huge” amounts of food but stays hungry most of the time. What should the nurse explain as the cause of hunger experienced by persons with type 1 diabetes?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Need for additional calories to correct the increased metabolism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fact that the cells cannot use the blood glucose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Need for exercise to stimulate insulin secretion

questions.rationale: The cells cannot use the glucose without insulin, so the patient with diabetes still feels hungry even though abundant glucose is circulating in the blood.

questions.stem: What does the lack of insulin in patients with type 1 diabetes cause that increases the risk for cardiovascular disorders?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High glucose levels that irritate and shrink the vessels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inadequate metabolism of proteins, which causes ketosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased metabolism of ketones, which causes hypertension

questions.rationale: The increase in fatty acid levels causes an increase in the level of triglycerides and an attendant rise in low-density lipoprotein levels.

questions.stem: The self-care goal of a patient with diabetes is to keep the blood sugar within normal limits. What causes hyperglycemia to occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood glucose levels rise, stimulating the production of insulin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin conversion of glycogen to glucose is inhibited.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The body responds to glucose-starved tissues by changing stored glycogen into glucose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glycogen is unable to be stored in the liver and muscles.

questions.rationale: The hypothalamus is receiving a message that the cells need glucose, so it responds by adding more glucose to the already overburdened blood.

questions.stem: A young patient complains that diabetes is causing her to “have no life at all. It’s too hard.” What is the most helpful response by the nurse?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “It’s hard, but with significant alterations in your lifestyle, you can live a long life.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “What’s hard about exercise, diet, and medicine?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Let’s talk about what makes it so hard.”

questions.rationale: Involving the patient in decisions about how she will cope with her diabetes will make the goals more realistic and personal, which will give her a greater chance of success in meeting them.

questions.stem: When a patient with type 2 diabetes says, “Why in the world are they looking at my hemoglobin? I thought my problem was with my blood sugar.” What should the nurse explain about the level of hemoglobin A1c?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shows how a high level of glucose can cause a significant drop in the hemoglobin level

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shows what the glucose level has done during the past 3 months

choices.label: C
choices.text: Indicates a true picture of the patient’s nutritional state

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reflects the effect of a high level of glucose on the ability to produce red blood cells (RBCs)

questions.rationale: By analyzing the amount of glucose bound to the hemoglobin, the level of blood glucose can be evaluated for the past 3 months because the glucose stays bound to the hemoglobin for the life of the RBC.

questions.stem: A patient with type 2 diabetes shows a blood sugar reading of 68 at 6 AM. What action should the nurse implement based on the reading of 72 mg/dL?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the charge nurse of the reading.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give regular insulin per a sliding scale.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give him 8 oz of skim milk.

questions.rationale: The patient is hypoglycemic and needs an immediate source of glucose, such as milk or orange juice. The oral hypoglycemic agent will not work quickly enough. The charge nurse can be notified later. Giving insulin per a sliding scale would lower the blood sugar level.

questions.stem: A nurse assigned to care for a patient with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) is aware that this is a life-threatening condition. What will DKA result in?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Disorder of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins metabolism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Storage of glycogen, resulting in a severe shortage of glucose in the bloodstream

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dangerously elevated pH and bicarbonate levels in the blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: Severe hypoglycemia, which can result in coma and convulsions

questions.rationale: DKA is mainly related to the use of fat as an energy source because of an inability of the body to use glucose. The metabolism of fat produces ketones.

questions.stem: A patient has been admitted to the hospital with the diagnosis of DKA. What vital signs should a nurse anticipate that the patient will exhibit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Temperature, 99 F; pulse, 62 beats/min; respirations, 16 breaths/min and shallow

choices.label: B
choices.text: Temperature, 98.6 F; pulse, 76 beats/min; respirations, 16 breaths/min and deep

choices.label: C
choices.text: Temperature, 98 F; pulse, 84 beats/min; respirations, 18 breaths/min and shallow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Temperature, 97.4 F; pulse, 110 beats/min; respirations, 26 breaths/min and deep

questions.rationale: DKA is caused by the attempt of the body to metabolize fat for energy, which results in an acidotic state. The classic signs of DKA are hypothermia, tachycardia, and Kussmaul respirations (rapid and deep) to blow off the acid ions via respirations. The respirations will have a fruity odor.

questions.stem: A home health care nurse is assessing a patient with type 1 diabetes who has been controlled for 6 months. The nurse is surprised and concerned about a blood glucose reading of 52 mg/dL. What action by this patient most likely caused this episode of hypoglycemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Taking a new form of birth control pill this morning

choices.label: B
choices.text: Using large amounts of sugar substitute in her tea this morning

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 2-hour long exercise class at the spa this morning

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administering an insufficient dose of insulin this morning

questions.rationale: Excessive exercise used up the glucose that was made available by the insulin taken by the patient. The patient now has too much insulin for the available glucose and has become hypoglycemic.

questions.stem: As part of a teaching plan in preparation for discharge, a patient with type 1 diabetes needs guidelines for exercise. Which guideline should be included?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Plan exercise so that it coincides with the peak action of insulin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin should be injected into the lower extremity before exercise because that site provides the greatest absorption.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exercise should be performed daily at the same time of day and at the same intensity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep exercise at a minimum to conserve your energy.

questions.rationale: If the body is using more glucose than available, the body will draw on fatty acids, which will give off ketones.

questions.stem: A patient has come into the emergency department accompanied by a friend who states that the patient had been acting very strangely and seems confused. The friend states that the patient has diabetes and takes insulin. Which signs of hypoglycemia might the nurse assess?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Slow pulse rate and low blood pressure

questions.rationale: When blood sugar levels fall, hormones are activated to increase serum glucose. One of the hormones is epinephrine, which causes these symptoms.

questions.stem: A patient has come to the physician’s office after finding out that her blood glucose level was 135 mg/dL. She states that she had not eaten before the test and was told to come and see her physician. She asks the nurse if she has diabetes. What is the most accurate nursing response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Having a fasting serum glucose that high certainly indicates diabetes.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “That test indicates that we need to perform more tests that are specific for diabetes.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “How do you feel? Do you have any other signs of diabetes?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do you have a family history of diabetes, stroke, or heart disease? We need to know before making a diagnosis.”

questions.rationale: The nurse needs to answer the patient’s question in a way that gives information and is not misleading. Although 135 mg/dL is high, a nonpathologic explanation may be found. More tests should be performed to evaluate the patient.

questions.stem: A nurse is formulating a teaching plan for a 22-year-old woman taking rosiglitazone (Avandia). What should the nurse include information about in this plan to caution this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased effectiveness of her birth control pills

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sudden drop in blood pressure with dizziness

questions.rationale: Avandia causes some birth control pills to be less effective.

questions.stem: A patient with type 1 diabetes has an insulin order for NPH insulin, 35 U, to be given at 0700. The patient has also been instructed not to take anything by mouth (NPO) in preparation for laboratory work that will not be drawn until 1000. What action should the nurse implement?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the insulin with a small snack.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hold the insulin until after the blood draw.

questions.rationale: Holding the insulin to adhere to the NPO order is appropriate. The patient will not be getting food until after the laboratory work; consequently, the insulin will not be needed until then. Giving the insulin as ordered will create a possibility of hypoglycemia before the blood is drawn. Giving a snack to a patient who is NPO is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A patient comes to the diabetes clinic and confides to the nurse that she does not follow the diet exchange program that she was given. What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The exchange program is a carefully developed and very important program that allows you to take control of your disease.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “A lot of people have trouble with that program. You aren’t the first one to go off your diet.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “We had better check your blood work to see what you’ve done to yourself.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Okay. Let’s talk about what you do eat and drink and how you manage your diabetes.”

questions.rationale: To evaluate the effectiveness of treatment, the nurse must first find out how the patient perceives the importance of diet, drugs, and exercise.

questions.stem: A patient with type 1 diabetes asks why his 0700 insulin has been changed from NPH insulin to 70/30 premixed insulin. What is the best explanation by the nurse that explains about 70/30 insulin mixture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is absorbed more rapidly into the bloodstream.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has no peak action time and lasts all day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It makes insulin administration easier and safer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It provides a bolus of rapid-acting insulin to prevent hyperglycemia after breakfast.

questions.rationale: 70/30 insulin is 30% rapid-acting insulin and 70% intermediate-acting insulin. The rapid action of the 0700 premixed insulin prevents hyperglycemia after the morning meal and the mixed drug reduces the risk of error in drawing up two insulins.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include when drawing up a patient’s diabetes teaching plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Develop an exercise plan because regular exercise helps control blood glucose levels.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Monitor blood sugar levels only if not feeling well to ensure that the fingertips are not pricked too much.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If nervousness, palpitations, or hunger is experienced, take a small dose (1 to 2 U) of regular insulin and call the physician.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use over-the-counter measures for any foot blisters, calluses, or wounds before seeking medical help.

questions.rationale: Exercise is an integral part of the patient’s ability to take charge of his or her diabetes and needs to be included in the teaching plan.

questions.stem: What has most likely occurred in a patient who has been diagnosed with endogenous hypoglycemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Taken an overdose of hypoglycemic drugs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Been following a very restricted fasting diet or is malnourished

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excessive secretion of insulin or an increase in glucose metabolism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exercised unwittingly without replenishing needed fluids and nutrients

questions.rationale: Endogenous refers to within; in this patient, it refers to internal factors, such as an increase of insulin or glucose metabolism. Both conditions would lead to hypoglycemia.

questions.stem: How long does it take for Humulin R 20 units to peak?
questions.rationale: Humulin R has its onset in approximately 30 minutes, but its peak is in 2 hours.

questions.stem: A nurse suspects that a patient with type 1 diabetes may be experiencing the Somogyi phenomenon. What symptom supports this suspicion?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 6 AM blood sugar of 58 mg/dL and nausea

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abdominal pain and elevated blood pressure

questions.rationale: The Somogyi phenomenon occurs because of a rebound hyperglycemia after a period of hypoglycemia during the early morning. The patient wakes with a headache, enuresis, nausea and vomiting, nightmares, and a high level of blood sugar.

questions.stem: A patient has been admitted with hyperglycemic hyperosmolar nonketotic syndrome (HHNS). The blood glucose level is very high (880 mg/dL) on admission. The physician believes that the condition is the result of large amounts of glucose solutions administered intravenously (IV) during renal dialysis. What should the nurse anticipate that the patient would exhibit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fruity breath and a high level of ketones in her urine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Severe dehydration and hypernatremia caused by the hyperglycemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exactly the same symptoms and signs as DKA

questions.rationale: IV solutions containing glucose bypass the digestive system; consequently, the pancreas is not triggered to release insulin. However, just enough insulin is present to prevent the breakdown of fatty acids and the formation of ketones.

questions.stem: What are functional causes of hypoglycemia? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Dumping syndrome, Addison disease, and prolonged exercise are functional causes of hypoglycemia. Overdose of insulin and chronic alcoholism are exogenous causes.

questions.stem: What should a teaching plan about foot care include for a patient with diabetes? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wash and carefully dry the feet every day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protect the feet from extreme temperatures.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Buy shoes that are comfortable and supportive.

questions.rationale: Washing, inspecting, and drying the feet, especially between the toes, are essential. Protecting the feet from heat and cold and wearing supportive shoes are important to good foot health. Lotion can be applied to the soles and tops of the feet but not between the toes. Walking barefoot is contraindicated for a person with diabetes.

questions.stem: A teaching plan for a patient with diabetes is focused on smoking cessation and the control of hypertension for the avoidance of microvascular complications. What are examples of microvascular complications? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Macular degeneration and ESRD are both microvascular complications. CAD, PVD, and CVA are all macrovascular complications.

questions.stem: How is the Whipple triad described? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low blood glucose levels are documented when symptoms are present.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Symptoms can be reproduced with an injection of regular insulin, 10 units.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Muscular activity does not have any effect on blood glucose.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Symptoms improved when the blood glucose level rises.

questions.rationale: Whipple triad is the presence of the symptoms of hypoglycemia (e.g., diaphoresis, pallor, tachycardia), the documentation of low blood glucose levels when symptoms are present, and the improvement of symptoms as the blood glucose level rises.

questions.stem: A nurse reminds a patient with type I diabetes to rotate the insulin injection sites to prevent ______.
questions.rationale: Using the same area for insulin injections causes swollen lumpy areas that interfere with the absorption of insulin.

questions.stem: A nurse instructs a patient about how insulin affects blood glucose. ______ (Arrange the events in sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta cells are stimulated to release insulin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glycogen is converted to glucose by alpha cells (glycogenesis).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glycogen is stored in the liver.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Insulin transports glucose to muscle cells.

questions.rationale: Insulin transports the glucose to muscle cells or converts it to glycogen, which is stored in the liver to be accessed when hypoglycemia occurs.

questions.stem: Which potential side effect should a nurse caution a patient who is taking danazol (Danocrine), an androgenic steroid, for the treatment of menorrhagia to be prepared for?
questions.rationale: Androgenic steroids cause masculinizing. The distressing signs are a deepening voice, development of chest hair, coarsening of the skin, clitoral enlargement, and hot flashes. Many patients reject the drug on the basis of these side effects.

questions.stem: What instruction should a nurse provide to a patient after a culdoscopy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clean the incision site daily with hydrogen peroxide.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Return to the clinic for suture removal in 7 days.

questions.rationale: Nothing should be inserted into the vagina (e.g., intercourse, tampons) for the time specified by the physician. No incision or sutures are present.

questions.stem: A patient asks a nurse, “Will the laparoscopy be painful?” What is the best reply by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “You will probably have some pain below the rib cage and around the shoulder.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Most patients state that the procedure is painless during and afterward.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You may have some mild to moderate pain around the umbilicus and in the back.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Every person is different. It’s difficult to say whether you’ll have pain.”

questions.rationale: During the immediate postoperative period, the patient tends to experience pain below the rib cage and in the shoulder area from the air injected into the abdominal cavity. The air is absorbed in 24 hours, and the pain disappears.

questions.stem: A nurse is instructing a patient on breast self-examination. When is the best time of the month to instruct this patient to perform a breast self-examination?

choices.label: D
choices.text: On the first day of the month

questions.rationale: Breast self-examination should be performed at the same time each month, at the end of the menstrual cycle.

questions.stem: Conjugated estrogen (Premarin) is indicated for treatment of menopause. Which side effects should a nurse explain before administering the medication to a woman who has just had a hysterectomy?
questions.rationale: A side effect of conjugated estrogen (Premarin) is breast tenderness.

questions.stem: A female student, seen in the campus clinic, states that she uses feminine hygiene douches every day and after intercourse. What is the best response from the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Douching has been used as an effective means of birth control for years.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Commercially prepared douches will neutralize the female vaginal tract.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Douching should only be done when ordered by a physician or nurse practitioner.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Douching protects the vaginal tract from microorganisms.”

questions.rationale: Women should not douche unless it is ordered by a physician or nurse practitioner.

questions.stem: Which medication is used to stimulate or mimic actions of natural pituitary gonadotropins in the treatment of infertility?
questions.rationale: Clomid stimulates the actions of natural pituitary gonadotropins.

questions.stem: The nurse recommends that an annual mammogram be performed for women older than _____ years of age. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: The American Cancer Society (ACS) recommends that mammograms be obtained in women at the age of 45 years with subsequent testing done annually thereafter until age 54 at which time the woman may be transitioned to biennial screening or continued annually for as long as they are otherwise in good health (ACS, 2015).

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a health class for 12- and 13-year-old girls about routine vaginal hygiene. What should the nurse be sure to include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wear clean polyester panties daily and at night.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Douche weekly with a mild vinegar solution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wash the external and internal genitalia daily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wipe the perineal–anal area from front to back.

questions.rationale: Transfer of Escherichia coli from the anus to the vagina or to the urinary system can be avoided by wiping the perineal–anal area from front to back.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching patients with endometriosis. What signs and symptoms of common complications should be included?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obstruction of the bowel and ureters

questions.rationale: A common complication of endometriosis is obstruction of bowel, ureters, or both, from the endometrial deposits, creating a medical emergency.

questions.stem: After a mastectomy, many patients experience lymphedema. What action can the patient take to minimize this problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep the arm elevated as much as possible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take Lasix, 20 mg twice daily as ordered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use a sling during the day to rest the arm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid exercising the arm for several weeks.

questions.rationale: To manage lymphedema, the patient should elevate the arm to a height above the level of the heart.

questions.stem: A patient tells a nurse that she is afraid of getting cervical cancer because her mother died of cervical cancer. What is the most appropriate response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “You need to a have a pelvic examination every 6 months because of your history.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If you have regular Pap stain, cervical cancer is usually diagnosed early and cured.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “There’s no need to worry so much. Cervical cancer does not run in families.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Cervical cancer is sexually transmitted. Don’t switch partners often, and you don’t have to worry.”

questions.rationale: For patients who have regular annual pelvic examinations and Papanicolaou (Pap) stain, cervical cancer is usually diagnosed and treated in its early stage.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient after a modified radical mastectomy for a breast tumor that has been determined to be positive for estrogen receptors (ER positive) and has been placed on a protocol of tamoxifen. The patient then asks the nurse, “Do you think I should take this medication?” Which statement is the most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Tamoxifen will probably not be effective because your tumor was ER positive.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “If I were you, I would do what the physician recommends.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “I think that you should take tamoxifen, because the tumor was ER positive.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Tamoxifen is an option to consider, because your tumor was ER positive.”

questions.rationale: For tumors that are ER positive, tamoxifen citrate may be prescribed because such drugs interfere with estrogen production. ER-positive tumors need estrogen to grow.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl seen in the student health clinic has been diagnosed with Trichomonas vaginalis and given medication for the infection. What should a nurse explain about treatment of the girl’s sexual partner?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He will require a 1-day treatment with the same drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He will not develop the infection or require treatment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He will be treated, although he is asymptomatic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He is required by law to be examined by a physician.

questions.rationale: The woman’s sexual partner(s) may be treated for some infections to avoid reinfection.

questions.stem: A patient makes an appointment with her gynecologist because she is having difficulty conceiving. Which laboratory test should the nurse anticipate the physician will order?
questions.rationale: Evaluation of the female partner includes basal body temperature, serum progesterone level, and endometrial biopsy to assess for infertility.

questions.stem: What action should a nurse implement when assessing for abnormal bleeding in a patient following a hysterectomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Record the number of perineal pads used.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place the patient’s bed in a high Fowler position.

questions.rationale: Recording the number of pads used and the appearance of the discharge is primary in posthysterectomy care. The abdominal dressing and perineal pad are checked every hour for the first 12 hours. Any excess bleeding is reported. The patient should be placed on her side in a semi-Fowler position. An abdominal binder, if used, has no effect on hemorrhage.

questions.stem: A nurse collecting data on the reproductive system from a female patient is told that the patient has a vaginal discharge that is cottage cheese–like in appearance. What should the nurse recognize this as a common sign of?
questions.rationale: Candida albicans infection has a distinctive odor and a cottage cheese–like appearance.

questions.stem: What must be true for a woman to be diagnosed with primary infertility?

choices.label: A
choices.text: She has never been able to conceive after 1 year of regular, unprotected sex.

choices.label: B
choices.text: She conceived once but did not deliver a viable infant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: She conceived once but was unable to conceive again.

choices.label: D
choices.text: She conceived three times in 1 year without a viable birth.

questions.rationale: To be diagnosed with infertility, the patient must have been unable to conceive after 1 year of regular, unprotected sex. Primary infertily is when the woman has never conceived; secondary infertility refers to a woman who has conceived at least once but is unable to conceive again. All other options are definitions of secondary infertility.

questions.stem: A patient is admitted with possible cancer of the ovary. What fact should a nurse know about malignant tumors of the ovary?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are frequently advanced and inoperable by the time they are diagnosed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The respond well to radiation and chemotherapy because of early detection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are easily detected because symptoms appear early in a woman’s life.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are directly related to PID and other infections.

questions.rationale: The high mortality rate for women with ovarian cancer is due to the fact that a malignant tumor of the ovary is asymptomatic until it is advanced.

questions.stem: A mastectomy care plan should address psychosocial problems of the patient. What should this be directed toward?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Caring for the wound and dressings

choices.label: C
choices.text: Educating for methods for controlling edema

choices.label: D
choices.text: Helping the patient express feelings and concerns

questions.rationale: The nurse should gently explore how the patient is feeling about the surgery and encourage her to express her concerns. The other options are significant care concerns but are not psychosocial in nature.

questions.stem: A nurse reads that a patient’s breast tumor is a stage II. What should the nurse realize about this tumor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is smaller than 2 cm, with no positive lymph node involvement and no metastasis evident.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It measures between 2 and 5 cm, with no or one positive lymph node involvement and no metastasis evident.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is larger than 5 cm, with no positive lymph node involvement and no metastasis evident.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It measures between 2 and 5 cm, with positive axillary lymph node involvement and metastasis evident.

questions.rationale: A stage II tumor is between 2 and 5 cm with no or one lymph node positive for cancer and no metastases present. All other options are for different stages of tumors.

questions.stem: A nurse teaching a seminar on breast cancer lists the signs that would alert a woman to the possibility of a tumor. What should those signs include? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Signs of breast cancer that should be investigated are dimpling of the skin of the breast, any discharge from the nipple, any thickening of breast tissue, or a dry rash in the areola. Although it should be investigated, bruising is not a classic sign of breast cancer.

questions.stem: What do the causes for menorrhagia include? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Common causes for menorrhagia include hormonal dysfunction, tumors (both benign and malignant), coagulation disorders, and endometrial hyperplasia. Excessive exercise is far more likely to cause amenorrhea than menorrhagia.

questions.stem: A nurse is aware that a tumor determined to be ER positive indicates that the tumor needs ______ for growth.
questions.rationale: A tumor that is classified as ER positive requires estrogen to grow.

questions.stem: Because small nonnodal metastases may be present, ______ is recommended after a lumpectomy.
questions.rationale: Radiotherapy is recommended after a lumpectomy even when no indication of metastasis is evident because of the threat of small metastases without nodal involvement.

questions.stem: What should a nurse explain to a young man being treated for infertility as the purpose of a semen analysis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Determine the history of sexually transmitted infections.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evaluate the potential for genetic problems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determine whether any urethral obstructions are present.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Microscopically assess the sperm for number and motility.

questions.rationale: Analysis of the semen is performed to assess male fertility based on number, appearance, and motility of the sperm.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the urethral smear?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Screens for human immunodeficiency viral (HIV) infection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Verifies fertility through a sperm count.

questions.rationale: A sterile swab is inserted into the urethra to obtain a specimen to detect sexually transmitted infections.

questions.stem: A patient is placed on estramustine (Emcyt) for prostate cancer. What should the nurse explain as a possible side effect of the medication?
questions.rationale: Gynecomastia is a side effect of estramustine (Emcyt).

questions.stem: When conducting an initial assessment on a 65-year-old male patient, a nurse assesses a mass in the left testicle that on transillumination glows red. What should the nurse note this indicates the presence of?
questions.rationale: A hydrocele will glow red on transillumination, but a hematocele will not. Phimosis is a foreskin that will not retract over the glans, and smegma is a cheeselike substance found under the foreskin.

questions.stem: What should a nurse inform a 50-year-old patient who has been prescribed nilutamide (Nilandron), a testosterone blocker, to expect while taking this drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: His urine will have a fishy odor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Liver functions will need to be monitored.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skin rash will appear on his face.

questions.rationale: The drug nilutamide (Nilandron) is hepatoxic. While taking the drug, the patient will have to have periodic evaluations of his liver function. The patient should also be informed to report any jaundice or darkening of the urine.

questions.stem: A patient has been prescribed flutamide (Eulexin) for the treatment of prostate cancer. What possible side effect should the nurse remind the patient might occur?
questions.rationale: Hot flashes, ED, edema, hypertension, and confusion are some of the side effects of flutamide.

questions.stem: What instruction should a nurse include when giving education about taking sildenafil (Viagra)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No more than two tablets should be taken in a 24-hour period.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nitrates should be taken at least 4 hours before taking Viagra.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tablet should be taken 1 hour before sexual activity.

questions.rationale: Viagra should be taken 1 hour before sexual activity. Erection depends on stimulation. No more than one tablet a day should be taken. Persons taking nitrates should not take Viagra because of the risk of a possibly fatal myocardial infarction.

questions.stem: When completing a health history with a patient, a nurse should encourage the patient to describe the symptoms in objective terms. Which statement is a good example of the description of pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “This spot seems to hurt more right here.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “This pain is the worst pain in the world.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “My pain reminds me of when I hurt my foot last year.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I would say my pain is a 5 on a scale from 1 to 10.”

questions.rationale: Encourage the patient to use descriptive terms such as stinging or aching and ask him to indicate the intensity on a scale from 1 to 10.

questions.stem: What direction should a nurse provide when instructing a patient who is to have a semen analysis? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Collect specimen in a rubber condom.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keep the specimen at room temperature until given to the laboratory.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep the specimen container in warm tap water.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bring the specimen to the laboratory within 4 hours.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abstain from sexual activity 2 to 3 days before the test.

questions.rationale: Sexual activity should be avoided for 2 to 3 days before collection. The specimen should be collected in a clean container, not a rubber condom, and presented to the laboratory within 1 hour of collection.

questions.stem: A nurse is performing the initial assessment on a man with a reproductive disorder. What type of interview techniques should be used to demonstrate a sensitive approach? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pin the patient down for truthful and specific information.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leave sensitive questions until later in the interview.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Share her or his professional opinion.

questions.rationale: The use of open-ended questions encourages the patient to explain his problem in greater detail, especially if sensitive issues are left until later in the interview. Pinning the patient down, sharing professional opinion, and asking why are not therapeutic.

questions.stem: When a significant elevation in the human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) level is noted on a laboratory report, the nurse is aware that this is a marker for ______ cancer.
questions.rationale: An elevation in the hCG is a marker for testicular cancer.

questions.stem: A nurse is reviewing the drugs taken by a 50-year-old male patient. What medication should the nurse recognize as the most probable cause of erectile dysfunction (ED)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antibiotic for an upper respiratory infection

questions.rationale: Vasodilators taken for the control of hypertension frequently cause ED.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient 2 hours after a transurethral resection and immediately reports to the charge nurse the presence of large clots in the catheter and drainage bag. What should the nurse anticipate the physician will order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instill ice water into the bladder.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decrease the amount of fluid in the balloon of the indwelling catheter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply traction to the catheter by taping it to the patient’s thigh.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prescribe a potent vasoconstrictor to reduce hemorrhage.

questions.rationale: Applying traction to the catheter may reduce the bleeding. An increase of fluid in the catheter balloon is also helpful, but ice water will have no better effect than the continuous bladder irrigation that is already in place.

questions.stem: A male student comes to the campus clinic complaining of painful scrotal edema, nausea, vomiting, chills, and fever. What should the nurse recognize these signs and symptoms as being associated with?
questions.rationale: Signs and symptoms of epididymitis are painful scrotal edema, nausea, vomiting, chills, and fever.

questions.stem: Which instruction should increase the comfort of a patient who is recovering from prostatitis?
questions.rationale: Stool softeners may be prescribed to prevent constipation, which is painful with prostatitis. Fluid intake is also encouraged to reduce the risk of constipation.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient indicates he understands the teaching relative to how to perform a testicular self-examination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “It’s not necessary to feel the testes; just look at them in a mirror.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “The best time to do a self-examination is after a shower, when my body is warm.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “It doesn’t really matter when I do it; just do it sometime.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The physician is really the best person to check this for me.”

questions.rationale: The examination is best done after a warm bath or shower.

questions.stem: What should a patient’s home instructions following a vasectomy include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Postoperative care consists of warm sitz baths.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vasectomies should be seen as usually permanent but are sometimes reversible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sexual intercourse should be delayed for up to 3 months.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The surgical procedure may interfere with ejaculation.

questions.rationale: Although a vasectomy can sometimes be successfully reversed, it should be considered permanent.

questions.stem: A mother of a newborn with cryptorchidism asks if this condition will be permanent. What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes, but your child can develop normally with hormone replacement therapy.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “No, several medical and surgical remedies can be performed after your baby’s first birthday.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Yes, but the undescended testicle is still able to function in a normal manner.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “No, the process of sexual maturity will cause it to descend at puberty.”

questions.rationale: Partial or incomplete descent of the testicles may be resolved by medical or surgical implementation. Medical implementation starts between the first and second years of life.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man who had a prostatectomy 3 weeks earlier asks if the ongoing incontinence will ever stop. What should the nurse understand about this procedure in order to answer the patient?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Postprostatectomy incontinence usually clears up in 6 months.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Although the constant dribbling will stop, stress incontinence will continue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Postprostatectomy incontinence frequently requires a second surgery to correct the problem.

questions.rationale: Postprostatectomy incontinence clears up in approximately 6 months in most patients.

questions.stem: After an accident at a track meet, a young male runner is brought to the emergency department complaining of intense pain in his scrotum and nausea and vomiting. What should the nurse suspect based on these initial findings?
questions.rationale: Symptoms of testicular torsion are intense pain, often accompanied by nausea and vomiting.

questions.stem: After a physician has left the room of a 30-year-old man who has been diagnosed with testicular cancer, the patient covers his face with both hands and sighs. What is the nurse’s most therapeutic intervention at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient, “Do you want to talk about your cancer?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leave the room and pull the door closed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Go to the nurse’s station and call the patient’s wife.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complete the patient care as quickly as possible.

questions.rationale: You can help the patient through active listening, providing information, and referring him for counseling as needed.

questions.stem: A nurse is formulating a teaching plan for a patient with acute prostatitis. What information should be included regarding treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There will be a 2-week period with a temporary catheter and the instillation of antibiotics in the bladder.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There will be a 6-week course of antibiotics and anti-inflammatory drugs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There will be a 6-week course of broad-spectrum antibiotics followed by a prostatectomy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There will be a 16-week protocol of sitz baths and the use of a sturdy scrotal support.

questions.rationale: Acute bacterial prostatitis is treated with antibiotics, analgesics, anti-inflammatory drugs, and sitz baths for a period of 6 weeks. Chronic prostatitis requires a 16-week period of antibiotic therapy.

questions.stem: A patient is recovering after surgery to correct a testicular torsion. Which possibility should the nurse caution the young patient about?
questions.rationale: After testicular torsion is corrected, lower sperm counts and infertility may follow.

questions.stem: What can men who have sustained spinal cord injuries with resultant ED use to aid in the ability to have sexual intercourse?
questions.rationale: Penile implants may be prescribed for patients with the inability to initiate, fill, or restore an erection.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a young man who is being treated for sterility and inquires whether the young man had any common childhood disease that may be the cause of sterility. What childhood disease might result in sterility?
questions.rationale: Mumps in a man or pubescent boy may result in acute orchitis or epididymitis. Infections that ascend to the epididymis may result in decreased fertility.

questions.stem: A patient has an elevation in the prostatic-specific antigen (PSA) level from 4 to 6 ng/L. What should the nurse suspect to be the cause?
questions.rationale: PSA levels over 4 ng/L may indicate cancer of the prostate; further studies are needed.

questions.stem: What should instructions relate to for a discharge teaching plan for a patient after undergoing a prostatectomy? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delay of sexual activity for 3 months

questions.rationale: Instruction relative to relieving bladder spasms, catheter care, perineal exercises to help reduce incontinence, and the avoidance of heavy lifting are appropriate. Sexual activity is usually resumed in 6 weeks.

questions.stem: A nurse in the outpatient clinic notes that a patient has been treated for syphilis three separate times in the past 2 years. What should the antibiotic treatment for this patient consist of this time?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Penicillin G today and a follow-up with another injection in 1 month

choices.label: C
choices.text: Penicillin G today and 3 months of oral tetracycline antibiotic medications

choices.label: D
choices.text: Penicillin G today and a 2-month protocol of oral antiviral agents

questions.rationale: The patient who has had syphilis for more than 1 year will need a long-term antimicrobial remedy, as well as an initial dose of penicillin G. Antiviral agents are not used in the treatment of a bacterial disease.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing a history of illnesses for a young woman who suspects she may have a sexually transmitted infection (STI). What specific symptom(s) should the nurse ask this patient if she experienced?
questions.rationale: With an STI, the patient usually complains of genital discharge.

questions.stem: A patient has been diagnosed with herpes simplex virus, type 2 (HSV type 2). What instruction should the nurse provide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid telling anyone about the condition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wash towels and personal items daily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Soak the sores with peroxide every day.

questions.rationale: Inform the patient that the virus can survive on objects such as towels.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient taking acyclovir (Zovirax). Which side effects of this drug should the nurse be alert for?
questions.rationale: Side effects include dizziness, headache, nausea and vomiting, renal failure, and seizures.

questions.stem: A woman diagnosed with gonorrhea is astounded and states that she had no idea that she had an STI. What should the nurse explain about gonorrhea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It produces no symptoms in half of those in the early stages of the infection.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It always produces a foul vaginal discharge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It causes a vaginal chancre that is not easily detected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It may appear to be an upper respiratory infection in the early stages of the infection.

questions.rationale: More than half of those in the early stages of gonorrhea have no symptoms at all. Symptoms of gonorrhea typically occur 3 days to 3 weeks after exposure and are more apparent in men than in women. No chancre is exhibited, as with syphilis, and gonorrhea does not produce a foul discharge.

questions.stem: A nurse giving instruction to a patient with an STI says, “I am supposed to tell you about STIs, but you probably know more about them than I do.” What is this nurse doing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Admitting her own ignorance about STIs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trying to get the patient’s attention

questions.rationale: Judgmental behavior on the part of health care providers discourages people from seeking appropriate medical care.

questions.stem: A patient at the outpatient clinic is reluctant to identify her sexual contacts. Why is the reporting of contacts essential?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Slows transmission and spread of infections.

questions.rationale: Confirmed cases are reported to the health department. The purpose is to identify and treat infected individuals so that transmission can be slowed.

questions.stem: A patient with gonorrhea is taking a protocol of tetracycline antibiotics. Which statement by the nurse is most likely to help overcome patient noncompliance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “You should take all of this medicine.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Failing to take the entire medicine amount will make your disease resistant to it.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You will become sterile if you do not complete the supply of medicines.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “The doctor wants you to take all of this medication.”

questions.rationale: Explain to the patient that medication-resistant disease is a real possibility if the entire amount of the prescription is not taken. Sterility is not related to noncompliance with the medication, but it is related to repeated occurrence of the gonorrhea.

questions.stem: A patient with syphilis is seen at the clinic and complains of body aches, pustules, fever, and sore throat. Which stage of syphilis should the nurse recognize these symptoms identify?
questions.rationale: Symptoms in the secondary stage are body aches, rash, pustules, fever, and sore throat.

questions.stem: Doxycycline (Vibramycin) has been prescribed for a patient who has gonorrhea. What instruction should the nurse provide to the patient before beginning the medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take the medication with food or crackers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Refrain from sexual relations for 4 weeks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Follow-up to determine if the treatment was effective.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep the medication in the refrigerator.

questions.rationale: Follow-up examinations are important to determine whether treatment has been effective to prevent reinfection of the partner.

questions.stem: A couple comes to the emergency department for the treatment of an STI. The man’s presenting symptoms include a creamy penile discharge and frequent urination. The woman has lower abdominal pain and a vaginal discharge. What should the nurse recognize these symptoms to characterize?
questions.rationale: Symptoms in men are penile discharge, thin at first and then creamy, and frequent urination. Symptoms in women are a vaginal discharge and lower abdominal pain.

questions.stem: A pregnant patient with HSV type 2 has a Herp-Test performed in the physician’s office 1 day before she is due to deliver by cesarean section. The test result is negative. What should the nurse know this means?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The delivery must be by cesarean section.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient must start on an antiviral protocol today.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The baby will have to have antiviral medication 24 hours after birth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The delivery may be accomplished vaginally.

questions.rationale: A negative Herp-Test result shows no active viral disease, and the birth can be accomplished vaginally if the physician prefers.

questions.stem: Which method is used to identify organisms of gonorrhea?
questions.rationale: Smears from genital discharge can be studied on a smear.

questions.stem: What instruction should be included by a health educator giving a presentation on how to use condoms correctly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Condoms are 100% effective when used correctly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The effectiveness of condoms deteriorates in heat.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any style and material of condom is safe to use.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use of petroleum jelly will ease application.

questions.rationale: Protect condoms from heat and sunlight to keep them from deteriorating.

questions.stem: A nurse asks a patient to repeat the instructions to evaluate her knowledge about safe sex practices. Which statement indicates an understanding?

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Vaginal intercourse with a properly used condom is safe.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Anal sex with a condom made of latex is a safe sex practice.”

questions.rationale: Body massage is considered a safe sex practice.

questions.stem: A female patient, newly diagnosed with gonorrhea, screams, “I am going to kill my husband. I mean it.” What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Are you sure it is your husband who gave you gonorrhea?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Shall I report your spouse as a sexual contact?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I can understand your anger. How best can you deal with it?”

questions.rationale: Provide an opportunity to talk. Help the patient focus on the source of anxiety with the use of open-ended questions.

questions.stem: What is characteristic of the primary stage syphilis?
questions.rationale: A typical lesion, a chancre, is the first sign of syphilis.

questions.stem: What does diagnosis with the human papilloma virus (HPV) increase a person’s risk for?
questions.rationale: Women with HPV or condylomata acuminate are advised to have annual Pap stain because they are at an increased risk for cervical cancer.

questions.stem: Which antiviral drug is commonly used to relieve symptoms of herpes simplex virus (HSV)?
questions.rationale: No cure is available for HSV infection, but oral antiviral drugs similar to acyclovir (Zovirax) help partially control the signs and symptoms during initial and recurrent episodes.

questions.stem: What does chlamydial infection place a person at greater risk for?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hepatitis B if exposed to it

questions.rationale: Patients who have a chlamydial infection are five times more likely to contract HIV if exposed to it.

questions.stem: A patient at the outpatient clinic who has received an intramuscular dose of ceftriaxone sodium (Rocephin) calls and complains of pain and induration at the injection site. What should the nurse advise the patient to do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Undergo 30 minutes of active exercise to speed absorption of the drug.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Make an appointment at the clinic for evaluation to initiate another drug.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediately come to the clinic for treatment of the allergic reaction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Place a warm compress on the area.

questions.rationale: A warm compress may be applied to the area because these symptoms are the expected results of the injection, not allergic reactions. Another drug is not needed.

questions.stem: What should a nurse caution a patient taking Flagyl for Trichomonas to do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Double the dose if any doses are missed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take the drug on an empty stomach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Abstain from alcohol while taking the drug.

questions.rationale: The use of alcohol while taking Flagyl has serious side effects. Dark urine is expected, doses should not be doubled, and the drug should be taken with a full glass of water.

questions.stem: A patient with a chlamydial infection is taking a 7-day course of doxycycline (Vibramycin). What information should the nurse provide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Return in 1 month for a follow-up culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take the drug on an empty stomach with a minimum of fluid.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Expect genital or anal itching or burning.

questions.rationale: The patient with a chlamydial infection should delay sexual activity until completely clear. Follow-up cultures are obtained 4 to 7 days after the initiation of the drug, and the drug should be taken with food or milk.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that STIs must be reported to the local public health department. Which are considered reportable diseases? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: All confirmed cases of HIV, AIDS, gonorrhea, Chlamydia, and viral hepatitis are reportable.

questions.stem: What body areas might systemic (disseminated) gonorrhea involve? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Systemic gonorrhea may damage all of the body areas mentioned except the eyes.

questions.stem: A nurse recognizes that a patient with an STI may not cooperate in reporting sexual contacts. What fears might prevent reporting? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Infection of others has already occurred.

questions.stem: A patient who is using imiquimod (Aldara) for genital warts asks the outpatient clinic nurse how long she must use the medication. The nurse replies that she must apply the medication for ______ weeks. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: The protocol for Aldara is application three times a day for 16 weeks.

questions.stem: Displaying her hands, a patient asks, “Do you think my liver is OK? Look at all these liver spots!” What is the most appropriate nursing response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “The spots could mean something is wrong; I will make a note of it.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “The spots are normal aging changes and have nothing to do with your liver.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Have you recently been exposed to hepatitis?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Don’t worry about them. They will fade during the winter.”

questions.rationale: Lentigines on sun-exposed areas are called liver spots because of their color; they have nothing to do with the liver or any disease process. They are normal changes of aging.

questions.stem: A confused patient has been restrained because of combativeness and hyperactivity. What skin assessment may occur as a result of the restraints?
questions.rationale: Purpura are purple bruises that resolve very slowly and are usually the result of minor trauma.

questions.stem: What should a nurse ask about when taking the functional assessment of a patient with a skin disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A sore that is slow to heal

questions.rationale: The functional assessment is a search for clues in the occupation and lifestyle of the patient. The other options are reference medical history and system review.

questions.stem: A daughter of an older adult patient who has just returned from surgery is distressed about her father’s pale, cold hands and feet. What is the best response by the nurse after covering the patient with an extra blanket?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Don’t be concerned. It is quite cold in the operating room. Your dad will be warm in a minute.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Older patients like your dad get a little shocky during surgery.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “When patients have blood loss during surgery, superficial vessels close off temporarily, resulting in cold extremities.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “We are watching the disturbed circulation in your dad’s hands and feet very carefully.”

questions.rationale: The 10% of the blood network that is in the skin can be reduced by constriction and shunted to the vital organs.

questions.stem: What information should a nurse provide to a patient with vitiligo receiving phototherapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Expose yourself to the sun for several hours before treatment to acclimate the skin surface.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Wear loose clothing such as sleeveless T-shirts and shorts after the treatment.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Leave off sunglasses after treatment so your eyes can more quickly accommodate.”

questions.rationale: Eight hours before and after each treatment, the patient should wear protective clothing, sunglasses, and sunscreen to decrease added ultraviolet exposure from other sources.

questions.stem: A nurse is screening patients that the plastic surgeon is considering for phototherapy. Which patient should the nurse exclude?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 23-year-old woman who is 3 months’ pregnant

questions.rationale: Persons with lupus erythematosus should avoid exposure to UV light.

questions.stem: Which age-related skin changes should a nurse anticipate when performing a physical assessment on an 80-year-old man? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Small macular lesions at the hairline

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased hair on the helix of the ear

questions.rationale: Increased hair in the nostrils and ear, flattened discolored nails, and seborrheic keratosis are common age-related skin changes. Macular lesions are abnormal.

questions.stem: Which conditions can be improved with negative pressure therapy? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: All ulcers, skin grafts, and dehisced wounds respond well to negative pressure therapy.

questions.stem: When assessing the capillary refill, a nurse should document as normal a refill time of _____ seconds. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: Capillary refill is a method of quick assessment of perfusion to the extremities. A normal capillary refill time is 3 to 5 seconds or less.

questions.stem: A nurse collecting tissue for a Tzanck test should: _____ (Select the appropriate interventions and place the steps in sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Open the lesion with a hypodermic needle.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the specimen in a culture tube and take it to the laboratory.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Saturate the sterile swab with exudates.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Place a pressure dressing on the lesion.

questions.rationale: The nurse washes the lesion, punctures the lesion with a needle, saturates a sterile cotton swab, places the swab in a culture tube, and takes the collected tissue to the laboratory. A pressure dressing is not needed.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pruritus. Which implementation can the nurse perform without a physician’s order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply topical corticosteroids to affected areas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bathe the patient in an oatmeal bath.

questions.rationale: Application of a lotion or lubricant to unbroken skin may be done without an order.

questions.stem: Which action should a nurse implement to make a patient with atopic dermatitis more comfortable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient to wear loose clothing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Add alcohol to the bath water.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provide a diet low in fat.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increase the room temperature between 78 F and 80 F.

questions.rationale: Loose clothing and a cool atmosphere allow the skin to stay cool and reduce sweating. Alcohol is drying to the skin.

questions.stem: Which sign or symptom suggests that a patient with skin breakdown is developing a systemic infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lesion on the patient’s leg that is swollen and warm to the touch

choices.label: B
choices.text: Temperature that has risen to 101 F

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood pressure that has risen from 126/84 to 130/86 mm Hg

choices.label: D
choices.text: Request by the patient for medication for severe itching

questions.rationale: A rise in temperature is a systemic response. Normal blood pressure, warmth, swelling, and itching are not evidence of a systemic skin infection.

questions.stem: Which is an appropriate implementation for a patient with severe psoriasis who has an altered self-concept?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reassuring the patient of a quick remission

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reminding the patient to bathe often

questions.rationale: To touch, interact, and care attentively for a disfigured patient communicates acceptance.

questions.stem: A patient with severe psoriasis who is to be treated with the systemic drug methotrexate sodium anxiously asks, “Is this cancer drug safe? Are there some side effects I need to know about?” What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Yes, methotrexate is used to treat cancer and psoriasis, and it has no severe side effects.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “No, it is not a cancer drug, but you should ask your physician about concerns regarding your therapy.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “We use this drug to treat many kinds of patients, including patients with cancer. You will have periodic blood tests.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “I don’t know if it is used with patients with cancer, but the drug can be used when conditions are as severe as yours.”

questions.rationale: Methotrexate is an immunosuppressive drug used to treat psoriasis that is nonresponsive to other protocols. Periodic blood tests are performed to assess for leukopenia. The other options either do not answer the patient’s question or offer erroneous information.

questions.stem: A family member of an older patient with severe dermatitis says, “I was always so careful to bathe him every day. I guess I just wasn’t careful enough.” What is the best response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Dermatitis is not caused by poor hygiene.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Don’t worry; we will bathe him thoroughly while he is here.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You will have a chance to do better when he is back at home.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “You shouldn’t feel like the skin condition is your fault.”

questions.rationale: Dermatitis is not a condition of poor hygiene. Implying that the family member is responsible for the condition is belittling and not therapeutic.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for an obese patient who has been bedridden for a long time and who has a high risk for infection. Where is the best location for the nurse to assess for the moist red lesions of Candida albicans?
questions.rationale: C. albicans infection appears most often in skinfolds.

questions.stem: What information is most essential for a nurse to gather when interviewing a young woman who is taking the drug isotretinoin (Accutane) for acne?
questions.rationale: Accutane can cause severe fetal deformities.

questions.stem: An excited mother of a teenage boy with severe acne furiously reports to the nurse, “I’ve told him a thousand times he should bathe more often! I’ve kept after him about all that junk food he eats. I jump on him when I see him squeezing his zits. I tried to get him to scrub his face three times a day!” Which statement indicates the most likely cause of the boy’s acne?
questions.rationale: Squeezing the lesions may cause them to spread and push the infection deeper into the follicles. Poor personal hygiene, eating junk food, and the need for facial scrubs are myths.

questions.stem: A patient who has undergone treatment for herpes simplex virus type 2 (HSV type 2) expresses relief that she is cured. What should the nurse include in her teaching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Daily douches of Burow solution are needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: HSV is permanently cured by acyclovir (Zovirax).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sexual partners are now safe from infection from her.

choices.label: D
choices.text: HSV lies dormant and can be triggered without any sexual contact.

questions.rationale: The virus goes dormant but can recur. Herpes is always present.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old patient comes to the emergency department with extreme pain and itching in the hip and leg and has herpetic vesicular lesions on the left hip. What should the nurse inquire about patient exposure to?
questions.rationale: Chickenpox is a virus that lies latent in the neural sheath and can be activated as shingles in older adults.

questions.stem: A physician asks a nurse to take a smear from herpetic lesions in an older patient’s hip to diagnose the disorder. What is the most probable test that will be performed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Culture and sensitivity test to a bactericide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tzanck test to test for viral culture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Complete blood count to assess the white blood count for response to a pathogen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Titration for the strength of the pathogen

questions.rationale: The Tzanck test rapidly confirms the specific virus. The results are available sooner than they would be from a culture.

questions.stem: How does cutaneous T-cell lymphoma differ from squamous cell and basal cell carcinomas?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Has a cause unrelated to sun exposure.

questions.rationale: Cutaneous T-cell carcinoma appears in areas protected from the sun. All three neoplasms can metastasize, and all three neoplasms can be treated by radiation or topically.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for an adult patient with extensive burns on the front of the trunk, including the genitalia, and the fronts of both legs. How should the nurse document the burn size using the rule of nines?
questions.rationale: Per the rule of nines, the front of the trunk equals 18%, the fronts of the legs equal 18%, and the genitalia equal 1%.

questions.stem: Which assessment by an emergency department nurse most indicates that a burn patient might be at risk for respiratory impairment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Burns on the face and neck

questions.rationale: Sooty sputum is the most indicative. Facial burns and flaring nares are not conclusive in themselves. Respiration rate of 18 breaths/min is normal.

questions.stem: What should a nurse be sure to frequently assess when caring for a burn patient with eschar formation around an entire arm?
questions.rationale: Eschar that encompasses a limb can compromise circulation.

questions.stem: During the first 24 hours after a burn, fluid replacement is the treatment priority. Which assessment should alert the nurse that the fluid protocol is ineffective?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Crackles in the lower left lobe

choices.label: D
choices.text: Significant edema in the burn area

questions.rationale: Decreased urinary output indicates that poor perfusion to the kidney still remains. Temperature elevation and edema are to be expected. Crackles in a patient who is dormant are not causes for alarm.

questions.stem: A nurse is alert for the expected fluid shift in the patient who was burned 24 hours earlier. _____ (Place the events in the appropriate sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood is shunted from the kidneys to compensate for a loss of fluid volume.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypoproteinemia causes fluid to move from the bloodstream to extracellular space

questions.rationale: Hypoproteinemia causes a fluid shift from the bloodstream to extracellular space, causing generalized edema; fluid volume deficit occurs; blood is shunted from the kidneys and gastrointestinal tract to make up for the fluid loss in the circulating volume; and urine output is decreased.

questions.stem: A nurse reads in a patient’s history that the patient has experienced otalgia. How should the nurse interpret this term?
questions.rationale: Otic- is the root word for ear, and -algia is the root term for pain of any type.

questions.stem: A nurse is assisting with a caloric test and notes that the specific patient response that indicates a hearing disorder is a problem in the labyrinth. Which response did the nurse witness?
questions.rationale: When warm or cold water is introduced into the ear, the appearance of nystagmus is a positive indication that the hearing problem has its cause in the labyrinth.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old patient has normal age-related changes in his ear. What change should not be considered a normal change in the aging patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dry and wrinkled skin on the auricle

questions.rationale: Otitis externa is an outer ear infection and therefore an exception. The other three options are normal age-related changes.

questions.stem: A patient reports that her hearing loss has become more severe over the past 3 months. The clinic nurse makes arrangements for an evaluation for a hearing aid. What health care provider should provide this service?
questions.rationale: Audiologists assess patients for hearing aids. The other specialists treat ear, nose, and throat (ENT) disorders.

questions.stem: When a patient has a suspected vestibular disorder, the physician orders an electronystagmography test. Which instruction should the nurse include when educating the patient about this test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use tea or coffee on the morning of test.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Electrodes will be placed on the scalp.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Air will be blown into the external ear.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient should have nothing to eat or drink (NPO) 3 hours before the test.

questions.rationale: Electronystagmography is used to detect vestibular lesions and requires a 3-hour period of NPO before the test. Coffee and tea should also be avoided before the test.

questions.stem: A nurse assessing the results of a Rinne test sees the notation of BC > AC. How should the nurse translate this result?
questions.rationale: When the bone conduction (BC) is greater than the air conduction (AC), the results of the Rinne test will read, BC > AC, which means the patient has a conductive hearing loss. The normal finding for the Rinne test is that AC is greater than BC (AC > BC).

questions.stem: A patient undergoing a Weber test says that the sound is louder in her left ear. What should this result indicate?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nerve damage from listening to loud music

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blocked ear canal in the right ear

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conductive hearing loss in the left ear

questions.rationale: With the Weber test, a conductive hearing loss is determined by the sound being heard loudest in the affected ear.

questions.stem: Which instruction should a nurse include when providing patient teaching information for a patient who will be self-administering ear drops for an ear infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tip the affected ear up and keep it in that position for several minutes after instilling the medication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keep the medication in the refrigerator to preserve it. Instill the medication with the affected ear tilted upward.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Touch the dropper to the opening of the ear canal to ensure that the drops are correctly instilled.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Warm the ear drops and then tilt the head downward.

questions.rationale: The head is kept in an upward position to ensure that the drops penetrate deep into the external ear.

questions.stem: What nursing action should be implemented when irrigating a patient’s ear?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Straighten the ear canal and irrigate with a large-tipped bulb syringe.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct the solution to the middle of the canal to avoid damaging the ear.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use a body temperature solution and have the patient hold a basin under the ear while directing the solution toward the top of the canal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Repeat the irrigation with hotter water.

questions.rationale: The irrigation is done with warm water using a small-tipped syringe. The flow is directed upward. If the cerumen does not wash out, the procedure can be repeated but with the same water temperature.

questions.stem: A nursing report on a newly admitted patient who is profoundly deaf says that the patient is confused and difficult to assess because she does not appropriately respond to questions or sometimes fails to respond at all. What should be the first action of the oncoming nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Consider asking the physician to assess the patient for dementia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the patient to determine whether her hearing aids are in.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Report to the physician that the patient is exhibiting signs of the sundown syndrome.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient’s medications to check for an overdose.

questions.rationale: Profoundly deaf persons can be mistakenly assessed as being confused or disoriented when not wearing their hearing aids.

questions.stem: Which patient problem would the nurse prioritize for a patient having ear surgery?
questions.rationale: Patients who have had ear surgery are at risk for vertigo, fluid accumulation, or pressure in the operative ear. Because of the surgery and potential postoperative conditions, the patient may be at risk for a fall.

questions.stem: What significant instruction should a nurse include to a patient being discharged after ear surgery?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Shampoo your hair with baby shampoo.
