questions.rationale: The patient should avoid blowing the nose to prevent back pressure in the eustachian tube. The patient should take stool softeners, limit activity until balance returns, and delay shampooing.

questions.stem: A patient with diabetes says that he needs a hearing aid because he cannot hear well, and everything sounds garbled and distant. What type of hearing loss should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: A patient with long-term diabetes may have a sensorineural hearing loss that is not helped by hearing aids.

questions.stem: Which is the most appropriate concern when considering the impact of a hearing deficit when planning care for a child who has been diagnosed with a hearing impairment?
questions.rationale: The loss of hearing and the mild stigma associated with hearing impairment place the newly diagnosed child at risk for social isolation.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old patient has had age-related changes in the cochlea. What is the most appropriate nursing action for the nurse to implement?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Communicate with the patient in writing.

questions.rationale: Assisting the patient when ambulating will diminish the risk of a fall. Changes in the cochlea will cause dizziness and ataxia.

questions.stem: A patient who has been taking opioid medication for postoperative pain exhibits pinpoint pupils. Which anatomic portion of the eye has been affected by the medication?
questions.rationale: The choroid of the eye contains the pupil and iris.

questions.stem: Which portion of the eye makes it possible for a person to see in a darkened environment?
questions.rationale: The eye uses rods to accommodate to dim light. Cones are the color receptors, the optic nerve transmits all sensory input from the eye to the brain, and the macula is an oval-shaped yellow spot near the center of the retina that mediates clear, detailed vision.

questions.stem: When being interviewed, a 50-year-old patient says that he cannot see the newspaper as well as he used to. What is the reason this patient vision has changed from near to far?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ciliary muscle changes the pupil size.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The lens of the eye changes shape as the ciliary muscle contracts and relaxes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clouding of the vitreous humor has occurred.

questions.rationale: Accommodation or adjustment of the lens by contraction and expansion of the ciliary muscle allows an individual to see far or near.

questions.stem: During the initial assessment of a very thin patient at the eye clinic, a nurse notes that the patient has very prominent eyes. What medical diagnosis might the nurse find in this patient’s history?
questions.rationale: The appearance of the patient and the prominence of the eye (exophthalmos) would lead the nurse to inquire about a thyroid disorder such as hyperthyroidism.

questions.stem: When asked about his vision, a patient says that the last time he had it tested, his vision was recorded as 20/50. What does this mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He can read at 20 feet what a person with normal vision can read at 50 feet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He can read at 50 feet what a person with normal vision can read at 20 feet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He needs to be 50 feet from objects to see them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He can see objects the best between 20 and 50 feet.

questions.rationale: The Snellen eye chart is read at 20 feet. The last line the patient can read with no more than two errors is recorded. This patient was able to read the 50-foot line at 20 feet, which means that he is reading at 20 feet what a person with normal vision can read at 50 feet.

questions.stem: Which intraocular pressure reading obtained by tonometry indicates a patient being evaluated for a visual impairment does not have glaucoma?
questions.rationale: The normal intraocular pressure is between 10 and 20 mm Hg. If the patient had glaucoma, the intraocular pressure would be abnormally high.

questions.stem: What does a pneumatonometric study of the eye require?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A pneumotonometer to be placed into the eye

choices.label: C
choices.text: A puff of air directed at the surface of the eye

choices.label: D
choices.text: An applanation performed with a slit-lamp microscope

questions.rationale: A pneumotonometer directs a puff of air at the surface of the eye, measuring intraocular pressure by measuring the resistance to the air. The eye is anesthetized before the evaluation.

questions.stem: What is an appropriate nursing action to implement when performing eye irrigation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient to tip up her head and run the irrigation fluid over her open eye.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct the irrigating fluid from the inner canthus to the outer canthus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not allow the patient to blink.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Place the irrigating syringe directly onto the corner of the eye and allow the fluid to move across the eye.

questions.rationale: The direction of the flow should be from the inner canthus to the outer canthus.

questions.stem: What information should a nurse include when providing information to a patient using topical eye medications?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Look upward and drop the medication into the inner canthus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pull the lower eyelid down and drop the medication into the conjunctival sac.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold both eyelids open and drop the medication onto the sclera.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tilt the head to the side and drop the medication into the outer canthus.

questions.rationale: The eye drops should be dropped into the lower eyelid, and the nurse should press the tear duct to slow absorption.

questions.stem: What does electroretinography measure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A fluorescein dye is injected intravenously (IV) into a vein in the arm, and the retina is observed as the dye circulates.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Electrodes are placed on the scalp, each eye is stimulated, and retinal activity is assessed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A small plunger is used to apply pressure on the sclera while the retinal vessels are evaluated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A contact lens electrode is placed on the eye and exposed to flashes of light to evaluate the retinal response.

questions.rationale: A contact lens electrode is placed on the eye, and retinal activity is assessed as lights are flashed into the eye. The other three options describe fluorescein angiography, visual-evoked response, and tonometry.

questions.stem: What information should a nurse relay to a patient when providing education about protecting vision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: After 40 years of age, eye examinations should be performed every 2 years.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Crusted eyelids on awakening are caused by decreased tear production.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Floaters are a sign of eye infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blurred vision without pain is temporary eye strain.

questions.rationale: Eye examinations every 2 years are recommended for persons older than 40 years of age. All the other options are indications that the person should consult a physician for an eye disorder.

questions.stem: How should a nurse assist a visually impaired patient to ambulate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold the visually impaired person by his or her nondominant arm and walk side by side.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hold the nondominant hand, wrap the arm around his or her waist, and walk side by side.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allow the visually impaired person to hold the helper’s arm, with the helper slightly ahead.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allow the visually impaired person to hold the shoulder of the helper and walk slightly behind the helper.

questions.rationale: Allowing the visually impaired person to walk slightly behind the helper and holding the helper’s arm is the most effective way to guide someone who is visually impaired.

questions.stem: A newly diagnosed patient with macular degeneration flings her book at the television set and furiously says, “I can’t read this blasted book, and I can’t see what is on the stupid TV!” How should the nurse define this behavior?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ineffective management of a therapeutic regimen

questions.rationale: Frequently, a grieving process accompanies the realization that deteriorating vision and ultimate blindness are inevitable with macular degeneration.

questions.stem: What is the correct term to use for a patient with a vision disorder?
questions.rationale: The term visual impairment is a medically accepted term to use for patients with a vision loss.

questions.stem: Which implementation is appropriate in the care plan for a visually impaired person?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Leaving the bed in the highest position

choices.label: C
choices.text: Announcing your presence when you enter and leave the room

choices.label: D
choices.text: Leaving the radio on all the time to help the patient know the time of day

questions.rationale: The nurse should announce her or his presence in the room and address the patient before touching him or her. The bed should be in the lowest position, and the door should be open to avoid social isolation.

questions.stem: Which patient behaviors should alert a nurse to a possible hearing deficit? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fails to respond when spoken to.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Turns the good ear to the speaker.

questions.rationale: Pulling at the ear is not a signal for hearing loss; all of the other options are.

questions.stem: Which common characteristics might a patient with conductive hearing loss display? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hears sounds but has difficulty understanding speech.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Has improved hearing with hearing aids.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Has a history of diabetes mellitus.

questions.rationale: Persons with conductive hearing loss can hear in a noisy setting and can have improved hearing with the use of hearing aids. Persons with conductive hearing loss speak at a normal or soft volume because they can hear themselves. Muffled sounds and a history of diabetes would be associated with sensorineural hearing loss.

questions.stem: A patient complains that his hearing aid is not working. What actions should a nurse implement to assess the device? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check to see if the device is turned on.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clean the earpiece and remove cerumen clogged in the vent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Open the earpiece to see if the microphone wire is connected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Examine the interior of the earpiece for water.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Validate that the battery is correctly placed.

questions.rationale: Cleaning the earpiece to remove clogged cerumen and checking the device to see if it is turned on and if the battery is placed correctly are all good options. The earpiece should not be opened. If the hearing aid is still not working, it should be evaluated by the dealer.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses an 80-year-old patient for age-related changes to the eye. What potential changes should the nurse anticipate? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Eyelash diminution is not a consistent finding in older adults. All of the other options are common eye changes related to advancing age.

questions.stem: What makes up the refractive media of the eye? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The retina is not part of the refractive media. All of the other options are components of the refractive media.

questions.stem: What actions should a nurse implement when assessing a patient’s accommodation? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold his or her finger approximately 20 inches in front of the patient’s eyes.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Move his or her finger slowly toward the patient’s nose.

questions.rationale: Assessment for blinking is not part of the accommodation assessment. All of the other options are part of the accommodation assessment. The nurse holds his or her finger approximately 20 inches in front of the patient’s eyes and slowly moves the finger toward the patient’s nose, assessing for pupillary constriction and convergence.

questions.stem: When planning care for a patient who cannot perceive or interpret sounds, a nurse takes into consideration that the patient may have a(n) ______ hearing loss.
questions.rationale: The inability to perceive or interpret sounds is referred to as a central hearing loss.

questions.stem: A nurse uses a diagram to show the physiologic sequence of hearing. After entering the external ear, the sound is then conducted through the: ______ (Arrange the options in sequence. Do not separate answers with a space or punctuation. Example: ABCD.)
questions.rationale: The sound impulse, after entering the external ear, is conducted through the tympanic membrane; into the malleus, incus, and stapes; through the oval window; into the sensory receptors in the inner ear; and then through the acoustic nerve to the brain.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old patient who has had an enucleation asks when he can get his prosthesis fitted. In approximately how many weeks should this patient expect to be fitted?
questions.rationale: After an enucleation, the patient is fitted with a prosthesis in 1 month.

questions.stem: A patient with glaucoma is taking a beta-adrenergic blocking agent, timolol (Timoptic). For which potential side effect should the nurse assess the patient?
questions.rationale: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents cause bronchospasm and tachycardia.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old patient reports that he must hold his paper farther and farther away from his face to read it. What is the nurse’s most informative response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “You are describing myopia. Glasses will help you read.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “You may have astigmatism, but your eyes will finally adjust.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You have presbyopia. Nonprescription reading glasses will help you.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “An eye infection may be the problem. Check with your physician for medication.”

questions.rationale: Presbyopia is a normal age-related change. Changes in the ciliary muscles cause the condition. Corrective lenses such as bifocals are used to correct this visual change.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that laser-assisted in situ keratomileusis (Lasik) and photorefractive keratectomy (PRK) are methods to correct refractive errors surgically. What do these procedures reshape?
questions.rationale: Both surgical procedures are used to reshape the cornea. The clinician will need to determine which structure of the eye will need surgery to correct the vision.

questions.stem: A patient reports to a home health care nurse of having cloudy vision and seeing spots and halos around lights. What should the nurse suspect based on these patient symptoms?
questions.rationale: Cataracts are the cause of cloudy vision and seeing spots or halos.

questions.stem: How does closed-angle glaucoma differ from open-angle glaucoma?
questions.rationale: Closed-angle glaucoma has an acute onset with eye pain and other systemic symptoms, such as nausea and vomiting. Reducing the intraocular pressure is an ocular emergency.

questions.stem: What is the cause of glaucoma?
questions.rationale: Glaucoma is caused by an increase in intraocular pressure.

questions.stem: A patient in the emergency department complains of severe pain in his eye and is seeing halos around lights and feeling nauseous. Which diagnosis should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Sudden onset of acute eye pain with nausea and vomiting and halos around lights are all symptoms of angle-closure glaucoma. The acute pain is caused by sudden blockage of the fluid channels in the eye.

questions.stem: Which surgical implementation is most effective with retinal detachment?
questions.rationale: Scleral buckling is used to hold the retinal repair in place. The band is left in place to keep together the layers of the eye tissue.

questions.stem: A patient who has had surgery this morning for cataracts is now going home. What should the nurse include when providing discharge instructions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid lifting anything heavier than 5 lb.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Do not wear an eye shield at night.

questions.rationale: After cataract surgery, the patient should sleep on the unaffected side with the eye shield in place. He or she should avoid heavy lifting and use stool softeners to prevent straining.

questions.stem: A 94-year-old patient is receiving gentamicin sulfate (Garamycin) in a continuous intravenous (IV) infusion. The nurse adds to the nursing care plan the patient problem “Potential injury.” What nursing action should be implemented?
questions.rationale: Reduced urine output would cause the drug to stay in the system rather than being excreted, which could result in a drug saturation. Gentamicin is ototoxic and can cause hearing impairment.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old patient reports to a nurse that although she has cleaned her ears with cotton-tipped applicators for weeks, she still cannot hear her television unless the volume is loud, and she misses a great deal of conversations. What should the nurse anticipate when examining her ears?
questions.rationale: Obstruction of the external canal with cerumen will result in a hearing loss. Cleaning the ears with something such as an applicator will pack the cerumen in the canal.

questions.stem: Which patient problem should take priority in planning care for a patient with Ménière disease?
questions.rationale: The patient problem that should take priority is that of preventing injury to the patient. A patient with Ménière disease is prone to falls because of dizziness.

questions.stem: What information should a nurse stress when teaching a patient with Ménière disease about managing the disorder?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoiding the use of alcohol and tobacco

questions.rationale: The use of alcohol and tobacco products affects the amount of fluid in the middle ear, worsening the symptoms of Ménière disease. The patient with Ménière disease should drink adequate fluid, use antiemetic medications as needed, and conserve energy during the day.

questions.stem: A patient comes to the primary care clinic complaining of a head cold and ear pain with drainage. What should the nurse suspect this patient is experiencing?
questions.rationale: Acute otitis media is connected with colds and drainage from the ear. A hearing loss may be experienced as well, but the pain and drainage place the need to intervene for the infection first.

questions.stem: A young woman being admitted to the clinic service states that all the members of her family have been hard of hearing. She says her hearing loss became more pronounced when she was pregnant. What term explains this type of hearing loss?
questions.rationale: Otosclerosis is hereditary, develops in young women, and worsens with pregnancy.

questions.stem: What should a nurse include when educating a patient with Ménière disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “When you feel dizzy, just stay in bed and take your medications.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Decrease your sodium intake and take your diuretic medication between attacks.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Vestibular rehabilitation might help, and you can still drink your morning coffee.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Your vertigo will get better if you take your medications. You won’t need any relaxation techniques.”

questions.rationale: A low-sodium diet and diuretic medications between attacks will prevent edema, which could cause an attack.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old patient reports having difficulty hearing in crowds but can hear just fine at home with his wife. What hearing disorder should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Presbycusis is a conductive hearing loss associated with normal aging and is caused by changes in the cochlea.

questions.stem: During an intake physical examination, a patient reports that he has been taking 10 aspirin tablets a day for his arthritis. What question should the nurse ask based on this information?

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Have you noticed deafness in just one ear?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Do you have ringing in your ears?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Do you experience dizziness when you stand?”

questions.rationale: A ringing in the ears (tinnitus) is an indication of aspirin toxicity. The patient should be advised to stop taking aspirin.

questions.stem: ______ is the creation of a small opening in the tympanic membrane to reduce pressure and allow fluid to drain.
questions.rationale: Myringotomy is the creation of a small opening in the tympanic membrane to reduce pressure and allow fluid to drain.

questions.stem: ______ are often formed when excess cells accumulate in a cyst or sac formed near the tympanic membrane.
questions.rationale: Cholesteatomas are often formed when excess cells accumulate in a cyst or sac formed near the tympanic membrane.

questions.stem: A patient is given anxiolytic medications for a mental disorder. What type of approach is this considered?
questions.rationale: The biologic approach attempts to manage the physiologic effects of mental illness using medications.

questions.stem: A patient states that he feels angry at work for no reason and often yells at his co-workers. The therapist asks the patient to describe events and then tells the patient to try different strategies to cope with these angry outbursts. What type of approach is this considered?
questions.rationale: The interpersonal approach helps the patient develop new coping skills.

questions.stem: What is the best action for the nurse to implement to effectively listen therapeutically to a patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Concentrate on the patient and not think of responses to the patient while he or she is speaking.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Determine the cause of the patient’s problem while the patient is speaking.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient why he thinks he feels the way he does.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell the patient that you have had similar experiences.

questions.rationale: To listen therapeutically, the nurse needs to concentrate on the patient and refrain from making up responses to the patient while he is speaking.

questions.stem: A nurse speaking to a patient who is depressed says, “So what you are saying is that you are feeling very sad today.” What is this considered?
questions.rationale: By reflecting the meaning of the patient’s statement, the nurse is using clarification. This technique validates that the therapist understands what the patient is saying, and it provides validation for the patient.

questions.stem: When performing a mental status examination, a nurse notes that the patient keeps repeating, “I didn’t do it. I didn’t do it. I didn’t do it.” This response would be an example of which one of the components of the mental status examination?
questions.rationale: Repetitive statements and thoughts are considered to be obsessive. This would be an element of the thought content component of the mental status examination.

questions.stem: A patient says, “I just don’t think I can keep going on. I just want it all to end.” The nurse assesses that this patient has suicidal ideation. What is the nurse’s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Do you have any thoughts of harming yourself?”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “You are just depressed. When you feel better, you won’t think that way.”

questions.rationale: The best response to a patient who may have suicidal ideation is to ask a simple direct question to determine the patient’s true intent. Having done that, this should be reported at once. All suicidal threats, even mild ones, should be reported and taken seriously.

questions.stem: What should a nurse suspect a patient diagnosis might be when the patient states, “I often feel restless, have a tight sensation in my chest, and have an increased heart rate at times”?
questions.rationale: The patient is reporting symptoms that reflect signs of an anxiety disorder.

questions.stem: A group of nursing students are taking their first major examination. What should the nursing instructor expect the students might experience?
questions.rationale: The students are usually experiencing mild anxiety, which can be beneficial as a motivator.

questions.stem: A patient who is admitted with posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) says he had a very stressful experience when in high school and has never really recovered. What is the most appropriate nursing action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encourage the patient to talk about what caused the traumatic event.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Guide the patient in relaxation techniques to distract him when flashbacks occur.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provide sleeping medication so that he can sleep at night.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allow the patient to talk about his condition as often as he likes.

questions.rationale: Patients with PTSD should not be encouraged to talk about the traumatic event. The patient should learn relaxation techniques to distract themselves when anxiety symptoms begin. Sedation does not address the problem of anxiety.

questions.stem: A patient admitted with a conversion disorder after an automobile accident insists he is paralyzed, although no physical cause for his paraplegia can be found. What is the best nursing response when the patient asks the nurse to push him to his room?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “There is nothing wrong with your arms. Roll yourself to your room.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “I will help you to walk to your room. I know you can walk.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Let me lift the foot rests so you can move your chair with your feet.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “OK. I am going that way myself.”

questions.rationale: The patient is experiencing dysfunction without a discernible cause, but this dysfunction is very real to him. The less attention brought to his coping mechanism, the better.

questions.stem: Which combination of medications could be used to treat an anxiety disorder?
questions.rationale: A combination of an antidepressant and anxiolytic medication is recommended as the appropriate drug therapy for the patient with an anxiety disorder. Xanax, Librium, Ativan, Valium, and Klonopin are all anxiolytic medications. Haldol is a neuroleptic medication, and Effexor is an antidepressant.

questions.stem: A patient who went away to college in September returns in October, thinking that he is a drummer in a popular rock band. What is this most likely a manifestation of?
questions.rationale: Schizophrenia occurs in adolescence or early adulthood. The patient experiences delusions that are characteristic of schizophrenia in a classic stress situation, which may have been the precipitating event that caused the thought disorder.

questions.stem: A patient is receiving large doses of chlorpromazine (Thorazine) and begins to exhibit extrapyramidal signs of involuntary muscle movement. Which classification of drugs should the nurse anticipate will be added to the patient’s protocol?
questions.rationale: Antiparkinsonian drugs will control the muscle movement and drooling that are the major signs of neuroleptic toxicity.

questions.stem: A long-time patient with schizophrenia in the inpatient unit has developed involuntary movements of his tongue. What has this patient developed?
questions.rationale: Tardive dyskinesia is a side effect of continued use of neuroleptic medications to control schizophrenia.

questions.stem: Which is not considered as a probable cause of mood disorders?
questions.rationale: A traumatic event in childhood could, most likely, cause PTSD. All other options are causes of mood disorders.

questions.stem: A patient with bipolar disorder is being treated with tricyclic medications. What should the nurse inform the patient to expect when teaching information concerning side effects?
questions.rationale: Orthostatic hypotension and urinary retention are side effects of tricyclic antidepressants. These drugs are commonly used to treat the depressant effects of bipolar disorders.

questions.stem: A patient is hyperactive with mania and has poor nutritional intake due to hyperactivity. What implementation is most appropriate when considering this diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offer nutritious finger foods and high-protein milk shakes to eat on the go.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spoon-feed the patient while the patient is seated at the table.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arrange for one large meal at noon to be eaten in the company of others.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Limit fluid intake to make the patient hungry at mealtime.

questions.rationale: Patients with mania are on the go. Nutritious foods that can be eaten while the patient is moving around will meet their dietary needs. The patient’s short attention span prevents him or her from sitting long enough to eat or to be fed by spoon. Limiting fluids is contraindicated for the patient who is hyperactive.

questions.stem: A patient is brought in from the emergency department after telling the physician that he is a relative of the president of the United States. He says that he should not be detained because he has important business to attend to that involves national security. He is dressed in a bright coat with plaid pants and gets very angry when you try to question him. What is this patient is experiencing?
questions.rationale: Inappropriate dress, self-aggrandizement, hyperactivity, and frustration are elements of a manic episode.

questions.stem: While in the dayroom, one of the patients becomes very agitated and begins to threaten to harm the other patients and is directing violence at the other patients and staff. What is the most appropriate nursing implementation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decrease the stimuli and use restraints if all other measures fail.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Offer to call the physician and ask another staff member to call security.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remove harmful objects and try to perform relaxation exercises with the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Restrain the patient and do not allow him or her to eat or drink anything by mouth.

questions.rationale: Because the patient is threatening to harm others, decreasing stimuli will be helpful to decrease the behavior and, if everything else fails, restraints will be needed. Restraints should be applied according to current policy. Patients who are being restrained need to have frequent checks and their nutritional and elimination needs monitored.

questions.stem: Since the summer weather began, a patient taking lithium for manic episodes has been walking daily. What important instruction should the nurse provide to this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stay in the shade when walking.

questions.rationale: Lithium toxicity can occur if the patient becomes dehydrated. The therapeutic range of lithium is narrow; consequently, toxicity to this drug is common.

questions.stem: A patient who has a history of episodes of road rage thinks that she is a very good driver and does not understand why she keeps being told she is a poor driver. She is losing her license now, and she tells the nurse that she is feeling very unhappy and abandoned. She feels like she might hurt herself. The nurse realizes that the patient is exhibiting which personality disorder?
questions.rationale: Difficulty controlling anger and an unstable sense of self are elements of a borderline personality disorder.

questions.stem: A co-worker is noted to be very organized. However, you see that he is always making lists and citing the rules of the organization. He wants all his projects to be perfect and gets very upset when things happen that make him miss his deadlines. The co-worker is exhibiting signs of which personality disorder?
questions.rationale: Patients who have a preoccupation with perfectionism, orderliness, and control have an obsessive-compulsive personality disorder. These patients may be anxious and attempt to maintain the perfectionism. The patient data in this question demonstrate the elements of obsessive-compulsive disorder.

questions.stem: What is a common cause of delirium?
questions.rationale: Neoplasms and Alzheimer disease are two causes of delirium. All of the other options are causes of dementia.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient with an acute stress disorder. What characteristics of this disorder should the nurse assess? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Individuals who have been overcome by an acute stress disorder exhibit diminished orientation, reality testing, and personal awareness, and they frequently experience amnesia. Irritability is not part of the syndrome.

questions.stem: A nursing instructor is explaining the difference between illusion and hallucination. Which examples provided by students in the class indicate an illusion? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: A car backfiring being perceived as gunfire

choices.label: B
choices.text: The television news being perceived as someone talking to you

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hearing God’s voice directing you to drive your car off the road

choices.label: D
choices.text: Seeing your dead spouse smile at you from a flower

choices.label: E
choices.text: A spot on the wall being perceived as a spider

questions.rationale: An illusion has an external stimulus that causes an erroneous translation. Anyone, even a person with intact sensorium, can have an illusion. An example of an illusion is seeing water on the highway, which is really only heat waves. A hallucination has no external stimulus. An example of a hallucination is a person with alcoholism seeing spiders crawling on the ceiling.

questions.stem: When a nurse asks questions like, “What day is today?” or “What time is it now?” the nurse is testing the patient’s ______.
questions.rationale: The sensorium is also referred to as orientation. The sensorium orients the person to person, place, and time.

questions.stem: A patient has been diagnosed with alcoholism. A nurse tells him that he has a physical illness with a genetic predisposition to alcoholism, and the only effective treatment is total abstinence from alcohol. This type of approach characterizes which theory?
questions.rationale: The biologic approach is based on the theory that there is a faulty physiologic process that causes a predisposition to an addiction. This theory includes the belief that such an addition is incurable and only total abstinence will be effective.

questions.stem: During the taking of a medical history, a patient who is addicted to heroin reports he only uses the drug a few times a week. What is the nurse’s best response when trying to disclose more precise information?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “OK. You only use heroin two times a week?”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “What do you mean when you say ‘a few?’”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Are you saying that in a week’s time would you use heroin only two times?”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Rate your weekly usage on a scale of 1 to 15.”

questions.rationale: When trying to get a health history on a patient with a substance abuse problem, the best method is an open discussion of the drug use. The discussion should be open and nonjudgmental. By requesting a scale, the patient’s response will be more informative.

questions.stem: A patient who has alcoholism with a history of heavy drinking is brought to the inpatient psychiatric unit saying that he does not know where he is or what day it is. What should the nurse suspect he is exhibiting?
questions.rationale: Blackouts are common with heavy drinking over time. The patient data support that the memory loss is associated with the alcohol use and that blackouts would be an expected outcome.

questions.stem: A nurse is discharging a patient with a pulmonary disorder to return home with her family. She has had a difficult time while in the hospital and has experienced withdrawal from tobacco. When the nurse tells her that community resources are available to help her to stop smoking, she says, “That’s OK. I can stop whenever I want to.” What does this exemplify?
questions.rationale: Denial is a common defense mechanism used by substance abusers.

questions.stem: A patient with an alcohol addiction says, “My drinking is all my wife’s fault. She makes me so crazy I just have to have a drink.” What does this exemplify?
questions.rationale: The patient in this scenario is projecting. He is blaming his wife for his drinking problem, rather than accepting that the drinking is his problem.

questions.stem: Which information about a patient should lead a nurse to suspect substance abuse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Abnormal liver function test result and a gastrointestinal (GI) bleed

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive syphilis screening result and varicose veins

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fungal infection and a potassium level of 4.2. mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased albumin level and creatinine level of 1.2 mg/dL

questions.rationale: An abnormal liver function test result and a gastrointestinal bleed indicate substance abuse.

questions.stem: A urine drug screen is ordered for a patient suspected of a driving while intoxicated (DWI). What drugs are included in this screening?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD), Valium, and Percocet

choices.label: B
choices.text: Crack, heroin, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)

questions.rationale: LSD, Valium, and Percocet have the correct combination of an illicit drug, an anxiolytic medication, and an opioid.

questions.stem: An alcoholic is brought into the emergency department in an intoxicated state at 2000. The day shift nurse assesses the patient to exhibit tremors, have increased blood pressure, and agitation at 0800 rounds. What do these signs and symptoms indicate?
questions.rationale: The signs of early withdrawal from alcohol are agitation and elevated vital signs. These warning signs usually occur 6 to 12 hours after the last drink.

questions.stem: Which syndrome of alcoholism is related to thiamine deficiency?
questions.rationale: Wernicke encephalopathy is a vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency. If not treated with vitamin supplements, the condition can progress to a more serious form.

questions.stem: A wife of a man with long-term alcoholism wants to help her husband quit drinking. The nurse tells her that an initial approach might be for a group of friends and family to confront the patient with his alcoholism. What should this group be prepared to indicate during this confrontation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He needs help; his drinking is out of hand.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are fed up with him and will no longer be his friend.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They reject his drinking but think he is a valuable person.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He is still their friend even if he does not stop drinking.

questions.rationale: The implementation for encouraging an alcoholic to go into treatment consists of a group of people telling the alcoholic that they think his drinking is destructive, but they do not reject him as a person.

questions.stem: A nurse recommends to an alcoholic that he join Alcoholics Anonymous. What is true about this organization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Based on a 12-step approach with a strong religious base

choices.label: B
choices.text: A social group of ex-drinkers who befriend one another in the process of maintaining sobriety

choices.label: C
choices.text: A religious support group that assists alcoholics during rehabilitation

choices.label: D
choices.text: An anonymous group of sponsors who offer help to alcoholics

questions.rationale: Alcoholics Anonymous is a group of people who come together to offer support to each other to stay sober. It has a religious base, recognizes a higher power, and uses a 12-step approach to sobriety.

questions.stem: What should be avoided to prevent the accidental activation of Antabuse?
questions.rationale: Mouthwash contains alcohol and can trigger the effect of an Antabuse reaction. The alcoholic taking Antabuse needs to be aware of the hidden alcohol in some commonly used mouthwashes and other over-the-counter drugs.

questions.stem: A nurse in the admissions unit is informed that a patient is being brought in who has been using “ice.” What should the nurse assess?
questions.rationale: Persons who use “ice,” a form of methamphetamine, are frequently violent while under its influence. The effect of “ice” can last as long as 12 to 14 hours.

questions.stem: An LPN is assisting with the development of a nursing care plan for a patient who has been using cocaine for 5 years. What should the nurse consider will most commonly need to be dealt with in this patient?
questions.rationale: Cocaine users can be depressed for as long as 2 years after quitting drug use. An antidepressant such as bupropion (Wellbutrin) is helpful in treating the depression.

questions.stem: What might the positive effects of marijuana be used to treat?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diet control in morbidly obese patients

choices.label: C
choices.text: Air hunger in patients with end-stage emphysema

questions.rationale: Marijuana has been studied for use to reduce nausea and vomiting in patients with cancer. Marinol (Dronabinol) is a marijuana derivative that is currently used to reduce chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting.

questions.stem: A nurse is counseling a pregnant cocaine abuser. What should the nurse inform this patient that she is placing her baby at significant risk for?
questions.rationale: Babies born to mothers who are addicted to cocaine have a higher incidence of hyperactivity and neurologic problems.

questions.stem: What is the rationale for using methadone in the treatment of heroin abuse?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Does not give the “rush” that a person addicted to heroin is looking for.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Is a synthetic opioid, which makes it less addicting.

questions.rationale: Methadone, as an extended-release medication, does not give the “rush” that addicts enjoy. It maintains the opioid level in the body but does so at a steady state and decreases cravings.

questions.stem: Narcan has been given to a person with respiratory depression who is addicted to heroin. What should the nurse be alert for indications of?
questions.rationale: Narcan is an opioid antagonist that causes the opioid to fall from receptor sites. When the antagonist is given, the sudden loss of the opioid causes acute withdrawal to occur.

questions.stem: Two treatment plans for abusers of alcohol and drugs are similar—90 meetings in 90 days and 12-step programs. What is the major difference in the success of the two types of programs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One program uses a 12-step approach, and the other program has a religious base.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Heroin addicts have a higher success rate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alcohol abusers have an easier time staying with the program.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relapse rates for patients using drugs other than alcohol alone are much higher.

questions.rationale: One difference between alcohol and drug rehabilitation programs is that drug abusers will have a lower success rate. Alcohol abusers have a higher rate of success when they follow the Alcoholics Anonymous program.

questions.stem: Which implementation can be used to help prevent relapse in a patient who has a substance abuse problem?
questions.rationale: Teaching stress management techniques to patients who have a substance abuse problem will assist them in managing the conflict and stress in their daily lives, which previously triggered the substance abuse.

questions.stem: In some cases, patients will use drugs or alcohol to treat the disturbing symptoms of a psychiatric disease. What can occur in this scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A mixed-drug reaction, which may heighten the effects of both drugs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased psychiatric symptoms, which makes the psychiatric condition harder to treat

choices.label: C
choices.text: An accidental overdose by mixing alcohol with anxiolytic medications, antipsychotic medications, or antidepressants

choices.label: D
choices.text: An increase in psychiatric symptoms because the psychiatric drugs become less effective in the presence of alcohol

questions.rationale: Combining alcohol and psychiatric drugs, anxiolytics, or antidepressants may cause an accidental overdose by increasing the central nervous system effects.

questions.stem: A nurse indicates that a urine sample is needed for screening in a case of DWI. What actions should be implemented? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Collection and witnessing by a staff member of the same gender

choices.label: B
choices.text: Documented with a chain-of-custody form signed by all who handle the specimen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep specimen under secure conditions if temporary storage is necessary

choices.label: D
choices.text: Never be out of sight until someone from law enforcement takes it

choices.label: E
choices.text: Place in a specially marked container

questions.rationale: The specimen, if placed in secure storage, does not have to be in sight nor does it need a specially marked container.

questions.stem: A nurse documents signs of Wernicke encephalopathy in a patient with long-term alcoholism. What do these signs include? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: The signs of Wernicke encephalopathy do not include projectile vomiting.

questions.stem: What does alcohol abuse in the older adult place him or her at risk for? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Alcohol abuse in the older adult makes the person at risk for falls, malnutrition, cirrhosis, and bone demineralization.

questions.stem: What is true regarding hair analysis done on an alcoholic? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Is not reliable on treated or dyed hair

choices.label: D
choices.text: Can be informative in the treatment of a short-term abuser

questions.rationale: Hair analysis requires sensitive technology, can be used on treated or dyed hair, and can assess a relapse. Hair analysis cannot confirm addiction, only use, and it is not informative on short-term abusers.

questions.stem: What factors make the adolescent extremely vulnerable to substance abuse (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Adolescents are prone to substance abuse because of peer pressure and their basic egocentrism, which interferes with good judgment, prediction of consequences, and impulse control.

questions.stem: A nurse explains that a test that can detect substance abuse for up to 1 year after only 2 or 3 days of use is performed on ______.
questions.rationale: Hair can indicate drug abuse up to a year even if the abuse was only a matter of days.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A patient is present with signs of severe airway obstruction. What term accurately describes the high-pitched noise the patient is exhibiting with inhalation?
questions.rationale: Signs of severe airway obstruction are poor or no air exchange, poor or no cough, high-pitched noise known as stridor on inhalation, respiratory distress, cyanosis, inability to speak, inability to move air, and clutching the neck.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: Standing in a fast-food line, the person in front, while munching on a cookie, begins to cough heavily, takes deep inspirations, and waves his arms around wildly. What should be the nurse’s first action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Start rescue breathing as quickly as possible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Start chest compressions as quickly as possible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Do nothing at this point as long as air is exchanged.

questions.rationale: When a person is choking but alert enough to attempt to cough and force the obstruction up and out by himself, allowing him to do so alone is best because more expelling force occurs that way. Only when the person shows signs of not being able to breathe beyond the obstruction should abdominal thrusts be applied.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: What is the initial intervention for an unconscious patient who is not breathing according to one-person CPR principles, as taught and practiced by professional nurses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lift the jaw to clear the airway.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remove patient clothing to visualize the chest.

questions.rationale: With one-person CPR, when the patient is unconscious and not breathing, the first thing to do is to call for help.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: While ambulating, a patient gasps and drops to the floor unconscious with no pulse or respiration. When is the nurse aware that brain cells begin to die?
questions.rationale: Without adequate perfusion, the brain cells begin to die in 4 minutes.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A nurse follows the protocol of SAMPLE when speaking to a victim of a fall in the parking lot of the hospital. What does the P stand for?
questions.rationale: The acronym SAMPLE that guides the victim interview means allergies, medications, past illness or pregnancy, last food and drink, and events related to injury.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: What instructions should the nurse provide for immediate treatment for epistaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: “Stand still, lean your head back so that the blood won’t get all over everything, and pinch your nose shut for at least 10 minutes.”

choices.label: B
choices.text: “Stand still, lean your head forward, and pinch your nose tightly for at least 10 minutes.”

choices.label: C
choices.text: “Sit down on a solid surface, lean your head forward to let the blood run out, and then pinch your nose closed for at least 30 minutes.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: “Sit down on a solid surface, lean your head forward so you don’t choke on the blood, and pinch your nose shut for at least 10 minutes.”

questions.rationale: Blood from a nosebleed in the anterior portion of the nasal cavity will usually stop with pinch pressure within 10 minutes. Blood from a nosebleed should not be swallowed.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: Which condition may complicate the assessment of an older adult patient with a suspected head injury?
questions.rationale: Sensory deficits, circulatory disorders, and communication problems make it more difficult to assess an older adult patient with a suspected head injury.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A nurse is called on to assist a neighbor who needs first aid. What should the nurse know is the legal responsibility for this action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Is legally bound to help in any way possible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Is expected to demonstrate the same skill, knowledge, and care that would be provided by other nurses in the same community with the same credentials.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Has no legal responsibilities outside the hospital setting and would be held accountable for nothing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Can legally perform any aid skill, even those not allowed the nurse in the hospital.

questions.rationale: U.S. laws protect nurses when they act in the same manner as others licensed at their level would do in the same circumstances.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A nurse comes upon a traffic accident where injured, unconscious people are lying on the highway. What should the nurse be aware of regarding the sanctioning of first-aid interventions in this scenario?
questions.rationale: Most states have Good Samaritan Acts, which protect voluntary caregivers from malpractice claims.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: What will the nurse giving discharge instructions to a patient who is severely allergic to insect stings caution the patient to do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wear bright colors to repel insects.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply perfume liberally as a protection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dress in sleeveless, easily removeable garments.

questions.rationale: An emergency kit with Benadryl or injectable epinephrine or both are recommended. Insects are attracted by bright colors and perfume. Arms and legs should be covered with clothing.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A patient is admitted to the emergency department after having been bitten on the hand by a black widow spider. Which nursing intervention should the nurse initiate?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wrap the hand in a warm compress.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Seat the patient upright in a chair.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevate the patient’s hand above his or her heart.

questions.rationale: Neurotoxins frequently cause anaphylaxis with severe respiratory distress and seizure. Therefore, the patient should be protected from falls, and the hand kept cool and below the heart to delay the spread of the toxin.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A nurse who is assisting victims of an automobile collision has already placed a towel secured with a belt on a bleeding leg wound. What action should the nurse take when the towel becomes saturated?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remove the towel and use the belt as a tourniquet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remove the towel and place the victim’s jacket over the wound and secure it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reinforce the towel with the victim’s jacket.

questions.rationale: Direct and continuous pressure is the intervention for a bleeding wound. The original dressing should not be removed but should be reinforced.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: An accident victim comes to the emergency department with an open chest wound. What should the nurse apply to the wound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Occlusive dressing taped on four sides

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vented dressing, taped on three sides

questions.rationale: A vented dressing taped on three sides allows no more air to enter the pleural space but allows the expanding lung to push air out. A four-sided dressing allows the trapped air to remain and possibly collapse the lung.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A homeless person is brought to the emergency department after having been found asleep on a park bench under a layer of snow. He has a rectal temperature of 95 F. Which additional symptoms should the nurse anticipate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diminished breath sounds and inadequate chest expansion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shivering, decreased heart rate, and increased blood pressure (BP)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased irregular heart and respiratory rates and decreased BP

questions.rationale: A patient with hypothermia will continue to chill as his or her vital signs deteriorate.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A mother brings in her 2-year-old who has ingested gasoline 1 hour earlier. What should the nurse implement following the initial assessment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prepare to administer syrup of ipecac.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turn the patient on his or her stomach to induce vomiting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prepare to administer Milk of Magnesia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prepare to administer bowel lavage and cathartics.

questions.rationale: Bowel lavage and cathartics will rid the body of the petroleum product. Inducing vomiting when the patient has consumed petroleum products is contraindicated. Ipecac is no longer recommended, and Milk of Magnesia will not be effective.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: While at the local pool on an extremely hot day, a nurse is called on to care for a woman who has evidently developed heat exhaustion. What is the initial nursing care for this person?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Move the patient into an air-conditioned or shaded area.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give her several glasses of ice water.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cover her with a wet towel.

questions.rationale: The goal for treatment of heat exhaustion is to cool the body initially and slowly in an air-conditioned or shaded area. After the patient is moved, applying wet towels and giving cool drinks are appropriate.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A patient comes to the emergency department with an evisceration after a knife attack. What is the best nursing intervention related to the exposed bowel?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gently replace the bowel into the abdominal cavity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place a sterile wrapped sandbag on the abdomen to prevent further evisceration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place the patient in a high Fowler position to allow the bowel to drop back into the abdominal cavity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cover the bowel with a moist saline dressing.

questions.rationale: The nurse protects the tissue from further injury and drying out by the application of a sterile saline dressing.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A patient visits the industrial nurse after an accident on the factory floor, which has amputated three toes. What is the most effective nursing intervention for the amputated toes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rinsing with normal saline and placing on a sterile towel

choices.label: B
choices.text: Placing toes as they are on ice in a sterile container

choices.label: C
choices.text: Placing the unwrapped toes in a saline bath

choices.label: D
choices.text: Placing the saline-wrapped toes in a plastic bag in a saline bath

questions.rationale: The toes should be wrapped in a saline dressing and placed in an airtight bag in a cool saline bath. The toes should not be in direct contact with ice or be placed in the freezer.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: Six hours after a beach picnic on a hot afternoon, a young man enters the emergency department with nausea and vomiting, headache, and diarrhea. On interview, the patient says that he ate potato salad and tuna fish sandwiches. What should the nurse suspect is the causative organism for these symptoms?
questions.rationale: Staphylococcus aureus incubates in undercooked foods, especially eggs and mayonnaise, and can cause gastrointestinal symptoms in as little as 6 hours after ingesting the food.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A child is brought to the emergency department with several deep dog bites on her legs. After cleaning the wounds, what should the nurse be primarily concerned with ascertaining?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The rabies status of the animal

choices.label: D
choices.text: The child’s allergy to the rabies vaccine

questions.rationale: Tetanus prophylaxis should be confirmed so that it can be brought up to date, if necessary. The whereabouts of the dog will be the responsibility of the proper authorities, who will also observe it for rabies. Inoculation for rabies is not an immediate concern unless the bites were on the head or face and can be delayed until the dog is found to be rabid or not.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A patient is brought to the emergency department having had a rock chip embedded in the right eye. What should be the nurse’s initial action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Turn the patient to the left side and prepare to remove the chip.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flush the right eye with normal saline.

questions.rationale: Stabilizing the rock chip is essential to prevent further damage; it should not be removed. Flushing the eye is done for chemical burns. Patient should not be kept flat. Covering both eyes is recommended to prevent eye movement, which could cause further damage.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A hospital visitor cuts herself on the arm and is bleeding profusely. What should be the immediate treatment by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Call any physician and immediately send the visitor to the emergency department.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply direct pressure to the arm with sterile dressing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take the visitor’s blood pressure and pulse.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immobilize the injured arm and send the visitor immediately to the emergency department.

questions.rationale: The first actions should be to stop the blood flow, protect the wound from infection, and call for more help or assist the visitor to the emergency department.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A young man is staggering out of a partially closed doorway to a garage, where a car is running inside. He speaks slowly in a very low voice, telling the nurse that he fell asleep in the car with the engine running. What is the immediate action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the person to lie flat right where he is; begin rescue breathing. If the nurse sees anyone else, the nurse should ask the individual to call someone who knows CPR.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lead the patient away from the garage and call for help. Stay with the patient and continually assess level of consciousness and respirations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have the patient lie flat. Keep the patient still and call for help.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have the patient breathe deeply and continually into a brown paper bag. Assess pulse, respirations, and color of conjunctiva. Check the patient’s pockets for a cellphone to call 9-1-1.

questions.rationale: The man is still conscious enough to speak, but slowly, and therefore does not yet need CPR. Fumes from the garage are entering the air around them. Therefore, getting the nurse and the man away from the fumes is necessary. The man may, at any time, lose consciousness. Sitting down would prevent a fall; consequently, the nurse should call for help. Staying with the young man reassures him and lessens his anxiety response.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: What should the emergency department nurse anticipate on the initial assessment of a patient with carbon monoxide poisoning?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respirations will be less than 10 breaths/min.

questions.rationale: Carbon monoxide binds to the hemoglobin more readily than oxygen, which causes hypoxemia. The mucous membranes will be cherry red, and the blood pressure will be elevated because of hypoxia.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A nurse in the emergency department knows that tissue damage has probably occurred in a person with hypothermia when a rectal temperature of _____  F is assessed. (Use numeric characters only.)
questions.rationale: When a patient has a rectal temperature of 95 F, tissue damage from hypothermia has probably occurred.

questions.chapter_title: First Aid, Emergency Care, and Disaster
questions.stem: A nurse has assessed the patient with a left-sided head injury and records this information: decreasing level of consciousness; slow pupillary response on the right side; blood pressure, 167/80 mm Hg; previous blood pressure, 160/72 mm Hg; and gradually decreasing respiratory and pulse rates. The nurse is aware that these assessments are indicators of increased ______. (Use the full name, not the acronym.)
questions.rationale: All of the listed assessments indicate increased intracranial pressure.
